a: Vendor-reported efficiency is not a wage-weighted task census.

rho: Current adoption is not a direct measure of five-year implementation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External businesses and households buy annual, monthly, quarterly, or repeat professional engagements. Revenue may be hourly, fixed-fee, subscription, value-priced, or a mix.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive finance units, shell entities, non-control investment vehicles, and firms whose work is solely nonrepeat project consulting are excluded.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. CPA firms serving external clients through recurring or repeat audit, tax, accounting, bookkeeping, payroll, client-accounting, and related advisory engagements, in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: S3 measures AI use cases and vendor-reported time savings across accounting, tax, audit, and advisory rather than the wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task mix of U.S. NAICS 541211 LMM firms. I map its evidence on reconciliations, transaction coding, document analysis, return preparation, research, reporting, and workflow triage to exposed task families, then bound for professional judgment, client interaction, and final review. This proxy can overstate exposure where audit and advisory wages dominate, and understate it where routine tax and bookkeeping work dominates.
caveats: The source combines firm sizes and accounting settings rather than isolating NAICS 541211.
Vendor-reported efficiency is not a wage-weighted task census.
a.change_evidence: Obtain anonymized time-and-billing extracts by service line from LMM CPA firms, paired with pre-and-post implementation staffing and throughput for tax, audit, bookkeeping, and CAS workflows.
a.rationale: The occupation is rich in structured documents, transactions, reconciliations, research, and recurring reporting. S3 documents automation and augmentation across those workflows, while retaining human review in audit and tax. The base treats a substantial share of current labor as exposed but not all professional work as substitutable.

d5.bridge: S2 projects national employment for accountants and auditors over a ten-year interval, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 541211 service basket over five years. I use its occupation outlook and stated drivers as a directional demand proxy, with the base converted to a five-year midpoint. It can overstate service demand if productivity reduces purchased labor and understate demand from advisory, tax complexity, and recurring compliance work.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity.
The BLS projection includes all employing industries for the occupation rather than only CPA offices.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain constant-price service-line revenue and engagement-volume data from AICPA MAP panels, tax software providers, and audit-practice benchmarking for LMM CPA firms.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued demand from economic activity, complex tax and regulation, and financial-record preparation and examination. The range is deliberately modest because the requested measure holds service quality and price constant while automation changes delivery.

caveats: No public source tabulates recurring-revenue eligibility at the specified EBITDA band.
Annual tax and audit engagements are treated as repeat service, although retention can vary by client.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level revenue survey that separates recurring, annual-repeat, and project-only revenue for NAICS 541211 firms in the LMM EBITDA band would directly test eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition centers on CPA establishments that audit and attest and permits related bookkeeping, tax preparation, and payroll processing. Those offerings are normally annual or ongoing external engagements. The deduction excludes the minority of LMM firms whose service mix is solely nonrepeat projects or lacks a transferable operating practice.

o.bridge: S2 describes routine accounting automation alongside continued accountant demand, and S3 documents human review, professional judgment, and advisory roles in accounting. I translate these occupation and profession-wide statements into the share of the future service quantity still needing an accountable external CPA-firm operator. The result may be too high if low-complexity clients self-serve with software, and too low if regulation and assurance requirements preserve human accountability.
caveats: Neither source measures future operator-required service quantity for the frozen lens.
The result differs materially by client complexity and by tax, audit, bookkeeping, and advisory mix.
o.change_evidence: Measure client churn to self-service products and the share of completed engagements requiring licensed review, sign-off, or advisory contact across LMM CPA firms.
o.rationale: Routine preparation, coding, and reconciliation can be automated or self-served, but attest, tax, exception handling, advice, liability, and client trust preserve a large accountable-operator role. The base leaves material exposure to software substitution without assuming the profession disappears.

q.bridge: S5 measures pricing practices in client-accounting-services respondents, not the discounted five-year retained share of AI benefit across all NAICS 541211 services. I map fixed and subscription-style fees to temporary operator retention of efficiency gains, then reduce the result for fee review, scope management, hourly billing, competition, and client sharing. It may overstate retention where tax and audit fees are regularly repriced, and understate it for well-scoped fixed-fee recurring engagements.
caveats: CAS is a subset of CPA-firm services and may have more recurring fixed-fee revenue than audit and tax practices.
Pricing form is an imperfect proxy for realized five-year benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: Collect engagement letters, pricing schedules, annual realization reports, and post-automation renewal outcomes from a representative LMM CPA-firm sample by service line.
q.rationale: Fixed pricing supports retention of operating efficiencies between repricing events, while annual fee review and a remaining hourly component constrain durable capture. The base assumes meaningful, but incomplete, retention after customer and competitive sharing.

rho.bridge: S4 measures finance and accounting respondents' current agent use, stated five-year expectations, and adoption barriers; S3 documents the staged implementation requirements in accounting. I translate those broader finance-and-accounting signals to the share of the exposed opportunity that LMM CPA firms can operationally implement in five years. It may overstate implementation because CPA-client confidentiality and audit liability are stricter than some respondent settings, but may understate adoption as accounting-specific platforms mature.
caveats: The Deloitte poll covers finance and accounting, not only CPA firms.
Current adoption is not a direct measure of five-year implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal AICPA or PCPS survey showing service-line deployment, workflow coverage, controls, and realized staffing changes at LMM CPA firms would materially narrow this estimate.
rho.rationale: Adoption is plausible across common workflows, but confidence, integration, data hygiene, privacy, controls, staff training, and human review constrain how much opportunity becomes operational. The base allows broad workflow use without assuming autonomous professional judgment.

caveats: The sources show market activity and succession relevance, not the probability of a qualifying transfer for this precise firm population.
Announced acquisitions and private-equity investments are not a census of LMM control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction database matched to firm revenue or EBITDA, plus AICPA or state-society owner-age and succession-plan survey microdata, would permit a direct LMM five-year transfer estimate.
s5.rationale: The base is a bounded five-year judgment for qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM firms. It reflects an active consolidation and succession market, while recognizing that visible transactions skew toward larger platforms and many small practices continue independently or close without a transferable operation.

records.principal_driver: Repeat external accounting work has many structured workflows that can be automated or augmented while preserving a role for accountable professional review.
records.principal_weakness: The most consequential uncertainties are the LMM transfer rate and the speed at which pricing and self-service software pass AI benefits through to clients.

caveats: S1 was reachable but did not render the cited code-specific definition. This weakens the supporting rationale for e, but e is already an explicit ESTIMATE; downgrading that citation does not change the MODEL_CONDITIONED action, and the resulting F factor is not close enough to a base-tier threshold for this source defect alone to meet the materiality test.

The a and rho bases are broad profession-level proxy judgments rather than measurements of wage-weighted exposure and five-year implementation in LMM CPA firms. S3, S4, and S9 support the direction and named constraints, and the packet states the population mismatch and plausible error directions, but the numeric bases remain weakly identified.

The q range is inferred from pricing form rather than observed benefit retention. S5 shows that CAS fixed fees are commonly reevaluated annually, and S6 only establishes a gradual profession-wide pricing shift; neither source directly supports a five-year retained-share base of 0.60. The bridge and caveats disclose this limitation, and the available evidence does not establish a corrected base low enough to change the base tier or action.

The s5 estimate is informed by visible consolidation and succession evidence that is concentrated in announced deals and larger firms. Neither S7 nor S8 supplies the qualifying LMM transfer denominator, but the record labels s5 as ESTIMATE and explicitly identifies the missing transaction evidence.

The d5 and o values translate occupation-wide employment and human-review evidence into CPA-office service quantity and operator need. Those transformations are disclosed as PROXY, but d5's 1.01-1.05 range is comparatively narrow given the occupation-to-industry and employment-to-service-demand bridges.

The lens covers materially different audit, tax, bookkeeping, payroll, CAS, and advisory mixes while marking heterogeneous=false. The narrative acknowledges this service-mix variation, so the flag is a presentation weakness rather than undisclosed favorable lens selection or a tier-changing defect.

records.title: Offices of Certified Public Accountants

caveats: a: Occupation mix is judgment-adjusted from a 4-digit NAICS aggregate (541200) that includes CPA/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments, not measured directly for 541214; BLS OEWS does not publish occupational detail at the 541214 6-digit level.

a: The BLS occupational-decline figure for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is a net employment projection (net of business growth, consolidation, and automation combined), not a pure task-exposure measure, so it overstates AI-specific exposure to an unknown degree.

a: This proxy uses a coarser NAICS level than the QCEW-sourced, 541214-specific wage data underlying the dataset-injected labor-share input, so the two are not drawn from perfectly matched populations even though both nominally describe this industry.

rho: No direct survey of AI-adoption timelines among independent payroll bureaus was found; this is bounded judgment built from adjacent evidence (vendor roadmaps, liability structure), not a measured implementation rate.

rho: Faster large-vendor AI shipping could pull small-bureau adoption forward, or could instead accelerate disintermediation of bureaus entirely (captured in `o`) rather than being 'implemented' by the bureau itself; that risk is carried in `o`, not double-counted here.

e: No public data source enumerates what share of 541214 LMM-band firms are franchise-affiliated, reseller/agent-only, or otherwise structurally impaired for a clean control transfer; this is judgment.

e: Franchise agreements are not necessarily disqualifying under the lens (a franchise territory can still undergo a genuine control transfer via resale to a new franchisee), but they add transaction friction and consent risk not present in fully independent bureaus.
s5: Owner-age evidence is drawn from CPA/accounting firms, not payroll bureaus specifically -- a population proxy, not a direct measurement.

s5: Deal-flow evidence is drawn substantially from one acquirer (Vensure/PrismHR) whose targets blend PEO/ASO and payroll-processing business models; the pure-541214 slice of that activity is not separately reported.

s5: The Rosenberg survey figures are from a 2018 publication; more recent AICPA retirement-wave commentary suggests the skew has not reversed, but current-year percentages are not independently confirmed here.

q: No bureau-level renewal-price panel was found; the qualitative claim of price/margin compression is well supported, but the specific retained-share number is bounded judgment, not a measured pass-through rate.

q: PEPM pricing ranges cited are current retail figures for the small-business market broadly, not specifically for LMM-band, more-complex clients, who likely see less commoditized pricing and possibly higher retention.

d5: Market-size trackers used here are not consistently scoped (some blend PEO, HCM-software, and payroll-processing revenue); the 541214-specific portion of demand is not separately reported by any source found.
d5: This is a nominal-dollar-market proxy for a real-quantity construct; the price/volume decomposition is judgment, not measured.
o: No direct measurement of the self-serve/embedded share of total industry volume was found; both bounding forces are well documented qualitatively but the net split is judgment.

o: Legal liability remaining with the employer is a reason clients might value an accountable operator's expertise, but it is not proof they will keep paying for one -- some clients may accept the residual liability themselves in exchange for lower self-serve pricing, which would pull this below the base case.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring subscription-like billing to external small- and mid-size business clients, almost always per-employee-per-month (PEPM, typically a small monthly base fee plus roughly $5-17 per employee per month) or per-payroll-run fees tied to headcount and complexity (multi-state withholding, garnishments, benefits deductions). Billing is fixed/volume-linked rather than cost-plus or hourly, with contract renewal (commonly annual) as the main repricing event.
lens.excluded_activities: Offices of Certified Public Accountants and other accounting/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments for which payroll is incidental to a broader accounting practice (541211/541213/541219, which BLS/Census group with 541214 under NAICS 541200 but which are excluded from this lens); Professional Employer Organizations and other co-employment/employee-leasing arrangements (NAICS 561330) even though economically adjacent and sometimes bundled with payroll bureaus commercially; in-house corporate payroll departments; and pure payroll software publishers with no service-bureau client relationship (NAICS 511210-type activity).
lens.included_activities: Establishments primarily engaged in collecting client hours-worked, pay-rate, and deduction data and using it to generate paychecks, payroll registers/reports, and payroll tax filings/deposits for external client businesses on a recurring, repeat basis, per the NAICS 541214 definition. Includes independently owned and franchise-model boutique payroll bureaus (e.g., locally operated territories of national payroll-franchise concepts) operating in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.

a.bridge: Source population is BLS OEWS national occupation employment/wage estimates for NAICS 541200 (Accounting, Tax Preparation, Bookkeeping, and Payroll Services) -- confirmed via the BLS OEWS national industry list to be the finest NAICS level BLS publishes occupational detail for; no separate 541214 breakout exists. That 4-digit aggregate is dominated by Accountants and Auditors (31.7% of the group's 1.1M employment, $93k mean wage) -- a higher-skill, more judgment-intensive occupation than the ones that actually staff a stand-alone payroll-processing bureau. Transformation: reweight toward the occupation mix judged representative of a payroll-service establishment specifically (payroll/timekeeping clerks, bookkeeping/accounting clerks, customer service reps, data entry, with smaller software-support and sales/management shares), informed by O*NET task content for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks (43-3051.00) and by BLS Employment Projections showing that occupation shrinking 16.7% (161,100 to 134,200) between 2024 and 2034 -- one of the steepest declines of any occupation BLS tracks, versus +3.1% for all occupations. Direction of error: using the unadjusted 541200 mix would understate `a` for actual payroll-services establishments, since it dilutes the payroll-clerical core with less-automatable CPA/audit work.
caveats: Occupation mix is judgment-adjusted from a 4-digit NAICS aggregate (541200) that includes CPA/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments, not measured directly for 541214; BLS OEWS does not publish occupational detail at the 541214 6-digit level.

The BLS occupational-decline figure for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is a net employment projection (net of business growth, consolidation, and automation combined), not a pure task-exposure measure, so it overstates AI-specific exposure to an unknown degree.

This proxy uses a coarser NAICS level than the QCEW-sourced, 541214-specific wage data underlying the dataset-injected labor-share input, so the two are not drawn from perfectly matched populations even though both nominally describe this industry.
a.change_evidence: A BLS OEWS or Employment Projections special tabulation breaking NAICS 5412 down to the 541214 6-digit level (or a commercial staffing-pattern extract, e.g. Lightcast, at that granularity) would move this from PROXY to OBSERVED. A time-and-motion or task-time-allocation study inside actual mid-size payroll bureaus (vendor-supplied or via a trade body such as the National Payroll Reporting Consortium / PayrollOrg) would sharpen the wage-weighting materially.
a.rationale: Core production work in a payroll bureau -- timecard/hours intake, wage and deduction calculation, paycheck/report generation, standard tax-filing preparation, first-line client support -- is structured, rules-based, and already partially automated by conventional (pre-AI) payroll software; AI/LLM tooling extends that into exception handling, anomaly detection, natural-language client Q&A, and reconciliation write-ups. BLS's own long-range occupational projections already price in a large piece of this: Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is one of very few occupations nationally projected to shrink by double digits over the next decade, which BLS commentary and industry sources attribute chiefly to software/automation displacement. Judgment-heavy, liability-bearing, or relationship-dependent tasks (new-client onboarding advice, non-standard payroll situations, tax-notice defense, sales) are judged materially less exposed, which keeps the base below the occupation-level decline rate.

d5.bridge: Source population is total US payroll-services/payroll-outsourcing market-size and growth research (Mordor Intelligence: USD 8.44B in 2025 to USD 11.61B by 2031, 5.47% CAGR; separately, IBISWorld and other trackers cite figures ranging from roughly USD 15.8B to USD 80.9B for overlapping but inconsistently scoped definitions of 'payroll services,' reflecting differing inclusion of adjacent HCM-software and PEO revenue). Transformation: these are nominal-dollar market-size CAGRs, which blend real volume growth, price change, and scope drift across trackers, while d5 requires constant-price, constant-quality quantity growth for the current service basket specifically. Direction of error: because per-employee pricing is under visible downward competitive pressure (aggressive entrant pricing from embedded-payroll infrastructure and self-serve SaaS), real quantity growth is plausibly at or above the nominal CAGR -- these nominal figures likely understate real volume growth of the underlying basket -- partially offset by the fact that some of the 'growth' in these market-size figures reflects platforms/features outside the strict 541214 service basket (HCM add-ons, benefits admin, earned-wage access) rather than the core payroll-processing basket itself.
caveats: Market-size trackers used here are not consistently scoped (some blend PEO, HCM-software, and payroll-processing revenue); the 541214-specific portion of demand is not separately reported by any source found.
This is a nominal-dollar-market proxy for a real-quantity construct; the price/volume decomposition is judgment, not measured.
d5.change_evidence: A volume-based industry measure (e.g., number of payrolls processed, or number of W-2/1099 filings processed by third-party reporting agents, from IRS/SSA administrative data) would give a real quantity series independent of price; a single, consistently-scoped market-size tracker followed over multiple years (rather than a cross-vendor comparison) would remove the scope-drift noise seen here.
d5.rationale: Underlying secular demand drivers are durable and mostly regulatory: expanding multi-state and remote-work tax/withholding complexity, new state paid-leave and pay-transparency mandates, and continued small-business formation all push more employers toward needing payroll processed correctly across more jurisdictions, not fewer. These are structural rather than cyclical. Against that, nominal market-growth estimates across trackers cluster in the mid-single-digit percent per year range, which nets to meaningful but not explosive five-year compounding once price effects are stripped out.

caveats: No public data source enumerates what share of 541214 LMM-band firms are franchise-affiliated, reseller/agent-only, or otherwise structurally impaired for a clean control transfer; this is judgment.

Franchise agreements are not necessarily disqualifying under the lens (a franchise territory can still undergo a genuine control transfer via resale to a new franchisee), but they add transaction friction and consent risk not present in fully independent bureaus.
e.change_evidence: A count of franchise-affiliated payroll bureaus (Payroll Vault and comparable concepts) versus fully independent bureaus, and typical franchise-transfer terms (consent rights, right of first refusal, non-competes), would sharpen the low end of this range; a SUSB/Census employer-vs-nonemployer split within 541214 would clarify how many of the smallest units are single-person referral shops rather than operating bureaus.
e.rationale: NAICS 541214 is already defined narrowly around establishments that provide payroll processing to external clients and explicitly excludes CPA offices, so the definitional risk of counting captive/internal payroll departments is low relative to broader NAICS codes. The main leakage is (1) franchise-model bureaus (e.g., Payroll Vault, a nationally franchised, low-fixed-cost, recurring-PEPM-revenue local-territory concept) where the operating business is a real, sellable client relationship but transfer may require franchisor consent or be structured as a territory resale rather than a clean asset/stock sale, and (2) referral/reseller arrangements (agents who sell but do not operate an ADP/Paychex-branded service) that may not constitute a standalone operating business with independent pricing power. Absent a franchise-penetration estimate for this segment, most of the national firm population is judged to clear the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541214 ($22,102,632,622) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541214 ($27,322,843,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No direct measurement of the self-serve/embedded share of total industry volume was found; both bounding forces are well documented qualitatively but the net split is judgment.

Legal liability remaining with the employer is a reason clients might value an accountable operator's expertise, but it is not proof they will keep paying for one -- some clients may accept the residual liability themselves in exchange for lower self-serve pricing, which would pull this below the base case.
o.change_evidence: Vendor-disclosed mix-shift data (share of new payroll relationships initiated as embedded/self-serve versus full-service-with-a-human-bureau) tracked over several years; a segmentation study of which client types (by headcount, state count, industry) churn to self-serve versus remain with a bureau.
o.rationale: Two offsetting forces bound this. Pulling down: self-serve SaaS (Gusto alone reports 400,000+ direct small-business customers) and embedded-payroll infrastructure (Check, Gusto Embedded, Zeal, Salsa, ADP Embedded) increasingly let a business, or the vertical software it already uses, run payroll directly with no ongoing human-staffed bureau relationship at all -- a real disintermediation channel concentrated at the simplest, single-state, low-headcount end of the market. Holding it up: regardless of which vendor or software a client uses, the employer (and, per the IRS Trust Fund Recovery Penalty, potentially its owners, officers, and even bookkeepers/controllers personally) remains legally liable for payroll-tax deposit accuracy and timing -- outsourcing does not transfer that liability -- which sustains willingness to pay for an accountable third party's expertise and risk-absorption, especially for multi-state, benefits-heavy, or otherwise complex clients not well served by pure self-serve tools. Net, a majority but a declining majority of year-5 quantity is judged to still require an accountable operator of the lens's kind.

caveats: No bureau-level renewal-price panel was found; the qualitative claim of price/margin compression is well supported, but the specific retained-share number is bounded judgment, not a measured pass-through rate.

PEPM pricing ranges cited are current retail figures for the small-business market broadly, not specifically for LMM-band, more-complex clients, who likely see less commoditized pricing and possibly higher retention.
q.change_evidence: Bureau-level renewal-price and gross-margin trend data (e.g., from a business broker's completed-deal file, or a trade-association benchmarking survey) showing realized price/margin change after a bureau adopts a specific automation feature; embedded-payroll provider disclosures on PEPM pricing trend versus prior years.
q.rationale: Billing in this industry is overwhelmingly a recurring per-employee-per-month (PEPM, roughly $5-17/employee plus a small monthly base) or per-payroll-run fee -- a fixed/volume-linked model rather than cost-plus or hourly, which in principle lets an operator retain efficiency gains it captures until the next renewal. In practice retention is compressed by three documented forces: (1) intense, well-capitalized competition -- ADP, Paychex/Paycor, Paylocity, Paycom, plus Gusto/Rippling in the SMB tier, are all shipping comparable AI features, narrowing any single bureau's differentiation; (2) the emergence of embedded-payroll infrastructure (Check, Gusto Embedded, Zeal, Salsa, ADP Embedded) that lets vertical software platforms become the payroll provider directly, at similar per-employee pricing, undercutting the need for a standalone bureau relationship at renewal; (3) low structural switching costs for the simplest clients once a plan is set up, even though setup friction and an ongoing tax-liability relationship provide some stickiness. Volume/client-loss effects are excluded here and carried in d5/o; this is only about how much of a given implemented efficiency gain the operator keeps versus passes through in price.

caveats: No direct survey of AI-adoption timelines among independent payroll bureaus was found; this is bounded judgment built from adjacent evidence (vendor roadmaps, liability structure), not a measured implementation rate.

Faster large-vendor AI shipping could pull small-bureau adoption forward, or could instead accelerate disintermediation of bureaus entirely (captured in `o`) rather than being 'implemented' by the bureau itself; that risk is carried in `o`, not double-counted here.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor product-adoption disclosures (e.g., ADP/Paychex/Paylocity earnings-call commentary on AI-feature attach rates among small/mid bureau customers, or a direct survey of independent bureau owners on AI-tool adoption) would sharpen this; documented case studies of bureaus that have removed a specific human review step (e.g., first-pass tax-notice triage) would be direct evidence.
rho.rationale: LMM-band bureaus are overwhelmingly technology consumers, not builders: they run client payroll on top of ADP Workforce Now, Paychex, Paylocity, Paycom, Gusto, QuickBooks, or Kronos/UKG rails, all of which are publicly racing to ship AI features (anomaly detection, natural-language support, compliance copilots) into their core platforms. That lowers the technical lift for a small bureau to capture exposed opportunity, since it mostly means adopting vendor features rather than building models in-house. Counterweighing that: payroll processing carries personal trust-fund-tax liability for whoever is responsible for deposits and filings (the IRS Trust Fund Recovery Penalty reaches business owners, officers, payroll managers, bookkeepers, and controllers), which makes bureaus and their clients cautious about removing human review from tax-filing and deposit steps even where technically feasible; multi-state regulatory heterogeneity and dependence on third-party platform release cadence (rather than in-house control over the roadmap) also slow full implementation. Net, roughly half of the exposed opportunity is judged achievable operationally within five years.

s5.bridge: Source population #1 is public-accounting/CPA firm partner-age data (19th Annual Rosenberg Practice Management Survey, via CPA Practice Advisor): in the $2-10M revenue firm category, 65.5% of partners are over age 50, rising to 71.1% for sole practitioners and smaller firms, with AICPA projecting 75% of today's CPAs retiring within 15 years. Transformation: payroll-bureau owners in the LMM band are judged demographically similar to this population (many bureaus are founded/run by accountants, bookkeepers, or former corporate payroll managers of comparable career stage), used as a proxy for owner-age skew in 541214; bridge risk is that payroll-bureau founders may run younger on average, since the industry has a more recent wave of tech-enabled entrants than the CPA profession. Source population #2 is observed deal flow in the adjacent/overlapping payroll-and-PEO bureau roll-up space: Vensure Employer Solutions (PE-backed via Stone Point Capital, merged with PrismHR) has completed 108 acquisitions of small payroll/HR service providers as of 2026, up from 75 six years prior, explicitly described as reducing the number of independent payroll companies, typically structured as 80% acquirer / 20% seller-rollover. Transformation: used as a proxy for buyer-side deal propensity across the lens population; error direction is ambiguous -- Vensure's targets skew toward PEO/ASO (co-employment) models more than pure 541214 payroll processing, which could overstate deal receptiveness for stand-alone bureaus that never took on PEO risk, but the scale and cadence of just this one acquirer (not counting Paychex/Paycor, ADP, or other PE-backed regional platforms) likely understates total market deal flow.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is drawn from CPA/accounting firms, not payroll bureaus specifically -- a population proxy, not a direct measurement.

Deal-flow evidence is drawn substantially from one acquirer (Vensure/PrismHR) whose targets blend PEO/ASO and payroll-processing business models; the pure-541214 slice of that activity is not separately reported.

The Rosenberg survey figures are from a 2018 publication; more recent AICPA retirement-wave commentary suggests the skew has not reversed, but current-year percentages are not independently confirmed here.
s5.change_evidence: A direct survey or trade-association (e.g., National Payroll Reporting Consortium, PayrollOrg member survey) of owner age and succession intentions among independent 541214 bureaus; a deal-count tally across all active acquirers (Vensure, Paychex/Paycor, ADP, PE-backed regional roll-ups, business brokers listing payroll bureaus specifically) rather than one acquirer's public press releases.
s5.rationale: Two independent signals both point toward an above-average five-year transfer probability for this industry relative to a generic small-business base rate: an aging, accounting-adjacent owner population, and unusually active, already-proven roll-up buyers explicitly targeting small payroll/HR service providers at a double-digit-per-year acquisition pace. Set below the top of the range because the owner-age proxy is imperfect (CPA firms, not payroll bureaus) and because a meaningful share of the documented buyer activity is PEO-flavored rather than pure payroll-processing-bureau M&A.

records.principal_driver: Durable, regulation-driven multi-state payroll-tax complexity sustains demand for an accountable third-party operator even as the underlying processing task automates.
records.principal_weakness: Thin, competitively-shopped per-employee-per-month pricing lets larger platforms and embedded-payroll infrastructure compete away most of any automation-driven efficiency gain before an LMM-band operator can retain it.

caveats: S7 (Outsource Accelerator) supports the 108-acquisition count and 80,000 worksite employees but does not contain the 80%/20% acquirer/seller-rollover structure or the 'explicitly described as reducing the number of independent payroll companies' characterization attributed to it in the s5 bridge; that color is corroborated by uncited third-party coverage but is over-attributed here. Not score-driving: s5 = 0.38 rests on the deal count/cadence and owner-age proxy, both verified.

S14 does not mention ADP Embedded; the packet names it among providers the article describes. The embedded-payroll disintermediation channel itself (Check, Gusto Embedded, Zeal, Salsa) is fully verified.

Within the cited 541200 OEWS aggregate, Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks are only ~17,780 of 1,100,840 jobs (~1.6%), so the a reweighting travels a long way from the observed 4-digit mix toward a hypothesized payroll-bureau occupation mix. The bridge discloses the direction honestly, but the magnitude of the judgment adjustment is large and unmeasured.

Narrative-score tension on value capture: the principal weakness states platforms compete away 'most' of any automation gain, yet q base 0.47 against the frozen 50%-retention anchor makes C = 9.4 the record's strongest factor. This is a consequence of the methodology's anchor (0.47 is consistent with 'most competed away'), not a research defect, but readers should not read C = 9.4 as strong pricing power.

The packet's S13 description cites a '$20-150+ monthly base' range not visible on the current page version; only the $5-17 PEPM figure and the existence of a monthly base fee are directly confirmed there.

Owner-age evidence (S8) is a 2018 publication about CPA-firm partners, not payroll-bureau owners — disclosed by the researcher, but it means the succession leg of s5 rests on an eight-year-old adjacent-population proxy.

The tier interval spans CONDITIONAL to HIGHEST_PRIORITY with no robust tier, and readiness is MODEL_CONDITIONED (five of seven researched primitives are ESTIMATE); the HIGHEST_PRIORITY base tier should be read strictly through the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

records.title: Payroll Services

caveats: a: The employment matrix is for NAICS 541200, which includes CPA, tax-preparation, and payroll firms outside this lens.

a: Existing accounting software has already automated some routine work, so gross task descriptions can overstate remaining opportunity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers on recurring monthly, quarterly, or annual engagements; billing may be fixed-fee, value-priced, subscription, or hourly, with the recurring operating service retained in scope.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA offices, tax-return-only providers, payroll-only providers, captive internal accounting units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose revenue is chiefly one-time cleanup, project, or software resale work.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market providers of recurring outsourced bookkeeping, general accounting, billing, close, reporting, and related accounting operations for external clients.

caveats: The employment matrix is for NAICS 541200, which includes CPA, tax-preparation, and payroll firms outside this lens.
No source measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated AI-substitutable tasks for 541219.
Existing accounting software has already automated some routine work, so gross task descriptions can overstate remaining opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A 541219 establishment-level payroll-by-SOC extract, time-and-motion study, and client-workflow sample separating transaction processing from review, advisory, and client-contact time.
a.rationale: The broad 5412 matrix is labor-intensive in accountants and auditors (31.4% of employment), bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks (9.4%), billing and posting clerks (2.5%), and wider office support (28.4%). O*NET describes substantial data processing, verification, reconciliation, coding, and record-maintenance work; these tasks create meaningful AI and workflow exposure, but judgment, exception review, compliance, and client communication limit the share that is substitutable. The range is a wage-weighted judgment for the narrower non-CPA 541219 lens, not a measured exposure index.

d5.bridge: S3 measures 2024 to 2034 employment for the broader NAICS 541200 accounting, tax preparation, bookkeeping, and payroll-services industry, not constant-price, constant-quality service quantity for 541219. Its 2.4% ten-year employment increase is converted to an approximate five-year central demand ratio of 1.01 and widened for differences between employment, quantity, and the excluded CPA, tax-only, and payroll-only segments. Automation can make employment grow more slowly than service quantity, so this proxy may bias demand quantity downward; competitive substitution by software can bias it upward.
caveats: No direct five-year, constant-price, constant-quality demand forecast is available for 541219.
Employment projections are not service-volume projections and the source industry is broader than the lens.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541219 receipts or client-account counts by service line, separated into recurring bookkeeping, billing, cleanup, and advisory work, collected annually for a representative panel.
d5.rationale: The broad industry projection is nearly flat-to-modestly growing, while clerical work faces automation and accountant work is expected to grow. For the current outsourced non-CPA accounting-service basket, the defensible central case is roughly stable real quantity, with a range allowing moderate contraction or growth.

caveats: Census classification identifies establishments, not firm-level recurring revenue or transfer eligibility.
Employer establishment counts do not identify the injected LMM firm population or distinguish independent firms from units of larger organizations.
e.change_evidence: A size-filtered 541219 business list enriched with service descriptions, recurring-revenue mix, customer concentration, and owner dependence from broker CIMs and interviews.
e.rationale: The official definition is already centered on non-CPA establishments providing accounting services other than tax-only or payroll-only work, and explicitly includes bookkeeper and billing offices. Most LMM firms in the injected size population should therefore fit an external recurring-services lens, while the discount recognizes one-time, predominantly non-recurring, owner-dependent, or otherwise non-transferable operations.

caveats: The cited occupational evidence is broader than 541219 and does not directly measure software-only substitution.
The operator-required share may be lower for microbusiness clients with clean bank feeds and standardized charts of accounts.
o.change_evidence: Client interviews and service-line revenue data measuring which recurring tasks are retained with an outsourced operator after adoption of cloud accounting, automated feeds, and AI workflow tools.
o.rationale: A substantial share of the service basket should still require an accountable operator for client-specific close, reconciliation exceptions, financial-record integrity, compliance, and communication. BLS expects routine tasks to be automated but expects accountants' advisory and analytical duties to become more prominent; that supports durability of accountable work, while low-complexity bookkeeping and billing can migrate to self-service or software-only alternatives.

caveats: The pricing survey covers accounting and bookkeeping firms generally, not the specific 541219 LMM lens.
Fixed fees do not guarantee retention when scope expands or clients can change providers.
q.change_evidence: A cohort of acquired-firm engagement letters and five-year client-level price, scope, labor-hour, churn, and gross-margin data, segmented by fixed-fee versus hourly billing.
q.rationale: Recurring bookkeeping commonly uses fixed-fee or value-based pricing: the cited benchmark reports 64% of participating bookkeeping firms use those approaches. That structure can let an operator retain savings during an engagement, but standardized software alternatives, renewal repricing, competitive proposals, and client demands for more advisory work should pass through part of the benefit over five years. The value is a discounted retention judgment after those commercial effects, separate from implementation.

caveats: This is an implementation judgment, not a technology-adoption survey result.
Implementation will vary with client system access, record quality, service scope, and the firm's review and liability policies.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after staffing, throughput, error, rework, and client-retention data from comparable bookkeeping platforms or acquired firms after a controlled AI workflow rollout.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be material but incomplete. O*NET reports repetitive work alongside exactness, external-contact, and daily decision-making requirements; BLS also emphasizes accuracy, integrity, and confidential financial documentation. AI can assist intake, coding, draft reconciliation, and exception triage, but accountable review, controls, client data quality, and workflow change constrain realized substitution or avoided hiring.

caveats: BizBuySell is a voluntary broker-reported marketplace indicator, not a 541219 LMM transfer-rate census.
The Census ABS page exposes relevant owner tables but does not itself report a five-year qualifying control-transfer rate for this code.
Owner retirement intentions are not equivalent to completed outside control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year 541219 closed-deal data from a broker network or PitchBook/Capital IQ, plus Census ABS owner-age and ownership-acquisition tabs filtered to the closest available industry and LMM revenue band.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer within five years is plausible for a fragmented, owner-operated service population but should be materially below general owner exit intentions because many exits are wind-downs, family transitions, internal reorganizations, or businesses that fail diligence. The observed small-business transaction market has active buyers but states that quality, recurring revenue, clean financials, experienced management, and financing affect closing; the range therefore reflects only transferable operating firms.

records.principal_driver: Recurring financial-operations workflows contain repetitive data and reconciliation work that can be redesigned while retaining accountable human review.
records.principal_weakness: Direct evidence for 541219 LMM transfer frequency, service-line demand, and realized AI labor savings is unavailable; the central estimates rely on broader-industry proxies and bounded judgment.

caveats: S1 and S2 are official Census routes, but their dynamic content did not expose the cited definition and establishment count in the reopened audit views; those exact claims remain only partially verified.

S3 could not be rendered by the audit client, leaving the exact 2.4% broad-industry projection and occupational shares unverified. This does not pass materiality: a and rho are already ESTIMATE, while d5 could be downgraded from PROXY to ESTIMATE without changing the HIGHEST_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The e base is a disclosed ESTIMATE that extrapolates from an establishment classification and broad employer count to recurring, transferable LMM firms; neither Census source directly measures that fit.

The s5 base is a disclosed ESTIMATE: S7 establishes an active but selective general small-business transaction market and S9 identifies potentially useful owner tables, but neither measures a five-year 541219 qualifying-control-transfer rate.

S8 supports fixed-fee/value-pricing prevalence, not the 55% five-year retention value. The packet honestly labels q ESTIMATE and gives a 0.40-0.70 range, so this is a bounded judgment rather than an observed retention result.

The d5 bridge is directionally coherent but uses employment in broader NAICS 541200 as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality 541219 service quantity; the packet discloses both population mismatch and opposing bias directions.

The occupational sources support task composition and automation direction, but not wage-weighted AI substitutability, realized implementation, or the operator-required share for the narrowed 541219 LMM lens. Those inputs are appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and carry broad ranges.

The recurring-services narrowing is disclosed in the included and excluded activities and is coherent with the acquisition lens. Calling deviation_from_default none is acceptable, though the underlying code still contains business-model and task heterogeneity not captured by heterogeneous false.

caveats: a: OEWS explains that its industry occupation estimates exclude self-employed workers, an important limitation for this code.
a: The injected compensation-to-receipts input may be misleading because the residual NAICS mixes labor models and its QCEW wage population need not match the selected repeat-service practices.
rho: This is an operational adoption judgment, not a measured industry implementation rate.
rho: Regulated and high-liability specialties inside the code may implement much more slowly than routine research and report-production practices.
e: Census owner-characteristic data cover employer firms broadly, not this exact LMM eligible subset.
e: The injected LMM firm count uses a size-to-EBITDA bridge and does not establish whether a firm has recurring work or transferable operations.
s5: BizBuySell's service-sector transactions are not NAICS 541990 and skew below the selected EBITDA band.
s5: No retrieved source measures qualifying control transfers for the selected 541990 practices.
q: Billing terms are not measured for this residual NAICS.
q: A practice with cost-plus or tightly benchmarked fees could retain far less than the stated range, while a scarce specialist may retain more.
d5: The BLS market-research projection is an occupation-wide measure across many industries and includes work outside this lens.
d5: Residual-code segments can have sharply different demand cycles and no exact constant-price demand series was retrieved.
o: The source documents analyst duties and a broad employment outlook, not the probability that future 541990 service quantity requires an operator.
o: The answer varies substantially by specialty, regulation, client risk tolerance, and whether the service is a report or an accountable opinion.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and institutional customers buy time, reports, opinions, inspections, or case work through hourly, project, retainer, per-engagement, and occasionally recurring-service contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because 541990 is a residual category spanning materially different professional services; retained only repeat outsourced services delivered by transferable operating practices.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off expert engagements, owner-only practices without transferable operations, captive units, internal reorganizations, non-control financings, software-only providers, and activities classified to a specific NAICS such as translation, veterinary, legal, accounting, engineering, or management consulting.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market employer firms in this residual NAICS that provide a repeat outsourced professional service to external customers, including repeat appraisal, dispute-resolution, research, technical-review, and similar specialist engagements where the practice, rather than a named individual alone, can be transferred.

caveats: OEWS explains that its industry occupation estimates exclude self-employed workers, an important limitation for this code.
The injected compensation-to-receipts input may be misleading because the residual NAICS mixes labor models and its QCEW wage population need not match the selected repeat-service practices.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted OEWS occupation mix for 541990, paired with task inventories and time-and-motion interviews at 15 to 20 retained-practice firms, would test the exposed share.
a.rationale: The retained work is document, data, research, report, scheduling, and client-communication intensive, so AI can substitute or avoid hiring for a meaningful share of wages. It is not assumed to substitute the professional judgment, field observation, licensed sign-off, or adversarial/client-facing work that varies across the residual code.

caveats: The BLS market-research projection is an occupation-wide measure across many industries and includes work outside this lens.
Residual-code segments can have sharply different demand cycles and no exact constant-price demand series was retrieved.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of 541990 product-line receipts deflated by a service-price index, client procurement-spend panels, and segment-level backlog data would replace the adjacent occupation signal.
d5.rationale: Demand for specialized external analysis, market information, technical review, and accountable opinions should remain broadly stable, with growth in data use supporting some segments. As an imperfect adjacent indicator, BLS projects market-research analyst employment to rise 7 percent from 2024 to 2034, but that occupation is not a measure of this industry's constant-quality service quantity.

caveats: Census owner-characteristic data cover employer firms broadly, not this exact LMM eligible subset.
The injected LMM firm count uses a size-to-EBITDA bridge and does not establish whether a firm has recurring work or transferable operations.
e.change_evidence: A business-register sample matched to websites, service descriptions, revenue concentration, employee count, and recurring-engagement history would measure eligibility directly.
e.rationale: The code is explicitly a catch-all for professional, scientific, or technical services not classified elsewhere. A substantial but far from universal share is likely to be a repeat outsourced service with transferable operations; excluded solo experts, one-off engagements, and nonrepeat practices lower eligibility.

The answer varies substantially by specialty, regulation, client risk tolerance, and whether the service is a report or an accountable opinion.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on willingness to buy AI-only versus human-accountable deliverables, plus retained-practice service-line revenue by workflow, would identify the operator-required share.

caveats: Billing terms are not measured for this residual NAICS.
A practice with cost-plus or tightly benchmarked fees could retain far less than the stated range, while a scarce specialist may retain more.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, renewal price histories, fee schedules, and win-loss interviews from a representative set of retained practices would identify contractual pass-through and repricing.
q.rationale: Hourly and project engagements expose labor savings to customer renegotiation, and standardized research or reports are vulnerable to price competition. Retainers, regulated accountability, client relationships, and specialized interpretation allow the operator to retain part of an implemented benefit, yielding a middle range rather than full retention.

caveats: This is an operational adoption judgment, not a measured industry implementation rate.
Regulated and high-liability specialties inside the code may implement much more slowly than routine research and report-production practices.
rho.change_evidence: Firm-level AI deployment logs, before-and-after staffing data, and interviews with responsible licensed professionals and insurers would show which workflows reached production use.
rho.rationale: Drafting, summarization, data preparation, search, and workflow routing can be implemented in five years, but human review remains necessary where an opinion, confidential client record, regulatory duty, or field evidence controls the deliverable. The broad retained population makes implementation consistency uncertain.

caveats: BizBuySell's service-sector transactions are not NAICS 541990 and skew below the selected EBITDA band.
No retrieved source measures qualifying control transfers for the selected 541990 practices.
s5.change_evidence: Transaction records from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, or local business brokers, plus owner-age and succession responses for 541990 LMM firms, would materially narrow this estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded above the annual transaction flow because succession can accumulate over five years, but below a simple retirement proxy because many professional practices depend on the owner and will close or internally reorganize. Census confirms that its owner survey captures owner age, while BizBuySell reports a live small-business service transaction market rather than an industry-specific transfer rate.

records.principal_driver: Repeat professional workflows contain document and data tasks that can be automated while a responsible operator still delivers the client outcome.
records.principal_weakness: NAICS 541990 is a catch-all category, so the available evidence does not measure the selected repeat-service practice population directly.

caveats: All seven researched primitives are explicitly ESTIMATE. The sources mainly establish category definition, data availability, adjacent work tasks, and a broad transaction market; none directly measures the selected 541990 practice population's automation, transfer, retention, or durable-demand primitives.

S3's occupation-wide market-research outlook is not a constant-quality demand measure for NAICS 541990, and it provides especially weak support for the numeric d5 and o ranges. The packet discloses both the population mismatch and the absence of an exact demand series.

S4 and S5 establish that owner characteristics and small-business service transactions exist, but do not identify qualifying control transfers in the selected LMM practices. The wide s5 range and MODEL_CONDITIONED action appropriately expose that weakness.

S5 uses a mutable latest-report URL: the current page is Q2 2026 and reports a 40 percent service share, while the cited 42 percent figure appears in the linked Q1 archive. This metadata/link imprecision does not change the broad-market inference or the estimate state.

The coherent narrowing to repeat, transferable outsourced practices is disclosed and is translated back to the code-level firm population through e, but heterogeneity remains large enough that firm-level service-line validation is essential.
