records.title: All Other Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services

caveats: a: Occupation mix is measured at NAICS 5415 (4-digit aggregate), not 541511 specifically; 541511 is very likely more coding-intensive than the aggregate, biasing this estimate downward.

a: Only 4 of 13 occupation categories in the mix have a directly sourced exposure figure; the rest (including the single largest category, Software Developers, at ~45% of payroll) are analogical judgment.

a: The injected labor-share ratio l (0.6736, QCEW 2024 wages / SUSB 2022 receipts, stated MED quality) predates the visible 2025-2026 AI-tooling adoption wave described in this packet; if compensation share is already compressing due to AI-assisted delivery, l may overstate the wage base to which a and rho apply going forward.

rho: Adoption and automation-share figures come from AI-vendor usage telemetry and developer-tool market surveys — self-selected users and self-selected tasks, not a representative sample of delivered 541511 work.
rho: The Gartner project-cancellation figure is a forward-looking prediction as of 2025, not a realized 2027 outcome.
e: No direct survey of 541511 LMM firms' revenue-recurrence or client-renewal rate was found; this is industry-structure judgment, not a measured share.

e: The injected firm count n (5,767) is itself a margin-bridged estimate using a generic Damodaran 'Business & Consumer Services' 15.65% EBITDA margin assumption; if actual margins in custom programming services (often higher, given labor-arbitrage-based staffing/consulting models) differ materially from 15.65%, the revenue band mapped to '$1-10M EBITDA' — and therefore which firms populate the eligible-firm population e is applied to — could be mis-sized in either direction.
s5: No 541511-specific succession or deal-count data was found; this is a cross-industry base rate applied by analogy.

s5: The PE/strategic tech-M&A statistics found were dominated by SaaS/product-company deals and large-cap transactions, not representative of $1-10M-EBITDA custom-programming-services sellers specifically.
q: The ~70% time-and-materials billing-share figure is sourced from an industry blog aggregator rather than a primary billing-practice survey.

q: No direct measurement exists of realized post-AI-adoption repricing or margin retention specific to 541511 sellers; this is inferred from the industry's billing structure and one advisory firm's qualitative framing.
d5: Employment growth is a labor-input proxy, not a direct constant-price/quality demand-quantity measure.
d5: BLS projection vintage (base year 2024) may not fully reflect the AI-adoption acceleration visible through 2026.

d5: Commercial market-size forecasts (~22% nominal CAGR) were not used directly because they blend price inflation, a broader product/services scope, and vendor-report incentives to describe large addressable markets.

o: The widely circulated 'majority of new custom business apps built by non-IT employees' figure originates in low-code-vendor-ecosystem marketing content without a verifiable primary citation; treated as a weak, directional data point only.

o: Gartner's agentic-enterprise-application figures measure AI features embedded within applications, not whether an accountable human operator remains responsible for the engagement — a related but distinct question from o.

o: Internal citizen-development substitution primarily displaces simpler internal workflow apps that may never have been outsourced spend in the first place, which could mean o for the specific externally-contracted 541511 basket is understated by these figures, not overstated.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business and government clients purchasing custom development on a time-and-materials/hourly basis, a dedicated-team retainer, or (a growing minority) a fixed-fee/outcome-based basis. Revenue is services fees for labor and delivery rather than product licensing. At LMM scale a meaningful share of revenue recurs through multi-phase or maintenance follow-on work with existing clients rather than single discrete projects, but the industry also contains a genuine spectrum from pure staff-augmentation body shops to retained development partners.
lens.excluded_activities: Packaged/off-the-shelf software publishing (NAICS 5112, out of scope by definition); genuinely one-off single-project engagements with no expectation of a renewing client relationship; in-house/captive corporate development units (not separately incorporated or classified under 541511 in any case); shell entities; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Firms whose primary NAICS 541511 activity is writing, modifying, testing, and supporting custom application and software code under ongoing or renewing engagements with external, unaffiliated clients: bespoke application development, systems customization/integration coding, dedicated or embedded development-team (staff-augmentation-with-delivery-accountability) arrangements, and post-launch maintenance/enhancement contracts that follow an initial build.

a.bridge: Source population is NAICS 5415 (Computer Systems Design and Related Services, BLS OEWS May 2023 industry occupation table), which is broader than 541511 alone — it also includes systems integration, facilities management, and other computer-related services sub-industries with a heavier support/management mix than pure custom programming. Transformation: wage-weighted the 5415 occupation mix (employment x mean wage, per occupation) against occupation-level AI task-exposure scores. Four occupations have a directly observed exposure figure from Anthropic's Economic Index 'Observed Exposure' ranking of ~800 US occupations (Computer Programmers 74.5%, the single highest-ranked occupation in the economy; Software QA Analysts/Testers 51.9%; Information Security Analysts 48.6%; Computer User Support Specialists 46.8%); the remaining occupations in the 5415 mix (Software Developers, Systems Analysts, Network Architects/Admins, DB Admins, Web Developers, IS Managers, Sales Engineers, Project/Management Analysts) have no occupation-specific figure in the sources reached and were scored by analogy to the same framework's stated logic that judgment-heavy, collaborative, or managerial work scores lower. Error direction: likely understates true 541511-specific exposure, since pure custom programming is more coding-intensive (and less consulting/support-heavy) than the 5415 aggregate; also likely stale relative to 2025-2026 model capability gains given the underlying wage/employment data vintage is 2023.
caveats: Occupation mix is measured at NAICS 5415 (4-digit aggregate), not 541511 specifically; 541511 is very likely more coding-intensive than the aggregate, biasing this estimate downward.

Only 4 of 13 occupation categories in the mix have a directly sourced exposure figure; the rest (including the single largest category, Software Developers, at ~45% of payroll) are analogical judgment.

The injected labor-share ratio l (0.6736, QCEW 2024 wages / SUSB 2022 receipts, stated MED quality) predates the visible 2025-2026 AI-tooling adoption wave described in this packet; if compensation share is already compressing due to AI-assisted delivery, l may overstate the wage base to which a and rho apply going forward.
a.change_evidence: A 541511-specific (not 5415-aggregate) OEWS occupation breakout, if BLS ever publishes one at the 6-digit level; an occupation-exposure re-score using 2025-2026-vintage model capability benchmarks rather than the 2023-based exposure rankings; a direct survey of custom-programming-shop task allocation (hours by task type: greenfield coding vs. architecture vs. client management vs. QA vs. support).
a.rationale: Wage-weighted across the 5415 occupation mix, roughly 45% of industry payroll sits in the 'Software Developers' category alone, which the Anthropic exposure ranking explicitly places below its top-10 list (unlike the narrower 'Computer Programmers' category) because the work is 'more complex, collaborative, and judgment-intensive.' A further ~20% of payroll sits in management, sales-engineering, and project/business-analyst roles that skew low-exposure. The remaining ~35% (support, security, network, database, systems-analyst, web-developer roles) spans a moderate-to-high exposure band. Combining these gives a wage-weighted exposed share meaningfully below the ~75% headline figure sometimes quoted for 'programmers' narrowly, because that figure describes one occupation, not the industry's full payroll mix.

d5.bridge: Source population is BLS Employment Projections 2024-2034 for the Computer Systems Design and Related Services industry (NAICS 5415, employment counts) and for the Software Developers occupation, not a direct constant-price/quality output-quantity series for the 541511 service basket. Transformation: BLS projects industry employment growth of at least 15% and Software Developers employment growth of 15.8% over the 2024-2034 (10-year) window; this was prorated (allowing for likely early-decade front-loading of AI-driven change) to the 5-year screen horizon and used as a labor-input proxy for real service-quantity growth, on the reasoning that BLS employment projections embed the Bureau's own expectation of quantity of work demanded net of assumed productivity change. Error direction: ambiguous. If realized AI productivity gains exceed BLS's assumptions, fewer added workers could be needed for the same or greater service quantity (this proxy would then understate quantity growth); conversely, if AI substitution suppresses total addressable demand faster than BLS assumed (e.g., via low-code/citizen-development erosion, see o), realized quantity growth could come in below this proxy. Commercial market-value forecasts for 'custom software development' (~22% nominal CAGR) were reviewed but not used as the primary anchor because they are dominated by price/mix inflation and a broader market definition (including packaged-software customization) than the 541511 services basket.
caveats: Employment growth is a labor-input proxy, not a direct constant-price/quality demand-quantity measure.
BLS projection vintage (base year 2024) may not fully reflect the AI-adoption acceleration visible through 2026.

Commercial market-size forecasts (~22% nominal CAGR) were not used directly because they blend price inflation, a broader product/services scope, and vendor-report incentives to describe large addressable markets.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price/quality output-quantity index specific to custom application development (analogous to a producer price index deflated series), or updated 2026-vintage BLS projections reflecting realized 2024-2026 AI adoption rather than 2023-based assumptions.
d5.rationale: BLS ranks Computer Systems Design and Related Services among the 15 fastest-growing industries in the economy and among the 15 industries projected to add the most jobs 2024-2034; Software Developers specifically are projected to add ~267,700 jobs (15.8% growth), the second-largest employment increase of any single occupation nationally. Prorated to a 5-year window and read as a demand-quantity proxy, this supports modest-to-solid real growth in the service basket, well above flat but below the market-value forecasts' much higher nominal figures.

caveats: No direct survey of 541511 LMM firms' revenue-recurrence or client-renewal rate was found; this is industry-structure judgment, not a measured share.

The injected firm count n (5,767) is itself a margin-bridged estimate using a generic Damodaran 'Business & Consumer Services' 15.65% EBITDA margin assumption; if actual margins in custom programming services (often higher, given labor-arbitrage-based staffing/consulting models) differ materially from 15.65%, the revenue band mapped to '$1-10M EBITDA' — and therefore which firms populate the eligible-firm population e is applied to — could be mis-sized in either direction.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level sample (e.g., a D&B/ZoomInfo pull cross-referenced against contract-renewal or multi-year-client disclosures, or a structured broker/buy-side survey of custom-software LMM sellers) to directly size the share of firms with no renewing client relationships.
e.rationale: The injected LMM-band firm count (n) is already restricted to employer firms in the $1-10M normalized-EBITDA band, a population that by construction consists of firms with material, multi-year commercial operations rather than nonemployer freelancers or sole proprietors (which sit below the SUSB employer-firm size classes used to derive n). At this scale, essentially all such firms serve external, unaffiliated clients — 541511 establishment classification itself requires providing programming services to others, so in-house captive IT departments are not separately counted in this population in the first place. The main plausible source of ineligibility is firms that operate on genuinely one-off, non-repeat project engagements with no renewing client relationship (excluded by the lens definition), plus a small residual of financing/holding vehicles. Industry structure (custom development projects typically generate follow-on maintenance/enhancement work) suggests this non-repeat share is a minority of the LMM population, not a majority.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541511 ($192,866,921,104) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541511 ($305,260,086,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $292,242 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541511); class <5: 45,975 firms, avg revenue/firm $413,913 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,777 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,238 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,817,988 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $284,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,153 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,749,458 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $586,790 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 4,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,255,622 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,761,505 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,244 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,288,390 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,183,133 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 640 firms, avg revenue/firm $224,003,744 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,056,586 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 5,767

caveats: The widely circulated 'majority of new custom business apps built by non-IT employees' figure originates in low-code-vendor-ecosystem marketing content without a verifiable primary citation; treated as a weak, directional data point only.

Gartner's agentic-enterprise-application figures measure AI features embedded within applications, not whether an accountable human operator remains responsible for the engagement — a related but distinct question from o.

Internal citizen-development substitution primarily displaces simpler internal workflow apps that may never have been outsourced spend in the first place, which could mean o for the specific externally-contracted 541511 basket is understated by these figures, not overstated.
o.change_evidence: An enterprise buyer survey asking directly whether a given custom-development need in the next 12-24 months was/will be fulfilled via an external accountable vendor, an in-house citizen developer, or an autonomous AI agent with no named accountable party; Gartner/Forrester enterprise application inventories tracking build-method (vendor-built vs. citizen-dev vs. agent-built) longitudinally.
o.rationale: Enterprise custom-development demand that involves legacy-system integration, regulatory/compliance requirements, security, and multi-system judgment is expected to keep requiring an accountable professional operator even as individual coding sub-tasks become heavily AI-assisted. Against that, two trends point toward real erosion of the accountable-operator share: Gartner projects task-specific AI agents will be embedded in roughly 40% of enterprise applications by 2026 (up from under 5% in 2025) and at least a third of enterprise software will be agentic by 2028 (up from under 1% in 2024); and low-code/citizen-development platforms are widely reported (though weakly sourced) to already account for a majority of new internal custom business applications built by non-IT staff. Both trends are directional signals of growing capability to fulfill 'custom software' needs without a named, accountable external vendor, but neither directly measures whether an accountable operator remains responsible for the specific service basket 541511 firms currently sell (which skews toward more complex, externally-contracted work than the internal low-code app use case).

caveats: The ~70% time-and-materials billing-share figure is sourced from an industry blog aggregator rather than a primary billing-practice survey.

No direct measurement exists of realized post-AI-adoption repricing or margin retention specific to 541511 sellers; this is inferred from the industry's billing structure and one advisory firm's qualitative framing.
q.change_evidence: Billing-mix data (T&M vs. fixed-fee vs. retainer share of revenue) specifically at the $1-10M-EBITDA custom-software-services segment; realized renewal-price and gross-margin trajectories from operators that have already adopted AI-assisted delivery at scale, ideally sourced from a PE-owned platform's post-acquisition financials rather than public market commentary.
q.rationale: The majority of custom programming engagements are billed time-and-materials/hourly (commonly cited near 70% of agile-model engagements), a structure that mechanically converts AI-driven labor-hour reductions into fewer billable hours rather than retained operator margin, unless the operator proactively repositions client relationships to fixed-fee or outcome-based pricing before clients or competitors force the issue. Outcome-based/fixed-fee contracting is growing as a share of new deals but from a small base. An IT-services M&A advisory analysis explicitly frames AI coding tools as 'materially raising developer productivity' and thereby 'reducing the labor content of software development engagements,' describing this as a structural headwind specifically for traditional (non-managed-service) development shops rather than for adjacent segments like managed services or cybersecurity. Renewal-time repricing, customer awareness of public AI-productivity narratives, and the ease of switching custom-dev vendors (low contractual lock-in relative to, say, managed infrastructure services) all point toward the operator retaining a minority, not a majority, of the discounted five-year implemented gross benefit.

caveats: Adoption and automation-share figures come from AI-vendor usage telemetry and developer-tool market surveys — self-selected users and self-selected tasks, not a representative sample of delivered 541511 work.
The Gartner project-cancellation figure is a forward-looking prediction as of 2025, not a realized 2027 outcome.
rho.change_evidence: Enterprise (not individual-developer) realized headcount- or hours-reduction data from IT-services billing/staffing systems specifically in custom-programming engagements; a repeated Gartner/Forrester enterprise agentic-AI maturity survey tracked 2026-2028 to see whether the 2027 cancellation prediction materializes; vendor (GitHub/Anthropic/OpenAI) enterprise completion-rate telemetry disaggregated by task type and industry rather than aggregated across all Claude/Copilot users.
rho.rationale: Developer-side AI tool adoption is already near-saturated (84% of developers using or planning to use AI coding tools, with daily-use rates reported climbing from ~18% to ~73% between 2024 and 2026 in one industry survey), and Anthropic's own product telemetry shows Claude Code sessions running 79% automated vs. 21% augmented, far higher than Claude.ai's general 49%/51% split — evidence that once AI is embedded directly in the delivery workflow, a large share of exposed coding tasks does get operationally implemented, not just theoretically exposed. However, this reflects self-selected, already-delegated tasks (JavaScript/TypeScript and HTML/CSS dominate at 31% and 28% of Claude Code coding queries; SQL and backend Python sit at only 6% and 14%, i.e., the harder/more judgment-heavy and higher-stakes categories lag), enterprise production adoption trails startup adoption sharply (13% vs. 33% of firms scaling agentic systems), and Gartner projects over 40% of agentic AI enterprise projects will be scrapped by 2027 on governance, cost, or unclear-value grounds. Netting a high technical-adoption ceiling against real enterprise implementation friction over a 5-year horizon gives a base near the midpoint of the exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: Source population is all US employer-business owners across industries (Gallup-reported small-business owner survey), not 541511-specific. Transformation: applied the reported '22% of employer-business owners have five-year plans to sell or transfer ownership' base rate directly as a proxy for the probability an eligible 541511 LMM firm undergoes a qualifying control transfer within five years. Error direction: ambiguous in net. Software/IT-services founders skew demographically younger than the all-industry small-business-owner population (52.3% of all US employer-business owners are 55+, a threshold custom-programming-shop founders reach later on average given the industry's relatively recent vintage), which would bias this base rate upward if used unadjusted for our narrower population (fewer near-retirement owners than the average small business). Working the other direction, private equity and strategic buyer appetite specifically in tech/IT services has been rising (PE backing ~40% of tech M&A deals in 2024, up from ~30% in prior years), adding demand-side transfer probability beyond owner-retirement-driven exits that the general small-business base rate does not capture.
caveats: No 541511-specific succession or deal-count data was found; this is a cross-industry base rate applied by analogy.

The PE/strategic tech-M&A statistics found were dominated by SaaS/product-company deals and large-cap transactions, not representative of $1-10M-EBITDA custom-programming-services sellers specifically.
s5.change_evidence: An industry-cut succession-intent survey (e.g., IBBA/Pepperdine Private Capital Markets Report broken out by NAICS or SIC sector) or observed closed-deal counts (PitchBook/Capital IQ) for control transactions among $1-10M-EBITDA custom-software-services sellers specifically, rather than all-IT-services or all-small-business aggregates.
s5.rationale: No industry-specific succession or realized-deal-count series for 541511 LMM sellers was found. The Gallup all-industry base rate (22% five-year sale/transfer intent among employer-business owners) is used as the anchor, nudged down slightly for younger average founder age in tech and up slightly for elevated strategic/PE buyer interest in IT services generally, netting to a base near the unadjusted anchor.

records.principal_driver: Whether an operator can convert client billing from hourly/time-and-materials to fixed-fee or retainer pricing faster than clients and AI-native competitors compete that value away.
records.principal_weakness: Value capture (q) is structurally weak: time-and-materials billing ties revenue directly to labor hours, so AI-driven efficiency gains show up by default as fewer billable hours rather than retained operator margin.

caveats: rho: the 'daily-use rates climbing from ~18% to ~73% between 2024 and 2026' figure appears in no registered source (S6, the only adoption-survey source cited, reports 82% daily-or-weekly use with no trend series), and S6's adoption figure is 'tried at least one tool' (84.4%) rather than the packet's stronger 'using or planning to use' phrasing. Not material: rho's base rests on the fully verified S4 telemetry and S12 enterprise-friction evidence, and no principled correction of this corroborative figure moves rho's base below ~0.41, the point at which H would cross the PRIORITY L-floor.

q: the ~70% time-and-materials billing-share figure is attributed by the packet's own caveat to an industry blog aggregator that is not registered in q's source_ids; the only cited source (S7) supports the qualitative structural-headwind framing but not the 70% number. Not material: with L set by H, the base tier is insensitive to q until it falls below ~0.08.

s5: the bridge's 'PE backing ~40% of tech M&A deals in 2024, up from ~30%' statistic could not be verified — S8 is a gated report landing page, and Corum's public material corroborates only the direction. Not material: the disclosed adjustments net to the unadjusted, fully verified Gallup anchor (0.22), and F is pinned at 10.0 across the entire s5 range.

a: the 'roughly 45% of industry payroll in Software Developers' claim holds within the disclosed 13-occupation wage-weighting mix (~43%), but that mix excludes the industry's sales, office/administrative, and other non-computer occupations (~24% share of full all-occupation payroll for Software Developers); the restricted-universe choice is implicit in the bridge but adds ambiguous-direction uncertainty beyond the stated proxy error.
e: an uncited structural-judgment ESTIMATE (empty source_ids), honestly disclosed as such; no tier effect since F = 10.0 across the full e/s5 low-high range.

Dataset anchors carry disclosed weaknesses upstream of the researched primitives: l mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts, and n is margin-bridged on a generic 15.65% cross-industry EBITDA margin; both are excluded from the envelope per the stated interval_scope.

The base tier margin is thin: L = H = 4.36 against the PRIORITY floor of 4.0, so modest downward revisions to a or rho would drop the base to CONDITIONAL — consistent with the record's own CONDITIONAL-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers, principally through recurring support or recovery arrangements and repeat lifecycle work; fixed-fee, retainer, managed-service, and time-and-materials billing may coexist.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because the residual code explicitly combines disaster recovery and software installation with other computer-related services; the screen retains recurring or repeat outsourced operating services and omits one-off work.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off project-only installation with no repeat service, software publishing, custom programming, systems-integration design, facilities management, internal IT units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market providers of recurring or repeat outsourced computer support, disaster-recovery, endpoint or software lifecycle installation, and related operational computer services sold to external customers.

caveats: BLS measures occupations across the economy and the broader 5415 family rather than this narrowed six-digit lens.
The evidence establishes automatable task types, not the wage-weighted share within eligible firms.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted occupational census for sampled 541519 firms, ticket-system task logs, and a before-and-after study of generative-AI use by managed-service and disaster-recovery providers would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The retained work includes ticket intake, problem description capture, knowledge retrieval, routine troubleshooting, documentation, and user guidance. BLS identifies these duties and expects chatbots to reduce user-support employment, while fault diagnosis, onsite work, client communication, exception handling, and recovery accountability remain labor-intensive. The base is a wage-weighted judgment for current tasks not already automated.

d5.bridge: S3 measures 2024 to 2034 wage-and-salary employment in NAICS 541500, Computer Systems Design and Related Services, not constant-price demand in narrowed six-digit 541519. Its 15.8 percent ten-year projection is transformed to a five-year ratio by applying a constant compound rate, giving the 1.08 base. Because 541500 includes custom programming, systems design, and facilities management that are excluded from 541519, this transformation is more likely to overstate than understate demand growth for the retained service basket.
caveats: The source is a five-digit parent industry projection, not six-digit 541519.
Employment growth can reflect wage, skill, or productivity changes rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand volume.
d5.change_evidence: A current NAICS 541519 revenue series deflated with an appropriate services price index, customer IT-outsourcing spend forecasts separated by disaster recovery and lifecycle services, and renewal-volume data from providers would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 15.8 percent employment growth for the broader 541500 industry over 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to IT infrastructure, software, cybersecurity, cloud computing, and AI-based systems. The base carries that broader forward signal into a shorter horizon with a conservative uncertainty band, while recognizing that employment is not constant-quality service quantity.

caveats: No current public product-line split isolates the recurring-service share of six-digit 541519 LMM firms.
The injected firm count is not re-estimated here and may itself include business models outside the narrowed lens.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS verification and revenue-by-service-line survey, supplemented by customer-contract samples from LMM firms, would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official definition places computer disaster recovery and software installation in 541519 but leaves the code residual and broad. The base judgment removes LMM firms whose work is principally one-off installation or otherwise lacks a recurring or repeat outsourced service, retaining the operational-service portion.

caveats: BLS occupation evidence does not measure the residual operator requirement for the narrowed industry.
Software vendor bundling and remote-management automation could displace a larger share than assumed.
o.change_evidence: Customer ticket-resolution data by complexity, managed-service contract renewal data, and interviews with IT buyers on willingness to use self-service or vendor-native support would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: BLS expects automated troubleshooting tools to reduce some user-support employment, but it also describes network maintenance, complex troubleshooting, installations, and disaster-recovery work that require accountable service delivery. The base assumes a substantial portion of future quantity still needs an operator, while routine queries and some setup are self-served or software-supplied.

caveats: The NAICS definition does not measure pricing or contract terms.
Provider mix between fixed-fee managed services and billable project work is unobserved for this lens.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples showing billing basis, renewal clauses, service-level commitments, and realized price changes after AI deployment would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Recurring support and recovery arrangements can preserve some workflow savings inside a fixed fee or retainer, but hourly and time-and-materials work passes labor savings through promptly. Renewal repricing, capable competitors, standardized remote tools, and customer procurement reduce durable capture. The base retains less than half of implemented gross benefit.

caveats: No representative implementation-rate survey was found for the narrowed 541519 population.
Implementation will differ sharply by client data access, tooling standardization, and regulated-customer mix.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-neutral benchmarks on AI deployment in managed-service providers, audited ticket-resolution quality data, and customer security questionnaires covering AI use would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Knowledge-based intake and documentation can be implemented in standard ticket workflows, but production remediation, incident response, security access, customer trust, and quality assurance constrain rollout. The base assumes most routine support workflows can be operationalized during the horizon, with complex technical judgment retained.

caveats: The marketplace category combines IT and software services, is not NAICS 541519, and its reported 2025 median cash flow of 309062 dollars is generally below the stated LMM EBITDA band.
Observed listings and completed marketplace transactions are not a representative census of control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal ownership file for 541519, broker and quality-of-earnings transaction databases filtered to qualifying control transfers, and owner-age or succession survey responses would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: BizBuySell reported 44 completed IT and software service transactions in its 2025 data, demonstrating observed small-business transfer activity but not a denominator suitable for this lens. The base is a bounded five-year transfer judgment for eligible firms, allowing for owner succession, brokered sales, and strategic acquisitions while excluding closures and reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: AI can compress routine support, documentation, and knowledge-work effort while recurring outsourced technical responsibilities remain valuable.
records.principal_weakness: The residual NAICS mix and exposure of routine support to chatbots make both lens fit and long-run operator need uncertain.

caveats: The narrowed recurring-or-repeat service lens is coherent and disclosed, but S1 supplies only the residual-code boundary; the eligible-firm share e and the mix of recurring, fixed-fee, and time-and-materials revenue remain unobserved judgments.

S2 establishes task content and the direction of automation pressure, not a wage-weighted automatable share, implementation success rate, retained pricing share, or residual operator-required quantity for the selected 541519 firms.

The transfer primitive s5 rests on a broad small-business marketplace category whose median cash flow is below the screen's stated LMM EBITDA band and whose 44 reported sales provide no population denominator.

Demand durability uses broader 541500 wage-and-salary employment as a proxy for constant-price demand in a narrowed 541519 service basket; the bridge and likely overstatement direction are disclosed, but the proxy remains weakly matched to the construct.

All researched factors remain estimate- or proxy-conditioned, and the attainable interval spans CONDITIONAL through HIGHEST_PRIORITY; the PRIORITY base should therefore be used only to direct validation, as the stated MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action require.

records.title: Other Computer Related Services

caveats: a: BLS OEWS has no published 6-digit table for 541512; the 541500 4-digit figures blend custom programming (541511), facilities management (541513), and other computer services (541519) alongside systems design (541512).
a: Occupation-level exposure ratings are the author's judgment informed by Eloundou et al. and the GitHub Copilot experiment, not a task-level survey of this industry specifically.
a: Wage weighting uses May 2023 wage levels; wage-mix could shift as AI tooling changes which occupations command a premium.

rho: Gartner's 40%-of-apps figure describes AI agents embedded in the applications being built or sold, not the rate at which systems-design firms themselves have operationalized AI in their own delivery workflow; it is used here only as a directional adoption-speed signal, not a direct measurement of rho.
rho: No source separates implementation speed for LMM-band 541512 firms specifically from large systems integrators, who may adopt faster given greater capital for tooling.
e: No source directly measures the share of 541512 LMM-band firms that are single-client or otherwise lens-ineligible; this is judgment, not observation.

e: Government-contractor subsidiaries within 541512 have concentrated (often single-prime) customer relationships that are commercially real but structurally look like the low end of this eligibility range.

s5: The 69%-PE-backed MSP deal statistic describes managed-services providers, not systems-design/integration firms specifically; the two segments overlap in practice but are not the same NAICS activity.
s5: The 73%-of-companies-will-transition-in-a-decade figure is economy-wide and includes informal, non-control, and family transitions that would not count as a qualifying transfer under this lens.
q: The primary evidence (S11) is drawn from the very top of the consulting market, a materially different competitive position than a $1-10M EBITDA systems-design firm.
q: No source directly measures renewal-repricing behavior inside 541512 contracts specifically.

d5: Headcount growth is not the same construct as constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket; it can diverge in either direction depending on AI-driven productivity and on how much of projected job growth is in genuinely new service lines.

d5: The 19.5%/487,600-job figure is a 2023-2033 projection made before the more recent tariff-driven IT-budget pullback documented in source S10; the two sources are somewhat in tension on near-term trajectory.

o: No source directly measures the share of future systems-integration demand that will still require an outside accountable operator versus being self-served or software-supplied; this is judgment built on adjacent adoption-rate evidence.
o: Large-enterprise insourcing trends are better documented than mid-market/SMB behavior, which is the more relevant population for LMM-band firms but the least directly evidenced here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Clients are enterprises, mid-market companies, and government agencies that need hardware/software/network systems planned, integrated, and kept running. Revenue is billed through a mix of time-and-materials/hourly billing (still the modal structure for scope-uncertain integration and staff-augmentation work), fixed-price project billing (for well-scoped builds), and retainer/managed-support arrangements for post-implementation work. This mix matters directly for value capture: T&M billing means AI-driven throughput gains show up first as fewer billable hours unless pricing is renegotiated, while fixed-fee and retainer structures let the operator keep more of a productivity gain until the next repricing or renewal.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT departments of non-tech companies (out of scope by NAICS definition itself, since 541512 establishments serve outside clients); shell entities and non-control financing vehicles; single-engagement freelance/solo contractor activity with no durable client base or employees; pure hardware resale or facilities-management/help-desk outsourcing that is classified elsewhere (541513) even if adjacent.
lens.included_activities: Establishments that plan and design computer systems integrating hardware, software, and communications technology for external clients, then typically install the system and train and support its users. In practice this spans (a) discrete project engagements (requirements, architecture, integration, cutover) and (b) the ongoing installation-support-upgrade relationship that usually follows a project with the same client. Both are treated as the same lens because the second is the normal continuation of the first for an operator of this kind, not a different business. Firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that sell this work on a repeat basis to outside customers are in scope.

a.bridge: Source population is NAICS 541500 (Computer Systems Design and Related Services, the 4-digit parent of 541511/12/13/19) from BLS OEWS May 2023, the nearest published occupational breakdown to 541512 (BLS does not publish a separate 6-digit OEWS table for 541512). Transformation: software/web developers, programmers, and testers (27.35% of the subsector's employment, mean annual wage ~$125,010), computer support specialists (7.51%, ~$64,670), and computer/information analysts, security analysts, database and network administrators/architects (~11.4% combined, ~$104k-$140k) are wage-weighted and then discounted to a task-level exposure share using Eloundou et al.'s LLM task-exposure classification (source S7) and GitHub's controlled Copilot experiment showing 55% faster task completion on coding work (source S6) as calibration points for how much of the job, not just the occupation, is actually exposed. Management, business/financial operations, and sales occupations (~29% of employment, largely relationship/scoping/financial-administration work) are weighted low. Direction of error: ambiguous rather than one-sided. 541512 (systems design/integration) likely carries relatively more business/systems-analyst and project-management labor and relatively less pure coding labor than 541511 (custom programming), which would pull true 541512 exposure below this 5415-blended estimate; but 541512's install/support tail is itself exposed to AI-driven L1/L2 support automation, which pulls the other way. The range is kept wide to reflect this.
caveats: BLS OEWS has no published 6-digit table for 541512; the 541500 4-digit figures blend custom programming (541511), facilities management (541513), and other computer services (541519) alongside systems design (541512).
Occupation-level exposure ratings are the author's judgment informed by Eloundou et al. and the GitHub Copilot experiment, not a task-level survey of this industry specifically.
Wage weighting uses May 2023 wage levels; wage-mix could shift as AI tooling changes which occupations command a premium.
a.change_evidence: A 541512-specific (6-digit) OEWS or ONET task-importance breakdown, if BLS ever publishes one, would remove the 5415-to-541512 bridging error. A vendor-side study benchmarking AI-tool time savings specifically on systems-integration/architecture tasks (not just green-field coding) would sharpen the high-exposure-occupation assumptions.
a.rationale: The subsector is wage- and headcount-dominated by software/web development and computer-support occupations, both of which have direct, well-documented current-generation AI tooling (code assistants, agentic coding tools, AI-assisted support) that measurably compresses task time today. But a large minority of the wage bill sits in management, business operations, and sales functions whose core tasks (client relationship management, scoping, contract administration, people management) are only lightly touched by current tools. Netting these together against 2023-vintage occupation shares gives a base wage-weighted exposure share of roughly two-fifths.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS's national employment-count projection for the 'Computer Systems Design and Related Services' industry (source S2), which is projected to grow 19.5% and add about 487,600 jobs from 2023 to 2033 -- the second-largest job gain of all 292 detailed industries BLS tracks, with the article explicitly naming this industry as the main driver of the professional/scientific/technical-services sector being the fastest-growing sector in the economy. Transformation: a 10-year headcount-growth projection is prorated to a 5-year window (roughly half the cumulative growth, allowing for a non-linear path) and used as a proxy for real, constant-quality demand for the industry's current service basket. Direction of error is two-sided: if AI raises output per worker (plausible, given adoption trends elsewhere in this packet), true demand growth for the current basket could run ahead of headcount growth, understating d5; conversely, some of BLS's projected job growth likely reflects new AI-specific services adjacent to, but not part of, 'the current service basket' as defined for this lens, which would overstate d5.
caveats: Headcount growth is not the same construct as constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket; it can diverge in either direction depending on AI-driven productivity and on how much of projected job growth is in genuinely new service lines.

The 19.5%/487,600-job figure is a 2023-2033 projection made before the more recent tariff-driven IT-budget pullback documented in source S10; the two sources are somewhat in tension on near-term trajectory.
d5.change_evidence: A real (inflation- and quality-adjusted) output series for NAICS 5415, rather than an employment-count projection, would materially improve this; BEA industry value-added data or a direct constant-price demand index, if one existed for this industry, would replace the employment-based bridge entirely.
d5.rationale: The industry sits at the center of BLS's single most-cited driver of professional-services employment growth over the coming decade, reflecting durable enterprise demand for cloud migration, cybersecurity, and IT modernization. That said, near-term data (source S10) shows IT-budget growth expectations were cut roughly in half in a matter of months in 2025 amid tariff and macro uncertainty, with over 40% of surveyed IT leaders deferring discretionary projects -- a reminder that the multi-year growth trend can be lumpy and is not guaranteed to be smooth over any specific five-year window.

caveats: No source directly measures the share of 541512 LMM-band firms that are single-client or otherwise lens-ineligible; this is judgment, not observation.

Government-contractor subsidiaries within 541512 have concentrated (often single-prime) customer relationships that are commercially real but structurally look like the low end of this eligibility range.
e.change_evidence: Client-concentration disclosures from an M&A/business-brokerage dataset (e.g., deal teasers or CIMs for 541512-classified sell-side mandates) would let this be measured rather than estimated; so would a Census microdata cut of single-establishment vs. multi-client firms if accessible.
e.rationale: NAICS 541512 is defined to capture establishments that provide systems design/integration services to outside clients; captive internal IT departments of non-tech firms are not separately counted in this code in the first place, which removes the largest source of lens-ineligible activity before it ever enters the count. The remaining risk is smaller: some fraction of small 541512 filers are effectively single-client or project-only arrangements (a solo contractor operating through an LLC, or a small team spun out to serve one former parent company) that would not meet a repeat/outsourced-to-external-customers bar. Absent firm-level client-concentration data, a high but not extreme eligible share is the defensible estimate.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures the share of future systems-integration demand that will still require an outside accountable operator versus being self-served or software-supplied; this is judgment built on adjacent adoption-rate evidence.
Large-enterprise insourcing trends are better documented than mid-market/SMB behavior, which is the more relevant population for LMM-band firms but the least directly evidenced here.
o.change_evidence: Client-side surveys asking mid-market IT buyers directly whether they expect to insource, self-serve via AI-native/low-code tooling, or continue outsourcing systems-integration work over the next five years would replace this with observed data; case evidence of SMB clients successfully self-serving integration work using AI agents (or conversely, failing to and re-engaging an integrator) would sharpen either bound.
o.rationale: AI agents are being embedded directly into enterprise applications at a fast clip (source S8: under 5% of enterprise apps in 2025 to a projected 40% by 2026), and larger enterprises are visibly investing in internal AI/agent capability rather than relying solely on outside integrators, both of which cut against the need for an external operator. But 'embedding an agent in an app' and 'building, securing, integrating, and maintaining the system the agent lives inside' are different jobs; the latter is exactly what this lens's firms sell, and it remains complex, client-specific, and compliance-bound. The natural customer base for lower-middle-market 541512 firms is mid-market and smaller enterprises and government bodies that lack the in-house engineering depth to self-serve this work even as tooling improves, which supports continued need for an accountable outside party for a majority, but well under all, of year-five quantity.

q.bridge: Source population is elite global strategy consulting (McKinsey, BCG, Bain), reported in source S11: McKinsey now sources roughly a quarter of its global fees from outcome-based arrangements explicitly because AI is compressing the hours behind the work, and Bain/BCG describe AI-linked revenue climbing toward 40-50% of their business within a few years, with the same underlying dynamic (clients capturing part of the time savings rather than the firm keeping it as pure margin). Transformation: applied to 541512 with a downward adjustment, since McKinsey/BCG/Bain have far more pricing power, brand differentiation, and client lock-in than a typical fragmented, lower-middle-market systems-design shop competing largely on price and availability; if even the most differentiated consulting brands in the world are ceding roughly a quarter of the AI productivity dividend to clients, a commodity-priced local systems integrator plausibly cedes as much or more. Direction of error: this likely overstates 541512 retention (i.e., true q is more likely to be at or below the McKinsey-implied rate than above it).
caveats: The primary evidence (S11) is drawn from the very top of the consulting market, a materially different competitive position than a $1-10M EBITDA systems-design firm.
No source directly measures renewal-repricing behavior inside 541512 contracts specifically.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data on renewal repricing terms in systems-integration/IT-services agreements (average rate change at renewal, prevalence of AI-linked pricing clauses) would replace this estimate with an observed figure; a survey of mid-market IT-services firms specifically (rather than elite strategy consultancies) on outcome-based fee adoption would remove the bridging gap.
q.rationale: Billing structure is the crux. Where engagements are time-and-materials, an AI-driven cut in hours mechanically cuts revenue on that engagement unless the firm re-prices; where fixed-fee or staff-augmentation retainers apply, the firm keeps the gain until the next renewal, at which point a fragmented, price-competitive market gives clients leverage to reprice down. Renewal cycles, customer sharing of demonstrated savings, and the broader market-wide move toward outcome-based and value-based pricing structures all point toward the operator retaining well under half of an implemented gross benefit on a discounted five-year basis.

caveats: Gartner's 40%-of-apps figure describes AI agents embedded in the applications being built or sold, not the rate at which systems-design firms themselves have operationalized AI in their own delivery workflow; it is used here only as a directional adoption-speed signal, not a direct measurement of rho.
No source separates implementation speed for LMM-band 541512 firms specifically from large systems integrators, who may adopt faster given greater capital for tooling.
rho.change_evidence: A vendor or industry-association survey tracking actual (not planned) agentic-AI deployment inside systems-integration/IT-services delivery teams over 2026-2028, or case data on time-to-production for AI-assisted delivery pipelines in regulated client environments, would directly inform this.
rho.rationale: Gartner's own adoption curve for enterprise-embedded task-specific AI agents (under 5% of enterprise apps in 2025 projected to 40% by 2026) implies an unusually fast production-adoption ramp for AI tooling inside software/systems delivery relative to most white-collar workflows, and coding-assistant tools are already mainstream inside development teams today. Against that, systems-integration work for enterprise, regulated, or government clients carries real friction: security review, change-control boards, procurement cycles, and client sign-off gates that a five-year horizon can accommodate for straightforward automation but will slow for anything touching production systems of record or compliance-bound environments. A little more than half of the exposed opportunity being operationally implemented within five years, net of these frictions, is a defensible central estimate; it is not a high-confidence number because no source measures implementation lag specifically for this industry.

s5.bridge: Two source populations, both broader than 541512. (1) CT Acquisitions' 2026 PE/MSP M&A tracker (source S4): 169 disclosed MSP M&A deals in 2025, ~69% with PE involvement, and an active roll-up market rewarding recurring revenue and longer contract terms with higher multiples. MSPs are a related but distinct activity (often classified under 541513 or blended codes), and this segment is more institutionalized/recurring-revenue-native than the median project-based 541512 systems-design shop, so applying MSP deal intensity directly would likely overstate 541512 transfer probability. (2) Project Equity's compilation of succession-planning surveys (source S5): an estimated 73% of privately held U.S. companies plan to transition ownership within a decade (a $14T opportunity), but only about half report a 'detailed' plan and 78% lack a formal transition team. That is an economy-wide, 10-year, any-transition-type figure; prorating it to 5 years and narrowing to arm's-length/control transfers (excluding informal family hand-offs, which this lens's 's5' should exclude as non-qualifying in spirit) implies a materially lower 5-year probability than the headline 73%. Net direction: both bridges point toward overstatement if taken at face value, so the base sits well below either source's headline rate.
caveats: The 69%-PE-backed MSP deal statistic describes managed-services providers, not systems-design/integration firms specifically; the two segments overlap in practice but are not the same NAICS activity.
The 73%-of-companies-will-transition-in-a-decade figure is economy-wide and includes informal, non-control, and family transitions that would not count as a qualifying transfer under this lens.
s5.change_evidence: A deal tracker or database (e.g., GF Data, Pitchbook, or a buy-side advisory's proprietary data) segmented specifically to 541512 rather than blended MSP/IT-services categories would materially sharpen this; owner-age survey data specific to systems-integration/consulting firm principals (versus small business owners generally) would also help.
s5.rationale: There is clear, currently active PE and strategic buyer interest in fragmenting IT-services/MSP-adjacent businesses, and a large generational cohort of small-business owners is aging toward exit. But 541512 systems-design shops skew more project-based and less recurring-revenue-native than the MSPs driving most of the observed roll-up volume, and the broad succession-intent statistics describe a decade-long wave, not a concentrated five-year event. A one-in-five probability of a qualifying control transfer within five years for an eligible firm is a reasonable middle estimate given active deal flow tempered by the industry's weaker recurring-revenue profile relative to the MSP segment actually driving reported deal counts.

records.principal_driver: Fast, BLS-validated durable demand growth (19.5% employment growth, 2023-2033, the sector's #1 driver) combined with a wage bill concentrated in occupations where AI tooling already measurably compresses task time today.
records.principal_weakness: Price-competitive, largely hourly-billed market structure means AI-driven efficiency gains are likely to be substantially competed away at contract renewal rather than retained as operator margin.

caveats: The memo's firm-availability narrative states 'roughly 41% of small-business owners nationally are 55 or older,' a figure that does not appear on the cited S5 page (which reports 2.9 million businesses owned by individuals 55+). It is an unsourced narrative statistic; it drives no primitive and s5's base does not rest on it, so it fails the materiality test, but it should be sourced or dropped in any future attempt.

The q bridge equates McKinsey's ~25% outcome-based fee share with 'ceding roughly a quarter of the AI productivity dividend to clients.' An outcome-based fee share is not a measurement of dividend retention, so S11 functions closer to a disclosed analogy than a population-to-population proxy. The direction of error is honestly stated (likely overstates 541512 retention), the low end (0.18) spans the plausible correction, and treating q as ESTIMATE rather than PROXY changes neither the base tier (L is set by H=5.16, and C at base 6.0 stays above the PRIORITY floor) nor the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so this is not material.

Gartner's S8 prediction reads 'up to 40% of enterprise applications' and is a vendor forecast rather than a measurement; the packet quotes it as a flat '40% by 2026' and leans on it for both rho and o. The use is disclosed as directional-only, but both D-side and H-side primitives share this single optimistic vendor signal.

s5 and q both draw on the same MSP M&A evidence (S4) and share the recurring-revenue driver; the packet discloses this correlation in risks_and_uncertainty, but the envelope treats their ranges as independent, so the joint downside (both marked down together) is only narratively, not numerically, represented.

S9 is a worldwide, all-category IT spending forecast whose 2026 growth is led by AI data-center hardware; its support for 541512-specific service demand is corroborating context only, as the packet itself acknowledges.
BLS (S1, S2) and Gartner (S8, S9) block direct programmatic fetches; all four were verified via reader proxy or browser and match the cited content.

caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and covers the entire NAICS code, not the narrowed lens.
a: O*NET task data describe an occupation, not all occupations or firms in the industry.
a: Exposure is not realized labor reduction; implementation, quality control, and demand effects are excluded here.
rho: The cited AI samples are not an industry implementation study.
rho: The five-year period assumes continued model, security, and procurement progress but does not assume fully autonomous research delivery.
e: No public source observed the eligibility definition at LMM-firm level.
e: Occupation composition is an imperfect indicator of firm revenue model.
s5: No public dataset was found that measures qualifying control transfers for the frozen NAICS and EBITDA lens.
s5: Government-contract novation, key-person retention, and customer concentration can make a signed sale nontransferable in practice.
q: No observed industry-wide pass-through measure exists for the narrowed lens.
q: The result is especially sensitive to public-sector procurement rules and customer concentration.
d5: BLS projects employment, not revenue or constant-quality service quantity.
d5: The projection covers the full NAICS industry and a ten-year horizon, not the narrowed LMM lens and five-year horizon.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat external research engagements, task orders, panels, program evaluations, surveys, and analytical deliverables, commonly priced as fixed-fee, time-and-materials, or reimbursable projects.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat commissioned research contractors because the code's observed occupation mix includes counseling, clinical, education, fundraising, and health-service roles alongside research; these are not one coherent outsourced research-service model.
lens.excluded_activities: University and captive research units, grant-funded research without an external service customer, clinical or counseling service delivery, one-off self-directed research, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent firms conducting repeat commissioned social-science, behavioral, economic, policy, survey, evaluation, and humanities research for external government, nonprofit, and commercial customers.

a.bridge: S1 measures the 2023 employment and wages of all establishments in NAICS 541720, and S2 measures task content for social-science research assistants rather than the frozen contractor lens. I wage-weighted the observed occupation mix qualitatively: research assistance, data preparation, statistical analysis, reporting, software, office work, and project management are exposed, while field collection, client framing, subject interaction, methodological judgment, and accountable review are not fully substitutable. This can overstate exposure because S1 includes noneligible establishments and understate it because it omits self-employed owner work and newer AI-enabled analytical tasks.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and covers the entire NAICS code, not the narrowed lens.
O*NET task data describe an occupation, not all occupations or firms in the industry.
Exposure is not realized labor reduction; implementation, quality control, and demand effects are excluded here.
a.change_evidence: A wage-billed task census from 15 to 20 LMM research contractors, segmented by data collection, analysis, reporting, quality review, and project management; client-approved AI workflow logs; and contract deliverable inventories.
a.rationale: S1 reports 31.50 percent of employment in life, physical, and social-science occupations, 15.17 percent in business and financial operations, 10.15 percent in computer and mathematical occupations, and 12.70 percent in office support. Within the industry, social-science research assistants alone represent 10.82 percent of employment; S2 documents exposed tasks including data cleaning, statistical analysis, reporting, databases, and clerical work. Material senior research judgment and fieldwork remain outside the opportunity.

d5.bridge: S5 projects NAICS 541720 wage-and-salary employment from 70.0 thousand in 2024 to 72.6 thousand in 2034, a 3.7 percent ten-year increase. I use the implied modest five-year direction as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket in the narrowed lens, adjusting down for labor-saving technology and the difference between employment and service quantity. It can overstate demand if employment rises from lower productivity or understate it if automation lets more research be purchased with fewer workers.
caveats: BLS projects employment, not revenue or constant-quality service quantity.
The projection covers the full NAICS industry and a ten-year horizon, not the narrowed LMM lens and five-year horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted bookings, backlog, recompete win rates, task-order volume, federal and state social-science R&D appropriations, and repeat-client spend from a representative contractor panel.
d5.rationale: The official projection implies only modest ten-year employment growth, so the central case is near-flat to slightly positive five-year service demand rather than rapid expansion. Research demand is supported by policy, evaluation, and commercial insight needs, while automation and clients' internal analytical capacity can constrain outsourced quantity.

caveats: No public source observed the eligibility definition at LMM-firm level.
Occupation composition is an imperfect indicator of firm revenue model.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level Census Business Register or D&B/PrivCo pull for NAICS 541720, revenue and employee size, ownership, government-contractor status, and service descriptions; followed by website and customer-contract review of the LMM population.
e.rationale: The LMM count is injected separately. Eligibility is below the full code because the narrowed lens excludes internal, university, grant-only, clinical/counseling, and nonrepeat-service activity; S1's heterogeneous occupation mix supports a substantial but uncertain exclusion. The estimate represents firms that plausibly have repeat external commissioned-research revenue and transferable operations.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: This is an estimate of demand that still requires an operator, not a forecast of employment.
The share will vary sharply between regulated human-subject research, government evaluation, routine survey administration, and desk research.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and renewal-loss analysis distinguishing software self-service from outsourced work; product adoption data for survey, analytics, and research-agent tools; and contract scopes showing which work packages still require named professional accountability.
o.rationale: Most commissioned research still requires an accountable operator for problem framing, sampling, respondent or stakeholder interaction, methodological choices, privacy and ethics controls, interpretation, and delivery accountability. S2 shows that a core research-assistant role also contains data, analysis, reporting, and clerical tasks that software can absorb, limiting the operator-required share.

caveats: No observed industry-wide pass-through measure exists for the narrowed lens.
The result is especially sensitive to public-sector procurement rules and customer concentration.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level pricing and renewal data from comparable firms, including fixed-fee versus time-and-materials versus reimbursable mix, proposal win rates, labor-category rate cards, customer MFN clauses, and actual post-automation renewal margins.
q.rationale: Research contractors can initially retain workflow savings in fixed-fee and repeat task-order work, but project bidding, labor-hour billing, cost-reimbursable contracts, renewal repricing, and sophisticated procurement are likely to share a meaningful portion with customers. The range is a discounted five-year retention judgment after those effects, without embedding volume effects or implementation difficulty.

rho.bridge: S3 reports observed Claude task success and speedup across its own November 2025 user sample, while S4 reports organization-wide AI adoption and early agent use; neither measures five-year operational implementation in LMM social-science research contractors. I translate their evidence of strong performance on complex knowledge work but uneven, early autonomous deployment into the share of the a opportunity likely to survive workflow design, validation, privacy, procurement, and customer acceptance. This likely overstates implementation if contractor data are more sensitive than the source populations and understates it if tooling improves rapidly.
caveats: The cited AI samples are not an industry implementation study.
The five-year period assumes continued model, security, and procurement progress but does not assume fully autonomous research delivery.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating metrics from comparable contractors, including cycle time, labor hours per deliverable, error/rework rates, privacy incidents, customer acceptance, and AI governance approval rates; plus agency and university IRB or data-use requirements.
rho.rationale: S3 finds higher speedups on complex tasks and a 66 percent success rate for college-degree-level tasks, which supports assisted analysis and drafting. S4 reports broad organizational AI adoption but agent deployment still in single digits in nearly all functions. Research protocols, confidential respondent data, reproducibility, and client review constrain deployment beyond individual use.

caveats: No public dataset was found that measures qualifying control transfers for the frozen NAICS and EBITDA lens.
Government-contract novation, key-person retention, and customer concentration can make a signed sale nontransferable in practice.
s5.change_evidence: Brokered-deal and closed-transaction data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, coded to 541720 and EBITDA band; owner-age and succession data from a targeted owner survey; and novation or consent records for government contracts.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer requires a buyer, durable contracts, transferable personnel and methods, and acceptable customer or clearance transition terms. The base is a bounded five-year judgment for eligible LMM professional-service contractors, not a measured NAICS transfer rate; it excludes closures and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: A large share of the operating work is digital research production, data handling, analysis, coordination, and reporting rather than physical delivery.
records.principal_weakness: The NAICS code is heterogeneous and no observed LMM data establish either transferable-firm supply or durable retention of AI-derived savings.

caveats: The a base is a qualitative transformation of a full-industry occupation mix and one occupation's task inventory; the stated wage-weighting is not numerically reproducible from the rationale, although the PROXY label, population mismatch, and error directions are disclosed.

The rho range extrapolates from a product-specific Claude-use sample and economy-wide adoption evidence to five-year implementation in LMM research contractors. The bridge is coherent and honestly labeled PROXY, but comparable-contractor operating evidence is absent.

The narrowed repeat-commissioned-contractor lens is disclosed and economically coherent, but firm-level data do not establish what share of the NAICS population matches it; e is appropriately an ESTIMATE.

Transfer incidence, retention, and operator-required demand remain bounded judgments without direct industry measurements. Their ESTIMATE treatment correctly drives MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The d5 bridge uses a ten-year full-industry employment projection as a five-year constant-quality service-demand proxy. The source supports the projection and the mismatch is disclosed, but bookings and revenue evidence for the narrowed lens would be stronger.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial and personal customers receive placement, policy service, renewal, claims assistance, and risk advice; agency revenue is primarily carrier commission, including renewal commission, with some fee-based advice.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal insurance units, carrier-owned sales forces acting solely for one insurer, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market insurance agencies and brokerages that procure, place, service, renew, and advise on insurance for external customers on a recurring or repeat basis.

caveats: No public source supplies a NAICS 524210 wage-weighted task inventory.
The occupational source includes sales agents outside agencies and excludes some agency support roles.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-by-wage matrix combined with time-and-motion samples from agency management systems, plus a task-level study of producer, account-manager, and CSR work, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The wage-weighted task share is estimated from the industry’s core sales-and-service work. Insurance sales-agent duties include interviewing clients, explaining policies, analyzing current coverage, renewals, claims assistance, and record maintenance; agency AI evidence identifies data entry, comparisons, routine service, document analysis, and administrative workflow as susceptible to AI assistance [S1, S2]. Advisory judgment, relationship building, licensed sales, and complex-risk placement remain outside the exposed share.

d5.bridge: BLS projects insurance-sales-agent employment to rise 4% from 2024 to 2034 and explains that independent-agent growth should be strongest as carriers rely more on brokerages, while online purchase reduces agent demand [S1]. This occupation-level ten-year nominal headcount outlook is converted to a five-year constant-price, constant-quality quantity outlook for the current agency-service basket. It may understate output growth if technology raises productivity and may overstate demand if headcount is retained despite lower service quantity.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation, not real output or the specified service basket.
Premium inflation, catastrophe cycles, and rate changes are excluded from this construct but can dominate observed agency revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar NAICS 524210 output data, carrier distribution-channel premium and commission data, and a cohort study of policies serviced per agency worker would materially change this estimate.
d5.rationale: The central case is near-flat to modestly positive service demand. Continued need for new customers and independent distribution offsets online self-service, but does not justify extrapolating the occupation projection as a direct measure of industry output.

caveats: There is no published LMM-band tabulation of captive, independent, fee-only, and non-operating firms within NAICS 524210.
Eligibility is a firm construct, whereas the cited source describes workers and roles.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 524210 business register matched to carrier appointments, ownership records, and revenue composition would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: Most LMM firms classified here are expected to be external-customer agencies or brokerages with recurring renewal and service work. The allowance below one excludes captive or otherwise non-transferable operations mixed into the code. BLS distinguishes captive agents from independent agents and describes brokers as client representatives; this supports, but does not measure, the operational distinction [S1].
