o.bridge: The Big I evidence comes from independent-agency members rather than every LMM firm: it reports that 85% to 90% of customers still want agent guidance for purchases and policy management, while routine data entry, comparison, service, and administrative work move to AI [S2]. BLS separately reports that online self-service reduces demand but agents remain needed for advice and policy choice [S1]. These findings are translated into the future share of the current service quantity requiring an accountable agency operator; survey respondents may overstate customer preference for agents, making the proxy potentially upward biased.
caveats: The customer-guidance measure is an industry association survey rather than observed customer behavior.
The share will vary sharply between simple personal lines and complex commercial or specialty placements.
o.change_evidence: Carrier and insurtech channel-level bind and renewal data, customer-choice experiments by line of business, and state enforcement data on digitally assisted sales would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Accountable operators should remain necessary for complex coverage explanation, client-specific advice, carrier access, licensed activity, renewals, and claims support, even as simple transactions migrate to digital self-service. State producer licensing reinforces that selling, soliciting, or negotiating insurance is regulated activity [S6].

caveats: Employee compensation is not the same as firm revenue, so the source supports the revenue-model inference rather than measuring retention.
Retention differs materially by personal, commercial, employee-benefits, and specialty business.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level commission schedules, client fee agreements, renewal-price histories, carrier compensation grids, and post-automation margin cohorts would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: The base reflects commission- and renewal-led revenue rather than cost-plus billing: BLS identifies commissions as the most common compensation form and states that their amount depends partly on whether business is new or renewal [S1]. This can preserve some labor productivity benefit, but competitive quoting, carrier platform automation, service expectations, and renewal repricing should share part of the gain with customers or carriers.

caveats: The Big I survey targets independent-agency members of varying sizes, not the frozen EBITDA band.
NAIC AI guidance addresses insurers and may constrain agencies indirectly through carrier requirements rather than directly.
rho.change_evidence: The full Big I longitudinal agency survey with workflow-level adoption, vendor telemetry from agency-management-system integrations, and post-implementation staffing data for LMM agencies would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementation is bounded below the exposed task share because only 8% of surveyed independent agencies had AI embedded in daily workflows, while 68% planned to increase AI use in the next year [S2]. Data privacy or compliance concerns, inaccurate outputs, disconnected systems, and limited formal policies constrain deployment. The base assumes human-reviewed automation of routine work rather than autonomous insurance advice or binding.

s5.bridge: Vertafore measures retirement intentions of insurance professionals rather than ownership-control transfers of LMM NAICS 524210 firms: about one quarter expected to retire within five years, and half of retiring owners and principals reported a succession plan [S5]. MarshBerry measures announced US insurance-brokerage transactions across all target sizes: 847 in 2024, with 593 by private-capital-backed buyers [S4]. These indicators are translated into an LMM qualifying-control-transfer probability after excluding employee retirements, non-transferable closures, and reorganizations. Worker retirement likely overstates owner exit, while announced-deal data likely understates local private transfers, so the net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: MarshBerry transaction counts cover all brokerage sizes and announced transactions, not LMM firms only.
The retirement survey is voluntary and includes employees as well as owners.
s5.change_evidence: A paid transaction database matched to target revenue or EBITDA, state producer-agency ownership filings, and a survey of LMM principals’ planned ownership exits would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: A meaningful five-year transfer flow is supported by both retirement/succession evidence and sustained brokerage M&A, but neither source gives transfers for the frozen firm band. The base deliberately remains below the broad workforce retirement signal because transfer requires an eligible owner, operating continuity, and a qualifying control transaction.

records.principal_driver: Recurring service and renewal relationships provide a durable operating base while routine workflow work is amenable to human-supervised AI.
records.principal_weakness: The best operational savings may be offset by limited implementation capacity and by pricing or service competition in simpler insurance transactions.

caveats: S2 does not substantiate the o bridge's quoted 85%-90% customer-guidance statistic. Removing that statistic weakens the calibration of o, but S1 and S6 still support the direction of accountable operator need, o is already labeled PROXY with an upward-bias warning and a broad 0.70-0.92 range, and a plausible correction does not reduce D below the binding H factor or change the CONDITIONAL tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

S5 misreads a succession-planning statistic as retirement incidence. This weakens the numerical calibration of s5, although S4 still supports active transfer activity and the packet already discloses that neither source measures LMM qualifying-control-transfer probability. Correcting s5 within an honest estimate/proxy treatment does not move saturated F enough to change the CONDITIONAL tier, and the action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS because the record is already MODEL_CONDITIONED.

The core conclusions remain highly model-conditioned: a, rho, e, q, and the dataset firm-count anchor are estimates, while s5, d5, and o depend on broad population or construct bridges. The ranges and narrative disclose these weaknesses, but Stage 2 use should validate workflow exposure, implementation realization, qualifying transfer incidence, and customer/channel behavior.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring episodes, visits, and care plans paid principally by government and commercial health plans, patients, or their representatives; Medicare-certified services are commonly reimbursed through a case-mix and wage-adjusted 30-day bundle.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive health-system units, shell entities, internal staffing departments, entities without control-transferable operations, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market home health agencies that provide recurring skilled nursing, therapy, aide, care-coordination, and related in-home services to external patients or payer-funded customers.

caveats: The published 58.14% is employment share, not wage share, and is from 2023.
The occupation table does not measure AI-susceptible task time or distinguish agency-specific workflow maturity.
a.change_evidence: A 621610 occupation-by-wage file plus time-and-motion studies or EHR audit logs that separate direct-care, travel, documentation, scheduling, authorization, and billing time; interviews with agency clinical and revenue-cycle leaders.
a.rationale: Home health and personal care aides represented 58.14% of industry employment in May 2023, and their documented core duties include hands-on personal assistance, household help, transport, and social engagement. Those duties limit substitution, while documentation, scheduling, intake, coding support, care coordination, and clinician administrative work create a meaningful but minority wage-weighted AI opportunity. The base is a judgmental blend, not a measured task-exposure index.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US employment of the broad home health and personal care aide occupation, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 621610. I transform its 17% 2024-2034 projection to a five-year compound ratio of about 1.08 and use it as a directional demand proxy. It may overstate service quantity if labor productivity rises or work shifts to other settings, and may understate it if aide demand shifts disproportionately into agency home health.
caveats: This is an occupational, national ten-year projection, not an industry-specific five-year real-demand forecast.
Employment growth can differ from service quantity because staffing intensity and productivity change.
d5.change_evidence: CMS home-health claims and beneficiary utilization forecasts by payer, state Medicaid home- and community-based-service projections, and agency census/visit-volume cohorts adjusted for case mix and price.
d5.rationale: The occupational projection is 17% over 2024-2034 and BLS attributes demand to an aging population and a shift toward home- and community-based long-term care. Applying half the projection horizon gives the base ratio; the range recognizes payer, labor-supply, and setting-mix uncertainty.

caveats: The supplied firm-band count is an estimate and does not identify captive status, transferability, or certification.
No published source was found that measures lens eligibility for 621610 LMM firms.
e.change_evidence: A named HHA-provider directory linked to ownership, Medicare certification, payer mix, branch structure, and quality/compliance records; a broker screen of LMM agency CIMs.
e.rationale: Most firms already placed in the supplied LMM band should be external-service operators because that is the NAICS activity, but the lens excludes captive units, nontransferable owner-dependent operations, and firms with licensing, compliance, payer, or customer-concentration barriers. The base therefore allows a material ineligible tail without treating it as observed.

o.bridge: S5 measures Medicare-certified HHA governance and clinical-management requirements rather than the full 621610 lens or the share of year-five demand. I use the requirement that an HHA retain responsibility for patient care, employ an accountable administrator, and provide clinical oversight as evidence that nearly all retained regulated service volume still needs an accountable operator. This may overstate the result for non-Medicare personal-care activity and understate it if compliance increases demand for supervised operators.
caveats: The regulation applies to Medicare-certified HHAs and does not prove future operator need for every service or payer in NAICS 621610.
The estimate does not model changes in benefit design or family-provided care.
o.change_evidence: Payer authorization and utilization data, state licensure requirements for personal-care providers, technology-enabled care substitution studies, and interviews with Medicare surveyors, clinical managers, and discharge planners.
o.rationale: AI can change routing, documentation, triage, and coordination but cannot itself assume the licensed agency's legal, clinical, quality, staffing, and patient-care responsibilities. A small reduction allowance covers digitally self-served or alternative-setting demand.

caveats: Medicare PPS is not the revenue model for all 621610 services or payers.
No source provides the discounted five-year operator-retention share of AI benefit.
q.change_evidence: De-identified payer contracts and renewal histories, agency payer-mix files, Medicare cost reports, state Medicaid fee schedules, and before-and-after AI productivity and rate data from comparable agencies.
q.rationale: For Medicare episodes, the standardized 30-day bundled rate is adjusted for case mix and local wages rather than each agency's realized administrative effort, allowing an efficient operator to retain some productivity benefit. Retention is curtailed by annual rate resets, the 2025 permanent negative payment adjustment, managed-care and Medicaid contracts, and competition for scarce clinicians. The base assumes moderate retention after those channels rather than full pass-through or full capture.

caveats: No source measures realized five-year AI implementation for 621610 firms.
Medicare-certified HHA regulations do not cover every non-Medicare service in the NAICS code.
rho.change_evidence: Agency-level AI deployment surveys, EHR-vendor implementation data, Medicare audit findings on AI-assisted documentation, and reference calls with compliance officers at multi-branch and independent HHAs.
rho.rationale: Implementation can be relatively quick for back-office drafting, scheduling, documentation preparation, and call handling, but clinical oversight, individualized plans of care, payer documentation rules, privacy controls, and fragmented EHR workflows constrain operational realization. The estimate assumes implementation of less than half of the exposed opportunity in five years.

caveats: BizBuySell reports marketplace transactions, listings, and broker sentiment rather than an industry-specific transfer rate.
The cited SBA owner-retirement research uses older 1992-2010 panel data and small-business owners generally.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, S&P Capital IQ, Axial, and proprietary healthcare-broker closed-deal data filtered to US 621610 businesses with 1 million to 10 million dollars of normalized EBITDA, plus owner-age and succession interviews.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying-transfer probability of 12% reflects a recurring small-business sale market but is deliberately below a simple accumulation of listings because some owners wind down, transfer internally, fail diligence, or lack transferable operations. Marketplace evidence reports active service transactions and buyer interest in recurring-revenue healthcare-related services, but does not measure 621610 transfers or an EBITDA-band denominator.

records.principal_driver: Durable, growing need for accountable in-home care paired with administrative workflow burden.
records.principal_weakness: AI addresses a limited portion of a predominantly hands-on workforce and value capture is constrained by payer and regulatory dynamics.

caveats: The labor-opportunity inputs a and rho are bounded judgment rather than direct task-time, wage-weighted exposure, or realized five-year implementation measurements; the wide H range and MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness appropriately disclose this limitation.

The firm-availability inputs e and s5 lack a 621610-specific eligible-firm census or closed-deal denominator, and the injected n anchor is itself an EBITDA-margin-bridged estimate; F should therefore be read as a screening estimate rather than an observed acquisition pipeline.

The demand proxy d5 derives five-year industry quantity direction from a ten-year national occupational employment forecast, while o generalizes Medicare-certified HHA governance requirements beyond the exact regulated population; both bridges identify plausible error directions and the envelope reflects substantial sensitivity.

The retention estimate q is supported only indirectly by Medicare payment design and a historical rate adjustment; payer mix, contract resets, clinician competition, and non-Medicare reimbursement could materially alter realized capture even though the estimate is honestly labeled and broadly ranged.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, government, and project-owner customers; revenue is commonly professional-services fees tied to projects, retainers, time and materials, milestones, or fixed scopes.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive engineering units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose work is not a recurring or repeat outsourced service to external customers.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market engineering firms that repeatedly provide outsourced engineering advice, feasibility work, plans and designs, technical support during installation, inspection, evaluation, and related services to external customers.

caveats: OEWS measures employees at establishments of all sizes and excludes self-employed persons.
Occupational titles do not reveal task allocation, software environment, client data restrictions, or licensure responsibility.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level time-and-motion study separating engineering design, review and signoff, field work, project management, estimating, and administration; paired with payroll by occupation and AI workflow usage logs.
a.rationale: S2 shows a technical and knowledge-work-heavy workforce: architecture and engineering occupations are 45.55 percent of employment, business and financial operations 13.21 percent, management 11.11 percent, and computer and mathematical occupations 8.65 percent. AI can substitute or avoid hiring for documentation, information retrieval, first-draft analysis, coding, proposal, estimating, and project-administration work, but not the full accountable engineering judgment, field observation, or professional signoff. The range is a wage-weighted judgment over current tasks rather than a measured AI-exposure index.

d5.bridge: S3 projects NAICS 541330 employment from 1,179.6 thousand in 2024 to 1,228.9 thousand in 2034, a 4.2 percent decade increase. I transform that employment projection into a five-year proxy for constant-price, constant-quality quantity demanded for the current service basket. Employment can diverge from demand because productivity, offshoring, mix, and prices change; the proxy could either understate or overstate demand.
caveats: S3 is an employment projection, not a constant-price measure of demand for the frozen service basket.
The BLS projection horizon is 2024 to 2034, whereas this packet uses a five-year horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar engineering-services revenue by NAICS, client-sector capital-expenditure forecasts, public infrastructure award pipelines, and a firm panel tracking backlog, bookings, utilization, and realized project volume.
d5.rationale: The BLS industry projection implies modest positive medium-term activity for engineering services. The base converts that direction into a near-flat five-year demand ratio, with downside for cyclical capital-project exposure and upside for sustained infrastructure, industrial, and compliance work.

caveats: NAICS classification is establishment-based and does not identify recurrence, customer concentration, control status, or EBITDA.
The lens exclusions cannot be observed from the injected firm-count estimate alone.
e.change_evidence: A target-company list with revenue concentration, customer recurrence, owner role, legal entity, service-line, and normalized EBITDA fields; supplemented by interviews with specialized engineering-services intermediaries.
e.rationale: S1 defines the industry around external engineering services, making many lower-middle-market firms directionally consistent with the lens. The estimate discounts firms that are effectively project-specific contractors, captive-like affiliates, nontransferable owner practices, or otherwise outside recurring or repeat outsourced service delivery.

caveats: The NAICS definition describes activities, not the fraction requiring a human accountable operator after five years.
Operator requirement varies by jurisdiction, discipline, safety consequence, and client procurement rules.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on willingness to self-serve design and analysis; claims and professional-licensure guidance; adoption data for automated engineering tools; and project-level records distinguishing automated self-service from engineer-reviewed delivery.
o.rationale: S1 defines the work as applying engineering principles to designs, systems, technical services, inspection, and evaluation. Even where software accelerates production, most current service quantity is expected to retain a need for an accountable operator to frame the problem, validate outputs, integrate site and client constraints, and accept professional responsibility. The range allows for self-service tools and productized templates to eliminate some lower-complexity work.

caveats: Revenue model and buyer bargaining power vary by discipline, client type, project size, and concentration.
The industry definition does not report contract terms or the incidence of fixed-fee versus hourly billing.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample coded by fee type, escalation and renewal terms, change-order rights, utilization targets, and client savings-sharing provisions; plus pre- and post-automation gross-margin data for comparable projects.
q.rationale: The retained share is estimated below a fully fixed-fee software-like outcome because clients can request lower fees at renewal and time-and-materials arrangements can expose saved hours. It remains material because firms may retain some benefit through greater throughput, shorter cycle times, improved bid capacity, and fixed-scope execution. This judgment isolates commercial retention after implementation rather than demand or implementation risk.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market engineering firms.
Implementation will differ substantially by engineering discipline, safety criticality, client security requirements, and the availability of clean historical project data.
rho.change_evidence: Interviews with principals, project managers, and professional-liability carriers; audit trails showing use of AI in design and document-control workflows; and client contract clauses on AI, confidentiality, and engineer-of-record review.
rho.rationale: The base case assumes firms operationalize a meaningful portion of exposed drafting, analysis, documentation, proposal, and project-control work within five years, while retaining review, validation, client-specific workflows, and accountable professional judgment. S1's industry definition includes designs, technical services during installation, inspection, and evaluation, all of which create quality and liability constraints that limit implementation.

caveats: No directly verified NAICS-specific five-year control-transfer rate was located.
Observed announced transactions would undercount small private deals and would not distinguish asset sales, minority investments, and qualifying control transfers without deal-level review.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data for NAICS 541330 from an M&A database, business-broker and investment-bank transaction logs, and a representative owner succession survey that records age, ownership concentration, and intended transfer timing.
s5.rationale: This is a bounded judgment for qualifying control transfers among eligible firms over five years. Technical founder dependence, project-client relationships, professional licensure, and buyer diligence can suppress transfers, while succession needs and a fragmented professional-services market can create opportunities. It excludes closures without transferable operations and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: A large technical and knowledge-work workforce creates identifiable workflow opportunities while accountable engineering service remains necessary.
records.principal_weakness: NAICS-level evidence cannot establish repeat revenue, firm transferability, or the contractual share of AI gains retained by a buyer.

caveats: S1 is reachable but did not render the cited code-specific definition, leaving the scope context for rho, e, and o unsupported on reopening; the affected inputs are already honest estimates, so this does not change the base tier or action category.

The base automatable share is a broad judgment inferred from occupational composition rather than a task-level exposure measurement; the ESTIMATE label and 0.22-0.45 range appropriately disclose that limitation.

The d5 bridge uses a ten-year employment projection as a proxy for five-year constant-price service demand. Productivity, service mix, offshoring, and pricing could move demand differently, although the PROXY label and bidirectional bridge are explicit.

The lens marks the industry as non-heterogeneous even though the memo describes material variation across disciplines and client sectors. The ranges and caveats acknowledge that variation, so the inconsistency does not change the tier or action.

caveats: a: S1 is May 2023 employment and wage data, not a task survey.
a: The industry includes several therapy disciplines with different documentation and payer workflows.
a: Owner-clinician compensation and contracted billing labor may not be fully represented in payroll occupations.
rho: No source directly measures five-year implementation of the defined opportunity in lower-middle-market therapy offices.
rho: Rates will vary sharply with EHR maturity, payer mix, and multi-state compliance requirements.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than a census of eligible practices.
e: S6 is a market-report announcement rather than a public microdata tabulation.
e: Eligibility requires facts about operating control and service recurrence that NAICS data do not provide.
s5: S8 is a small, unweighted, cross-specialty survey and does not identify therapy practices separately.
s5: Publicly announced transactions omit many small private deals and cannot reveal the number of eligible firms offered for sale.
s5: Corporate-practice and professional-ownership rules can affect whether an economic deal is a qualifying control transfer.
q: No public source measures five-year benefit retention for the defined AI changes in this industry.
q: Payer mix and local competition can dominate the result.
q: This estimate excludes volume changes and implementation feasibility, which belong in other primitives.
d5: National occupational projections include hospitals, schools, home health, and other settings outside the frozen lens.
d5: Employment growth is not identical to quantity demand because productivity and staffing mix may change.
d5: The basket includes speech and audiology, for which this proxy is less direct.
o: The estimate is for the current service basket, not for all future digital rehabilitation products.
o: Some low-acuity exercise and education visits may migrate to self-service or software-assisted models.
o: The degree of direct physical contact differs across PT, OT, SLP, and audiology.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patient-facing professional therapy and audiology services, paid principally per visit or episode by Medicare, Medicaid, commercial insurers, employers, and self-pay patients; referrals and repeat treatment visits create recurring demand.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments, school-district staff, home-health and nursing-facility operations classified elsewhere, captive employer clinics, shells, internal service units, and financing vehicles without operating control.
lens.included_activities: Independent and regional outpatient offices furnishing physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, or audiology to external patients and referral sources, where the service is a repeat care episode and the firm is in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: S1 measures employment and mean wages by occupation in NAICS 621340, rather than the wage-weighted share of current tasks exposed to AI substitution or avoidable hiring. I classify the administrative, documentation, intake, scheduling, billing, coding, authorization, and management portions of that mix as materially exposed, while retaining direct evaluation, treatment, exercise assistance, physical set-up, and accountable clinical judgment as largely unexposed; this likely overstates exposure where documentation is inseparable from clinician judgment and understates it where revenue-cycle work is concentrated in back-office staff.
caveats: S1 is May 2023 employment and wage data, not a task survey.
The industry includes several therapy disciplines with different documentation and payer workflows.
Owner-clinician compensation and contracted billing labor may not be fully represented in payroll occupations.
a.change_evidence: A clinic-level time-and-motion study that separates clinician documentation, authorization, billing, scheduling, intake, and direct-treatment hours; payroll by occupation for target firms; and an EHR task-log extract from outpatient therapy clinics would materially refine the wage weighting.
a.rationale: The industry has a high-paid clinician core: healthcare practitioners and technical occupations were 44.30% of employment in the industry, including physical therapists at 19.08%, occupational therapists at 8.98%, and speech-language pathologists at 9.19% in S1. Direct care remains hands-on and licensed, but aides perform clerical work and scheduling, and therapists and managers carry documentation, billing, authorization, referral, and communication workflows. The resulting opportunity is meaningful but minority wage exposure.

d5.bridge: S2 and S9 measure national decade employment projections for physical and occupational therapists across all settings, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 621340 service basket. I convert the reported decade growth into a five-year directional demand proxy and temper it for the industry’s mix of therapy disciplines and payer-limited outpatient utilization. The proxy may understate office demand if aging shifts care toward outpatient therapy, or overstate it if employment growth reflects other settings, productivity changes, or school and hospital demand.
caveats: National occupational projections include hospitals, schools, home health, and other settings outside the frozen lens.
Employment growth is not identical to quantity demand because productivity and staffing mix may change.
The basket includes speech and audiology, for which this proxy is less direct.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar outpatient therapy visit volumes by payer from CMS claims, commercial claims vendors, and state all-payer claims databases; referral-source data; and specialty-specific forecasts for speech and audiology offices would materially refine the ratio.
d5.rationale: The major clinical disciplines have positive projected employment demand: physical therapists are projected to grow 11% over the decade, and occupational therapists 14%. BLS attributes therapy demand to an aging population, chronic conditions, disability, rehabilitation, and nonopioid pain management. This supports moderate growth in constant-quality service demand, with reimbursement and referral constraints preventing an assumption that all occupational growth maps into office revenue.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than a census of eligible practices.
S6 is a market-report announcement rather than a public microdata tabulation.
Eligibility requires facts about operating control and service recurrence that NAICS data do not provide.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level list from SUSB or a commercial provider database matched to ownership, site count, payer mix, clinician headcount, and EBITDA-normalization records would identify actual eligible firms.
e.rationale: Most firms represented by the frozen lower-middle-market count should be operating external-care providers rather than shells or captive units because the NAICS is specific to therapist and audiologist offices. The industry is reported as fragmented, with small to medium regional providers predominant, supporting a high but not universal eligibility assumption. The deduction recognizes that some firms may be single-clinician, referral-dependent, non-repeat, professionally restricted, or otherwise unsuitable despite falling in the EBITDA band.

caveats: The estimate is for the current service basket, not for all future digital rehabilitation products.
Some low-acuity exercise and education visits may migrate to self-service or software-assisted models.
The degree of direct physical contact differs across PT, OT, SLP, and audiology.
o.change_evidence: Payer coverage policies and claims volumes for digital musculoskeletal rehabilitation, remote therapeutic monitoring, tele-rehabilitation, and self-directed exercise programs; state scope-of-practice changes; and clinic-level substitution data would test the remaining operator requirement.
o.rationale: The service remains predominantly accountable clinician work. Physical therapists require a DPT and state licensure, occupational therapists require graduate education and state licensure, and aides and assistants are supervised while performing physical, patient-facing activities. Software can support home exercise, triage, monitoring, documentation, scheduling, and communication, but it does not remove the need for licensed evaluation, treatment-plan responsibility, in-person handling, or payer-acceptable clinical records for most of the current basket.

caveats: No public source measures five-year benefit retention for the defined AI changes in this industry.
Payer mix and local competition can dominate the result.
This estimate excludes volume changes and implementation feasibility, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: Target-firm payer contracts, fee schedules, renewal history, gross-margin bridges after automation, and payer negotiations that explicitly cite productivity or staffing would determine actual pass-through and repricing.
q.rationale: Savings from internal administrative labor, faster collections, lower denial rework, and improved clinician utilization can initially remain with the operator because most service revenue is billed per visit or under negotiated schedules rather than cost-plus reimbursement. Retention is materially limited over time by Medicare fee schedules, commercial-payer negotiations, referral competition, and customer pressure to share productivity through lower prices or more service. The base assumes more than half of implemented benefit remains after those mechanisms, but far less than full retention.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year implementation of the defined opportunity in lower-middle-market therapy offices.
Rates will vary sharply with EHR maturity, payer mix, and multi-state compliance requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Named EHR vendor adoption and outcome data for WebPT, Clinicient, or comparable rehabilitation systems; payer audit findings on AI-assisted documentation; and interviews with clinic operators, compliance officers, and revenue-cycle leaders would move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Workflow AI can be implemented relatively quickly for intake, scheduling, call handling, documentation drafts, coding support, authorization preparation, denial follow-up, and patient communications. Implementation is constrained by protected health information, EHR integration, payer documentation rules, clinical supervision, state-specific scope rules, and the need to validate output before submission. APTA respondents specifically report administrative burden and payment pressure, which creates adoption incentive but also makes accuracy failures costly.

s5.bridge: S8 surveys 112 independent healthcare-practice owners, mostly physicians, on their stated likelihood of selling or retiring within five years; it does not measure completed control transfers among lower-middle-market therapy and audiology firms. I translate its 35% at-least-somewhat-likely response into a lower completed-transfer probability by excluding weak intent, closures, employment exits, minority sales, and transactions that do not convey control. This may overstate transfer probability because the survey is non-probability and physician-heavy, or understate it if strategic buyers convert more therapy practices than owners expect.
caveats: S8 is a small, unweighted, cross-specialty survey and does not identify therapy practices separately.
Publicly announced transactions omit many small private deals and cannot reveal the number of eligible firms offered for sale.
Corporate-practice and professional-ownership rules can affect whether an economic deal is a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A proprietary transaction database filtered to NAICS 621340 and EBITDA band; broker CRM data on signed letters of intent and closed sales; and a representative owner-age and succession survey for PT, OT, SLP, and audiology offices would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: There is credible availability pressure but not a direct therapy-firm transfer rate. S8 reports that 35% of independent practice owners were at least somewhat likely to sell or retire within five years and that retirement was the most-cited reason for a sale. The PT market also recorded 65 publicly disclosed transactions in 2025, including many small-clinic transactions, but disclosed deals are not a denominator-based rate. The base therefore discounts broad owner intention to completed qualifying control transfers.

records.principal_driver: Durable, aging-linked outpatient rehabilitation demand combined with automatable administrative workflows around a licensed clinical core.
records.principal_weakness: The largest wages buy direct clinical care that is difficult to substitute, while reimbursement and ownership constraints can limit both savings capture and transferability.

caveats: S8 understates its own stated likelihood result by treating the 35% somewhat-likely response as the total at-least-somewhat-likely share rather than adding the 17% very-likely and 6% completely-likely groups. Correcting this would directionally raise s5 and F, but H remains the base weakest link at 3.04, so the base tier remains CONDITIONAL and the action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS; the error therefore fails the methodology's materiality test.

S4 and S7 are PT-specific while the lens includes PT, OT, SLP, and audiology. The packet discloses this population mismatch, so it limits confidence in rho, s5, and d5 without constituting an undisclosed proxy.

S5 establishes Medicare fee-schedule policy context but does not directly substantiate the numerical q range or most implementation constraints assigned to rho. Both primitives are honestly labeled ESTIMATE and the record already carries the model-conditioned action.

The lens combines disciplines with meaningfully different workflows and substitution paths while marking heterogeneous as false. The narrative and caveats expressly acknowledge the mix, and there is no favorable exclusion or lens shopping, but discipline-level diligence is necessary.

The numeric bases for a, rho, e, q, and o remain judgment-heavy. Their evidence states, bridges where required, uncertainty ranges, and change-evidence requests are sufficiently candid for screening, not underwriting.

caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed people, an important potential component of consulting firms.
a: No public source measured AI-exposed task share specifically for the retained 541690 population.
rho: This is a five-year operating judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
rho: Safety, security, and clinical work may require more human review than economic or report-heavy advisory work.
e: No public dataset reports repeat-revenue share and normalized EBITDA jointly for NAICS 541690.
e: The injected firm-count estimate already supplies the size bridge and is not re-estimated here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, farm, public-sector, and nonprofit customers buy specialist advice through recurring retainers, periodic assessments, monitoring programs, compliance cycles, or repeat engagements; revenue may be fixed-fee, retainer, or time-and-materials.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because the code combines recurring technical advisory providers with episodic expert and project work; the screen retains only the recurring or repeat outsourced-service population specified by the default lens.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off expert opinions, litigation support, episodic project consulting without repeat service, motion-picture consulting, captive internal advisory units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent providers of recurring outsourced scientific and technical advisory services, including safety, security, energy-management, agricultural, biological, chemical, geological, and clinical advisory work where customers repeatedly retain the provider for monitoring, assessment, reporting, or specialized guidance.

a.bridge: S3 describes management-analyst work rather than NAICS 541690 employment or wages. I map its information gathering, analysis, documentation, records processing, and report-review tasks to the repeat advisory workflows retained in the lens, while excluding client accountability and domain judgment. The proxy likely overstates the code-wide wage-weighted opportunity because 541690 also contains safety, clinical, chemical, security, and scientific judgment work with less substitutable liability-bearing decisions.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed people, an important potential component of consulting firms.
No public source measured AI-exposed task share specifically for the retained 541690 population.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a NAICS 541690 payroll-weighted occupation extract from BLS OEWS, then interview operating leaders across safety, security, energy, agricultural, and clinical providers about task time, AI use, and avoided hiring by workflow.
a.rationale: Recurring providers can automate research synthesis, first-draft reports, knowledge retrieval, document review, data classification, and administrative coordination. Site observation, measurement, regulated sign-off, specialist interpretation, client trust, and recommendations under professional liability remain material. S3 supports the split between information-processing tasks and advisory judgment.

d5.bridge: S7 projects wage-and-salary employment for NAICS 541600, Management, scientific, and technical consulting services, from 1,864.7 thousand in 2024 to 2,039.3 thousand in 2034, a 9.4 percent ten-year increase. I convert that broader projection to an approximate five-year constant-quality demand ratio for the retained 541690 service basket. This proxy has upward-risk bias because faster-growing management and technology consulting are included in 541600; it can understate regulated safety and compliance niches.
caveats: Employment is not a direct measure of constant-price, constant-quality service quantity.
The BLS projection is broader than 541690 and spans ten years rather than the exact five-year horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Acquire recurring-revenue cohort data and end-market forecasts for safety, security, energy, agricultural, scientific, and clinical advisory providers, segmented by customer industry and contract type.
d5.rationale: The base applies the observed broader-industry projection over roughly half its horizon, yielding modest growth rather than assuming all technical advisory demand tracks the total consulting group. The range allows for cyclical client budgets and uneven end-market exposure.

caveats: No public dataset reports repeat-revenue share and normalized EBITDA jointly for NAICS 541690.
The injected firm-count estimate already supplies the size bridge and is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Purchase establishment-level revenue-model and size data from a business-information vendor, validate with broker teasers, and conduct owner interviews that distinguish retainer or repeat revenue from project revenue.
e.rationale: The code contains several activities compatible with repeat outsourced service, but the Census examples and questionnaire also show disparate, potentially episodic scientific, economic, security, and other consulting work. The estimate removes firms whose revenue is principally one-time expert or project work and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.

caveats: This is not a prediction of firm survival; it is the share of current service quantity still needing an accountable provider.
The retained subsegments have materially different regulatory and liability requirements.
o.change_evidence: Interview customers about which deliverables they would self-serve, inspect contract and insurance requirements for named professional review, and measure defection after deployment of vendor or self-service software.
o.rationale: Most retained demand should continue to require an accountable operator because clients buy domain interpretation, verification, sign-off, and advice, not only an information product. Software may eliminate lower-complexity research, reporting, and self-service questions. S1 defines the industry as advice and assistance on scientific and technical issues, and S2 identifies safety, security, clinical, and energy-management activities where human accountability is especially relevant.

caveats: No public source provides billing-model or realized pass-through shares for the frozen lens.
This estimate excludes volume loss, which belongs in demand durability.
q.change_evidence: Review a representative set of customer contracts, rate cards, renewal outcomes, utilization reports, and win-loss records by subsegment and billing model.
q.rationale: Specialist accountability, customer relationships, and domain know-how can support retention of part of a gross productivity benefit, especially under fixed-fee or retainer arrangements. Time-and-materials billing, procurement scrutiny, renewal repricing, and competing AI-enabled consultants can pass a substantial part to customers. S2 confirms that the code spans multiple service types, so billing terms cannot be treated as uniform.

caveats: This is a five-year operating judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Safety, security, and clinical work may require more human review than economic or report-heavy advisory work.
rho.change_evidence: Collect firm-level workflow adoption data, quality-control policies, client contract restrictions, and before-and-after staffing data from recurring technical-advisory operators.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be slower than technical availability: the retained work commonly needs validated source material, customer-specific context, accountable specialist review, and defensible records. The broad mix documented in S1 and S2 makes an implementation rate highly variable across subsegments.

caveats: Service-sector transactions in S6 include businesses outside this NAICS code and may be much smaller than the lens population.
The estimate excludes closures and reorganizations without a transferable operating business.
s5.change_evidence: Use Census ABS owner-age microdata or tabulations at the most detailed available industry level, M&A-advisor closed-deal records tagged 541690, and a sample of owner succession plans and broker listings.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying control transfer is plausible for owner-led specialist firms, but no source observed that probability for eligible NAICS 541690 firms. S6 observed service-sector transaction volume growth of 4 percent in 2025, establishing an active but not code-specific market; S4 identifies Census owner-age tables that could replace this judgment.

records.principal_driver: Repeat advisory workflows combine substantial information-processing work with specialist services that customers continue to buy from accountable providers.
records.principal_weakness: The six-digit code is heterogeneous, and public evidence does not isolate the recurring, transferable lower-middle-market provider population.

caveats: The recurring outsourced-service lens is a disclosed and coherent narrowing, but S1 and S2 do not measure how much of 541690 actually has recurring revenue; e therefore carries substantial model risk despite its honest ESTIMATE label.

The a bridge relies on management analysts rather than a payroll-weighted 541690 occupation mix, and S5 only establishes an OEWS coverage limitation. The wide 0.25-0.58 range and PROXY label are honest, but H remains weakly evidenced.

The s5 base is an explicit judgment: S4 identifies possible future owner-age evidence and S6 shows only broad marketplace activity, neither a five-year qualifying-transfer probability for the lens population.

The q and o values are reasoned estimates rather than source-observed retention or operator-required quantity shares; S2 establishes activity heterogeneity but not contract economics, liability requirements, or self-service behavior.

The d5 transformation is directionally plausible but maps a ten-year parent-industry wage-and-salary employment projection into five-year demand for a narrower service basket. The record discloses both population and construct mismatch.

The base PRIORITY tier is sensitive: A is 7.47 and the weakest-link H is 4.13, while the registered interval spans LOW_PRIORITY through HIGHEST_PRIORITY. The MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action appropriately prevent the base tier from being presented as robust.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, government, and developer customers purchase repeat regulatory, due-diligence, monitoring, and project advisory work through scoped engagements, retainers, and recurring compliance programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive environmental departments, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, physical remediation contractors, and environmental engineering establishments classified outside 541620.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly provide outsourced environmental compliance, permitting, monitoring, assessment, risk evaluation, and remediation-planning advice to external customers.

caveats: No published 541620 wage-weighted task inventory was located.
The environmental-scientist occupation is important but does not represent every employee in the industry.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 541620 occupational staffing file paired with a task-level time-and-motion study or a representative survey of environmental consulting firms about AI use and avoided hiring.
a.rationale: Environmental-scientist work includes compiling, analyzing, reporting, and reviewing environmental data and permits, which can be assisted by AI. Field sampling, accountable interpretation, compliance judgment, and client advice remain material, so the estimate excludes those tasks from the exposed share.

d5.bridge: S3 measures national employment for environmental scientists and specialists, not constant-price demand for NAICS 541620 services. Its 2024 to 2034 projection is transformed into a five-year service-demand anchor and adjusted with uncertainty for staffing mix, productivity, and project cycles; it can understate demand served by other occupations and overstate purchased consulting if clients internalize work.
caveats: Occupation growth is not equivalent to industry demand or real revenue.
Federal, state, and local policy and construction activity can move demand materially.

d5.rationale: BLS projects 4 percent employment growth for environmental scientists and specialists from 2024 to 2034 and says businesses are expected to continue consulting them on pollution, resource, and development issues. That supports modest five-year quantity growth, while the lower case allows for project cyclicality and insourcing.

caveats: Published census industry definitions do not report the share of lower-middle-market firms with repeat-service revenue.
NAICS establishment coding can obscure adjacent engineering and remediation activity.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue composition from Quality of Revenue reports, environmental-consulting customer-contract samples, and a targeted owner survey separating repeat compliance work from one-off projects.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on external advice and assistance, inspection, risk evaluation, and recommendations, consistent with the lens. The estimate excludes firms whose revenue is chiefly isolated project work, owner-dependent practices without repeat service capability, or activity that belongs in engineering or physical remediation.

o.rationale: The service remains tied to inspections, samples, analysis, compliance with formal requirements, risk evaluation, and accountable recommendations. caveats: No authoritative US 541620 billing-method mix or realized repricing series was located.
The value retained depends on contract language, specialization, and local competition.
q.change_evidence: A sample of executed environmental-consulting master service agreements and statements of work, renewal-price history, utilization records, and win-loss interviews with procurement buyers.
q.rationale: A consultant can retain some workflow productivity through fixed scopes, backlog conversion, and differentiated expertise, but customers can seek lower fees on repeatable documentation and data work. The midpoint reflects mixed scoped, retainer, and labor-based engagement economics without assuming that every hour saved becomes margin.

caveats: This is a judgment about adoption after controls, not a measure of current tool usage.
Implementation will differ substantially by specialty, client data access, and state regulation.
rho.change_evidence: Interviews with operations leaders at environmental consulting firms, a deployment log showing task adoption and quality-control results, and insurer or regulator guidance on AI-assisted environmental reports.
rho.rationale: Document drafting, information extraction, data organization, and first-pass analysis are practical workflow targets, but professional accuracy, regulations, field inputs, and client-facing accountability limit implementation of the exposed opportunity within the horizon.

caveats: The Census owner-age evidence is for US employer-business respondents broadly and is dated to reference year 2018.
The sector M&A source is qualitative and does not measure control-transfer probability for the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A NAICS 541620 owner-age and succession survey, lower-middle-market transaction records from GF Data or PitchBook, and brokered-sale close-rate data by firm size.
s5.rationale: Census reports that more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older in the cited employer-business survey, creating a succession tailwind. Sector commentary also reports acquisitions of both large and mid-sized environmental and sustainability consulting firms, including micro acquisitions. The estimate recognizes that many older owners will not complete a qualifying control sale in the horizon.

records.principal_driver: Repeat compliance and environmental-risk work retains an accountable-operator requirement while offering automatable data and documentation tasks.
records.principal_weakness: There is no directly observed lower-middle-market 541620 evidence for billing economics, qualifying transfers, or wage-weighted task exposure.

caveats: The numerical a, rho, e, s5, q, and o values remain bounded judgments rather than measurements; the MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action correctly restrict reliance on them.
S1 establishes industry scope but not repeat-service prevalence, contract structure, repricing, retention, or the estimated operator-required share.
S2 and S3 concern one occupation rather than a wage-weighted 541620 workforce; S3's ten-year employment projection is only a directional proxy for five-year constant-price industry demand.
S4 is broad 2018 owner-age evidence for responding employer-business owners, and S5 is qualitative AEC-sector commentary; neither measures lower-middle-market 541620 control transfers.
The LOW_PRIORITY-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY envelope is appropriately wide and indicates that direct operating, contract, transaction, and service-line demand evidence is needed before stage-two reliance.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business and organizational clients; revenue is commonly project, retainer, or time-and-materials advisory fees rather than a controlled product subscription.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off advisory engagements with no repeat service relationship, captive corporate or government units, holding-company shells, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US firms in NAICS 541611 that provide repeat or recurring external administrative-management, operating, strategic, organizational-planning, budgeting, records-management, or business-process-improvement advice.

caveats: O*NET measures the management-analyst occupation, not the six-digit industry or lower-middle-market firms.
The NAICS industry also contains owner-led specialists and work not represented by that occupation.
a.change_evidence: A 541611-specific time-and-motion study or anonymized timesheets segmented into research, analysis, drafting, client interaction, and partner review; client-approved AI workflow logs by role.
a.rationale: The closest occupational anchor is management analysts: O*NET describes analysis, research, organizational-process work, and decision-making. AI can reduce drafting, research, data preparation, and first-pass analysis, but client discovery, judgment, stakeholder alignment, and accountability remain material. The estimate treats only the currently unautomated, wage-weighted portion of that mix as exposed. S2 supports the task characterization.

d5.bridge: S3 projects US management-analyst employment from 1,075,100 in 2024 to 1,169,700 in 2034, an 8.8% ten-year increase. Applying roughly half of that projected change over five years gives a 1.04 demand proxy for this industry's constant-price, constant-quality service quantity. The occupation includes in-house analysts and excludes some industry roles, so it can overstate industry demand if clients internalize work and understate it if outsourced consulting gains share.
caveats: Employment is not constant-quality service quantity.
The BLS series is national and occupational, not a five-year forecast for NAICS 541611 LMM firms.
d5.change_evidence: BEA real output and price indexes for the relevant consulting product category, 541611 revenue panels deflated to constant prices, and client procurement spend by service type.
d5.rationale: The BLS projection supports modest growth rather than a collapsing service need. The narrowed five-year interpolation is deliberately lower than the full ten-year occupation change and allows a downside case where software or client self-service offsets growth.

caveats: No public Census table directly measures recurring revenue or transferability for this NAICS and EBITDA band.
This does not re-estimate the injected firm count.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level CRM or billing extracts showing repeat-client revenue, contract duration, owner billable-share, and client concentration for a representative 541611 LMM sample.
e.rationale: The industry definition is external operating advice and assistance to businesses and organizations, so it broadly matches the service side of the lens. Eligibility is below the whole LMM population because a substantial part of consulting is episodic, founder-personal, or dependent on a nontransferable individual relationship rather than a repeat outsourced service. S1 supports the industry scope.

caveats: This is a forward-looking judgment about accountable-operator demand, not an observed retention rate.
It is sensitive to whether the firm has proprietary methods, embedded workflows, and durable executive relationships.
o.change_evidence: Client interviews on whether AI tools replaced external advisers, recurring-service churn cohorts, procurement records, and controlled comparisons of self-service versus operator-led outcomes.
o.rationale: Even with AI tools, clients generally need an accountable party to frame problems, validate recommendations, manage stakeholder adoption, and own outcomes. The lower tail recognizes standardized research, templates, and simple planning work that clients may self-serve or obtain directly from software. S1 and S2 support the advisory and decision-oriented work characterization.

caveats: Public sources do not provide a 541611 LMM contractual pass-through rate.
The estimate excludes volume loss, which belongs in demand durability.
q.change_evidence: Executed master service agreements and statements of work showing fixed-fee versus time-based billing, rate-card changes, renewal pricing, margin bridges, and win-loss interviews for AI-enabled proposals.
q.rationale: Project, retainer, and time-based advisory fees create room to retain savings when scope and client value are differentiated, but transparent labor-intensive pricing, competitive bidding, renewal repricing, and client expectations of AI-enabled productivity should share a large portion of the gain. The base assumes less than half of implemented gross benefit remains with the operator.

caveats: This is a workflow-adoption judgment, not a measured implementation rate.
It excludes pricing, demand, and competition effects by construction.
rho.change_evidence: A sample of 541611 firms' AI policies, client data-processing clauses, adoption budgets, utilization reports, and before/after staffing plans for recurring engagements.
rho.rationale: Implementation should lag technical exposure because consulting deliverables require verification, client-specific context, confidentiality controls, and senior review. Reusable internal knowledge, standardized analyses, and controlled drafting can be operationalized, while bespoke diagnosis and executive facilitation are harder to change within five years. S2 supports the judgment-heavy nature of the anchor work.

caveats: The cited owner-age statistic covers small businesses generally, not eligible LMM consulting firms.
Qualifying control transfers exclude closures and internal reorganizations, neither of which is measured by the benchmark.
s5.change_evidence: A closed-transaction database filtered to US 541611 businesses at the stated EBITDA band, plus broker CIMs, seller-age and succession-plan survey data, and post-close client-retention outcomes.
s5.rationale: Five-year qualifying transfers should occur for only a minority of eligible firms. A Census-reported all-small-business benchmark cited by AP says 51% of owners are older than 55, indicating a meaningful succession cohort, but age does not establish sale probability and many advisory firms will wind down or remain owner-dependent. The range therefore remains conservative. S4 supports the owner-age benchmark.

records.principal_driver: AI can reduce labor in repeatable research, analysis, and deliverable workflows while an accountable advisory operator remains valuable.
records.principal_weakness: Many firms sell bespoke, relationship-led projects whose revenue and client trust may not transfer cleanly after a control change.

caveats: The source-backed portions of a, rho, and o establish the Management Analysts task mix, not the numeric primitive ranges or a representative 541611 lower-middle-market population; the ESTIMATE labels and packet caveats make this honest but highly model-conditioned.

The d5 range is comparatively tight for a bridge from ten-year occupational employment to five-year, constant-quality industry service quantity. The occupation/industry and employment/quantity mismatches could widen the sensitivity interval, but correcting the range alone would not change the registered base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

S4 is a secondary report of a broad small-business owner-age statistic and does not identify its underlying Census table or vintage. It supports succession context rather than the s5 value, which remains appropriately labeled ESTIMATE; downgrading that contextual support would not change the base tier or action.

The lens focuses on repeat or recurring work and excludes one-off engagements while recording no deviation from default. The packet transparently reflects episodic and founder-dependent firms in e and its narrative, so this is not material lens shopping, but readers should not interpret the result as applying uniformly to all 541611 establishments.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers; revenue is principally project, retainer, recurring assessment, or repeat advisory fees rather than freight, warehouse, or software-license revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal advisory units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, one-off engagements without a repeat-service relationship, engineering design, computer-systems integration, freight arrangement, and warehousing operations.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 541614 that provide repeat outsourced operating, process-improvement, inventory, distribution, warehouse-utilization, transportation, or materials-management advice to external customers.

a.bridge: S2 measures tasks for the broad Management Analysts occupation, not employment or wage weights within NAICS 541614. Its data gathering, analysis, document preparation, report review, and procedure-design tasks are mapped to the desk-based component of this industry's consulting work; the estimate excludes client observation, judgment, relationship management, and implementation leadership. This likely overstates exposure for field-intensive engagements and understates it for standardized analytics-heavy firms.
caveats: No published wage-weighted task mix for NAICS 541614 was located.
Management Analysts includes consulting work outside process and logistics consulting.
a.change_evidence: A 541614-specific OEWS occupation matrix with wages, together with time-and-billing exports from a representative set of LMM firms that separate analysis, documentation, observation, sales, and implementation hours.
a.rationale: The industry's defined activities are advisory and analytical. O*NET reports core tasks of gathering information, analyzing data, documenting findings, and preparing recommendations, which AI can assist or substitute in part; it also reports interviewing, onsite observation, and implementation work that remain labor-intensive. The range reflects the unknown wage mix between analyst production and senior client-facing work. [S1][S2]

d5.bridge: S4 measures US employment growth for Management Analysts across all industries over 2024-2034, and S5 does the same for Logisticians; neither measures constant-price demand for NAICS 541614 services. The five-year range interpolates these ten-year occupational projections to the industry's advisory service basket, with the lower end anchored nearer general consulting and the upper end nearer logistics. This may overstate service demand if clients internalize work or software replaces projects, and understate it if supply-chain complexity drives outside advisory use.
caveats: Occupational employment growth is not industry receipts or outsourced-service quantity.
The source projections cover a ten-year period and all employing industries, requiring a five-year and industry bridge.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541614 industry receipts or product-line revenue over several annual releases, paired with a buyer survey measuring outsourced process and logistics consulting spend versus internal and software alternatives.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 2024-2034 employment growth of 9% for management analysts, citing demand for consulting efficiency work, and 17% for logisticians, citing e-commerce, inventory, multiple supply chains, and delivery timeliness. These are favorable but indirect signals for a modest increase in constant-quality external process and logistics advisory demand over five years. [S4][S5]

caveats: The injected LMM count is not re-estimated here.
NAICS assignment does not reveal recurrence, ownership structure, or the share of revenue in an included activity.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level commercial database screen of 541614 LMM companies, corroborated by websites and interviews, classifying repeat external revenue, service lines, and excluded activities.
e.rationale: Census defines the industry as operating advice to external businesses across process and logistics topics, so much of the code is compatible with the service lens. Eligibility is below the whole code because some establishments are chiefly episodic project advisers, very small owner practices, or economically tied to excluded engineering, systems-integration, freight-arrangement, or warehouse activities. [S1]

caveats: This is a judgment about accountable operator need, not a forecast of employment.
The service basket may shift toward software-enabled delivery even when a human operator remains required.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win/loss data that distinguish self-service software substitution from external consulting purchases, plus evidence of end-to-end autonomous implementation in client operations.
o.rationale: Even after AI and software absorb portions of analysis and reporting, clients still need accountable operators to diagnose operations, validate data, align stakeholders, and implement changes. The lower bound recognizes that standardized diagnostics and planning may become self-served; the upper bound reflects the industry's onsite, client-facing, decision-consequential work. [S1][S2]

caveats: The BLS occupation source is not a pricing survey for NAICS 541614.
Customer concentration and whether the firm sells outcome-based work can materially change retention.
q.change_evidence: Executed master-service agreements and statements of work from LMM firms, with billing basis, renewal pricing, utilization, and realized gross-margin changes after AI workflow deployment.
q.rationale: Project and hourly advisory fees can allow an operator to retain some internal productivity gains, especially where scope is fixed, but repeat customers can demand lower fees or more output at renewal. BLS notes that contracted management analysts bid proposals and that self-employed analysts may be paid by the hour or project, supporting mixed rather than purely cost-plus value capture. [S4]

caveats: This is a five-year judgment, not a measured adoption forecast.
Client security rules and availability of structured operational data can move implementation substantially.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after utilization, headcount, realization-rate, and quality-control data from LMM operations-consulting firms that have deployed AI workflows for at least twelve months.
rho.rationale: AI assistance can be deployed in research, analysis, drafts, and reporting, but full operational realization needs trusted client data, validation, onsite context, and adoption of the recommended process. O*NET shows frequent interpersonal contact and material responsibility for outcomes, supporting a materially lower implemented share than technical task exposure alone. [S2]

caveats: No NAICS 541614 five-year control-transfer rate was located.
The cited succession evidence covers private employer businesses generally and is not a transaction-rate measure.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed lower-middle-market transaction records tagged to 541614 from a transaction database, plus owner-age and succession-plan survey results for the same NAICS and revenue band.
s5.rationale: The range allows for a meaningful retirement and succession pipeline but excludes closures, internal reorganizations, and firms without transferable operations. Project Equity reports that over half of private employer businesses have owners older than 55 and that many lack succession plans or struggle to find buyers; that broad employer-business evidence supports succession pressure but does not measure qualifying transfers in this NAICS code. [S3]

records.principal_driver: A large share of advisory production consists of analysis and documentation that can be redesigned with AI while clients still need operational accountability.
records.principal_weakness: No direct NAICS-specific evidence was located for wage-weighted AI exposure, qualifying transfer rates, retained savings, or constant-price outsourced demand.

caveats: The base case is highly estimate-dependent: direct NAICS-specific evidence is absent for wage-weighted AI exposure, implementation realization, eligible-firm share, five-year transfers, retained savings, and operator-required demand.

The H factor is especially sensitive to the broad Management Analysts proxy and the judgmental implementation rate; its registered range runs from 1.88 to 8.29 and helps drive the overall LOW_PRIORITY-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY tier interval.
The s5 value uses broad, promotional succession context rather than a measured NAICS 541614 control-transfer rate; this is honestly labeled ESTIMATE and does not create an unsupported observed claim.

The d5 range converts national occupational employment projections into five-year outsourced-service demand. The bridge is plausible and discloses both error directions, but direct constant-price industry receipts or buyer-spend evidence would be materially stronger.

The repeat-service lens is clearly disclosed, but Census industry classification does not establish recurrence, ownership suitability, or service-line purity at the firm level; the resulting eligibility range remains judgmental.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, task-order, hourly, unit, and occasionally retainer revenue from developers, contractors, utilities, governments, and property owners; repeat work is retained only where a firm continues to be outsourced.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive owner, contractor, utility, or government units; non-control financing vehicles; geophysical surveying; software-only mapping vendors; and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Independent US firms providing repeat or recurring land, boundary, construction, topographic, aerial, GIS, photogrammetric, or mapping services to external customers in the lower-middle-market band.

caveats: The available industry occupation mix is NAICS 541300, not 541370.
No source measures current task-level AI substitution for the frozen lens.
The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio may be distorted because owner labor and subcontractors are not consistently represented in payroll.
a.change_evidence: A 541370 six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage extract plus time-and-motion studies from land-survey firms separating field capture, office processing, licensed review, and administration.
a.rationale: S1 shows the broader 541300 industry has meaningful surveyor, cartographer, and surveying-technician employment, while S2 describes record search, measurement, verification, drafting, reporting, GIS, CAD, and client presentation. AI can reduce office interpretation, coding, drafting, QA triage, and administrative work, but field capture, boundary judgment, and legal review remain substantial. The range is a wage-weighted judgment, not a measured automation share.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US employment of the surveyor occupation across employers, not constant-price demand for the 541370 lens. Its 4 percent 2024-34 projection is converted to an approximate 2 percent five-year change and widened for cyclicality and for occupation demand differing from service quantity. Because productivity may allow employment to understate output, the proxy can understate demand; construction exposure can also make it overstate a recession-period service basket.
caveats: The source is an occupation projection, not six-digit industry output or quantity demand.
The source's horizon is ten years and includes employment in other industries.
d5.change_evidence: BEA/Census service-output or receipts series at 541370, bid-volume data from public agencies, and a customer-segment revenue panel deflated for service price and quality changes.
d5.rationale: S2 projects surveyor employment from 56,100 in 2024 to 58,600 in 2034 and identifies continuing boundary certification, construction-site review, road repair, and mining needs, while noting drones and other technology can limit employment growth. That supports roughly stable to modestly growing service demand over five years, with a broad interval for construction and real-estate cyclicality.

caveats: No public source measures lens eligibility among the injected LMM count.
Six-digit classification may not isolate mixed engineering-and-survey practices cleanly.
e.change_evidence: Business-register microdata or a D&B/Grata/SourceScrub list reviewed for receipts, ownership, customer type, and service mix; supplemented by firm interviews.
e.rationale: The NAICS title and S2's descriptions point to externally supplied surveying and mapping work, but the injected LMM count can include firms with nonrepeat projects, mixed engineering businesses, captive-like arrangements, and service lines outside the frozen lens. The base allows for those exclusions while recognizing that the classification is primarily a service industry.
