Noncertified aerial, GIS, and visualization work may face faster software substitution than boundary surveys.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state scope-of-practice review, customer interviews on willingness to accept unlicensed digital deliverables, and project-level data separating legal surveys from noncertified mapping.
o.rationale: S2 says surveyors establish boundaries for legal documents, verify measurements, and work in the field; S3 says public surveying services and certification of legal documents require licensure in every state and the District of Columbia. Automation can shrink labor per job and enable self-service for lower-risk mapping, but a large share of the service basket should still need an accountable operator for legal, accuracy, and site-specific work.

caveats: No public industry source reports contract-form shares or AI-related repricing.
Retention will vary sharply between regulated boundary work, construction support, government work, and commodity mapping.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized sample of 541370 proposals, MSAs, task orders, utilization reports, and win-loss data showing billing basis, price resets, and margins before and after automation.
q.rationale: The lens uses project, hourly, task-order, and unit billing. Office efficiency can be retained during a fixed-price engagement, but competitive bids, hourly billing, public procurement, renewals, and customer expectations of lower turnaround costs should share a meaningful portion of benefits. The base assumes a mixed book rather than full pass-through or full retention.

caveats: State licensing and client acceptance differ materially.
This excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects by construction.
rho.change_evidence: A representative 541370 software-use survey, implementation case studies with before-and-after labor hours, and state-board guidance on AI-assisted deliverables and sign-off.
rho.rationale: S2 documents work that can be digitized, but also field measurement, accuracy verification, legal records, and client-facing responsibility. S3 says licensing is required in all states and the District of Columbia to provide public surveying services and certify legal documents. Those controls make workflow implementation materially slower and narrower than technical capability alone.

caveats: Occupation exits are not business-owner retirements.
Press releases identify completed transactions but do not provide a denominator or represent the LMM population.
Deaths, closures, internal reorganizations, and minority investments are excluded from the construct but not measured here.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched transaction census from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and state licensure/firm-registration records, with deal type and revenue size verified.
s5.rationale: S2 reports 3,900 average annual surveyor openings and attributes many to workers leaving the labor force, including retirement; S4 gives examples of recent acquisitions of surveying practices. These facts support a nonzero succession and transaction pipeline, but neither measures qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM firms, so the five-year probability is a bounded judgment.

records.principal_driver: Licensed, accountable boundary and site work remains necessary even as office processing becomes more automated.
records.principal_weakness: There is no direct public measurement of six-digit task exposure, transferable-firm incidence, contract pass-through, or constant-quality service demand.

caveats: The rho and o rationales misattribute the all-jurisdictions public-surveying licensure statement to S3. S3 instead says cartographer and photogrammetrist licensing varies; S2 supports the exact surveyor-licensure claim, so correcting the citation does not change either primitive, any factor, the base tier, or the action category.

The record labels the lens non-heterogeneous even though it combines regulated boundary and construction surveying with aerial, GIS, photogrammetric, and other mapping work that the packet itself says may differ sharply in licensure, substitution risk, and value retention. The segmentation weakness is disclosed and the affected inputs are already broad ESTIMATE ranges, so the audit does not establish a correction that changes the base tier or action.

S2 does not measure contract-form shares or AI-related value retention for q, and S4-S5 are examples rather than a denominator for the s5 transfer probability. The packet expressly discloses both gaps and classifies q and s5 as ESTIMATE; these are reasons to validate assumptions, not material defects under the review rule.

caveats: a: The source does not isolate NAICS 541110, U.S. firms, or LMM firms.
a: Time saved is not identical to task exposure and may be redeployed into higher-value legal work.
rho: The respondent base is global and not limited to the screened firm band.
rho: Reported benefits and intentions do not establish realized labor substitution.
e: No national NAICS-level dataset reports recurrence of legal engagements or eligibility for a control transfer.
e: Professional-ownership rules may make otherwise attractive practices ineligible for a given buyer structure.
s5: The principal rate is cross-industry and includes firms outside the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Rule 5.4 is a model rule; state adoption and permitted buyer structures vary.
q: There is no source measuring five-year retained gross AI benefit for the specified LMM lens.
q: Contingency, hourly, fixed-fee, and subscription practices have materially different pass-through behavior.
d5: Employment is an imperfect proxy for service quantity and is affected by productivity and staffing mix.
d5: The forecast is national and does not separate LMM firms or repeat-service practices.
o: No national measure observes the five-year residual operator requirement for this lens.
o: State rules and court procedures can materially change the result by jurisdiction and matter type.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External individual and business clients; revenue is commonly matter-based, hourly, retainer, contingency, subscription, or fixed-fee and is often relationship- and attorney-dependent.
lens.excluded_activities: In-house legal departments, government and nonprofit legal offices, firms without repeat or recurring external-client work, shells, captive units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent and multi-attorney U.S. law practices in the LMM EBITDA band that deliver repeat or recurring legal representation, advisory, transaction, compliance, litigation, estate-planning, or related outsourced legal work to external clients.

a.bridge: S2 measures globally surveyed legal, tax, and risk/compliance professionals' predicted AI time savings, not wage-weighted task exposure in U.S. LMM law firms. I use its 12 hours per week over a 40-hour week as an upper contextual benchmark, then reduce it for legal judgment, advocacy, client counseling, and verification identified in S1; this likely overstates exposure for bespoke litigation and understates it for standardized document, research, and intake work.
caveats: The source does not isolate NAICS 541110, U.S. firms, or LMM firms.
Time saved is not identical to task exposure and may be redeployed into higher-value legal work.
a.change_evidence: A 541110 occupational staffing-and-pay file paired with matter-level time entries, tagged by task type and AI-assisted completion, from a legal-practice-management vendor or a representative law-firm panel.
a.rationale: Research, analysis, interpretation, document preparation, and file work create substantial AI-addressable time, but representation, persuasion, advice, supervision, and fact-specific accountability remain material. The central estimate treats a minority, rather than most, of wage-weighted work as exposed to substitution or avoidable hiring.

d5.bridge: S1 projects U.S. lawyer employment across all employing sectors over ten years, not constant-price, constant-quality external legal-service demand for NAICS 541110 over five years. I convert the stated 4% ten-year occupational projection to an approximate five-year demand anchor and widen it downward for automation and price competition expressly noted by BLS; the broad occupation measure can overstate outsourced law-firm demand because it includes government and in-house roles.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect proxy for service quantity and is affected by productivity and staffing mix.
The forecast is national and does not separate LMM firms or repeat-service practices.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar NAICS 541110 output forecast separated by practice area and client type, plus matter-volume and legal-spend data from corporate clients and consumer legal-service panels.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued legal-work demand while also warning that routine work may be automated or outsourced and price competition may alter law-firm staffing. This supports a roughly stable current service basket, with modest growth possible rather than a strong volume-growth assumption.

caveats: No national NAICS-level dataset reports recurrence of legal engagements or eligibility for a control transfer.
Professional-ownership rules may make otherwise attractive practices ineligible for a given buyer structure.
e.change_evidence: A 541110 firm-level database with revenue by practice area, client recurrence, ownership structure, and EBITDA, followed by a review of state ownership and sale restrictions for the sampled firms.
e.rationale: Most firms in this industry sell external legal services, but the fixed lens removes practices whose work is principally one-off and relationship-bound without repeat or recurring demand, as well as structures unsuitable for a qualifying control acquisition. The range reflects wide variation by practice area and client concentration.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541110 ($150,269,970,536) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541110 ($356,242,120,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $330,420 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541110); class <5: 116,668 firms, avg revenue/firm $419,995 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,729 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 22,518 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,416,234 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $221,641 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 10,001 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,187,546 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $498,851 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 6,245 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,168,102 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,591,308 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 948 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,651,846 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,179,014 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 203 firms, avg revenue/firm $612,665,074 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,882,084 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 7,193

caveats: No national measure observes the five-year residual operator requirement for this lens.
State rules and court procedures can materially change the result by jurisdiction and matter type.
o.change_evidence: Matter-level substitution studies by practice area that measure customer self-service, unauthorized-practice constraints, attorney review requirements, and the share of completed work delivered without an outside law firm.
o.rationale: Most residual demand should still need a licensed, accountable provider for tailored advice, representation, advocacy, supervision, and client trust, while standardized research, drafting, intake, and some low-complexity matters can migrate toward software or self-service. The estimate preserves a meaningful elimination risk for routine work.

caveats: There is no source measuring five-year retained gross AI benefit for the specified LMM lens.
Contingency, hourly, fixed-fee, and subscription practices have materially different pass-through behavior.
q.change_evidence: Pre- and post-AI engagement data from acquired law firms showing pricing model, hours billed per matter, realization, collection, matter volume, client renewal, and margin by practice area.
q.rationale: The central estimate assumes an operator keeps some implemented productivity through capacity, service quality, and fixed-fee or retainer matters, but hourly billing, invoice scrutiny, rate pressure, and competitive repricing share much of the benefit with clients. Clio's billable-work funnel illustrates the prominence of time capture and realization in law-firm economics.

rho.bridge: S3 measures law-firm respondents' views and reported AI benefits rather than the share of exposed tasks operationally implemented at U.S. LMM firms. I translate reported current benefits, stated transformation expectations, and limited visible strategy into a five-year implemented share below anticipated organization-wide adoption; survey optimism and larger-firm representation likely bias the result upward for smaller practices.
caveats: The respondent base is global and not limited to the screened firm band.
Reported benefits and intentions do not establish realized labor substitution.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of U.S. LMM law firms reporting approved-tool deployment, task-level production use, governance controls, and realized headcount or throughput changes by practice area.
rho.rationale: AI use is already producing a benefit for many surveyed law firms, and the expected five-year impact is high, but only 22% report a visible AI strategy. Confidentiality, supervision, accuracy, workflow redesign, practice-area variation, and buyer integration make full task implementation implausible within five years.

s5.bridge: S4 measures five-year sale or transfer plans for all U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM law firms. I use the 22% employer-business plan rate as a starting point and discount it for founder-attorney dependence, client portability, and legal ownership constraints; it may understate transfer pressure where partner retirement drives consolidation.
caveats: The principal rate is cross-industry and includes firms outside the LMM EBITDA band.
Rule 5.4 is a model rule; state adoption and permitted buyer structures vary.
s5.change_evidence: State-bar practice-sale notices, law-firm transaction databases, and an LMM broker dataset with announced and closed legal-practice transfers, seller age, and post-close client retention.
s5.rationale: A meaningful portion of employer-business owners report a sale or transfer plan within five years, and law-firm merger activity is observable. Plans are not completed control transactions, and a law practice may close, merge without control transfer, or lose clients when an owner exits.

records.principal_driver: A large share of legal-service workflows involves research, drafting, documents, and process work that can be augmented with AI while demand for accountable legal advice persists.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly billing, client repricing, professional-ownership constraints, and portable founder relationships make retained value and qualifying control transfers uncertain.

caveats: The a bridge calls S2's 12/40 = 0.30 predicted time-saving share an upper contextual benchmark and says it is reduced, yet the registered a base is 0.38 and high is 0.52. S1 supplies qualitative task content but no numerical basis for exceeding that benchmark. This incoherence is not material under the required test: setting a base to 0.30 would lower H from 3.42 to about 2.70 and A from 6.89 to about 6.71, while the base tier remains CONDITIONAL and the action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The lens says deviation_from_default is none while explicitly excluding firms without repeat or recurring external-client work. The favorable narrowing is disclosed rather than hidden, but its effect on a, q, and o is not quantified and the industry is marked non-heterogeneous despite substantial practice-area and billing-model variation.

The s5 base of 0.20 is only modestly below Gallup's cross-industry 0.22 planned-transfer rate even though plans are not completions and law firms face client-portability and ownership constraints. The bridge and caveats are honest, and F is already capped with 7,193 estimated firms, so the defect is not shown to change the base tier or action.

The d5 base is transparently anchored to an approximate five-year conversion of BLS's ten-year occupational projection, but the 0.95-1.08 range is judgmental and the upper bound exceeds the converted growth anchor without independent quantitative support. The proxy label and caveats prevent this from being presented as observed NAICS demand.

q, e, and o remain broad, practice-mix-sensitive judgments without direct measurements. They are correctly labeled ESTIMATE, make readiness MODEL_CONDITIONED, and support the restricted VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action rather than an advancement recommendation.

caveats: a: Management Analysts includes employees outside consulting and excludes some occupations used by consulting firms.
a: The source describes task importance, not measured AI substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: No industry-specific evidence measures implemented AI labor reduction for the frozen lens.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customer revenue from recurring retainers, recurring advisory engagements, repeat projects, or managed consulting relationships; the target firm is in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off project-only engagements, captive corporate or government units, internal cost centers, software-only vendors, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent firms that repeatedly provide outsourced specialist management advice, analysis, implementation support, or ongoing advisory services to external customers.

a.bridge: S2 measures management-analyst tasks across occupations and employers rather than wages within NAICS 541618 firms. I map information gathering, data analysis, research-report drafting, document editing, and procedure documentation to AI-exposed work, while retaining interviewing, onsite observation, stakeholder alignment, and accountable advice as less substitutable; this likely overstates exposure for relationship-intensive specialists and understates it for document-heavy teams.
caveats: Management Analysts includes employees outside consulting and excludes some occupations used by consulting firms.
The source describes task importance, not measured AI substitution or avoided hiring.
a.change_evidence: A 541618 payroll-by-SOC extract from BLS OEWS staffing data or an industry payroll file, paired with a task-level time-and-motion survey of retained consulting teams, would materially change this value.
a.rationale: The closest occupational proxy assigns high importance to gathering information, analyzing data, preparing recommendations, and producing materials, which generative AI and workflow tools can accelerate. Core client interaction, contextual diagnosis, and implementation ownership constrain the wage-weighted opportunity.

d5.bridge: S3 projects U.S. Management Analyst employment to grow 9 percent during 2024 to 2034, rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the frozen NAICS 541618 service basket. I use a five-year portion of that outlook as a directional demand proxy, widening it for consulting specialties and for productivity-driven divergence between employment and service quantity; employment growth can overstate service demand when productivity rises.
caveats: Occupational employment is not an industry output series.
The forecast spans all management analysts and a ten-year horizon, while this primitive concerns the frozen service lens over five years.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541618 industry receipts, client procurement-spend panels, and recurring-contract renewal volumes segmented by specialty would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: BLS attributes management-analyst growth to organizations seeking efficiency and cost control and specifically expects strength in smaller specialist consulting companies. The proxy supports modest demand resilience, not a direct industry revenue forecast.

caveats: The official NAICS definition identifies the industry boundary but does not measure recurring revenue or transferability.
The eligible proportion can vary by specialty and customer concentration.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 541618 revenue-recurringness survey, quality-of-earnings reports, and a broker dataset tagged by contract type would materially change this value.
e.rationale: The classification excludes several separately coded consulting fields, yet it remains broad and contains telecommunications and utilities consulting as well as other specialist work. Many engagements are discrete projects; the eligible subset is limited to firms with repeat outsourced relationships and transferable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541618 ($14,897,754,779) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541618 ($11,352,826,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required portion of NAICS 541618 demand.
Utilities and telecommunications specialists may have different accountability requirements from other included consultants.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on willingness to self-serve, renewal cohorts after AI-tool deployment, and specialty-specific software substitution data would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Software can self-serve portions of analysis, reporting, and workflow design, but retained specialist consulting continues to require accountable diagnosis, client communication, contextual judgment, and implementation support. The durable share is below one because repeatable advisory outputs may be productized.

caveats: BLS describes contracting and proposals, not operator benefit retention.
Retention differs by billing basis, client bargaining power, and whether efficiency visibly changes delivered output.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts showing rate cards, renewal clauses, and cost-sharing terms, plus win-loss and renewal pricing data from comparable firms, would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Contract and project pricing can allow an operator to retain some delivery-efficiency benefit before renewal, but sophisticated customers procure competitive proposals and can demand lower fees or expanded scope. Recurring relationships support retention more than hourly pass-through arrangements.

caveats: No industry-specific evidence measures implemented AI labor reduction for the frozen lens.
Implementation differs materially by specialty, client data access, and whether delivery is onsite.
rho.change_evidence: Pre- and post-adoption staffing data from comparable specialist consultancies, client security requirements, and interviews with delivery leaders about workflow deployment would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Adoption can be practical for research, analysis, proposal, documentation, and internal knowledge workflows, but client-specific data, quality review, confidentiality, domain validation, and change management limit conversion of exposure into operating implementation within five years.

caveats: S4 studies self-employed people over age 50 rather than transfers in this industry and uses older survey data.
The estimate excludes shutdowns and transactions without transferable operations by definition.
s5.change_evidence: Closed lower-middle-market transaction data for 541618 firms, owner-age data, and broker records distinguishing control sales from internal reorganizations would materially change this value.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment for eligible lower-middle-market firms. Older self-employed owners provide a succession motive, but client concentration, founder-led delivery, and intangible client relationships can prevent a transferable transaction.

records.principal_driver: AI can reduce research, analysis, proposal, and documentation effort while retained clients still value accountable specialist judgment.
records.principal_weakness: A broad, often project-based industry definition makes recurring revenue and transferable-firm availability uncertain.

caveats: The score is model-conditioned: rho, e, s5, q, and o are bounded judgments, while S2 and S3 provide occupational or contextual evidence rather than direct measurements of their numeric values.
S4 is old, cross-industry self-employment evidence about retirement expectations, not transaction evidence; it gives only weak directional context for s5.

The lens and narrative acknowledge materially different specialties and business models within 541618, yet lens.heterogeneous is false. The narrowing to repeat outsourced relationships is explicit and e is already an ESTIMATE, so this metadata tension is a caveat rather than a demonstrated material defect.

The fixed compensation-to-receipts anchor is anomalous and combines mismatched vintages and populations. The packet discloses this and does not interpret H as margin or forecasted savings, but the scenario interval holds that anchor fixed.

caveats: a: No published six-digit occupational mix was located.
a: Exposure is not realized labor removal and excludes work already automated.
a: The source includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside the target.
rho: This is a five-year judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
rho: Adoption differs sharply between computational engineering and wet-lab or secure government programs.
e: No public dataset classifies 541715 firms by repeat outsourced service and normalized EBITDA.
e: NAICS establishment classification does not identify captive status or customer concentration.
s5: Owner intent is not a transaction close rate.
s5: Public transaction marketplaces underrepresent clearance-dependent and highly technical R&D firms.
q: The FAR governs federal procurement, not the whole industry.
q: No public revenue-weighted contract-type mix exists for the frozen lens.
d5: Employment growth is not a direct constant-price demand measure.
d5: Business R&D figures include internal performers and companies outside the target code.
o: No source measures replacement of 541715 services by software-only offerings.
o: The estimate varies by discipline, with computational work more substitutable than laboratory and field work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External commercial or government customers buy project, task-order, retainer, or repeat-program research and development services; work is commonly specialist labor- and facility-intensive.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate, university, government, and nonprofit research units; biotechnology and nanotechnology R&D; prototype manufacturing; one-off firms without repeat outsourced customer work; and financing shells.
lens.included_activities: Independent physical, engineering, and non-biotechnology life-science R&D firms that sell recurring or repeat research, development, testing, engineering, or laboratory programs to external customers and can fall in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: S2 measures the 2023 occupation and wage mix for NAICS 541700, which includes 541715 plus biotechnology and social-science R&D. I apply that mix to 541715 and count documentation, coding, analysis, literature synthesis, scheduling, proposal, and administrative work as AI-exposed while excluding much experimental design, physical experimentation, safety, and accountable technical judgment. The broad source likely overstates exposure for the target because its biotechnology and computer-intensive components may have more digital task content than the excluded target activities.
caveats: No published six-digit occupational mix was located.
Exposure is not realized labor removal and excludes work already automated.
The source includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside the target.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541715 payroll-by-SOC extract from Lightcast or BLS microdata, paired with task interviews across engineering laboratories, contract research firms, and government R&D contractors, would permit a wage-weighted task decomposition.
a.rationale: The proxy industry has 922280 workers; life, physical, and social science occupations are 23.16 percent, computer and mathematical 14.29 percent, management 20.28 percent, and business and financial operations 12.14 percent. High-wage scientific and engineering work has material AI assistance potential, but the core experimental and accountable portions are not substitution-ready.

d5.bridge: S8 projects employment in the broader Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services sector to rise 7.5 percent from 2024 to 2034. I use approximately half of that decade trend as a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand proxy for the current 541715 service basket; it could understate R&D demand if research budgets outgrow the sector or overstate it if R&D is displaced by internal teams or software.
caveats: Employment growth is not a direct constant-price demand measure.
Business R&D figures include internal performers and companies outside the target code.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar revenue and contract-award series for 541715 from the Census Annual Services Survey, USAspending, and commercial customer procurement panels would replace the sector proxy.
d5.rationale: The central case is modest real demand growth rather than a step change. Business R&D remains large and 5417 has a 21.5 percent R&D-to-sales ratio among R&D-performing companies, but that evidence does not isolate the target code or outsourced demand.

caveats: No public dataset classifies 541715 firms by repeat outsourced service and normalized EBITDA.
NAICS establishment classification does not identify captive status or customer concentration.
e.change_evidence: Dun & Bradstreet or PitchBook firm-level NAICS 541715 records with customer concentration, recurring-revenue, employee, ownership, and EBITDA fields would allow the lens filters to be counted directly.
e.rationale: The code covers many external R&D laboratories and services, but it also contains captive-like and project-specific activity that does not meet the repeat-outsourced-service lens. The Census definition spans aerospace, agriculture, electronics, engineering, environmental, medical, and other laboratories, so no single observed eligibility share is defensible.

caveats: No source measures replacement of 541715 services by software-only offerings.
The estimate varies by discipline, with computational work more substitutable than laboratory and field work.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win-loss data separating purchases of operator-led R&D from software-only simulation, analysis, and self-service tools would test the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand should still require a responsible technical operator to formulate questions, run or oversee experiments, validate findings, manage facilities, and bear contractual or regulatory accountability. AI can remove some self-service analysis and lower-complexity research support, so the estimate is below one.

caveats: The FAR governs federal procurement, not the whole industry.
No public revenue-weighted contract-type mix exists for the frozen lens.
q.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541715 commercial MSAs and federal contract records, coding fixed-price, cost-reimbursement, time-and-materials, renewal, and gain-sharing terms, would measure retention directly.
q.rationale: Custom research can retain part of a workflow productivity gain under fixed-price, milestone, or capacity-constrained engagements, but R&D's labor-heavy, custom nature limits durable capture. Federal guidance says cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate when R&D specifications and cost estimation are uncertain, which passes labor savings through to a customer on that material channel.

caveats: This is a five-year judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
Adoption differs sharply between computational engineering and wet-lab or secure government programs.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of 541715 firms measuring production deployments, FTE avoided, validation requirements, and restricted-data usage, plus vendor implementation case studies, would materially change this range.
rho.rationale: A five-year rollout can implement AI in software, documentation, search, data handling, and proposal workflows, but validation, IP and export-control restrictions, regulated quality systems, experimental reproducibility, and customer acceptance constrain deployment. Observed Claude use is still substantially augmentative, supporting a below-majority implementation estimate.

s5.bridge: S5 measures US small-business owners' stated plans in the next five years, not completed qualifying control transfers and not 541715 firms. I convert the broad intent signal into a lower completed-transfer probability for eligible lower-middle-market R&D firms; stated plans likely overstate completed transferable sales because technical founder dependence, security clearances, customer consents, and shutdowns prevent some exits.
caveats: Owner intent is not a transaction close rate.
Public transaction marketplaces underrepresent clearance-dependent and highly technical R&D firms.
s5.change_evidence: GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and business-broker closing records filtered to 541715 and the specified EBITDA band, together with Census owner-age microdata, would directly measure this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that owners aged 55 or older were more likely to plan sale or transfer in five years than younger owners, 17 percent versus 10 percent. A qualifying external control transfer should be less frequent than that planning rate, yielding an 8 percent central estimate.

records.principal_driver: High-skill knowledge workflows create a meaningful but bounded AI implementation opportunity alongside continuing need for accountable technical delivery.
records.principal_weakness: Six-digit evidence on outsourced recurring revenue, LMM transfer closings, contract terms, and task mix is unavailable, leaving key values dependent on broad-industry proxies and estimates.

caveats: The a base is not quantitatively reproducible from the cited four-digit occupational mix: the bridge names exposed task types and likely bias direction but does not provide a wage-weighted task calculation. This is appropriately labeled PROXY, and downgrading it to ESTIMATE would not change the already MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The d5 bridge converts broad-sector employment growth into constant-price 541715 demand by judgment, while S4 describes R&D performance rather than outsourced demand. This proxy distance is explicit and the low-to-high range spans contraction through growth.

The q base rests mainly on a federal contracting rule plus judgment; S6 does not add retention evidence, and no revenue-weighted contract mix is available. The ESTIMATE label and 0.25-0.60 range make the uncertainty visible.

The o source documents demanding life-science workflows but not the numerical operator-required share or the physical and engineering portions of 541715. The value remains an explicitly bounded ESTIMATE.

The packet sets heterogeneous to false even though its narrative says outcomes differ markedly across defense engineering, environmental, medical, and computational R&D. This metadata inconsistency does not alter a primitive, factor, base tier, or action.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business customers buy continuing operational coverage, monitoring, maintenance, incident response, and facility or systems administration under managed-service, support, or staffing contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive client IT units, software publishers, systems integrators that do not operate the environment, hardware resellers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market providers that operate and manage clients' computer systems or data-processing facilities on a recurring outsourced basis.

caveats: O*NET describes the administrator occupation rather than the wage mix of 541513 firms.
The estimate excludes customer-side software substitution and revenue effects.
a.change_evidence: A 541513 employee census mapped to O*NET tasks, ticket-system exports with issue categories and resolution times, and time-and-motion interviews with service-delivery leads.
a.rationale: The core administrator task set includes monitoring, log review, routine configuration, backup, recordkeeping, troubleshooting, and access administration. AI can reduce triage, knowledge lookup, documentation, and routine remediation effort, but hands-on changes, security judgment, root-cause diagnosis, and accountable escalation remain material.

d5.bridge: BLS measures constant-dollar output for the broader 541500 Computer systems design and related services industry: 799.0 billion in 2024 and 1,096.2 billion in 2034. Converting that ten-year ratio to five years gives 1.17. The broader category includes activities outside 541513 and likely overstates on-site facilities-management demand if cloud migration substitutes for client-site operations.
caveats: The BLS projection is for a broader industry and extends through 2034 rather than exactly five years from the run date.
CES payroll employment is not constant-price demand and is only corroborative.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit Economic Census services-receipts series, contract renewal volumes, and customer migration data separating managed on-site systems from cloud and software-delivered alternatives.
d5.rationale: The broader industry output outlook supports growth in demand for related technical services, while the range discounts it for the narrower on-site operations definition and for customer self-service or cloud substitution.

caveats: NAICS classifies establishments by primary activity and does not establish the share of revenue that is recurring or external.
The injected LMM count may have establishment-to-firm and size-bridge error.
e.change_evidence: A provider-level business-description review using company websites and contracts, plus a deal database screen for independent versus captive ownership.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already centered on operating clients' systems or data-processing facilities, so most LMM firms should meet the outsourced-service requirement. The deduction allows for mixed-revenue contractors, captive-like affiliates, and firms whose reported primary activity does not match the recurring-service lens.

caveats: The occupation source spans employers outside outsourced facilities management.
The five-year operator-required share has no direct published 541513 measure.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on insourcing and cloud migration, vendor contracts allocating outage and security responsibility, and a cohort of accounts that moved to software-only or platform-managed alternatives.
o.rationale: Production networks and systems still require a party accountable for availability, security, maintenance, and exception handling. Automation and Networks-as-a-Service can remove portions of routine administration or shift work to platforms, so the estimate does not assume the current operator is indispensable for all delivered quantity.

caveats: No verified 541513 contract-price panel was located.
Customer concentration and public-sector procurement can make retention materially lower than the central estimate.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample identifying fixed-fee, cost-plus, time-and-materials, gain-share, renewal, and benchmarking clauses, combined with renewal price and gross-margin cohorts.
q.rationale: Managed-operation customers can observe staffing and service-level changes at renewal or through benchmarking, making labor savings contestable. Retention remains possible where the operator owns the runbook, integration knowledge, SLA accountability, and delivery capacity; the range allows for shared savings and competitive repricing.

caveats: There is no published five-year implementation measurement for the defined 541513 lens.
BLS occupational projections are not a direct measure of AI implementation at service providers.
rho.change_evidence: Customer contract change-control provisions, incident postmortems, production change-failure data, and pilots measuring autonomous versus human-approved remediation.
rho.rationale: Routine work can be deployed through runbooks, monitoring tools, and supervised copilots, but the service includes production systems, security, and incident work. The implementation range therefore recognizes both automation of routine administrator tasks and quality-control, change-management, and customer-approval limits.

caveats: ABS publishes owner characteristics for employer-business respondents but is not a 541513 five-year sale-rate series.
No verified industry-specific control-transfer denominator was located.
s5.change_evidence: A 541513 owner-age tabulation from the Census Annual Business Survey, five years of closed lower-middle-market transactions, and broker interviews on failed versus completed mandates.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment: a mature technical-services provider can be sold, yet certifications, customer concentration, and key-employee dependence can prevent a transferable sale. The interval excludes shutdowns and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: Recurring production-system operations combine repeatable monitoring and workflow tasks with a continuing need for accountable service delivery.
records.principal_weakness: The narrow on-site facilities-management model may be displaced by cloud and platform-managed alternatives, and verified six-digit transfer evidence is absent.

caveats: S1 supports the industry definition but not the numerical e or q estimates; both are honestly labeled ESTIMATE, and the packet explicitly identifies the missing provider and contract evidence.
S2 and S3 describe a broad occupation rather than the 541513 workforce mix and do not quantify a, rho, or o; the packet discloses those limitations and labels all three values ESTIMATE.

S5 mislabels the reported months: 78.4 thousand is December 2023 and 80.9 thousand is November 2024 in the cited December 2024 table. S5 is expressly corroborative, while the d5 base is derived from S4, so correcting the labels does not change the base tier or action.
S6 establishes availability of owner-age data but not an industry-specific five-year sale rate; s5 remains bounded judgment and is correctly labeled ESTIMATE.

Cloud and platform substitution is discounted in both d5 and o, creating some downward overlap. Removing that overlap from either leaf can raise D only from 9.594 to its cap of 10 at the registered bases, which does not change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The stated default lens omits the NAICS definition's explicit on-site qualifier and mentions support or staffing contracts, which can admit adjacent managed-service activity. The included-activity statement still requires operation and management of client systems, and no correction supported by the audited evidence demonstrates a base-tier or action change.

caveats: a: No observed occupation-by-wage mix exists for the narrowed six-digit lens.
a: The BLS occupation source covers workers across legal services and other employers, not independent specialist providers.
rho: This is a five-year operational judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
rho: State unauthorized-practice rules, client data restrictions, and patent-prosecution quality requirements vary by service and jurisdiction.
e: The published NAICS description does not report establishment shares or revenue by illustrative activity.
e: The injected LMM count is an estimate and its industry-size bridge may not preserve this service-line mix.
s5: The owner-age figure is for small businesses generally, not LMM specialist legal-support providers.
s5: A retirement intention or sale listing is not a qualifying control transfer.
q: Evidence is chiefly for law firms and broader professional services, not the narrowed provider population.
q: Pass-through depends on service commoditization, customer bargaining power, and disclosure rules.
d5: The source is a four-digit legal-services projection, not the six-digit narrowed lens.
d5: Industry output is an imperfect proxy for demand for outsourced specialist support.
o: The figure is a judgment about the narrowed service basket, not a measured operator-retention share.
o: Patent-agent regulation does not cover every retained paralegal workflow.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers purchase matter-based, subscription, retainer, or repeat project support; revenue is commonly tied to documents, filings, research, or prosecution work rather than ownership of a legal claim.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541199 combines paralegal and patent-agent services with notary and process-serving services. The retained specialist-support population has a coherent knowledge-work and repeat-outsourcing model; the excluded transaction services have distinct labor, billing, and software-substitution dynamics.
lens.excluded_activities: Offices of lawyers, title abstract and settlement offices, captive units, transaction-only notaries, stand-alone process servers, one-off consumer filings, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent specialist paralegal and patent-agent providers that sell recurring or repeat outsourced document, research, filing, and prosecution support to law firms, corporate legal departments, and inventors.

caveats: No observed occupation-by-wage mix exists for the narrowed six-digit lens.
The BLS occupation source covers workers across legal services and other employers, not independent specialist providers.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 541199 occupation file from BLS or a payroll extract from specialist providers, paired with time-and-motion studies by task and verified AI workflow logs.
a.rationale: The retained work includes research, document organization, drafting, correspondence, filing, patent search, and prosecution support. BLS identifies research, organizing files, and drafting documents as core paralegal work, while the ABA identifies research, review, due diligence, and drafting as AI-assisted legal tasks [S2, S4]. Substitution is limited because accountable review, client context, and regulated representation remain human work.

d5.bridge: S6 measures real chained-dollar output for all NAICS 541100 Legal Services, projecting 348.5 in 2024 and 408.9 in 2034. Taking the five-year equivalent of that ten-year real-output ratio yields the base for the narrowed 541199 specialist-support basket. It may overstate demand because lawyer-office work can grow without outsourced support, and it may understate demand if specialist support gains share through outsourcing.
caveats: The source is a four-digit legal-services projection, not the six-digit narrowed lens.
Industry output is an imperfect proxy for demand for outsourced specialist support.
d5.change_evidence: A time series of 541199 receipts by service line, client procurement spend, patent filings, and outsourced paralegal utilization, all deflated to constant quality and prices.
d5.rationale: The BLS all-legal-services real-output projection provides the closest reachable constant-price demand proxy [S6]. The retained services support legal work but are not separately projected, so the range admits mix and self-service uncertainty.

caveats: The published NAICS description does not report establishment shares or revenue by illustrative activity.
The injected LMM count is an estimate and its industry-size bridge may not preserve this service-line mix.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 541199 business-directory pull coded by service line, customer type, revenue recurrence, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The code explicitly includes specialized legal or paralegal services and lists notary, paralegal, patent-agent, and process-serving examples [S1]. The lens retains recurring specialist-support firms and excludes the transaction and delivery-oriented activities, so eligibility is materially below the full injected firm population.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The figure is a judgment about the narrowed service basket, not a measured operator-retention share.
Patent-agent regulation does not cover every retained paralegal workflow.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and churn studies that classify work retained by external providers, shifted in-house, self-served through software, or eliminated after AI deployment.
o.rationale: Much of the remaining quantity should still require an accountable provider: patent agents must be registered to prepare and prosecute applications, and legal AI users retain duties of competence, confidentiality, supervision, and accuracy [S3, S4]. Some standardized documents, searches, and filings can nevertheless migrate to self-service software or in-house workflows.

caveats: Evidence is chiefly for law firms and broader professional services, not the narrowed provider population.
Pass-through depends on service commoditization, customer bargaining power, and disclosure rules.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and billing extracts segmented by hourly, fixed, retainer, and per-filing revenue, then matched to realized labor savings and renewal pricing.
q.rationale: Repeatable document and filing workflows can support fixed, retainer, or value-oriented pricing, permitting some retention of efficiency. Retention is not assumed complete: the Thomson Reuters survey reports that 40% of firm respondents expect alternative fee arrangements to increase because of GenAI, and the ABA describes client pressure for alternatives to hourly billing [S5, S6].

caveats: This is a five-year operational judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
State unauthorized-practice rules, client data restrictions, and patent-prosecution quality requirements vary by service and jurisdiction.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of specialist paralegal and patent-agent firms showing task-level adoption, quality exceptions, rework, and headcount avoided after deployment.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be material but constrained: the ABA says users must protect client information, supervise, provide competent representation, and charge reasonable fees; it also notes unreliable output and hallucination risk [S4]. Those requirements preserve review, workflow design, and tool-governance work.

caveats: The owner-age figure is for small businesses generally, not LMM specialist legal-support providers.
A retirement intention or sale listing is not a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-deal data from Axial, GF Data, or a legal-services-focused business broker, reconciled to owner age and continuity of operations.
s5.rationale: A meaningful owner-retirement pipeline is plausible, but no observed five-year control-transfer rate was located for eligible 541199 firms. AP reports that 51% of small-business owners are over 55 and describes sale, family succession, and closure as distinct exit paths [S7]; only qualifying transfers with continuing operations count here.

records.principal_driver: Repeatable specialist legal-support workflows combine substantial document and research work with continuing accountability requirements [S2, S4].
records.principal_weakness: Public data do not isolate the retained service lines or their LMM transaction history within the heterogeneous six-digit code [S1].

caveats: The q rationale misattributes an ABA/client-pressure claim to S6, which is a BLS industry-output table. S5 still supports the stated 40% alternative-fee-arrangement expectation, q is already labeled ESTIMATE, and correcting the citation claim would not change the base tier or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The narrowed specialist-support lens is coherent and explicitly disclosed, but public data do not measure the eligible share of 541199 firms; e=0.50 and the resulting F factor therefore remain judgmental despite the honest ESTIMATE label and broad range.

The task-share, implementation, transfer, retention, and operator-retention bases are not directly observed for independent specialist paralegal and patent-agent providers. Their ESTIMATE labels, caveats, change-evidence statements, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, and LOW_PRIORITY-to-PRIORITY interval appropriately expose that limitation.

ABA duties directly govern lawyers, and USPTO registration directly covers the patent-agent portion of the lens; extrapolating those accountability constraints to the full retained paralegal workflow is directionally plausible but not measured.

caveats: a: The sources describe the Interior Designers occupation, not the entire wage mix of NAICS 541410 firms.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based professional services, commonly billed through hourly, fixed, cost-plus, percentage-of-project-cost, or combined fee arrangements; repeat revenue arises from renovation cycles, multi-site customers, referrals, and ongoing customer relationships rather than a pure subscription.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house design departments, furniture-store services supplied free with merchandise, one-off decorative retail sales without an outsourced service relationship, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent interior-design firms that provide repeat or recurring outsourced planning, design development, specifications, procurement coordination, construction-administration, or project-management services to external residential, commercial, hospitality, healthcare, and retail customers.

caveats: The sources describe the Interior Designers occupation, not the entire wage mix of NAICS 541410 firms.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a QCEW-versus-SUSB vintage and coverage mismatch; this task-share estimate does not correct it.
Source task descriptions do not measure AI task performance or adoption.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study or payroll-coded task survey of NAICS 541410 firms, separating designers, project managers, procurement staff, and administrative staff, plus observed usage logs from CAD, BIM, specification, and project-management systems.
a.rationale: The task inventory supports exposure in CAD/construction-document production, research, cost estimation, material selection, rendering, and routine client communications. It also includes client discovery, design judgment, code research, contractor coordination, site inspection, and accountable approval; those tasks limit the wage-weighted opportunity. The estimate treats generative drafting, visualization, specification support, and administrative acceleration as substitution or avoided hiring, not autonomous delivery.

d5.bridge: S1 measures US employment for the Interior Designers occupation, projected to grow 3 percent from 2024 to 2034, rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the NAICS 541410 service basket. Scaling that ten-year occupational projection to a five-year midpoint gives the 1.015 base proxy; it may understate service demand if productivity allows output growth without employment, or overstate it if employment rises for tasks outside the screened outsourced-service firms.
caveats: Employment is not output or constant-quality demand and covers designers working outside specialized design services.
The BLS projection spans 2024 to 2034 and may not capture near-term construction cycles or changes in service mix.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted annual receipts or service-output data for NAICS 541410, segmented by residential and commercial customer type, combined with project-volume measures from design firms and construction-permitting cohorts.
d5.rationale: The occupational outlook gives a modest baseline: renovation demand, building-code attention, and accessibility needs support work, while the long-horizon employment projection is only 3 percent. The range permits modest contraction or a somewhat stronger five-year service-demand outcome without using nominal construction spending as a demand substitute.

caveats: Census product definitions establish the service scope but do not measure eligibility or transferability.
The injected LMM firm count is an estimate derived from size-class bridging and is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Target-level business-broker or deal-database records with service mix, customer concentration, owner role, legal entity, employee count, and normalized EBITDA for NAICS 541410 prospects.
e.rationale: Most lower-middle-market establishments that genuinely sell external design services should be operating businesses capable of a control transfer, but some are founder-dependent personal practices, retail-adjacent operations, captive relationships, or lack durable repeat customer relationships. The base case excludes those without a transferable outsourced-service operation.

caveats: Sources establish task and service characteristics, not the future share of demand served without an accountable operator.
The estimate may be too high in low-complexity residential decorating and too low for regulated or complex commercial projects.
o.change_evidence: Customer-choice surveys and completed-project data comparing software-only design, retailer-provided design, and independent-firm delivery, with outcomes by project complexity, code exposure, and customer segment.
o.rationale: A high share of the service quantity should still require an accountable operator because the work involves client-specific functional and aesthetic decisions, building-code and accessibility considerations, construction documents, contractor coordination, and site inspection. Software can remove portions of low-complexity ideation, visualization, and product-search work, so the estimate is below one.

caveats: The ASID chapter guidance describes fee methods, not their national revenue shares or pass-through behavior.
Residential and commercial work can have materially different fee mixes and buyer power.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of executed client agreements and post-AI renewal proposals, coded by hourly, fixed, cost-plus, percentage, and hybrid fee type, with realized margin and price changes.
q.rationale: Hourly work can retain productivity gains until competition or staffing reprices rates; fixed fees can retain savings during the engagement but reset at renewal; cost-plus and markup arrangements may retain procurement economics but customers can negotiate margins. The mixed billing evidence supports a midrange retention assumption rather than full pass-through or full retention.

caveats: No source measures five-year implemented AI savings for this NAICS industry.
State-specific title, licensure, and code requirements can materially change implementation speed.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal sample of independent interior-design firms with workflow-level AI deployment data, measured cycle times, staffing changes, error/rework rates, and client or insurer requirements.
rho.rationale: Operational implementation can occur in documentation, visualization, search, estimating, and internal coordination, but design firms must validate outputs against codes, accessibility, actual site conditions, product availability, and client intent. Client and contractor coordination, inspections, and professional accountability keep implementation below the technical exposure level over five years.

caveats: No reachable public source was found that reports five-year qualifying-control-transfer incidence for the defined NAICS 541410 LMM population.
Observed announcement counts would understate private small-business sales and would not identify qualifying control transfers reliably.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transaction data from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, and business brokers, matched to NAICS 541410, plus owner-age and succession responses from an industry association survey.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer is plausible for an established service firm but is constrained by founder-centered client relationships, variable project pipelines, and the small number of firms likely to combine sufficient EBITDA with transferable operations. The range intentionally allows for owner retirement, strategic acquisitions, and sponsor-backed platforms while excluding closures and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: Workflow acceleration in documentation, visualization, research, estimating, and coordination while retaining accountable professional delivery.
records.principal_weakness: Limited public evidence on qualifying transfers and a project-based, founder-dependent revenue model with mixed fee pass-through.

caveats: The a, rho, and o estimates are inferred from occupational task descriptions rather than measured AI performance, implementation, wage-weighted industry workflows, or future operator-required demand for NAICS 541410.
The e and s5 estimates lack direct evidence on target eligibility, transferability, and five-year qualifying control-transfer incidence; the resulting F factor is suitable for screening only.
The q estimate rests on qualitative Minnesota chapter fee-method guidance that does not establish national revenue shares or realized AI-related price pass-through.
The d5 base uses a ten-year occupational employment projection as a five-year service-demand proxy; employment, industry output, and near-term residential and commercial project cycles can diverge.

Residential, commercial, hospitality, healthcare, and retail work are compressed into one primitive set despite potentially material differences in workflow exposure, regulation, buyer power, and software-only substitution.

sources.supports: BLS describes duties including plans, specifications, cost estimates, installation oversight, contractor coordination, site visits, codes, CAD/BIM use, 2024 employment, and projected 3 percent employment growth from 2024 to 2034; it also identifies renovation, codes, and accessibility as demand factors.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and organizational clients; recurring retainers, repeat engagements, and ongoing advisory or managed commercial-support fees rather than media spend or a one-time strategy project.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off-only advisory projects, advertising agencies, public-relations agencies, direct-mail advertising, market-research and polling firms, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US marketing, sales-management, customer-service-management, new-product, pricing, forecasting, and marketing-strategy consultants that supply a repeat or recurring outsourced service to external clients and fall in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET measures tasks for the national Management Analysts occupation, and BLS measures occupations in the broader NAICS 541600 consulting industry, rather than wage-weighted work in NAICS 541613. I map the documented information gathering, data analysis, report preparation, and document-editing tasks to AI substitution or avoided hiring, while excluding client interviews, relationship work, contextual recommendations, and accountable decision-making; this likely overstates exposure for senior relationship-led boutiques and understates it for analysis-heavy retainers.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers, who may be especially important in small consulting firms.
The occupation proxy includes consultants outside marketing consulting and the broader industry includes scientific and technical specialties.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS occupational staffing-and-compensation file, time-and-motion studies from marketing-consulting firms, or anonymized task-level timesheets split between retainer and project work.
a.rationale: The task evidence supports substantial exposure in research, synthesis, drafting, reporting, and administrative coordination, but a material share of marketing advice depends on client discovery, market context, persuasion, and senior accountability.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in the broader NAICS 541600 Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services to increase from 1,864.7 thousand in 2024 to 2,039.3 thousand in 2034, a 9.4 percent increase. I translate that broader ten-year employment projection into a five-year constant-quality demand proxy, centered below a mechanical half-period extrapolation because marketing consulting faces more direct software substitution than some technical consulting specialties; the proxy may overstate durable demand if clients internalize work.
caveats: The BLS projection is an employment proxy for a broader industry, not a constant-price, constant-quality output forecast for NAICS 541613.
Employment growth can diverge from client demand if productivity changes materially.
d5.change_evidence: A current forecast of US marketing-consulting real output at NAICS 541613, client procurement-spend surveys, and six-digit revenue series deflated with an appropriate service-price index.
d5.rationale: The broad consulting outlook indicates continuing client need for efficiency and specialized advice, while the narrower marketing-consulting basket is vulnerable to digitized research, content, and analytics. The range permits modest real expansion without assuming that all historical consulting demand remains intact.

caveats: No public six-digit source was found that separates retainer, repeat-engagement, and one-off revenue or identifies the specified EBITDA band.
The NAICS boundary excludes adjacent advertising, PR, direct-mail, and research firms that may have different recurrence.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue-recognition data from a marketing-consulting industry survey, QoE reports for marketed firms, or a broker dataset coding retainer concentration and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The classification covers advice on strategy, forecasting, product development, pricing, sales, and customer service. Those activities can be sold on retainer or repeat cadence, but the definition does not require recurrence and a meaningful share is likely discrete project work; the eligibility estimate therefore excludes one-off-only practices.

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for this industry.
The estimate holds service quality constant and does not assume that AI-created categories of work offset displacement.
o.change_evidence: Client surveys on replacing external marketing consultants with software or internal teams, plus renewal and churn data for recurring advisory contracts after AI adoption.
o.rationale: Even after software absorbs portions of research and production, clients still need accountable operators to frame commercial problems, obtain internal information, make tradeoffs, persuade stakeholders, and stand behind recommendations. The exposure of routine work prevents treating the entire current service basket as durable.

caveats: Billing mix and pass-through behavior are not publicly reported for this six-digit industry.
This is retention of implemented gross benefit and does not include volume loss or implementation feasibility.
q.change_evidence: Executed client contracts identifying fixed-fee, hourly, and retainer revenue; renewal-price histories; and win-loss records following AI-enabled delivery changes.
q.rationale: Client work is commonly sold by project or hour, with proposals compared on scope, schedule, and cost. That supports partial retention through better delivery economics, but periodic renewal, competitive rebids, and customer demands for lower fees should pass a meaningful portion of gains through to buyers.

caveats: No source was found that measures five-year implementation within the six-digit industry.
The estimate is about operational implementation, not revenue growth, pricing, or demand effects.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of marketing-consulting firms showing measured labor-hour reductions after workflow deployment, plus audit results for client-facing AI-assisted deliverables.
rho.rationale: AI can be deployed in internal research, first drafts, analysis, proposal preparation, and routine client reporting, but implementation is constrained by client-data access, source verification, brand and factual risk, bespoke engagements, and the need for senior review before advice reaches a client.

caveats: The available owner-age evidence is for privately held employer businesses generally, not this industry or the EBITDA band.
It does not measure actual control-transfer probability, so the numeric probability is a bounded judgment.
s5.change_evidence: Closed lower-middle-market transaction records tagged to NAICS 541613, broker books showing marketed and closed mandates, and owner-age plus succession-plan survey microdata for this industry.
s5.rationale: Owner-aging evidence creates a succession tailwind, but a qualifying control transfer is narrower than retirement intent: founder dependence, client concentration, and weak recurring revenue can make an advisory practice untransferable. The estimate reflects only the eligible firms under the lens.

records.principal_driver: Supervised AI can reduce time spent on the research-to-recommendation production chain while recurring client advisory still requires accountable human delivery.
records.principal_weakness: Public data do not reveal how much NAICS 541613 revenue is recurring and transferable rather than founder-led, one-off strategy work.

caveats: The central values for a, rho, q, and o rely on the national Management Analysts occupation and the broader NAICS 541600 consulting industry rather than direct evidence for NAICS 541613. The packet discloses these scope limits and uses PROXY or ESTIMATE states, so this is an evidence limitation rather than a material defect.

The d5 range of 1.03-1.08 is narrow relative to the transformation from a broader-industry ten-year employment projection to a six-digit five-year constant-quality demand index. A wider range would better reflect industry and metric mismatch, but plausible corrections do not change the base CONDITIONAL tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

The d5 bridge lowers the broad growth proxy partly for software substitution while o separately reduces demand to the operator-required share, creating some risk that software substitution is counted twice in D. Removing that overlap would raise D, but H remains the binding 3.28 factor and the action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS, so the issue fails the literal materiality test.

S5 is a broad Project Equity toolkit statement rather than industry-specific transfer evidence and does not document an actual control-transfer probability. The packet appropriately labels s5 as ESTIMATE and identifies direct transaction and succession data as the evidence needed to move it.

The recurring or repeat-revenue share, pass-through economics, and transferability of the eligible 541613 subset are not observed. Those gaps are plainly disclosed, reflected in estimate states and ranges, and carried into MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness.

The lens still spans strategy, pricing, sales-management, customer-service-management, forecasting, and new-product consulting, so heterogeneous=false understates residual business-model variation within the recurring-service subset. This metadata judgment does not drive the tier or action.

rho: ISO 17025 applies to testing and calibration laboratories generally, not every firm in the NAICS population.
rho: The estimate treats implementation as workflow adoption and excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, government, and institutional customers buy test programs, samples, inspections, reports, certificates, and recurring compliance or quality work; revenue is typically per sample, project, retainer, or contracted program.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital and physician diagnostic laboratories, captive manufacturer laboratories, internal government laboratories, research-only units, equipment makers without an outsourced testing service, and financing shells.
lens.included_activities: Independent testing, analytical, calibration, environmental, materials, and product-quality laboratory services sold to external customers on a repeat or recurring basis.

caveats: O*NET occupations are not a wage-weighted staffing census for NAICS 541380.

The injected labor ratio combines 2024 QCEW wages and benefits with 2022 SUSB receipts; the vintage and payroll-to-receipts universe mismatch may misstate labor intensity and has not been re-estimated.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupational staffing extract for NAICS 541380, laboratory information-system workflow logs, and time-and-motion interviews with accredited laboratory managers.
a.rationale: A material share of technician and support work is documentation, data processing, report drafting, scheduling, customer communication, and result compilation. Core sampling, sample preparation, instrument operation, method execution, calibration, safety, and accountable interpretation remain physical or controlled work. The supplied labor-to-receipts input is retained without adjustment.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national wage-and-salary employment for NAICS 541380 from 179.5 thousand in 2024 to 186.5 thousand in 2034, a 3.9 percent change. Scaling that ten-year employment trend to a five-year horizon is used as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity. Employment can understate quantity when productivity rises and can overstate it when automation reduces labor without comparable service demand growth.
caveats: BLS employment is not service quantity and spans the entire NAICS industry.
The source is a ten-year forecast, while this primitive requires a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand ratio.
d5.change_evidence: Industry-specific constant-dollar revenue or sample-volume series segmented by environmental, materials, calibration, and product-testing end markets; major-client procurement forecasts; and regulatory testing-volume data.
d5.rationale: The BLS industry outlook indicates modest decade growth, supporting a near-flat to modestly positive five-year demand view rather than a high-growth assumption.

caveats: Eligibility is a judgment because public industry files do not identify recurring external revenue, normalized EBITDA, or captive status.
The injected firm count is an SUSB size-margin bridge and therefore does not directly observe the LMM EBITDA population.
e.change_evidence: Business-register ownership flags, establishment-level customer concentration and revenue mix, and normalized financial statements for a representative LMM laboratory sample.
e.rationale: Many establishments can sell external laboratory work, but the industry includes captive, government, hospital-linked, very small, specialist, and capital-intensive operations that may not fit the recurring outsourced-service and normalized-EBITDA lens. The supplied LMM count is retained without adjustment.
