caveats: The estimate is not a measured operator-required share and will differ sharply by test type.
Some unaccredited, commodity, or fully digital testing-adjacent work may be more substitutable than the industry-level estimate.
o.change_evidence: Client requirements for signatures and accredited reports, regulatory acceptance rules, laboratory automation penetration by test category, and interviews with quality directors and customers.
o.rationale: Much future work should still require an accountable operator for physical sampling, chain of custody, sample preparation, instrument operation, calibration, quality review, and release of trusted results. Software can reduce administrative work and automate selected instrumentation without replacing the service provider in many regulated or quality-critical workflows.

caveats: The NAICS code does not publish contract-type or price-pass-through data.
Accreditation may support retention for some work but does not establish pricing power for all laboratories.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, renewal-price histories, bid tabs, client concentration data, and interviews with procurement leads across environmental, materials, and product-testing programs.
q.rationale: Per-sample, fixed-program, and recurring compliance revenue can let an operator retain part of an implemented cost benefit, especially where turnaround, accreditation, and client workflow integration matter. Competitive bids, customer procurement, and renewal repricing can share much of the benefit, so full retention is not assumed.

caveats: ISO 17025 applies to testing and calibration laboratories generally, not every firm in the NAICS population.
The estimate treats implementation as workflow adoption and excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Validated laboratory-information-management-system deployment case studies, accreditation audit findings, method-validation records, and interviews with quality managers at independent laboratories.
rho.rationale: Document generation, intake triage, data review assistance, quality-system search, and reporting can be implemented, but controlled methods, validation, traceability, client acceptance, and quality review slow operational adoption. ISO requirements make an unrestricted automation assumption unsuitable.

caveats: Census owner tables are broad employer-business data and the listed 2022 survey covers reference year 2021.
BizBuySell is a marketplace-reported small-business transaction sample, not a census of laboratory transfers or LMM control deals.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-coded closed-deal data from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, or business brokers; owner-age records; and seller interviews for independent laboratories.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying control transfer is plausible for a minority of eligible firms. Census provides owner-age and acquisition-year tables for employer businesses, and BizBuySell reports closed small-business transactions, but neither source measures transfers for this industry and EBITDA band; the range is therefore judgment rather than an observed rate.

records.principal_driver: AI can reduce recurring laboratory documentation, reporting, data-handling, and coordination work while accountable physical testing remains necessary.
records.principal_weakness: Public evidence does not directly measure LMM laboratory eligibility, five-year control-transfer probability, contract pass-through, or AI adoption by laboratory workflow.

caveats: S2 and S3 establish that selected technician occupations combine physical laboratory work with documentation and reporting, but they do not establish occupational prevalence, wage weights, or task-time shares within NAICS 541380; the packet appropriately treats a as ESTIMATE.

S4 establishes quality and accreditation requirements but supplies no empirical adoption, retention, or operator-required share. The numeric rho, q, and o values remain judgment estimates despite the coherent directional rationale.

S5 and S6 show that broad owner-characteristic and marketplace transaction data exist, but neither source estimates a five-year qualifying transfer probability for eligible testing laboratories in the LMM band; s5 is therefore weakly anchored but honestly labeled ESTIMATE.

S1 is a ten-year employment projection rather than a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand measure. The d5 transformation is directionally plausible and discloses both the horizon conversion and the error directions, but the resulting demand range remains provisional.

The lens marks the industry as not heterogeneous even though the narrative expressly identifies substantial variation across environmental, materials, calibration, and product testing. This metadata inconsistency should be disclosed, but it does not establish a correction that would change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External residential and commercial customers, primarily served through scheduled recurring agreements and repeat treatments, with some one-time and ancillary work.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive facilities units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose business is principally one-time remediation, wildlife removal, product sales, or internal pest-control work.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators providing recurring or repeat residential, commercial, termite, rodent, insect, and related structural pest-control services to external customers.

caveats: OEWS employment and mean wages are from May 2023, while the frozen compensation input uses 2024 wages.
Occupation categories do not measure task time or AI exposure, and owner labor may be underrepresented.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from a representative pest-control operator, payroll-coded task data, and a technician/office survey that measures time in inspection, application, route administration, quoting, customer communications, and documentation.
a.rationale: The 2023 industry staffing table places 61.05% of employment in pest-control workers, whose core work is physical inspection, treatment selection, application, driving, and barrier creation. Office and administrative support is 17.22%, sales is 6.37%, and management is 6.29%; those functions have substantially more exposure to AI-assisted customer communication, booking, dispatch, reporting, billing, and sales preparation. The estimate also assigns a limited task share to technicians for routing, documentation, quotes, and customer follow-up rather than treating field treatment as substitutable.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US employment for the pest-control-worker occupation, not constant-price and constant-quality service quantity for the lens. It projects 5% occupation growth from 2024 to 2034; applying the half-decade compound equivalent gives approximately 1.025 for year five. Employment can overstate service-quantity demand when productivity rises and understate it when labor supply or automation constrains staffing, so the interval allows both directions.
caveats: Occupation projections cover the whole US occupation and are not restricted to LMM operators or the frozen service basket.
The BLS outlook spans ten years and does not separate quantity from labor productivity.
d5.change_evidence: BEA constant-dollar industry output, a multi-year panel of completed service visits and active accounts from major field-service platforms, or NPMA unit-volume data split by residential, commercial, termite, and geography.
d5.rationale: The occupational projection is modestly positive and is consistent with an essential repeat service rather than rapid volume elimination. The low case permits self-service, weather variation, and productivity; the high case permits invasive-pest and property-protection demand. This is a demand proxy, not a revenue forecast and not a nominal-growth estimate.

caveats: NPMA reports residential revenue rather than LMM firm eligibility, and Rollins is a large multi-brand operator rather than the target population.
No published count separates LMM firms by recurring versus principally one-time revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level LMM census or broker dataset that labels recurring-revenue mix, service lines, customer concentration, and owner route participation for NAICS 561710 listings and transactions.
e.rationale: The default lens fits most LMM firms: the trade is sold externally and scheduled service dominates the public comparables. NPMA reports that recurring revenue was 85.4% of residential service revenue in 2025, and Rollins reports 75% recurring services plus 10% ancillary services. The estimate excludes firms concentrated in one-time termite treatment, wildlife, remediation, or owner-dependent incidental work.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: Licensing and restricted-use pesticide rules vary by state and product; not every service visit entails a restricted-use pesticide.
Termites, wildlife, mosquitoes, rodents, and general recurring prevention may have different operator requirements.
o.change_evidence: Service-line visit data showing resolution via customer self-service, remote monitoring, technician dispatch, and regulatory changes that alter who may inspect or apply treatments; customer willingness-to-pay research for professional versus DIY control.
o.rationale: Most remaining service quantity should still require an accountable operator because technicians travel to customer sites, inspect premises, identify pests, select and apply treatment, use equipment, and create barriers. Most states require worker licensing, and commercial applicators handling restricted-use pesticides must demonstrate safety, pest-management, equipment, and legal knowledge. Software can support diagnosis, records, routing, and self-service prevention, but it does not replace accountable on-site work for the majority of the current basket.

caveats: Public recurring-revenue mix does not measure contractual pass-through or net benefit retention.
The industry has meaningful residential/commercial, seasonal, and one-time revenue variation.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples and renewal price-change data for LMM operators, paired with pre/post implementation margins, churn, service-frequency changes, and bid-win data by residential and commercial segment.
q.rationale: Scheduled service agreements create a credible route to retain a substantial share of an implemented gross benefit through labor productivity, denser routes, fewer manual contacts, and lower collection work. Rollins describes recurring services as ongoing prevention under scheduled agreements, states that many relationships extend over multiple years, and says customers generally sign an initial one-year contract. Retention is below full because annual renewals, competitive price comparison, negotiated commercial accounts, and service-quality expectations can transfer savings to customers.

caveats: The software vendor documents available features rather than independently measured adoption or savings.
Certification rules apply to restricted-use pesticides and may not cover every treatment, but they illustrate the compliance constraint on field substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor cohort data for small pest-control customers showing active AI-feature use and realized office-hours, route-hours, technician-hours, and headcount changes; interviews with operators that adopted or rejected these tools.
rho.rationale: Scheduling, recurring appointments, route ordering, payment nudges, CRM records, reporting, and AI-assisted booking are already packaged in pest-control-specific software. Operational uptake should be meaningful within five years, but the realized labor opportunity is bounded by licensed field work, safety-sensitive treatment decisions, data cleanup, small-firm implementation capacity, and customer-service quality control.

caveats: Rollins acquisitions include businesses of all sizes and may include locations or non-US operations; they are not a denominator-based LMM transfer rate.
BizBuySell figures are across its tracked US small-business market, not pest control, and planned reasons do not equal completed qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: NAICS 561710 transaction records from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, S&P Capital IQ, BizBuySell closed-deal microdata, and a confidential owner-age and succession survey of LMM pest-control firms.
s5.rationale: The estimate reflects demonstrated strategic buyer appetite but does not equate buyer acquisitions with the probability for every eligible LMM firm. Rollins completed 94 acquisitions over three years, including 26 in 2025, and says it targets profitable businesses with customer loyalty. The broader small-business sale market recorded 2,117 completed sales in Q2 2026, while retirement was the leading planned-sale reason at 45%. These facts support a material but not universal five-year control-transfer probability.

records.principal_driver: Recurring route-based service relationships paired with AI-addressable office, dispatch, documentation, and customer-contact work.
records.principal_weakness: Most industry employment is licensed, physical field treatment work, limiting direct AI substitution and making LMM transfer rates unobserved.

caveats: The labor-addressability and five-year implementation estimates are inferred from occupation mix, physical duties, regulation, and vendor feature availability rather than measured pest-control task time, adoption, or realized labor savings.

Eligibility and five-year transfer probability lack denominator-based LMM pest-control evidence; recurring-revenue and acquisition facts come partly from a large public consolidator, while the retirement evidence covers the broader small-business market.

The retention estimate is not directly measured: recurring service relationships support the direction of q, but the cited sources do not quantify how much operating benefit LMM firms retain. Because q is already an honest ESTIMATE and plausible corrections do not change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action, this does not satisfy the materiality test.

The d5 bridge uses projected occupation employment as a service-quantity proxy and holds dataset anchors fixed; productivity, labor supply, weather, and service-line mix may cause actual unit demand to differ.

caveats: a: OEWS is for broader NAICS 541800, excludes self-employed workers, and is not a task-exposure measure.
a: No source reports a wage-weighted task-substitution measure for the frozen NAICS 541810 lens.
rho: The surveys are not a representative NAICS 541810 lower-middle-market sample.
rho: Reported use and intentions are not verified reductions in required labor.
e: NAICS classification does not disclose client recurrence, ownership, profitability, or revenue concentration.
e: The estimate may overstate eligibility where campaign work is sporadic or depends on a founder's personal relationships.
s5: Intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: The available evidence is cross-industry and does not isolate the EBITDA band or NAICS 541810.
q: Billing practices vary sharply among retainers, project fees, commissions, and media-related compensation.
q: The proxy does not measure actual customer-sharing or competitive price changes.
d5: The BLS line is broader than NAICS 541810 and its output concept is not an exact measure of current service-basket demand.
d5: The conversion from a ten-year projection to the requested horizon assumes a smooth path.
o: No source directly measures the operator-required share of the frozen lens's year-five service quantity.
o: Platform product changes and client in-housing can move this measure quickly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business clients, paid through retainers, project fees, media-related fees, commissions, or combinations of those arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off internal marketing teams, captive units, pure software vendors, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and agencies without recurring or repeat external-client service revenue.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market advertising agencies that create campaigns, creative assets, media plans, campaign operations, and performance reporting for external clients on recurring retainers or repeat engagements.

caveats: OEWS is for broader NAICS 541800, excludes self-employed workers, and is not a task-exposure measure.
No source reports a wage-weighted task-substitution measure for the frozen NAICS 541810 lens.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-linked NAICS 541810 occupation file, time-and-motion studies from advertising agencies, and client-level before-and-after staffing records for creative, media, and account workflows.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is concentrated in management, arts/design/media, advertising sales, and office roles. Drafting, versioning, research, campaign reporting, workflow coordination, and routine production are substantially AI-exposed; client persuasion, strategy, taste, negotiation, and final accountability remain materially human. The estimate weights the higher-paid management and specialist work more heavily than headcount alone.

d5.bridge: BLS projects output for the broader 541800 advertising, public relations, and related-services industry from 197.3 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 257.3 billion in 2034, not five-year constant-quality demand for NAICS 541810 alone. I compound that ten-year output path to a five-year ratio and widen it for the NAICS and service-basket mismatch; inclusion of public relations and related services can bias the result in either direction.
caveats: The BLS line is broader than NAICS 541810 and its output concept is not an exact measure of current service-basket demand.
The conversion from a ten-year projection to the requested horizon assumes a smooth path.
d5.change_evidence: BEA industry output and input-output detail separating advertising agencies, agency revenue panels adjusted for media pass-through, and client marketing-spend surveys broken out by outsourced agency service.
d5.rationale: The published broader-industry output path supports moderate real demand growth, but the low case allows client in-sourcing, self-service platforms, and automation-driven substitution to offset that growth for the current agency service basket.

caveats: NAICS classification does not disclose client recurrence, ownership, profitability, or revenue concentration.
The estimate may overstate eligibility where campaign work is sporadic or depends on a founder's personal relationships.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Dun & Bradstreet or PrivCo revenue descriptions, agency billing-system data, and a buyer-quality-of-earnings sample that identifies retained clients and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on creating advertising campaigns and placing advertising for clients, which is generally an external service. Eligibility is below one because the code does not identify recurring revenue, repeated-client economics, firm-level EBITDA, or control-transfer suitability.

caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share of the frozen lens's year-five service quantity.
Platform product changes and client in-housing can move this measure quickly.
o.change_evidence: Client surveys that track outsourced-agency spend versus in-house and self-serve platform substitution, plus agency cohort retention data by service line and AI-enabled platform disclosures.
o.rationale: Clients can self-serve media tools and use generative systems for basic content, research, and campaign operations. However, accountable operators should remain needed for positioning, creative judgment, integrated execution, client coordination, brand safety, and performance accountability. The wide range reflects the contested boundary between software-enabled agency work and software replacement.

q.bridge: The 4As survey measures agency views about GenAI remuneration and anticipated business-model change, rather than discounted five-year retention of implemented gross benefit. I use the reported treatment of GenAI as a cost of business and expectations of pricing-model change as evidence of pass-through and competition; survey responses may overstate future pricing discipline because they are stated expectations rather than contract outcomes.
caveats: Billing practices vary sharply among retainers, project fees, commissions, and media-related compensation.
The proxy does not measure actual customer-sharing or competitive price changes.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of agency MSAs, SOWs, renewal proposals, and realized gross-margin cohorts that separately track AI-enabled output, labor hours, fee changes, and client volume.
q.rationale: Retainers and value-based work can preserve some productivity benefit, but competitive bidding, client procurement, scope expansion, and clients' expectations of AI-enabled speed make full retention unlikely. The base assumes material but incomplete pass-through after renewal and repricing.

rho.bridge: The 4As survey measures respondent agencies' current GenAI use and stated operating barriers, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor opportunity for lower-middle-market NAICS 541810 firms. I translate broad adoption, limited work-share use, and reported legal, quality, skill, and business-model barriers into a bounded five-year operational implementation judgment; the survey's likely larger and more sophisticated agency respondents may bias implementation upward for the lens.
caveats: The surveys are not a representative NAICS 541810 lower-middle-market sample.
Reported use and intentions are not verified reductions in required labor.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal 4As or ANA agency panel reporting labor hours by workflow before and after deployment, plus audited vendor usage logs segmented by agency revenue and employee count.
rho.rationale: Agency adoption is already widespread, but use remains shallow for much of the work and legal, privacy, reliability, and talent constraints limit unattended deployment. Five years should permit workflow redesign beyond experimentation, while preserving human review for brand, client, and platform-risk decisions.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures plans among U.S. business owners, including all employer-firm sizes and industries, rather than completed qualifying control transfers among eligible lower-middle-market advertising agencies. I use its employer-firm five-year sell-or-transfer response as an anchor, lower it for plans that may not close or may not meet the control-transfer definition, and allow an upper case for more transferable established agencies; general employer-firm figures may bias either direction because advertising-agency client concentration and founder dependence are unmeasured.
caveats: Intentions are not completed transactions.
The available evidence is cross-industry and does not isolate the EBITDA band or NAICS 541810.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, or business-broker CRM records tagged to NAICS 541810, together with owner-age and succession-plan data for agencies in the EBITDA band.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year transfer intentions, while a substantial portion of owners report closure or uncertainty. The range excludes closures without transferable operations and does not treat internal reorganizations as transfers.

records.principal_driver: A large share of agency production and coordination work can be redesigned with AI while specialist client service remains valuable.
records.principal_weakness: AI-enabled productivity is likely to be competed away or absorbed by clients through repricing, self-service, and broader scope expectations.

caveats: The record is estimate-heavy and correctly remains MODEL_CONDITIONED with action VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS; its LOW_PRIORITY-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval accurately signals that the base PRIORITY result is not robust.

The a estimate is directionally grounded but triangulated from broader-NAICS occupational data, one managerial occupation's task list, and agency adoption evidence rather than a wage-weighted task study for 541810.

The rho, q, and o evidence comes from agency surveys that do not establish a representative lower-middle-market 541810 sample and do not directly measure implemented labor reduction, retained gross benefit, or surviving operator-required quantity.

S6 is inappropriately cited for s5 because an industry employment/output projection contains no ownership-transfer evidence. This is not material under the required test: S5 still supplies the disclosed broad five-year intentions anchor, s5 is already labeled PROXY, and removing S6 alone changes neither the base tier nor the action category.

The s5 base of 0.20 applies only a small haircut to Gallup's 22% employer-owner intention rate even though intentions can fail to close or qualify; the wide 0.12-0.30 range and explicit bridge disclose the weakness, and the available record does not establish a correction that would change the base tier or action.

The d5 proxy correctly transforms the cited BLS ten-year 541800 output path, but broader-industry coverage and a smooth five-year conversion remain important limitations for advertising agencies specifically.

The lens coherently targets recurring or repeat external-client agencies, but its data and survey anchors cover broader populations; the packet discloses this mismatch rather than demonstrating that the narrowed lens is representative.

caveats: a: There is no six-digit wage-by-task dataset for the frozen lens.
a: S2 covers the broader NAICS 541600 industry and excludes self-employed owners.
a: S3 describes HR specialists across employers, not HR consulting firms, and includes recruiting tasks outside the lens.
rho: The sources describe risk-management needs and legal guidance, not implementation rates for this industry.
rho: Implementation will vary substantially by client data access, service line, and the operator's review controls.
e: No public data directly measures recurring external revenue or transferability for six-digit LMM firms.
e: The injected firm count spans the full NAICS code and can include work outside the recurring-service lens.
s5: S4 is a survey of US business owners generally, rather than NAICS 541612 LMM firms.
s5: Reported intent to sell or transfer is not an observed completed control transfer.
q: Public sources do not provide contract economics for the frozen lens.
q: Retention can differ sharply between administration retainers and bespoke executive advisory work.
d5: Occupation employment is not a direct measure of demand for outsourced HR consulting.
d5: The projection horizon is longer than the five-year horizon and the source is national rather than lens-specific.
o: This is a judgment about accountable-service need, not a forecast of headcount.
o: The surviving share may be lower for standardized administrative work and higher for regulated, conflict-sensitive, or executive advisory engagements.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers; recurring retainers, administration fees, repeat advisory assignments, and fixed-fee or time-based consulting engagements.
lens.excluded_activities: One-time-only engagements, captive internal HR units, employment-placement agencies, executive-search firms, training providers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced human-resource policy, benefits, compensation, wage-administration, and personnel-management advice or administration to external customers.

caveats: There is no six-digit wage-by-task dataset for the frozen lens.
S2 covers the broader NAICS 541600 industry and excludes self-employed owners.
S3 describes HR specialists across employers, not HR consulting firms, and includes recruiting tasks outside the lens.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level time-and-payroll study that maps billed hours by task, role, wage, and AI-assisted workflow; client-approved productivity measurements for HR policy, benefits, and compensation engagements; and legal-review rework rates.
a.rationale: The task evidence supports meaningful exposure in drafting, records, data processing, policy research, reporting, and preliminary screening, while client counseling, conflict resolution, judgment on employment law, and accountable recommendations remain less substitutable. The wage-weighted estimate treats the latter work as a material share of consulting labor rather than assuming all computer-based work is replaceable. S2 shows business and financial operations are 33.12% of the broader consulting workforce and S3 documents both administrative and interpersonal HR work.

d5.bridge: S5 measures US employment of human resources specialists across all industries, rising from 944.3 thousand in 2024 to 1,002.7 thousand in 2034. I convert its ten-year 6.2% change to an approximate five-year demand anchor for the current HR-consulting service basket, then widen the range for substitution between in-house and outsourced work. This can overstate demand because growth in in-house specialist employment need not create outsourcing demand, and can understate demand if regulatory or benefits complexity causes customers to outsource more work.
caveats: Occupation employment is not a direct measure of demand for outsourced HR consulting.
The projection horizon is longer than the five-year horizon and the source is national rather than lens-specific.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price industry revenue and volume series for HR consulting; client procurement spend by service line; renewal and retention cohorts; and a survey separating outsourced HR, benefits, compensation, and wage-administration demand from in-house staffing.
d5.rationale: The proxy indicates modest growth in the adjacent HR work base, so the central demand case is modestly above current quantity rather than dependent on price inflation. The low case allows client budget pressure and self-service to offset this growth; the high case allows additional outsourcing of specialized HR work.

caveats: No public data directly measures recurring external revenue or transferability for six-digit LMM firms.
The injected firm count spans the full NAICS code and can include work outside the recurring-service lens.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment file matched to revenue concentration, contract cadence, owner dependence, EBITDA, and service-line descriptions; broker confidential-information memoranda; and customer-contract samples.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition includes HR policies, benefits planning and administration, compensation systems, and wage administration. Those activities can support repeat outsourced service, but the code also contains project-oriented advisory work and firms too dependent on a principal or a single engagement to meet the lens. The estimate therefore retains a majority, not all, of the injected LMM count.

caveats: This is a judgment about accountable-service need, not a forecast of headcount.
The surviving share may be lower for standardized administrative work and higher for regulated, conflict-sensitive, or executive advisory engagements.
o.change_evidence: Client workflow studies identifying which engagements are replaced by software, self-service, or retained operator review; product adoption and churn data from HR software vendors; and post-implementation interviews with buyers who ended or reduced consulting contracts.
o.rationale: A substantial portion should still require an accountable provider for client-specific policy interpretation, sensitive employee matters, compliance assessment, benefits and compensation design, and final recommendations. S3 shows HR work includes interpersonal relationships, judgment, compliance evaluation, conflict resolution, and advice alongside recordkeeping and information processing; the latter tasks are more vulnerable to self-service or software-only supply.

caveats: Public sources do not provide contract economics for the frozen lens.
Retention can differ sharply between administration retainers and bespoke executive advisory work.
q.change_evidence: Executed master-service agreements, statements of work, renewal notices, rate cards, utilization reports, and client interviews that identify fixed-fee, time-based, gain-share, and cost-pass-through terms.
q.rationale: Repeat advisory and administration relationships can retain part of the benefit through capacity, speed, and quality, especially where the provider bears workflow risk. However, hourly and project consulting fees expose savings to utilization reductions, client procurement, renewal repricing, and competing AI-enabled providers. S1 confirms that the industry is principally advice and assistance, rather than a regulated utility with a protected tariff.

caveats: The sources describe risk-management needs and legal guidance, not implementation rates for this industry.
Implementation will vary substantially by client data access, service line, and the operator's review controls.
rho.change_evidence: Audited adoption data from HR consultancies showing task-level before-and-after hours, human-review rates, client security approvals, and production error or adverse-impact findings.
rho.rationale: Drafting, knowledge retrieval, document comparison, workflow triage, and structured analysis can be introduced with review, but client data access, system integration, validation, and employment-law risk slow operational implementation. S6 establishes that employment uses of software and AI require ADA-related consideration, and S7 describes governance, mapping, measurement, and management activities for AI risk.

caveats: S4 is a survey of US business owners generally, rather than NAICS 541612 LMM firms.
Reported intent to sell or transfer is not an observed completed control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-specific closed-deal data from business brokers and transaction databases, together with owner-age, succession-plan, EBITDA, customer-concentration, and seller-discretionary-earnings records for firms in the lens.
s5.rationale: Gallup found that 22% of employer-business owners reported plans to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The base is below that intended-transition figure because a qualifying control transfer requires a transferable operating business and excludes closures, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable owner-dependent practices.

records.principal_driver: Document-intensive HR workflows offer operational leverage when paired with accountable expert review.
records.principal_weakness: Public evidence does not isolate recurring LMM HR-consulting firms or their contract economics from the broader NAICS population.

caveats: The numeric bases for a, rho, e, s5, q, and o are bounded judgments rather than direct industry measurements; this is honestly labeled ESTIMATE, drives MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS, and does not by itself meet the rejection test.

The a and o rationales rely on a four-digit consulting workforce table and an occupation observed across all employers rather than a six-digit, wage-by-task study of HR consultancies; the population mismatch is disclosed and the ranges are reasonably wide.

The s5 anchor measures general employer-owner transition intentions, not completed qualifying control transfers among NAICS 541612 LMM firms; the downward adjustment to 0.17 remains judgmental but is explicit.

The d5 bridge converts a national ten-year occupational-employment projection into a five-year outsourced-consulting demand proxy. Its bidirectional error is clearly stated, but it remains the principal empirical weakness in D.
S1 establishes industry scope but does not measure the recurring/transferable share e or contract-based retention q; those assumptions require the firm and contract evidence identified in the packet.

The scenario envelope spans LOW_PRIORITY through PRIORITY, so the CONDITIONAL base tier is not robust even though the narrative, principal driver, and principal weakness accurately reflect the registered evidence.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business customers commission discrete design phases, project scopes, hourly work, or continuing design support; the screened population is limited to firms with repeat customer work rather than a single non-repeat engagement.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive manufacturer design departments, independent one-off artists without a repeat outsourced-service practice, engineering-only work classified elsewhere, software publishers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing repeat or recurring outsourced industrial and product-design work to external customers, including product concept development, research, CAD and rendering, prototyping support, materials and manufacturability input, and production-ready design documentation.

a.bridge: S3 measures Claude conversation use across broad occupation categories and task interactions, not wage-weighted task exposure in NAICS 541420. I map its arts/design usage and augmentation evidence to the computer-mediated portions of the industrial-designer duties documented in S2, then exclude physical prototyping, manufacturing evaluation, safety judgment, client discovery, and accountable approval. This likely overstates current substitution because consumer and general arts/design use need not represent paid industrial-design workflows, but could understate avoidable hiring in standardized CAD, visualization, research, and documentation work.
caveats: No published wage-weighted occupation mix for this exact six-digit service industry was located.
AI-use data reflect a model provider's observed conversations rather than firm-level production deployment.
a.change_evidence: A paid task-time study of industrial-design consultancies, separating research, concepting, CAD, documentation, prototyping, manufacturing coordination, and client work, with before-and-after AI tool usage and payroll weights.
a.rationale: The task list contains research, sketching and rendering, virtual modeling, documentation, and cost-oriented analysis that can be accelerated by AI. It also contains prototypes, materials and manufacturing assessment, safety and practicality evaluation, and client approval, so the exposed share is materially below the share of work performed at a computer.

d5.bridge: S2 projects national employment for commercial and industrial designers, an occupation employed both inside and outside the screened NAICS industry, to rise 3 percent from 2024 to 2034. I translate that ten-year occupation signal into a roughly five-year constant-quality demand proxy for the current outsourced industrial-design basket, while widening the range for the different industry population. The proxy may overstate outsourced demand if manufacturers retain work internally, or understate it if consulting demand grows faster than occupation employment.
caveats: Occupation employment is not a measure of industry revenue, outsourced demand, price-adjusted volume, or constant-quality service demand.
Manufacturing cycles and product-category exposures can differ materially across firms.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar US service-revenue series for NAICS 541420, supplemented by customer product-development budgets and contract volumes by end market.
d5.rationale: The occupation outlook is modestly positive and notes demand for innovative high-technology designs, offset by manufacturing declines. That supports a near-flat to modest-growth service-demand view rather than a strong secular expansion assumption.

e.bridge: S1 defines NAICS 541420 as establishments creating and developing product designs and specifications, including industrial-design consulting; it does not identify lower-middle-market firms with repeat external revenue. I use this external-service classification as a proxy and reduce it for one-off practices, firms below or above the screened economics, and engagements without repeat outsourced demand. The classification may overstate eligibility if many establishments are project-only, while excluding manufacturer captives reduces the opposite error.
caveats: NAICS establishments are not the same unit as LMM firms.
The classification does not disclose contract recurrence, owner type, or profitability.
e.change_evidence: Firmographic records and interviews for the SUSB-derived size classes, including customer concentration, repeat-revenue share, ownership, captive status, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is principally an external design-service classification, which supports meaningful eligibility. The lens adds repeat-service and lower-middle-market tests that establishment classification cannot observe.

caveats: This is a judgment about future service substitution, not a measured retention rate.
The share may be lower for simple visualization work and higher for products requiring physical validation or specialized manufacturing knowledge.
o.change_evidence: Customer research on make-versus-buy after adoption of generative design and CAD tools, measured separately for simple product concepts, technical production documentation, prototypes, and regulated or safety-critical products.
o.rationale: The accountable service remains important for physical prototypes, manufacturability, materials, safety, practical-function review, and client approval. Software can self-serve simpler concepts and visualization, but it does not remove the need for a party accountable for translating a client need into a viable manufacturable product in the more complex work retained by the lens.

caveats: S6 is a freelance marketplace hiring guide, not a representative survey of lower-middle-market industrial-design firms.
The estimate excludes any demand or valuation effect and addresses only retention of implemented gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: A contract and invoice sample from industrial-design firms showing revenue by hourly, fixed-fee, retainer, and royalty model, alongside renewal prices, scope changes, realized hours, and win-loss data after AI workflow adoption.
q.rationale: Defined-deliverable project fees can let a firm retain early workflow savings until renewal or repricing, and continuing support can create some operational leverage. Hourly work, competitively bid projects, transparent milestones, and buyer access to freelance alternatives make customer sharing and price competition likely over a five-year period.

caveats: This is a forward-looking operational judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Specialized regulated-product firms and small generalist studios may implement at very different rates.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US industrial-design studios that reports production AI adoption, task-level cycle-time changes, QA rework, tool costs, and reductions in external or employee hiring.
rho.rationale: Within five years, design firms can operationalize AI for research synthesis, visual ideation, first-pass renderings, documentation, and internal business workflows. Implementation is bounded by the need to validate designs against materials, manufacturing constraints, safety, usability, intellectual-property terms, and customer-specific requirements.

caveats: S4 covers marketplace-reported service transactions rather than the screened industry or all private transfers.
S5 reports broad privately held businesses and warns that many retirements result in closure rather than a sale.
s5.change_evidence: A NAICS 541420 owner-age and succession survey matched to transaction databases, state corporate-control filings, SBA change-of-ownership lending data, and brokered and unbrokered deal records for firms in the screened EBITDA band.
s5.rationale: Older ownership creates a meaningful potential supply of transfers, and service-business transactions are active, but the available sources do not give a NAICS 541420 five-year control-transfer incidence or distinguish closures and internal reorganizations. The estimate therefore treats only a minority of eligible firms as completing a qualifying transfer in the horizon.

records.principal_driver: Computer-mediated concept, visualization, modeling, research, and documentation work can be accelerated while physical-product accountability remains valuable.
records.principal_weakness: There is no direct NAICS-specific evidence for transferable-firm incidence or wage-weighted AI task substitution, and much work is project-based rather than recurring.

caveats: S3's broad arts-and-design usage increase was primarily writing, not industrial design, and Claude conversation data do not directly identify wage-weighted task exposure or five-year implementation in NAICS 541420. The record appropriately treats a as PROXY and rho as ESTIMATE; this gap does not by itself establish a correction large enough to change the CONDITIONAL base tier, and the action is already VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The 0.60 eligibility share is not observed in S1: Census establishes the external-service classification but not repeat revenue, LMM economics, ownership, or profitability. The bridge, PROXY state, and 0.45-0.75 range disclose that transformation; an evidence-state downgrade would not change the base tier or the already model-conditioned action.

The five-year transfer estimate relies on broad voluntary marketplace activity and broad owner-age evidence rather than a NAICS 541420 control-transfer incidence. This is honestly labeled ESTIMATE with a wide 0.04-0.15 range, so the evidence weakness is not itself a material defect under the methodology.

The demand bridge translates an all-industry occupation-employment projection into outsourced constant-quality service demand. That mismatch is expressly disclosed and classified as PROXY; it supports only a near-flat directional view, not direct industry demand measurement.

S6 supports the existence of hourly, fixed-price, milestone, and ongoing-support arrangements but is not representative of LMM design firms. Accordingly, q and o remain judgmental estimates rather than empirically measured retention or operator-required shares.

caveats: a: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure specifically for NAICS 541820 LMM agencies.
a: The cited AI evidence is a leader survey and describes potential task efficiency, not realized labor removal.
rho: Expected task efficiency is not equivalent to operational implementation or avoidable hiring.
rho: Regulated public affairs and crisis work may have lower adoption than general commercial PR.
e: The Census definition does not publish the revenue share of political consulting or one-off work.
e: Eligibility is conditional on the injected LMM-band count and is not an establishment-count estimate.
s5: Intentions are not completed transfers and Gallup is not industry specific.
s5: PRovoke's identified-deal count has unknown geographic coverage, firm-size coverage, and denominator.
q: The pricing survey covers branding, creative, digital, marketing, PR, social-media, and web agencies rather than PR alone.
q: Relationship tenure does not prove that an agency retains AI-created savings at renewal.
d5: Occupational employment is not PR-agency revenue or constant-quality demand.
d5: Only 13% of public-relations specialists worked in the broader advertising, public relations, and related-services industry in the cited BLS profile.
o: No source measures the share of NAICS 541820 service quantity that will remain operator supplied after five years.
o: Crisis, public-affairs, and commodity-content engagements can have materially different substitution rates.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External organizations buy advisory and execution services through agency-of-record retainers, monthly fees, hourly engagements, or projects; the screen retains only firms with recurring or repeat service revenue and roughly $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541820 explicitly combines public relations, lobbying, and political consulting. Election-cycle political consulting has a materially different buyer, timing, and repeat-revenue pattern, so it is excluded to make the recurring outsourced-service screen coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Election-cycle political campaign consulting, isolated one-off campaigns, captive communications teams, media buying, advertising placement, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent commercial, nonprofit, and public-affairs agencies providing repeat outsourced reputation, earned-media, stakeholder, crisis-communications, and lobbying counsel to external clients.

caveats: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure specifically for NAICS 541820 LMM agencies.
The cited AI evidence is a leader survey and describes potential task efficiency, not realized labor removal.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from sampled LMM PR agencies, with time-sheet roles and pre/post-AI workflow measurements, would directly measure exposed labor by occupation.
a.rationale: BLS lists writing releases and speeches, responding to media, social-media monitoring, opinion evaluation, and promotion evaluation among core PR-specialist duties (S2). PR leaders report AI use for content, research, pitches, memos, summaries, distribution, and administration (S3). Those activities create substantial wage-weighted exposure, but relationship building, message judgment, executive counsel, and crisis decisions remain human work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects U.S. public-relations-specialist employment to rise 5% from 315,900 in 2024 to 331,000 in 2034 (S2). Converting that ten-year occupational change to a five-year ratio gives 1.025 as a proxy for constant-quality demand for this lens's service basket. It is likely upward-biased for outsourced agency demand because the occupation spans in-house, government, education, and other employers; the operator-elimination risk is separately represented by o.
caveats: Occupational employment is not PR-agency revenue or constant-quality demand.
Only 13% of public-relations specialists worked in the broader advertising, public relations, and related-services industry in the cited BLS profile.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar forecast for U.S. outsourced PR-agency fees by service line, with spend separated from in-house communications employment and political-cycle work, would materially improve this primitive.
d5.rationale: The proxy supports modest quantity growth rather than contraction: BLS attributes continued need to community outreach, customer relations, rapid information spread, and social-media use (S2). It does not establish a direct industry revenue or outsourced-demand forecast, so the range accommodates weaker demand and substitution.

caveats: The Census definition does not publish the revenue share of political consulting or one-off work.
Eligibility is conditional on the injected LMM-band count and is not an establishment-count estimate.
e.change_evidence: A NAICS 541820 firm-level revenue survey separating retainer, repeat, project, lobbying, and political-campaign revenue, cross-tabulated by EBITDA, would identify the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition covers agencies designing and implementing PR campaigns and also includes lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting (S1). The included recurring external-client lens fits many agencies using retainers or repeat accounts, but election-cycle political work and isolated campaign work do not satisfy it; the estimate reflects that mixed code without treating every establishment as eligible.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source measures the share of NAICS 541820 service quantity that will remain operator supplied after five years.
Crisis, public-affairs, and commodity-content engagements can have materially different substitution rates.
o.change_evidence: Client procurement data comparing outsourced PR spend, in-house communications headcount, and software substitution by service line over multiple renewal cycles would directly test operator dependence.
o.rationale: BLS describes PR work requiring communication with media and the public, client representation, judgment on sensitive issues, speaking, and writing (S2). These accountability-intensive activities support continued operator demand, while S3 reports AI support for content, research, pitching, summaries, distribution, and administrative work and notes that in-house teams can use AI for duties formerly outsourced. The estimate therefore retains a majority of the current basket while recognizing software and self-service displacement.

caveats: The pricing survey covers branding, creative, digital, marketing, PR, social-media, and web agencies rather than PR alone.
Relationship tenure does not prove that an agency retains AI-created savings at renewal.
q.change_evidence: LMM PR-agency contract and renewal data showing billing method, AI-enabled hour reduction, rate resets, retained margin, and client churn would directly measure benefit retention.
q.rationale: Time-based billing exposes labor savings to client repricing: a PR-industry report says most PR agencies generate revenue from time and cited 1,685 billed hours per employee in 2022 (S3). In a broader agency survey, hourly billing was 28%, productized or subscription packages 28%, retainers 10%, and projects 25% (S7). Countervailing retention comes from AOR relationships: the ANA and 4As report roughly seven-year average client-agency tenure, with public relations among the longer-tenure agency types and a majority of top-ten clients in AOR or retainer relationships (S4).

caveats: Expected task efficiency is not equivalent to operational implementation or avoidable hiring.
Regulated public affairs and crisis work may have lower adoption than general commercial PR.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year adoption and staffing data from PR agency benchmarking surveys, plus agency interviews documenting which AI-supported workflows reduce hiring or hours after quality review, would materially revise this estimate.
rho.rationale: The task set in S2 includes material that can be drafted, researched, monitored, or summarized with AI, and 88% of PR leaders expected positive speed and efficiency effects in S3. Implementation is limited by client approvals, factual accuracy, confidentiality, brand voice, sensitive stakeholder relationships, and the need for experienced judgment in crises.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions among U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying transfers of LMM public-relations agencies: 22% reported an intended sale, transfer, or public offering (S5). The base scales that intention rate down for uncompleted plans, closures, and owner-dependent firms; it is likely upward-biased relative to completed qualifying transfers. PRovoke's count of 80 identified PR acquisitions in 2025 (S6) confirms an active sector market but does not supply a firm denominator or LMM share.
caveats: Intentions are not completed transfers and Gallup is not industry specific.
PRovoke's identified-deal count has unknown geographic coverage, firm-size coverage, and denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A private-company transaction database matched to NAICS 541820, EBITDA, buyer type, and control-transfer status, together with an owner-age and succession survey of LMM agencies, would directly measure the probability.
s5.rationale: Owner succession is relevant because 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people at least 55 and employer owners report five-year transfer intentions (S5). Sector transaction activity is visible, although 2025 PR acquisition volume declined and larger agencies formed a higher share of completed deals (S6), supporting a cautious conversion from intent to transfer.

records.principal_driver: A large share of recurring PR workflow is information production and coordination that can be AI-assisted, while reputation-sensitive advisory work preserves an operator role.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly and project pricing can transfer AI productivity to clients, and the code's political and one-off work complicates both recurring-revenue eligibility and transferability.

caveats: Every researched base primitive is an ESTIMATE or PROXY, and the injected firm-count anchor n is also ESTIMATE; the PRIORITY base is therefore model-conditioned rather than directly observed.

The base tier is sensitive to the broad national-owner transfer proxy: s5 at its registered low reduces F enough for the all-base/low-s5 case to fall below the PRIORITY breadth cut. This is uncertainty already represented by the LOW_PRIORITY-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY envelope, not a source defect.
The demand bridge uses a national occupation dominated by employers outside advertising, PR, and related services; it is directionally useful but cannot isolate outsourced agency demand.
Pricing and relationship sources span broader agency populations and do not directly measure how much AI productivity an LMM PR agency retains after repricing and renewal.

The disclosed recurring-service lens coherently excludes election-cycle and isolated one-off work, but a whole-code lens would likely produce different eligibility, transferability, pricing, and demand primitives.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Families buy a time-sensitive, episodic service at need or pre-need; revenue is generally an itemized or package charge for professional services, facilities, transportation, preparation, disposition coordination, and optional merchandise.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive cemetery or hospital units, stand-alone software vendors, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1–10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market funeral homes and funeral-service operators providing arrangements, removal and preparation coordination, ceremonies, disposition coordination, pre-need planning, and related merchandise or facilities to external families.

caveats: No source measures AI-exposed task hours or compensation for NAICS 812210 directly.
Occupation descriptions cover funeral service workers nationally and include work outside the LMM-firm lens.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-pay study from a representative sample of independent funeral homes, split among directors, arrangers, attendants, embalmers, and back office; paired with task-level AI pilot logs showing hours removed rather than merely assisted.
a.rationale: The exposed work is concentrated in intake, document drafting and filing support, obituary and communications drafting, scheduling, CRM follow-up, accounting, marketing, and information retrieval. Core paid work also includes bereavement counseling, family decisions, body handling, transportation, ceremony execution, supervision, and regulatory judgment; those tasks limit the wage-weighted substitution share. The base treats AI primarily as administrative capacity and avoidable hiring rather than replacement of licensed, in-person care.

caveats: Death counts are not a one-for-one measure of demand for the current funeral-home basket.
NFDA's revenue projection is nominal and incorporates third-party IBISWorld analysis, so it is not used as a direct real-volume observation.
d5.change_evidence: Annual state-level vital statistics linked to funeral-home disposition and service-package data, plus a longitudinal NFDA GPL panel separating unit counts, real prices, ceremonies, direct cremations, and merchandise per death.
d5.rationale: Underlying deaths are projected to rise after 2025, but the current service basket faces a shift from burial toward lower-revenue cremation and direct cremation. NFDA reports only slow nominal industry-revenue growth through 2029 and identifies cremation as a major revenue challenge. The base therefore keeps constant-price, constant-quality quantity near flat rather than converting a nominal revenue forecast into demand quantity.

caveats: The frozen LMM firm count is an estimate and is not independently recomputed here.
BizBuySell describes listed and sold businesses, not the full NAICS population.
e.change_evidence: Establishment-level Census/County Business Patterns microdata or a licensed NAICS 812210 business database coded for service mix, ownership, customer type, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: Most firms counted in the frozen LMM band are expected to be external-facing funeral-service operators, and sold-business descriptions confirm the relevant service bundle. The share is below one because the code and practical market can include operators whose economics are dominated by crematory, cemetery, merchandise, or other activities that may not satisfy the recurring/repeat outsourced-service lens coherently.

caveats: The source documents duties and licensing but does not measure the future operator-required share.
Operator necessity varies by state and by whether the firm owns a crematory.
o.change_evidence: Disposition-level data on direct cremation and no-service cases by state, funeral-home versus stand-alone-crematory market shares, and state licensing-board records on required responsible persons for each service step.
o.rationale: Nearly all residual demand is expected to require an accountable local operator because services involve removal, disposition coordination, physical preparation or transport, legal documents, licensed oversight, and sensitive counseling. The deduction allows for direct cremation, self-directed online planning, virtual or nontraditional ceremonies, and standalone crematories that reduce the portion delivered by the incumbent-style operator.

caveats: No source directly measures AI-savings pass-through in funeral services.
Retention varies with local concentration, pre-need obligations, and the mix of merchandise versus services.
q.change_evidence: A panel of general price lists and realized invoices before and after workflow automation, matched to local competitor counts and direct-cremation share; contract and pre-need-plan repricing data would test pass-through directly.
q.rationale: Operators can retain a substantial part of back-office and coordination savings because much revenue is service and facility based, but the Funeral Rule requires itemized, current pricing and consumers report that GPL availability affects provider choice. Growing online research and price comparison make full retention unlikely, especially for standardized direct-cremation offers. The base assumes savings are initially retained but are partly shared through renewal, package design, and local competition.

caveats: Implementation rate is a five-year judgment, not an observed adoption statistic.
State licensing, electronic-filing, and privacy requirements differ materially.
rho.change_evidence: A 2026–2031 NFDA member technology survey with workflow-level adoption and realized labor-hour data, plus state-board guidance or enforcement records on use of AI in death-certificate and arrangement workflows.
rho.rationale: Standard document, communications, scheduling, and knowledge workflows can be deployed within five years, but implementation is constrained by sensitive family interactions, short 24–72-hour operating windows, licensed judgment, and the need for accurate legal filings. The base assumes workflow assistance is adopted more readily than autonomous customer-facing or compliance decisions.

caveats: BizBuySell completed-sales data are a selected marketplace sample and its reported earnings are generally below the frozen EBITDA band.
Self-employment of managers is not equivalent to owner age or a sale probability.
s5.change_evidence: NFDA member ownership-age and succession survey cross-tabbed by revenue/EBITDA, confidential broker closed-deal records, and state funeral-establishment license ownership-change filings for 2021–2026.
s5.rationale: Control transfers are plausible because a large share of funeral home managers are self-employed and completed small-business transactions are observable, but no source reports a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible $1–10M EBITDA firms. The broad range reflects succession uncertainty, local licensing and reputation dependence, and the fact that marketplace sales undercount off-market and strategic transactions.

records.principal_driver: A non-discretionary death-care need still requires trusted local coordination, documentation, and accountable physical execution.
records.principal_weakness: Cremation-led mix shift and transparent itemized pricing can compress the value of the current service basket and share operational gains with customers.

caveats: All seven researched primitives are honestly labeled ESTIMATE. The occupational and industry sources support direction and constraints, but none directly measures AI-removable wage share, five-year implementation, eligible-firm share, five-year control transfers, savings retention, real basket demand, or operator necessity; MODEL_CONDITIONED and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS are therefore appropriate.

The five-year transfer estimate is particularly weakly identified: S1 self-employment is not a transfer probability and S5's sold businesses generally sit below the stated EBITDA band. The broad s5 range and explicit caveats keep this from being a material defect.

The frozen n value is an ESTIMATE derived from employment-size-class averages and a broad margin bridge; with only one class treated as inside the band, F is sensitive to within-class firm dispersion that is not represented in the research-input envelope.

The provisional 2025 death count in S6 exceeds the older NFDA 2025 projection used as the starting point for S3's long-range increase. That reduces the implied growth to 2030, but the near-flat d5 base and 0.95-1.07 range already accommodate this uncertainty.

The q range is entirely above the C-factor saturation threshold, so the published scenario envelope shows no C sensitivity even though no source measures AI-savings pass-through. This is transparent under the frozen formula and does not, on the evidence reviewed, change the base CONDITIONAL tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

Service mix, crematory ownership, local regulation, and direct-cremation exposure create meaningful operator heterogeneity despite the packet's heterogeneous=false flag; the lens, primitive ranges, and caveats acknowledge most of this variation, so the flag does not create a material conclusion error.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Transaction-based fees paid directly or through closing costs by buyers, sellers, lenders, insurers, and their agents; repeat referral relationships create recurring order flow rather than subscription revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Law offices, captive lender or insurer units, title-insurance underwriters and non-control financing vehicles; one-off internal real-estate administration is outside the lens.
lens.included_activities: Independent title abstract, title-search, settlement, closing, and related real-estate document-preparation offices that sell repeat services to external transaction participants.

a.bridge: S1 measures tasks and work activities for the title-examiner occupation, not wage-weighted employment in NAICS 541191 or AI substitution. I treat document retrieval, transcription, structured extraction, record updating, and first-pass summarization as exposed, while reserving legal interpretation, exception clearing, and closing coordination. This likely overstates industry-wide exposure because the occupation omits some less-substitutable settlement and relationship work.
caveats: O*NET task importance is not a wage share or an automation measurement.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has the stated QCEW-2024/SUSB-2022 vintage mismatch and IPS harmonization limitation.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-linked NAICS 541191 occupation census plus time-and-motion data from independent agencies, split between search, examination, curative, closing, sales, and administration, would materially move this range.
a.rationale: The occupation's core work is examining documents, summarizing recorded instruments, maintaining title records, and processing information, which makes a substantial share suitable for AI-assisted extraction and drafting. Accuracy, compliance judgment, and exception resolution prevent treating all of that work as avoidable labor.

d5.bridge: S8 forecasts U.S. mortgage-originations dollars through 2028 and S4 reports title-insurance premium volume, whereas this primitive is five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand for the broader NAICS service basket. I use the near-term mortgage outlook only as directional evidence and extend it cautiously; it likely overstates constant-quality demand because mortgage dollars include house-price effects and does not fully represent cash transactions or commercial work.
caveats: Premiums and mortgage originations are dollars rather than constant-quality service quantities.
The available mortgage forecast ends before the requested five-year horizon and is not specific to NAICS 541191.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of purchase, refinance, cash, and commercial real-estate transaction counts by state, linked to title-order incidence and agency revenue mix, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Title and settlement demand is tied to property transactions and mortgage activity. Recent premium growth and the mortgage forecast support a modest central expansion, but cyclicality in rates, inventory, refinancing, and transaction volumes warrants a wide downside range rather than a straight-line extrapolation.

caveats: No public source was found that measures eligibility inside the frozen EBITDA-band firm count.
The frozen firm-count input is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using the provided Damodaran margin and may not identify captive or affiliated units.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS business list matched to title-insurer appointments, lender affiliation, customer concentration, and EBITDA would establish the eligible share directly.
e.rationale: Most lower-middle-market firms classified here plausibly sell external title, search, or settlement work repeatedly, but the lens excludes captive offices and businesses whose economics are principally underwriting or affiliated lending rather than an outsourced operator service.

caveats: This is a judgment about future service delivery, not a regulatory measurement of required staffing.
S5 reflects an industry voice and may understate future software substitution.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state licensing and settlement-responsibility requirements, underwriter delegation agreements, and adoption data for end-to-end digital closings would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: A large majority of remaining demand is expected to require an accountable operator to review title exceptions, coordinate parties, manage funds and closing execution, and take responsibility for errors, even as software handles more intake and record processing. Some standardized transactions may migrate to lender, underwriter, or software-led flows.

caveats: This is not a measured pass-through rate.
Retention differs materially by state, purchase versus refinance mix, underwriter relationship, and local referral concentration.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level fee schedules, lender and realtor referral contracts, title-underwriter remittance terms, and post-automation gross-margin cohorts would directly measure retained benefit.
q.rationale: Automation savings can be retained through faster service and lower rework, but closing services are often shoppable, comparison prices are visible, and lenders or referral partners influence provider selection. Those features make material sharing through price, service levels, and competitive response plausible.

caveats: This is a forward implementation judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
S5 is an industry-association publication and its statement on human judgment is not an independent measurement.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hours, error, claim, and cycle-time data from a representative independent title agency cohort using AI in production, along with underwriter approval rules by state, would materially change implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient for first-pass search, intake, document extraction, and workflow-assist deployment, but title work remains accuracy-sensitive and state practice varies. Human review remains important for ambiguous records, underwriting objections, and fraud-sensitive closings.

caveats: Observed announcements are selected transactions, not a representative sale-rate sample.
The estimate excludes closures and reorganizations that do not preserve transferable operations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction database covering private title and settlement firms, matched to firm size and ownership age, plus owner succession interviews, would materially narrow the estimate.
s5.rationale: Reported acquisitions demonstrate that transferable title agencies are bought, including agencies with long-tenured leadership, but public announcements do not provide a denominator, owner-age distribution, or a five-year control-transfer rate for eligible lower-middle-market firms. The range therefore remains deliberately broad.

records.principal_driver: Document-centric title-search and production work offers a practical target for AI-assisted workflow automation.
records.principal_weakness: Real-estate transaction cyclicality and the need for accountable human exception handling constrain both durable demand and full labor substitution.

caveats: S8 could not be reopened, leaving its mortgage-origination figures unverified. This is not material under the prescribed test: d5 is already labeled PROXY, S4 independently supports the recent directional relationship, and downgrading or removing S8's evidentiary support alone would not change the MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The numeric bases for e, s5, q, rho, and o are judgments rather than measurements. Their sources support service scope, competitive conditions, transfer occurrence, and implementation constraints, but not the selected percentages; the ESTIMATE labels, ranges, caveats, and change-evidence statements make that treatment honest.

The d5 evidence is based on nominal premium and mortgage-origination dollars, has a forecast horizon ending before the requested five years, and does not directly measure the broader NAICS service quantity. The PROXY bridge states these limitations and gives a plausible upward-bias direction.

S6 and S7 establish that agencies are acquired but are selected announcements with no eligible-firm denominator, so they do not independently substantiate the s5 base of 0.15; the record appropriately presents s5 as a broad ESTIMATE.
