caveats: a: The occupational proxy includes government and construction roles outside this NAICS industry.
a: No NAICS-specific task-time or wage-by-task dataset was located.
rho: This is a five-year judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
rho: State and locality rules vary, and the source evidence does not measure AI deployment.
e: NAICS establishment definitions do not identify recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
e: The supplied firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.
s5: No NAICS-specific longitudinal ownership-transfer series was obtained.
s5: Occupational self-employment statistics are not a measure of control transfers for lower-middle-market firms.
q: The agreement is a member resource and is not representative pricing evidence.
q: The figure concerns retained implemented benefit, not revenue growth or demand effects.
d5: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for paid service quantity.
d5: The source includes large public-sector and construction-employer populations outside the lens.
o: This is a judgment about future accountability, not a measured software displacement rate.
o: Remote-inspection growth may vary sharply by property type and jurisdiction.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Typically a client-specific inspection engagement documented in a report; repeat demand may come from referral networks, property portfolios, construction milestones, or recurring customer relationships. Fees and scope are set in a pre-inspection agreement.
lens.excluded_activities: Government code-enforcement units, captive inspection departments, pest and hazardous-material inspections classified elsewhere, one-person practices without transferable operations, and businesses outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Independent, external-customer building and home inspection providers in the lower-middle-market band that earn repeat or recurring outsourced work, including repeat work generated through real-estate, property-owner, insurer, or construction relationships.

a.bridge: S3 measures tasks for the broader Construction and Building Inspectors occupation, including public code inspectors and construction inspectors rather than employees of NAICS 541350. I map plan review, report drafting, image/log organization, scheduling, and client communications to AI-exposed work while retaining on-site visual examination, instrument use, safety judgment, and accountable defect calls as non-substitutable. This may overstate exposure if private inspectors spend more time on-site, and understate it if report production dominates their workflow.
caveats: The occupational proxy includes government and construction roles outside this NAICS industry.
No NAICS-specific task-time or wage-by-task dataset was located.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion sample from multi-inspector firms, inspection-software activity logs, and payroll by role would show the actual share of paid time in site work, report production, intake, scheduling, and plan review.
a.rationale: The occupation includes high-volume document and information tasks that can be assisted by AI, but the service requires an inspector to observe accessible property systems and make a defensible condition judgment. A minority-to-material share of wage-weighted work is therefore exposed, not the inspection itself.

d5.bridge: S2 projects total U.S. Construction and Building Inspector employment, not constant-price and constant-quality demand for private NAICS 541350, from 147,600 in 2024 to 146,500 in 2034. I convert the reported ten-year change to a five-year midpoint ratio of approximately 0.995 and widen the range for the industry's greater exposure to housing transactions. This may understate private demand if government remote-inspection savings drive the projected decline, or overstate it if residential transaction volume weakens.
caveats: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for paid service quantity.
The source includes large public-sector and construction-employer populations outside the lens.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts and realized series for existing-home sales, housing starts, commercial construction inspections, and NAICS 541350 receipts by service line would materially refine the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: The broad occupation projection is essentially flat to slightly declining and explicitly attributes some reduction to remote inspections, while safety and construction quality support continuing demand. A near-flat five-year constant-quality demand assumption is more defensible than extrapolating a short-term housing cycle.

caveats: NAICS establishment definitions do not identify recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
The supplied firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Company-level revenue concentration and customer-cohort data, state licensing rosters matched to payroll, and broker confidential-information memoranda would establish the share with transferable staff, repeat customers, and lower-middle-market earnings.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is external property-condition inspection, and it explicitly excludes government code enforcement, supporting eligibility for a substantial share of firms. Eligibility is reduced because some providers depend mainly on one-off buyer transactions, individual inspector reputation, or non-transferable referral relationships rather than a repeat outsourced-service operation.

caveats: This is a judgment about future accountability, not a measured software displacement rate.
Remote-inspection growth may vary sharply by property type and jurisdiction.
o.change_evidence: Claims data, state practice-rule changes, lender or insurer acceptance policies, and adoption data for remote or self-service inspection products would reveal how much service quantity can be eliminated or supplied entirely through software.
o.rationale: The durable core requires a person to examine a site, use testing equipment, interpret defects, and deliver a written finding under defined professional practices. Remote capture and AI can remove some visits or tasks, but most demand should continue to require an accountable inspector for physical observation and liability-bearing judgment.

q.bridge: S5 is an association model agreement for home-inspection members, not a survey of NAICS 541350 economics. It identifies a separately stated fee and payment timing, which I use as evidence of per-engagement contracted pricing and translate to partial, rather than full, operator retention of internal efficiency benefits. This may overstate retention where local referral competition quickly lowers fees and understate it where capacity is scarce or scope expands.
caveats: The agreement is a member resource and is not representative pricing evidence.
The figure concerns retained implemented benefit, not revenue growth or demand effects.
q.change_evidence: A panel of quote-level fees, inspection duration, report turnaround, and gross margin from firms adopting AI report tools would measure retention and any subsequent repricing.
q.rationale: A pre-inspection agreement can preserve some savings within a job fee, especially for report-production and administration improvements. However, local price competition, agent and customer expectations, and faster turnaround can pass benefits to buyers through lower fees or more included services over time.

caveats: This is a five-year judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
State and locality rules vary, and the source evidence does not measure AI deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor adoption data from Spectora, HomeGauge, and Inspector Toolbelt; insurer underwriting requirements; and interviews with state-licensed inspection firms would test which workflows are in production and whether reviewer time falls.
rho.rationale: Report drafting, image labeling, appointment intake, and plan summarization can be deployed quickly, but implementation is constrained by state practice rules, proof of work quality, client liability, and the need for a credentialed person to validate findings. The base assumes implementation of many back-office and report-assist uses, not autonomous field inspection.

caveats: No NAICS-specific longitudinal ownership-transfer series was obtained.
Occupational self-employment statistics are not a measure of control transfers for lower-middle-market firms.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction records from BizBuySell and regional business brokers, state license ownership-change filings, and seller interviews stratified by employee count would directly measure five-year qualifying control transfers.
s5.rationale: A qualifying transfer is plausible for firms with multiple credentialed inspectors, documented processes, and diversified referral sources, but the industry also contains owner-operator businesses whose local reputation and license are difficult to transfer. The estimate excludes shutdowns and reorganizations that do not preserve a transferable operating business.

records.principal_driver: Report and workflow automation can improve productive capacity while physical inspection and accountable judgment remain required.
records.principal_weakness: Transferability and recurring revenue are not measured in public NAICS data and may be weak for principal-dependent, transaction-driven firms.

caveats: The lens says there is no deviation while its included population is limited to providers earning repeat or recurring outsourced work and its exclusions remove one-person practices without transferable operations. This is a favorable narrowing that should be explicitly described as such. The e and s5 estimates nevertheless reintroduce one-off demand and principal-dependence, so the record does not establish that correcting the disclosure would change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action; under the brief's literal test this is a caveat, not material lens_shopping.

S2 and S3 describe the broader Construction and Building Inspectors occupation, with substantial government, engineering, construction, code-enforcement, and public-works representation. Their use for private NAICS 541350 is disclosed and appropriately labeled PROXY or ESTIMATE, but the population mismatch leaves a, rho, e, s5, d5, and o weakly anchored.

The q bridge is especially weak: a contract that states an engagement fee and payment timing does not demonstrate that 45%-75% of implemented AI benefit is retained. The proxy is disclosed, and the current source does not establish a corrected q; therefore a base-tier or action-category change cannot be demonstrated and the issue does not pass the materiality test.

The 0.995 five-year midpoint used for d5 is a coarse conversion of the BLS 147,600-to-146,500 ten-year projection; a simple linear midpoint is about 0.996. The difference is immaterial to the factor and tier.

The record is estimate-heavy and its LOW_PRIORITY-to-CONDITIONAL envelope holds the dataset anchors fixed, so transferability, recurring-revenue quality, and retention remain Stage 2 validation priorities.

caveats: a: Graphic Designers are employed across many industries, while 541430 also employs account, production, and leadership roles.
a: The proxy identifies task types but does not measure current AI substitution rates or wage shares.
rho: The arts-and-design alumni survey is not a NAICS 541430 agency sample and reports use rather than realized labor removal.
rho: Tool capability and client policy may change faster than the evidence vintage.
e: Census establishment counts are not LMM firm counts and do not identify recurrence, revenue concentration, or transferability.
e: The injected LMM count is intentionally not re-estimated here.
s5: The owner-age statistic is broad small-business evidence rather than a 541430 measure.
s5: It does not distinguish sales from closure, family succession, recapitalization, or internal reorganization.
q: No public industry dataset was found that measures AI savings retained by 541430 operators.
q: Copyright and approval requirements may preserve differentiated human work but do not ensure pricing power.
d5: Occupation employment projections are not constant-price receipts or NAICS 541430 demand.
d5: The projection horizon is ten years and includes employment in other industries.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for this NAICS.
o: Copyright registration guidance is a constraint on some AI-generated work, not a measure of customer demand for agencies.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers; a mix of project, hourly, fixed-fee, and retainer revenue, with the screened population limited to recurring or repeat outsourced relationships.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS includes independent commercial artists and project-only work alongside agencies; the lens retains only repeat or recurring outsourced-service firms because a control-transfer and retained-operator screen is otherwise incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off freelance commissions without repeat service, captive in-house creative teams, fine-art sales, display-ad placement, and firms without transferable operating businesses.
lens.included_activities: US graphic-design firms in the lower-middle-market band that provide repeat or recurring outsourced visual-communication work to external customers, including brand-system upkeep, campaign production, packaging refreshes, and production design.

a.bridge: S2 measures task content for the Graphic Designers occupation, not the wage-weighted occupation mix of NAICS 541430. I map its computer-graphics, layout, concept, and collaboration tasks to the industry workforce and count only ideation, asset variation, layout, production, and administrative portions as exposed; this likely overstates exposure where art direction, client discovery, and accountable approval occupy more paid time.
caveats: Graphic Designers are employed across many industries, while 541430 also employs account, production, and leadership roles.
The proxy identifies task types but does not measure current AI substitution rates or wage shares.
a.change_evidence: A 541430 payroll-by-occupation extract paired with a time-and-motion survey of agency teams, segmented by retainer versus project work, could replace the occupation proxy.
a.rationale: O*NET lists developing graphics and layouts, creating concepts and sample layouts, computer-generated graphics, and collaboration. Generative tools can substitute or avoid hiring in first-pass concepts, asset variants, resizing, layout, and production, but not fully in client discovery, brand judgment, approvals, and final accountability.

caveats: Occupation employment projections are not constant-price receipts or NAICS 541430 demand.
The projection horizon is ten years and includes employment in other industries.
d5.change_evidence: Annual 541430 receipts deflated for design-service prices, split between recurring agency revenue and project/freelance revenue, plus customer procurement surveys, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: BLS projects Graphic Designer employment to grow 2 percent over 2024 to 2034 and cites continuing digital-presence needs, but also explicitly says AI may reduce contracting with freelance graphic designers. For the current outsourced service basket, those opposing forces support a near-flat to modestly declining five-year constant-quality demand estimate.

caveats: Census establishment counts are not LMM firm counts and do not identify recurrence, revenue concentration, or transferability.
The injected LMM count is intentionally not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level database screen of 541430 companies for revenue concentration, contract type, employee count, and owner dependence, validated by broker interviews, could measure this directly.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition spans branding, printed materials, packaging, advertising, signage, and illustrations. Many providers are one-person or project-oriented; the eligible subset is limited to firms with repeat external service relationships and transferable operations, so eligibility is materially below the whole LMM count.

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for this NAICS.
Copyright registration guidance is a constraint on some AI-generated work, not a measure of customer demand for agencies.
o.change_evidence: A buyer survey separating outsourced design spend into self-serve, software-only, and agency-managed workflows, together with agency revenue cohorts by service line, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: An accountable operator is likely to remain needed for brand strategy, stakeholder alignment, production-ready files, review, and rights-sensitive work. Lower-complexity social, template, and first-pass asset work is increasingly self-served or software-supplied, limiting the operator-required share.

caveats: No public industry dataset was found that measures AI savings retained by 541430 operators.
Copyright and approval requirements may preserve differentiated human work but do not ensure pricing power.
q.change_evidence: Client contracts and renewal cohorts from 541430 agencies, showing fee basis, rate-card revisions, scope volumes, and gross margin before and after AI workflow deployment, would materially move the estimate.
q.rationale: Repeat design relationships and brand-system knowledge can retain some workflow savings, but project, hourly, and competitive agency pricing expose productivity gains to client renegotiation and lower-cost alternatives. Retainers and fixed-fee scopes support the upper end; commoditized production work supports the lower end.

caveats: The arts-and-design alumni survey is not a NAICS 541430 agency sample and reports use rather than realized labor removal.
Tool capability and client policy may change faster than the evidence vintage.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US graphic-design agencies showing tool use by workflow, realized cycle-time change, and headcount avoided would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: More than half of arts and design alumni report using generative AI at work, indicating a workable adoption base. Implementation remains constrained by client approvals, brand consistency, IP review, source-file production, and quality control; therefore only a moderate share of exposed work is expected to become operationally implemented in five years.

caveats: The owner-age statistic is broad small-business evidence rather than a 541430 measure.
It does not distinguish sales from closure, family succession, recapitalization, or internal reorganization.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year 541430 transaction data from a named lower-middle-market broker or PitchBook/DealStats export, plus state business-registration ownership changes, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner-aging evidence supports a meaningful succession pipeline, but a qualifying control transfer is less common than retirement intent because agencies can wind down, lose key clients, sell only assets, or remain owner-dependent. The range is therefore below the broad share of owners nearing retirement.

records.principal_driver: A substantial portion of design production can be accelerated while recurring agency relationships may preserve demand for accountable creative and production oversight.
records.principal_weakness: The industry is fragmented and project-oriented, while AI-enabled self-service and fee repricing can erode both demand and retained savings.

caveats: Every score-driving researched primitive is a disclosed PROXY or ESTIMATE. The cited sources verify contextual anchors, but none directly identifies the numerical base values; the MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action appropriately preserve that limitation.

The disclosed lens narrows the broad NAICS to recurring or repeat outsourced-service firms with transferable operations. This is coherent for the roll-up question, but the score should not be read as applying to project-only freelancers, independent commercial artists, or the full 541430 establishment population.

The demand range converts a ten-year occupational employment projection into a five-year industry-service demand estimate. The source supports directionally offsetting forces, not the 0.85-1.03 range itself.

The succession estimate relies on broad small-business owner-age evidence rather than sector-specific completed transfer data; age does not distinguish sale from closure, family succession, recapitalization, or continued ownership.

Copyright registration constraints support a need for human-authorship review in some deliverables, but they do not demonstrate customer willingness to retain an agency or permit the agency to retain productivity savings.

caveats: a: BLS measures the customer-service occupation across employers, not the wage mix of the LMM outsourced-contact-center lens.
a: The NAICS code includes inbound and outbound programs with materially different task complexity.
rho: No industry-wide observed implementation rate exists for the frozen lens.
rho: The estimate excludes revenue and pricing effects by construction.
e: Eligibility is not a Census or BLS field.
e: The injected firm-count estimate may include firms whose EBITDA, ownership, or client model cannot be determined from public data.
s5: No authoritative observed control-transfer rate is published for eligible US LMM firms in this six-digit code.
s5: Transaction databases can omit small asset deals and cannot alone distinguish qualifying control transfers from reorganizations.
q: S4 is a global public-company comparable, not a direct sample of US LMM firms.
q: The retained share varies sharply by client concentration, program criticality, and whether automation reduces billed volume or internal cost.
d5: Employment is not constant-quality service quantity and spans employers outside NAICS 561422.
d5: The BLS forecast horizon begins in 2024 rather than the packet run date.
o: No source measures the future operator-required share for this lens.
o: Outbound consumer telemarketing and inbound customer care face different substitution and regulatory paths.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: An outsourced operator delivers recurring client programs, commonly with fees tied to minutes, hours, FTEs, transactions, or calls.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive client service departments, one-off staffing placements, software-only vendors, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US contact-center operators that provide recurring inbound customer care, outbound sales, collections, appointment, order, or related voice/contact services to external clients, and whose normalized EBITDA is in the lower-middle-market band.

caveats: BLS measures the customer-service occupation across employers, not the wage mix of the LMM outsourced-contact-center lens.
The NAICS code includes inbound and outbound programs with materially different task complexity.
a.change_evidence: A client-program-level payroll extract with agent, supervisor, QA, workforce-management, and sales wages, paired with contact-reason and containment data from a representative set of LMM operators.
a.rationale: Customer-service work includes information provision, order entry, records, billing, routing, and scripted sales, making much of agent and quality-assurance labor susceptible to AI assistance or substitution; escalations, regulated judgment, and relationship-sensitive selling remain human work. The estimate is wage-weighted for the lens rather than an asserted task count. S2 and S3 support the task characterization.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US customer service representative employment across all employers from 2,814,000 in 2024 to 2,660,300 in 2034, a 0.945 ten-year ratio. Applying that ratio proportionally for five years gives approximately 0.97, then the range allows outsourced contact-center service quantity to differ from the all-employer occupation because of client outsourcing, program mix, and automation. This proxy may overstate demand if outsourced low-complexity calls automate faster, or understate it if clients outsource more complex work.
caveats: Employment is not constant-quality service quantity and spans employers outside NAICS 561422.
The BLS forecast horizon begins in 2024 rather than the packet run date.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price US contact-center-services revenue-volume series split into inbound care, outbound sales, collections, and regulated programs, plus client program forecasts distinguishing outsourced from captive delivery.
d5.rationale: The closest national forward-looking measured quantity is the BLS customer-service occupation projection, which forecasts decline as self-service systems, social media, and mobile applications handle simple tasks. It is a proxy rather than an industry forecast and supports a near-flat but uncertain five-year service-basket view.

caveats: Eligibility is not a Census or BLS field.
The injected firm-count estimate may include firms whose EBITDA, ownership, or client model cannot be determined from public data.
e.change_evidence: A NAICS 561422 firm list enriched with ownership, client concentration, service mix, revenue, normalized EBITDA, and captive-status interviews or commercial profiles.
e.rationale: The code is directed at telemarketing bureaus and other contact centers, so external recurring service is usually consistent with the lens; the estimate discounts captive-like operations, short-lived campaigns, firms without transferable operating substance, and firms outside the required EBITDA band. S1 establishes the classification and S6 identifies the available establishment-size data, but neither observes eligibility under this lens.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source measures the future operator-required share for this lens.
Outbound consumer telemarketing and inbound customer care face different substitution and regulatory paths.
o.change_evidence: Program-level year-five forecasts that separate self-service containment from live-agent, supervisor, and outsourced-operator requirements, validated with client compliance and quality leaders.
o.rationale: Simple inquiries and transactions can move to self-service, but complex complaints, exceptions, account actions, sales conversations, and regulated telemarketing controls preserve a substantial need for an accountable operator. S2 identifies continuing complex inquiries and S3 lists exception, billing, complaint, and sales tasks; S5 shows compliance obligations that make unattended automation unsuitable for some programs.

caveats: S4 is a global public-company comparable, not a direct sample of US LMM firms.
The retained share varies sharply by client concentration, program criticality, and whether automation reduces billed volume or internal cost.
q.change_evidence: Executed LMM client contracts and renewal cohorts showing unit-price resets, minimum commitments, gain-share terms, term length, and client savings pass-through after automation.
q.rationale: Per-minute, per-hour, per-FTE, per-transaction, and per-call fee structures create a direct route for clients to request lower rates or fewer billable units after automation. Operators can retain some benefit through execution, capacity utilization, and contract timing, but recurring competitive renewals limit retention. S4 documents these billing bases at a large comparable provider.

caveats: No industry-wide observed implementation rate exists for the frozen lens.
The estimate excludes revenue and pricing effects by construction.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after AI deployment records from LMM contact-center programs showing containment, handle time, staffing, QA failures, sales conversion, client approval timelines, and audit exceptions.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should trail technical exposure because operators must integrate client knowledge, protect quality and conversion outcomes, retrain or redeploy agents, and preserve telemarketing disclosures, consent, do-not-call, and recordkeeping controls. S5 documents material operational requirements; S2 documents that technology can gradually take on more simple tasks.

caveats: No authoritative observed control-transfer rate is published for eligible US LMM firms in this six-digit code.
Transaction databases can omit small asset deals and cannot alone distinguish qualifying control transfers from reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from a business-broker database and state merger filings, reconciled to an ownership-age survey and verified deal interviews for NAICS 561422 LMM firms.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer requires both a willing owner and a transferable operation with durable client contracts. The estimate allows for ordinary small-business succession and sale activity but discounts closures, asset-only sales, internal reorganizations, and weak programs that do not transfer as operating businesses.

records.principal_driver: Large portions of routine contact handling can be redesigned around AI while keeping human escalation and accountability.
records.principal_weakness: Automation can remove the contacts and FTEs that outsourced operators bill for, while compliance and client approval constrain implementation.

caveats: The declared lens combines inbound care, outbound sales, collections, appointments, and order programs despite the packet itself acknowledging sharply different substitution and regulatory paths; marking heterogeneous=false understates that mix risk, although the record's ranges and caveats disclose much of it and no supported correction is shown to change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The q rationale invokes fewer billable units after automation while d5/o already capture surviving service quantity and operator-required share. This creates a potential boundary overlap between value capture and demand/substitution, but the evidence does not establish a corrected q that would cross the base-tier threshold, so it does not satisfy the literal materiality test.

The e rationale partly discounts firms outside the EBITDA band even though injected n is defined as the LMM-band firm count. Given n's class-average construction this may compensate for anchor imprecision, but the distinction should be explicit to avoid apparent double counting; removing that discount alone would not change the base tier or action.

The record is necessarily estimate-heavy: a, rho, e, s5, q, and o are bounded judgments, and d5 is an occupation-employment proxy for industry service quantity. The broad LOW_PRIORITY-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action appropriately communicate that limitation.

caveats: a: The only usable BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541800, which includes creative, PR, and media-representative businesses outside this lens.
a: Exposure is a task judgment, not an observed automation rate.
rho: No source measures implemented AI labor savings for the frozen six-digit lens.
rho: Implementation rates will differ sharply between programmatic specialists and relationship-led traditional-media buyers.
e: The NAICS definition identifies activity, not EBITDA, ownership independence, customer concentration, or transferability.
e: Marketplace data omit many privately negotiated sales and cannot establish eligibility for all firms.
s5: S8 uses a nonrandom convenience sample that is weighted to small employer firms and is not industry-specific.
s5: S9's transactions are voluntarily reported and do not distinguish control transfers, LMM EBITDA, or the NAICS code.
q: ANA survey respondents are marketer members and may not represent LMM agencies.
q: The sources describe compensation practices, not the discounted fraction of an AI-generated gross benefit retained by an operator.
d5: Digital ad revenue is nominal spend and includes inventory purchased without an external agency.
d5: National occupational projections are not a forecast for NAICS 541830 or a five-year constant-quality service basket.
o: The cited programmatic figures are from a 2019 ANA report and its surveyed advertisers are not a census of LMM customers.
o: No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share for the frozen lens.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers buy recurring campaign-management and media-buying services through fees, retainers, project scopes, commissions, or combinations; media spend itself is typically a client pass-through rather than agency revenue retained as operating value.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive advertiser buying teams, media-owner sales representatives, full-service creative agencies whose buying is incidental, shell entities, and financing vehicles are excluded.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market agencies that repeatedly buy or manage advertising time or space for external advertiser or agency customers, including planning, trafficking, optimization, reporting, and negotiated media purchasing.

a.bridge: S2 measures the broader NAICS 541800 workforce rather than six-digit media buyers. I use its occupation and wage mix to judge the wage-weighted task exposure of 541830, placing more weight on its directly relevant market-research, project-management, purchasing, sales, and administrative work and less on creative-production roles; this likely overstates exposure if six-digit work is unusually negotiation- and relationship-intensive, and understates it if routine trafficking and reporting dominate.
caveats: The only usable BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541800, which includes creative, PR, and media-representative businesses outside this lens.
Exposure is a task judgment, not an observed automation rate.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage cross-tab, time-and-motion studies from independent media agencies, and anonymized workflow logs separating trafficking, reporting, optimization, negotiation, and account-management hours.
a.rationale: The broader advertising-services workforce has 16.77% management, 16.65% business and financial operations, 24.48% arts/design/media, 13.28% sales, and 11.22% office support. Market research analysts and marketing specialists alone are 8.15% of that workforce. Generative analysis, reporting, planning support, QA, documentation, and administrative work are materially exposed, while client stewardship, media negotiations, accountability, and judgment over brand suitability limit substitution.

d5.bridge: S7 measures nominal U.S. internet advertising revenue, while S3 and S4 measure national occupation employment projections, rather than constant-price, constant-quality quantity demand for the current 541830 service basket. I use the strong current digital-ad-spend signal and 6–7% decade occupational growth indications as directional evidence, then discount for price inflation, media-mix shifts, platform disintermediation, and the fact that spend growth need not produce proportional outsourced buying demand; this can overstate demand if spend moves in-house and understate it if fragmented media expands agency need.
caveats: Digital ad revenue is nominal spend and includes inventory purchased without an external agency.
National occupational projections are not a forecast for NAICS 541830 or a five-year constant-quality service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar U.S. advertising-expenditure forecasts by channel; agency fee-revenue and client-retention cohorts; platform data on advertiser self-service adoption; and a longitudinal survey of outsourced-versus-in-house media buying by advertiser size.
d5.rationale: Internet advertising revenue reached $258.6 billion in 2024, up 14.9% year over year; market research analysts and marketing specialists are projected to grow 7% over 2024–34, and advertising, promotions, and marketing managers 6%. The evidence supports continuing commercial activity but does not establish a real-volume forecast for independent media buyers, so the base is only modestly above current demand.

caveats: The NAICS definition identifies activity, not EBITDA, ownership independence, customer concentration, or transferability.
Marketplace data omit many privately negotiated sales and cannot establish eligibility for all firms.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level ownership and revenue-concentration data from PitchBook, Capital IQ, or Axial; CIMs from media-agency brokers; and interviews with transaction advisors on buyer exclusions for small agencies.
e.rationale: The code definition is directly aligned with outsourced media buying, so many LMM firms should meet the service criterion. Eligibility is reduced for firms that are effectively founder-dependent, tied to a single client or platform, lack transferable operating controls, or are establishments inside networks rather than stand-alone control-transfer targets.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541830 ($2,332,376,359) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541830 ($9,013,654,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The cited programmatic figures are from a 2019 ANA report and its surveyed advertisers are not a census of LMM customers.
No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share for the frozen lens.
o.change_evidence: Advertiser surveys tracking in-house media buying, DSP and platform self-service penetration, agency client-retention cohorts, and contract data on accountability for brand safety, measurement, and platform execution.
o.rationale: The core defined service is purchasing media for clients, and fee-based agencies remain responsible for outcomes, transparency, and contract performance. That supports persistent accountable-operator demand. Offsetting this, ANA reports programmatic use by 91% of respondents and says 35% use another third party or handle some programmatic buying in-house, showing that a material portion can shift to platforms or client teams.

caveats: ANA survey respondents are marketer members and may not represent LMM agencies.
The sources describe compensation practices, not the discounted fraction of an AI-generated gross benefit retained by an operator.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of actual media-agency MSAs, fee schedules, renewal amendments, and client procurement interviews showing whether productivity gains change fees, staffing commitments, commission economics, or scope.
q.rationale: Fee-based, labor-based, retainer, and fixed-output structures let an operator retain some workflow savings, especially before renewal. However, clients can demand lower fees or more output at renewal, and media-buying contracts are scrutinized for transparency: 69% of ANA respondents had updated contracts within three years and 60% said rebates or discounts were returned to marketers. Programmatic and digital commissions also create different pass-through incentives. These facts support partial, not full, retention.

caveats: No source measures implemented AI labor savings for the frozen six-digit lens.
Implementation rates will differ sharply between programmatic specialists and relationship-led traditional-media buyers.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after staffing and throughput data from comparable agencies; advertiser contract permissions for AI-assisted work; platform audit requirements; and client interviews on review, privacy, and brand-safety constraints.
rho.rationale: Within five years, agencies can implement AI in reporting, audience research, campaign setup support, reconciliation, and internal knowledge work, but implementation is constrained by client approvals, platform-specific controls, measurement quality, data access, privacy, brand safety, and the need to retain accountable buyers. The range is deliberately below full technical exposure.

s5.bridge: S8 measures age among all U.S. employer-firm primary owners and S9 measures voluntarily reported small-business marketplace activity across sectors, not qualifying control transfers of 541830 LMM firms. I translate the broad owner-retirement pipeline and observed active small-business transaction market into a conservative five-year probability; the proxy may overstate transfers for founder-dependent agencies and understate them for brokered professional-services firms.
caveats: S8 uses a nonrandom convenience sample that is weighted to small employer firms and is not industry-specific.
S9's transactions are voluntarily reported and do not distinguish control transfers, LMM EBITDA, or the NAICS code.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed six-digit media-agency transactions from GF Data, PitchBook, or S&P Capital IQ; SBA loan data coded to this NAICS; owner-age and succession-intent microdata for advertising-services firms; and broker interviews with completed versus failed agency sale processes.
s5.rationale: In the Federal Reserve employer-firm sample, 33% of primary owners are 55–64 and 22% are 65 or older, indicating a meaningful succession pipeline but not an intent-to-sell rate. BizBuySell reports closed transactions submitted voluntarily by brokers across 65 industries, confirming an active but selective small-business sale channel. Agency transferability, client concentration, and platform dependence justify a modest base probability rather than equating older ownership with sale.

records.principal_driver: Repeatable analytical, operational, and administrative media-buying workflows create a bounded but material opportunity for AI-assisted labor reduction.
records.principal_weakness: Platform self-service and client procurement can both reduce outsourced demand and pass agency productivity gains back to customers.

caveats: S2's rationale calls 13.28% the broader-industry sales share, but that percentage is only Sales Representatives, Services; the Sales and Related major group is 18.41%. This is a descriptive imprecision in an already disclosed four-digit proxy, and correcting it does not establish a different a value or change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The operational lens includes agencies that manage media buying and treats media spend as a client pass-through, while S1's strict 541830 definition is purchase and resale of time or space. Agency-as-agent activities may overlap adjacent advertising-agency classifications. The possible effect on a, e, q, d5, and o is directionally ambiguous, and the record's explicit estimates and disclosed adjacent-activity exclusions do not demonstrate a correction that would change the base tier or action.

The score rests heavily on disclosed judgment: rho, e, q, and o are ESTIMATE; a uses four-digit occupational data; s5 uses cross-industry owner and marketplace evidence; and d5 uses nominal digital spend plus national occupation projections. These limitations justify MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS rather than rejection.

S6 is a 2019 advertiser-member survey and therefore may not represent current lower-middle-market customer behavior. The age and population are disclosed, and no supported correction is shown to change the base tier or action.

rho: AI policy, client approval requirements, and the inventory systems of media owners can vary materially by firm.

e: The supplied firm-count input is not re-estimated here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The customer is a media owner; the representative is commonly compensated through commissions or other performance-linked revenue tied to selling the owner's inventory, with recurring account relationships and campaign renewals where present.
lens.excluded_activities: Media-owner captive sales teams, media buying agencies, advertising agencies, software-only ad-placement products, non-control investments, and representatives whose work is solely a one-off transaction without a repeat outsourced-service relationship.
lens.included_activities: Independent representatives that sell media time or space for media owners and provide repeat account management, prospecting, proposal, contract, and campaign-support work to external media-owner customers.

a.bridge: S1 measures tasks for advertising sales agents across employing industries, not wage-weighted work inside NAICS 541840. I mapped its documented proposals, account paperwork, cost estimates, research, and sales-material work to AI-assisted drafting, research, CRM, and administrative automation, while retaining relationship building, persuasion, negotiation, and accountable advice as less substitutable. This likely overstates exposure if independent representatives spend more time in relationship-led selling, and understates it if their back office is unusually manual.
caveats: The occupation includes employees outside the target NAICS and omits proprietor labor.
No observed wage-weighted task allocation is available for the frozen lens.
a.change_evidence: A representative sample time-and-motion study of independent media representatives, or payroll-by-SOC data for NAICS 541840 combined with task diaries, would permit wage weighting and materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: The occupation contains substantial information, proposal, documentation, cost-estimation, and account-processing work, but also continuous persuasion, negotiation, client rapport, and judgment. The range reflects an unmeasured NAICS-specific occupation and wage mix.

d5.bridge: S2 projects total US advertising-sales-agent employment, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541840 services. I translate the documented occupation decline and its stated drivers into a five-year demand proxy, allowing a wider range because employment also reflects productivity and the NAICS excludes some employer types. The occupation projection may overstate contraction of durable outsourced demand if AI raises productivity without reducing service quantity, and may understate it if self-service placement displaces representatives faster than projected.
caveats: Employment is not equivalent to constant-price service quantity.
The BLS projection spans a longer interval than the frozen horizon and covers a broader occupation.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted NAICS 541840 receipts, media-owner outsourcing spend, and renewal-volume cohorts by channel would directly measure the current service basket and materially update this proxy.
d5.rationale: BLS expects fewer advertising sales agents as print circulation falls and digital placement automates, while also stating that digital advertising continues to grow and agents remain needed. That supports broadly flat to modestly declining demand rather than a single observed NAICS demand forecast.

caveats: The classification definition identifies activity, not recurring revenue, size-adjusted profitability, or control eligibility.
The supplied firm-count input is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A NAICS 541840 firm survey or a commercial company database review that identifies client concentration, contract renewal, ownership, and normalized EBITDA would directly test lens eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition is independent representation for media owners, which normally is an external service and can recur across inventory sales and account renewals. The discount allows for establishments with episodic representation, insufficient operating depth, or revenue arrangements that do not meet the frozen lens.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: S2 states that digital ad placement can be automated but that agents are still needed in digital media, and S1 documents relationship, persuasion, negotiation, and client-advice tasks. Neither source measures the future share of NAICS 541840 service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I infer that automation removes a meaningful share of transactional placement but leaves operator work for relationship management, selling, and complex inventory; this may overstate durability if self-serve platforms displace account work and understate it for specialized media inventory.
caveats: The result depends heavily on media channel, inventory complexity, and whether the media owner preserves third-party representation.
No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the exact lens.
o.change_evidence: Channel-level media-owner data comparing self-serve inventory, managed-service revenue, and representative-managed renewal cohorts would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Automated placement directly challenges the transactional core of the service, but the source evidence indicates continued need for agents and documents customer-facing, negotiated, and advisory work that software alone may not supply.

q.bridge: S2 reports that advertising-sales-agent compensation commonly includes commissions and bonuses linked to sales, but it does not measure contractual benefit retention for independent media representatives. I use performance-linked compensation and monthly sales pressure as evidence that efficiency gains face commission, customer, and competitive pass-through; the resulting retained-benefit range is a judgment. The proxy likely understates firm-level retention where contracts have fixed commissions and overstates it where sellers or media owners capture the gain.
caveats: BLS evidence concerns workers across the occupation rather than independent firms under the lens.
Contracts may be fixed-fee, commission, salary-plus-commission, or negotiated individually.
q.change_evidence: Executed representative agreements, commission schedules, renewal-price histories, and customer-switching data from a sample of target firms would directly measure retention.
q.rationale: Commission-linked selling and competitive quotas reduce confidence that a representative can retain all labor savings. Recurring account knowledge and service quality can preserve part of the benefit, particularly when savings improve coverage rather than reduce customer-facing effort.

caveats: This is a judgment about realized operational adoption rather than a measured adoption rate.
AI policy, client approval requirements, and the inventory systems of media owners can vary materially by firm.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation evidence from independent media-representative firms, including before-and-after staffing, CRM adoption logs, error rates, and customer-retention cohorts, would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Drafting, research, CRM updates, and administrative work can be operationalized with review controls, but proposals, pricing, client-specific claims, and contract commitments need human approval. The relationship-led portion also limits workflow removal within the horizon.

caveats: This is not an observed sale probability.
Owner dependence, agreement assignability, and customer concentration can create wider variation than the aggregate range conveys.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data and lost-deal reasons from named lower-middle-market brokers, plus owner-age and succession responses in a NAICS 541840 survey, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A small specialist representation firm can be transferable when accounts, media-owner agreements, sales processes, and management depth survive the owner, but personal relationships and commission-dependent revenue make many firms less transferable. No observed NAICS-specific control-transfer rate was located.

records.principal_driver: AI can reduce the proposal, research, documentation, and account-administration burden while preserving customer-facing sales coverage.
records.principal_weakness: Self-service digital placement and owner-dependent relationships can erode both service demand and transferability.

caveats: S1 and S2 describe the broad advertising-sales-agent occupation across multiple employing industries, not proprietor-inclusive work within independent NAICS 541840 firms. The record discloses this bridge and uses PROXY or ESTIMATE states, but a, rho, and o remain sensitive to an unobserved target-industry task mix.

S2's worker compensation and quota evidence does not directly measure the share of AI-enabled benefit retained by an independent media-representative firm. The q range is therefore a broad, explicitly bridged judgment rather than observed contract economics.

The d5 bridge translates a ten-year occupation-employment projection into five-year constant-price service demand. Employment, productivity, channel mix, and outsourced-service quantity can diverge, although the record states that limitation and supplies a wide range.

S3 establishes the industry boundary but does not establish that 80 percent of LMM-band firms meet the recurring-service, profitability, and control-eligibility lens. The e input is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and caveated.

The transferability input s5 has no direct transaction evidence and is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE; owner dependence, agreement assignability, and customer concentration could produce substantial firm-level dispersion beyond the aggregate base.

caveats: a: No published occupation mix exists for six-digit NAICS 541490 or the narrowed repeat-service lens.
a: The estimate excludes already automated work and does not assume generated output is commercially acceptable.
rho: This is an implementation judgment, not an AI-capability forecast.
rho: Small studios may lack integration capacity, while standardized high-volume work may implement faster.
e: The injected firm count is not re-estimated here.
e: NAICS classification does not identify repeat revenue, control eligibility, or whether design is sold separately from goods.
s5: Owner age is only a potential succession indicator; it does not establish sale probability.
s5: The estimate excludes closures without a transferable operating business and internal reorganizations.
q: This is a commercial-retention judgment and excludes demand-volume effects and implementation constraints.
q: Revenue-model shares are unavailable for the narrowed lens.
d5: Employment is not constant-price service quantity and is national rather than lens-specific.
d5: The projection covers fashion designers, not all included specialized-design activities.
o: The boundary between software-assisted service and self-service may change quickly.
o: This quantity judgment is separate from labor implementation and client price capture.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External manufacturers, brands, retailers, and wholesalers buy fixed-fee, retainer, or repeat project design, specification, material-selection, and development services.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because NAICS 541490 combines recurring product-design studios with episodic float and costume work; the latter does not share a repeat outsourced-service revenue model.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off event float and costume work, independent theatrical designers, manufacturers selling their own goods, captive design teams, nonrepeat consumer commissions, shells, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US outsourced fashion, footwear, jewelry, textile, and related product-design studios serving external business customers on repeat seasonal, collection, or product-development engagements.

caveats: No published occupation mix exists for six-digit NAICS 541490 or the narrowed repeat-service lens.
The estimate excludes already automated work and does not assume generated output is commercially acceptable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion survey of the included studios, paired with payroll occupation codes and workflow-level generative-AI use logs.
a.rationale: The included work contains AI-exposed concept exploration, reference research, rough illustration, specifications, documentation, costing support, and coordination. O*NET also shows client discussion, material selection, sample review, testing, and artistic judgment, which limit substitution. The specialized-design sector occupation mix includes management, sales, project-management, office, and market-research roles in addition to designers.

d5.bridge: BLS measures US employment of fashion designers, an occupation rather than demand for the narrowed NAICS 541490 external-service basket. Its 2 percent 2024–2034 projection is transformed to an approximately 1 percent five-year quantity proxy. It may overstate service demand when firms internalize or automate work, and may understate demand for adjacent footwear, jewelry, textile, and repeat product-development services.
caveats: Employment is not constant-price service quantity and is national rather than lens-specific.
The projection covers fashion designers, not all included specialized-design activities.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price revenue or unit-demand series for independent fashion, footwear, jewelry, and textile design studios, separated from manufacturing and internal design labor.
d5.rationale: BLS projects fashion-designer employment growth of 2 percent from 2024 to 2034. That near-flat outlook supports a central constant-quality demand ratio close to one, with downside for reduced outsourcing and upside for repeat collection and product-development needs.

caveats: The injected firm count is not re-estimated here.
NAICS classification does not identify repeat revenue, control eligibility, or whether design is sold separately from goods.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 541490 prospect list with revenue, employee count, ownership, customer concentration, and repeat-revenue fields from business-information vendors and management interviews.
e.rationale: Eligibility is limited to firms in the injected LMM band that actually deliver repeat external outsourced services. The NAICS definition includes several relevant design categories, but it also includes activities and ownership structures outside the lens.

This quantity judgment is separate from labor implementation and client price capture.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and renewal analysis testing willingness to buy AI-enabled operator-led design versus self-service tools, plus usage data on vendor-supplied design platforms.
o.rationale: Clients still require accountable operators to translate brand direction, select materials, validate samples, make production-aware tradeoffs, and coordinate approvals. Some routine concept, research, and visualization work can instead be self-served or supplied through software.

caveats: This is a commercial-retention judgment and excludes demand-volume effects and implementation constraints.
Revenue-model shares are unavailable for the narrowed lens.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, renewal histories, statement-of-work pricing, and win-loss interviews that identify fixed-fee, retainer, and time-based revenue shares.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee and retainer work can initially retain some efficiency gains, but repeat clients and competitive studios can demand lower fees, larger deliverables, or faster turnarounds. Hourly and project billing also make pass-through and renewal repricing material.

caveats: This is an implementation judgment, not an AI-capability forecast.
Small studios may lack integration capacity, while standardized high-volume work may implement faster.
rho.change_evidence: Pilot results from comparable fashion, footwear, jewelry, and textile studios showing task-level cycle time, rework, quality acceptance, and headcount effects after adoption.
rho.rationale: Over five years, studios can implement AI for ideation, research, briefs, administrative support, and first-pass visual or specification work, but client approval, brand originality, fit, material behavior, production constraints, and quality review retain human workflow gates.

caveats: Owner age is only a potential succession indicator; it does not establish sale probability.
The estimate excludes closures without a transferable operating business and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed lower-middle-market transactions and quality-of-earnings records for NAICS 541490, supplemented by owner-age and succession-intent interviews with eligible firms.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment because no observed transaction series isolates eligible LMM firms in this six-digit industry. Census makes owner-age tabulations obtainable for employer firms, but the published ABS owner-characteristic table is not a control-transfer measure.

records.principal_driver: AI can reduce effort in repeatable research, visualization, specification, and coordination around operator-led product design.
records.principal_weakness: Observed evidence does not isolate transferable repeat-service firms or their demand, pricing, and transaction behavior within NAICS 541490.

caveats: The disclosed lens selects repeat outsourced product-design studios from a heterogeneous six-digit code, but no cited evidence measures the prevalence, firm count, or revenue model of that subset; e and q therefore remain consequential bounded estimates rather than population observations.

S2 is occupation-level evidence for fashion designers and S3 is industry-group evidence for all Specialized Design Services, so neither directly measures the narrowed 541490 studio workforce used to motivate a, rho, and o.
The d5 bridge is directionally coherent but converts a national occupation employment projection into demand for an external-service basket; the broad disclosed range and PROXY label are necessary.
The record is estimate-heavy across H, F, C, and D, producing a LOW_PRIORITY-to-PRIORITY interval. Its MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action accurately restrict the conclusion.
