n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $134,841 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541940); class <5: 8,547 firms, avg revenue/firm $415,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,071 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,573 firms, avg revenue/firm $930,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $145,689 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 7,178 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,671,661 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $261,615 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3,831 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,892,081 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $609,111 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 133 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,557,188 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,156,200 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,440,955 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $82,075,009 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 133

caveats: The estimate does not separately model companion, livestock, mobile, testing, and specialty providers.
Software-assisted service can still reduce operator time without eliminating the accountable operator.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state scope-of-practice changes, telemedicine rules, and audited evidence on autonomous triage, prescribing, or home-testing substitution by service category would materially alter this share.
o.rationale: The current basket centers on animal examination, diagnosis, treatment, surgery, testing, vaccination, and client counseling. BLS describes veterinarians as performing these medical functions, and O*NET records hands-on, close-proximity technician work with serious consequences of error. Software can absorb administrative tasks and assist decisions, but most service quantity should still require an accountable veterinary operator over the horizon.

caveats: The AVMA revenue categories do not measure contractual pass-through or the incidence of AI savings.
Retention can differ substantially by companion, food-animal, equine, emergency, and specialty practice type.
q.change_evidence: Longitudinal practice-level data linking AI workflow deployment to labor hours, service fees, visit volume, compensation, and retention would materially change the retained-benefit estimate.
q.rationale: Practices principally sell discrete veterinary services directly to external customers rather than operating on a cost-plus contract. AVMA reports veterinary services as the largest revenue category and reports owners spending most of their time with clients and patients, supporting scope to retain workflow savings through capacity, service quality, and operational leverage. Local price competition, wage pressure, customer fee sensitivity, and eventual reinvestment in service temper retention.

caveats: Technology enthusiasm is not measured deployment or sustained productivity.
The AVMA practice-owner sample may not represent the lower-middle-market subset.
rho.change_evidence: A current AVMA or VHMA survey measuring production use of specific AI workflows, together with adoption and quality metrics from veterinary practice-management-system vendors, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Administrative and documentation tools can be deployed without replacing clinical judgment, but implementation is limited by practice-management-system integration, record quality, privacy, medical-error liability, workflow redesign, and owner willingness. AVMA reports that 40.8% of surveyed practice owners were enthusiastic about new technology, leaving most respondents hesitant or skeptical; that supports partial rather than universal operational realization within five years.

s5.bridge: AAHA reports a Packaged Facts projection that 30% of veterinary practices would be put up for sale during the 2018 to 2028 period. I translate that ten-year listing projection into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability for eligible lower-middle-market firms, with a lower base because a listing is not necessarily a completed control transfer. This proxy likely overstates completed transfers, although unlisted succession and corporate transactions could offset part of that bias.
caveats: The cited projection concerns practices put up for sale rather than closed transactions.
The cited source discusses general and specialty practice markets, whereas the lens covers all eligible lower-middle-market veterinary services.
The underlying Packaged Facts projection predates the later slowdown in consolidation described by AAHA.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction database with closed dates, control acquired, revenue or EBITDA band, practice type, and geography from Ackerman Group, Antelligence, PitchBook, or an AVMA owner survey would materially revise this probability.
s5.rationale: Veterinary practice succession and consolidation create a credible transfer channel, but the proxy is deliberately discounted for failed sales, owners who remain employed after a transaction, and firms that are too small or geographically unattractive to transfer as operating businesses.

records.principal_driver: Repeat, clinically necessary animal-health services remain dependent on accountable operators while administrative workflow is amenable to AI assistance.
records.principal_weakness: No direct industry evidence measures realized AI labor savings or completed lower-middle-market control transfers, so both are bounded judgments or proxies.

caveats: The a estimate is a wage-weighted task judgment rather than a measured veterinary AI-exposure statistic; the occupational sources establish the work mix and task content but not the 0.28 base.
The rho estimate uses general enthusiasm toward new technology as contextual evidence, not direct evidence of AI deployment or sustained productivity in lower-middle-market practices.

The q source establishes direct veterinary-service revenue and owner time use but does not measure incidence or retention of AI savings; moreover, the entire registered q range maps to the C-factor cap of 10, so it expresses no factor-level sensitivity. This cannot plausibly change the current CONDITIONAL base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action under the registered score geometry.

The s5 proxy rests on an old ten-year projection of practices listed for sale rather than completed qualifying control transfers; the packet discloses and discounts that bridge, but direct lower-middle-market transaction evidence remains absent.

The d5 proxy converts veterinarian employment growth into constant-price, constant-quality service quantity even though employment also reflects productivity and occupational demand outside NAICS 541940; the mismatch is disclosed and the evidence state is appropriately PROXY.

The lens spans companion-animal, equine, food-animal, mixed, specialty, and testing businesses despite marking heterogeneity false. The narrative and primitive caveats acknowledge these differences, so this is a presentation caveat rather than undisclosed favorable lens selection.

caveats: a: No public source located measures AI-exposed task share or occupation wages specifically for the frozen LMM lens.
a: The industry contains both labor-intensive detailing and low-attendance self-service formats.
rho: This is a bounded judgment, not a measured AI adoption rate.
rho: Existing automatic and self-service formats may already have captured some non-AI labor reduction.
e: No source gives the eligibility share inside the injected LMM firm count.
e: A car wash located at a fuel retailer may be classified elsewhere when it is not the establishment's primary activity.
s5: S5 is not car-wash-specific in its headline transaction sample and its reported typical financials are below the frozen EBITDA band.
s5: S7 is a large platform transaction, so it is evidence of deal flow rather than a denominator-based small-firm transfer rate.
q: Member renewal intent is survey evidence, not realized retention or a direct measure of savings capture.
q: Value capture differs sharply by local density, real-estate constraints, wash quality, and mix of membership versus retail revenue.
d5: VMT is national and does not capture the local micro-markets where car washes compete.
d5: The forecast is a travel measure rather than a direct forecast of washes, revenue, or price-adjusted car-wash demand.
o: No public source measures the share of future car-wash quantity requiring a separately accountable operator.
o: Operator-required does not mean a staffed attendant must be present for every wash.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer and small-fleet vehicle owners buy individual washes, detailing, or self-service time; many automatic and tunnel operators also bill monthly unlimited-wash memberships.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive dealership, fleet, rental-car, or gasoline-station wash units not operated as a separately transferable service business; holding companies; and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators of automatic, tunnel, hand-wash, detailing, mobile, and self-service car-wash sites that sell washing or cleaning services to external vehicle owners and have repeat or membership revenue where applicable.

a.bridge: S2 measures tasks for the SOC occupation Cleaners of Vehicles and Equipment, not the wage-weighted employment mix of NAICS 811192. S1 confirms this industry also contains automatic and self-service production modes. I translate the documented manual cleaning, inspection, machine-monitoring, inventory, and information tasks into a modest wage-weighted AI opportunity, counting customer communication, membership administration, scheduling, sales coaching, and reporting but not physical wash, drying, detailing, safety, or equipment repair. The proxy can overstate exposure at labor-heavy hand washes and understate it at digitally managed tunnel platforms.
caveats: No public source located measures AI-exposed task share or occupation wages specifically for the frozen LMM lens.
The industry contains both labor-intensive detailing and low-attendance self-service formats.
a.change_evidence: A 2026 NAICS 811192 occupational staffing and wage file, plus site-level time-and-motion studies separating attendant, sales, manager, and back-office payroll, would materially change the wage weighting.
a.rationale: The core occupation's listed work is predominantly physical cleaning, drying, spraying, moving, and machine operation, which limits AI substitution. Information and workflow work around memberships, customer service, exception handling, staffing, and reporting is exposed, but it is a minority of current labor cost.

d5.bridge: S6 forecasts national light-duty VMT, rather than constant-price demand for professional car-wash services. I use its 0.5 percent annual long-run VMT growth as a baseline directional driver of the number of vehicles and use occasions, then allow for weather, consumer discretionary spending, home washing, local saturation, and visit frequency. VMT can overstate wash demand if trips do not increase cleaning frequency.
caveats: VMT is national and does not capture the local micro-markets where car washes compete.
The forecast is a travel measure rather than a direct forecast of washes, revenue, or price-adjusted car-wash demand.
d5.change_evidence: Monthly same-store paid-wash volumes from a broad operator panel, regional weather-normalized wash counts, and consumer survey data on professional versus home-wash frequency would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: FHWA projects moderate growth in light-duty travel, which supports a modest increase in the addressable use base over five years. That transport indicator is not sufficient to assume equal car-wash volume growth, so the central estimate stays near flat and the downside permits a service-quantity decline.

caveats: No source gives the eligibility share inside the injected LMM firm count.
A car wash located at a fuel retailer may be classified elsewhere when it is not the establishment's primary activity.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 811192 census with ownership links, customer mix, site lease status, and recurring-revenue flags would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is itself an external vehicle-cleaning service, and its listed products include automatic, hand, self-service, and detailing activity. Most LMM firms therefore fit the external-customer and repeat-service lens. The deduction allows for captive-like operations, one-off detail specialists, thinly staffed self-service assets, and entities that cannot transfer operating control independently of a parent or site owner.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 811192 ($6,105,051,106) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 811192 ($14,968,891,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $89,975 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 811192); class <5: 9,362 firms, avg revenue/firm $236,758 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,053 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,475 firms, avg revenue/firm $711,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $111,287 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,148,854 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $179,796 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,542 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,320,900 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $363,221 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,254,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,604,829 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $59,966,484 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,384,755 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 186

caveats: No public source measures the share of future car-wash quantity requiring a separately accountable operator.
Operator-required does not mean a staffed attendant must be present for every wash.
o.change_evidence: A format-level count of automated, self-service, mobile, and full-service sites; operator interviews on remote supervision; and consumer substitution surveys would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: The service requires physical water, chemistry, equipment, facilities, and accountability for vehicle handling and quality. S1 confirms that automatic and self-service options already coexist with hand work, so an accountable operator can serve demand with less on-site labor. The deduction reflects customers washing at home, self-service displacement of full-service activity, mobile alternatives, and further unattended-format adoption; software alone cannot supply the physical wash basket.

caveats: Member renewal intent is survey evidence, not realized retention or a direct measure of savings capture.
Value capture differs sharply by local density, real-estate constraints, wash quality, and mix of membership versus retail revenue.
q.change_evidence: Site-level membership contracts, price-change and churn panels, competitor-radius data, and before-and-after margins from operators that deployed comparable automation would materially change this retained-share estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed monthly memberships and individual-service pricing give the operator a route to keep part of implemented workflow savings rather than immediately passing them through as billed labor. ICA reports that nearly nine in ten members plan to renew and nearly eight in ten customers would accept a price increase when value and quality hold. Rinsed reports member revenue growth alongside retail revenue decline, reinforcing the shift to operator-controlled recurring billing. Retention is limited by the same ICA evidence that identifies rising competitive density and saturation.

caveats: This is a bounded judgment, not a measured AI adoption rate.
Existing automatic and self-service formats may already have captured some non-AI labor reduction.
rho.change_evidence: Interviews with operators owning several LMM wash sites, payroll records before and after membership, POS, or AI-agent deployments, and vendor implementation cohorts with realized labor hours would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful for centralized customer messaging, membership recovery, lead handling, scheduling, pricing support, training, and manager reporting. It is constrained because the physical cleaning, quality control, safety, damage handling, and equipment-maintenance work documented for the core occupation remain operationally necessary, and multi-site software deployment does not by itself remove attendants.

s5.bridge: S5 records completed marketplace sales for a broader automotive-service population and reports transaction benchmarks, while S7 documents a large car-wash acquisition. Neither source measures five-year transfer incidence for eligible NAICS 811192 firms in the injected EBITDA band. I use them only to establish that arm's-length operating-business transfers occur, then apply a conservative bounded conversion to the frozen population; marketplace reporting favors firms actively offered for sale and may therefore overstate incidence, whereas unreported sponsor and bilateral transactions may understate it.
caveats: S5 is not car-wash-specific in its headline transaction sample and its reported typical financials are below the frozen EBITDA band.
S7 is a large platform transaction, so it is evidence of deal flow rather than a denominator-based small-firm transfer rate.
s5.change_evidence: Census Annual Business Survey owner-age microdata tabulated for 811192, transaction records from PitchBook or GF Data filtered to relevant EBITDA, SBA change-of-ownership loan records, and brokered closing data with seller age would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: There is clear, current car-wash consolidation activity and a functioning small-business sale channel, but no owner-age or LMM transfer-rate dataset specific to this industry. The estimate remains below a high-turnover assumption because qualifying transfers exclude closures, passive real-estate trades, internal restructurings, and assets that lack transferable operations.

records.principal_driver: Recurring membership billing can make centrally managed customer, sales, and administrative workflow improvements economically useful across multiple sites.
records.principal_weakness: Most of the core service is physical and the sector's mixed formats and local saturation make labor opportunity, demand, and transferability highly site-specific.

caveats: The s5 range is only weakly anchored: S5 is a broader, smaller-business marketplace sample and S7 is one large platform deal, so neither supplies a car-wash denominator or five-year transfer incidence. The bridge discloses this and the value is best read as bounded judgment; changing its evidence label alone would not change the MODEL_CONDITIONED action, and a plausible correction within the registered range does not change the base LOW_PRIORITY tier.

The q sources measure renewal intent, price tolerance, and membership-versus-retail revenue trends rather than the fraction of AI-enabled savings retained by an operator. The ESTIMATE label is honest, but C's capped base score should not be read as directly observed pricing power.

The a proxy relies on one broad vehicle-and-equipment-cleaner occupation rather than the wage-weighted employment mix of 811192, while the included formats range from hand detailing to unattended self-service. The packet discloses both limitations and uses a wide H range, but site-format diligence is essential.

The lens marks the industry as non-heterogeneous even though the narrative correctly emphasizes materially different labor and operating models across detailing, hand-wash, tunnel, mobile, and self-service formats. This metadata inconsistency does not alter the inclusive default lens, the score, or the action.

sources.supports: Defines washing and cleaning as services that may use automatic machines, manual labor, self-service facilities, or a combination; identifies automatic washing, hand washing, self-service washing, and self-service vacuuming as products produced by NAICS 811192.

sources.supports: Reports that nearly eight in ten customers would accept a price increase subject to value and quality, nearly 90 percent of members plan to renew, and saturation and competitive density are leading concerns.

sources.supports: Reports 5.2 percent year-over-year combined same-location member and retail revenue growth, 13.2 percent member-revenue growth, 7.1 percent retail-revenue decline, and average monthly member revenue of 30 dollars.

sources.supports: Projects national light-duty vehicle miles traveled to grow at an average annual rate of 0.5 percent through 2053, with 0.6 percent annual growth from 2023 through 2043.

caveats: a: REALTOR membership is not identical to all NAICS 531210 firms.
a: The provided wage-based labor input does not directly observe owner-agent commissions or self-employed labor.
rho: The technology survey is self-reported and membership-based.
rho: Firm software provision does not establish that agents use the tools consistently or that a workflow is compliant.
e: Office count and commission volume are not EBITDA.
e: The NAR firm survey may not represent nonmember brokerages or all NAICS establishments.
s5: Firm age is not owner age or sale intent.
s5: No source located measures five-year control-transfer probability for LMM brokerages, and closures are excluded from the construct.
q: The sources establish negotiability and market structure, not the retained share of AI benefit.
q: Commission arrangements vary materially by local market, franchise contract, residential versus commercial work, and agent split.
d5: Occupation projections span work outside NAICS 531210 and do not equal service quantity.
d5: Constant-quality demand is especially difficult to separate from home-price movements and commission-rate changes.
o: The consumer survey is residential and recent-transaction based, whereas this NAICS includes commercial and rental brokerage.
o: Use of an agent today can reflect norms and financing practices as well as irreplaceable operator need.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat or episodic outsourced representation for buyers, sellers, landlords, and tenants, normally paid through negotiated transaction, lease, or referral commissions rather than cost-plus billing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house sales units of developers or owners, listing platforms and lead-generation businesses that do not provide brokerage representation, property managers, title and settlement businesses, and passive referral shells.
lens.included_activities: Independent and franchised brokerage firms providing residential, commercial, or rental representation, listing, marketing, transaction coordination, and negotiation to external customers.

a.bridge: S1 describes broker and agent duties, including lead solicitation, pricing advice, listings, promotion, showings, offers, local-market knowledge, and legal compliance; S2 measures AI-tool use among REALTOR members rather than wage-weighted task exposure in NAICS 531210. I map text-heavy listing, marketing, document, search, CRM, and first-response work to substitutable or hiring-avoidable work, while treating showings, negotiation, relationship work, local judgment, and licensed accountability as largely non-substitutable. The proxy likely overstates exposure if self-employed agents already absorb low-value tasks without payroll, and may understate administrative-work automation at larger offices.
caveats: REALTOR membership is not identical to all NAICS 531210 firms.
The provided wage-based labor input does not directly observe owner-agent commissions or self-employed labor.
a.change_evidence: A NAICS 531210 time-and-motion study or payroll/contractor-task ledger segmented by broker, agent, and transaction coordinator; interviews with LMM brokerage operators that quantify hours by listing, lead, compliance, showing, and negotiation workflow.
a.rationale: The documented duty mix contains material drafting, information retrieval, marketing, and coordination work, but the core service also depends on in-person showings, negotiation, local knowledge, and licensed judgment. The frozen compensation ratio may understate total labor economics because BLS reports that 54% of brokers and sales agents are self-employed.

d5.bridge: S1 projects national employment for real estate brokers and sales agents to grow 3% from 2024 to 2034, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the NAICS 531210 service basket. I convert the ten-year occupation projection into an approximately half-horizon central demand proxy and widen it for housing-cycle, rate, and commercial-property uncertainty. The proxy may understate demand if productivity allows transaction volume to grow faster than employment, and may overstate it if automation decouples employment from service demand.
caveats: Occupation projections span work outside NAICS 531210 and do not equal service quantity.
Constant-quality demand is especially difficult to separate from home-price movements and commission-rate changes.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts of residential and commercial transaction sides from NAR, Fannie Mae, MBA, CoStar, and local MLS systems; fee-revenue series split among sales, rentals, and commercial brokerage; and transaction-volume sensitivity to mortgage rates.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued demand as people relocate, buy homes, and purchase property for business, while explicitly noting that the market is sensitive to economic conditions and interest rates. The central case is near flat over five years, with asymmetric downside exposure from transaction cyclicality.

e.bridge: S3 measures NAR-profiled real estate firms by office count, legal form, and independence rather than the frozen $1–10M normalized-EBITDA subset. I treat independent brokerage firms serving external clients as lens-eligible and exclude referral-only, captive, and non-brokerage activity; the one-office and independent-firm prevalence supports high eligibility. This proxy likely overstates eligibility because many one-office firms may be below the LMM EBITDA band, while it may understate it where larger independent offices fit the band.
caveats: Office count and commission volume are not EBITDA.
The NAR firm survey may not represent nonmember brokerages or all NAICS establishments.
e.change_evidence: Business-sale listings and closed-deal data from BizBuySell, Axial, GF Data, or brokerage M&A advisers matched to firm financials, office ownership, franchise status, and revenue mix for the LMM population.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is closely aligned with external brokerage, and the firm profile reports predominantly independent, single-office firms. Eligibility is below one because the LMM band can include referral structures, franchise-related units, and mixed-activity firms that do not offer a coherent transferable outsourced operating service.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531210 ($30,836,359,913) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531210 ($180,013,344,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $432,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531210); class <5: 140,557 firms, avg revenue/firm $449,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,803 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,638 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,862,814 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $113,367 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,095 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,465,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $256,039 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,204 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,484,213 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $652,775 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,286,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,912,143 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $373,541,367 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,792,238 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 259

o.bridge: S4 reports that 88% of recent home buyers and 91% of home sellers used an agent or broker, and reports the services customers want; it measures current residential consumer use, not the five-year share of all NAICS 531210 service quantity that requires an accountable operator. I use this as a durability proxy while reducing it for self-service search, software-mediated matching, and nonresidential and rental differences. The proxy likely overstates operator requirement because observed preference for an agent is not proof that a future human operator is necessary.
caveats: The consumer survey is residential and recent-transaction based, whereas this NAICS includes commercial and rental brokerage.
Use of an agent today can reflect norms and financing practices as well as irreplaceable operator need.
o.change_evidence: Annual NAR buyer-seller surveys with service-component questions; MLS, portal, and closing-provider data comparing represented and unrepresented transactions; state complaint, E&O, and licensing data; and controlled studies of AI-assisted self-service transactions.
o.rationale: Current customer use remains high and customers cite negotiation, market explanation, marketing, pricing, and transaction-timing help. Licensed accountability and complex transaction coordination support durability, but routine discovery, content, screening, and communication can migrate to software or self-service channels.

caveats: The sources establish negotiability and market structure, not the retained share of AI benefit.
Commission arrangements vary materially by local market, franchise contract, residential versus commercial work, and agent split.
q.change_evidence: LMM brokerage P&Ls showing agent commission splits and administrative payroll; buyer and listing agreements before and after the 2024 policy changes; local commission-rate panels; and customer win-loss interviews on fee reductions or service expansion.
q.rationale: Brokerage revenue is usually tied to negotiated transaction compensation rather than cost-plus labor reimbursement, allowing an operator to retain part of a productivity gain. Retention is constrained by direct agent splits, low switching costs, intense franchise and single-office competition, and post-settlement transparency: buyer agreements must disclose compensation and compensation is fully negotiable. The range therefore assumes partial, not complete, retention of implemented gross benefit.

rho.bridge: S2 measures reported AI use by REALTOR members, not the share of exposed labor opportunity that will be operationally implemented at LMM NAICS 531210 firms. I use the reported frequency of AI use and reported business impact as adoption evidence, then discount for transaction-specific quality control, fair-housing, privacy, brokerage workflow integration, and the large share of work requiring physical presence. The proxy likely overstates implementation because tool use is not measured labor removal.
caveats: The technology survey is self-reported and membership-based.
Firm software provision does not establish that agents use the tools consistently or that a workflow is compliant.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal NAR Technology Survey cross-tab by brokerage size and AI workflow, or vendor telemetry showing production use, agent hours saved, quality exceptions, and headcount changes at firms with $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
rho.rationale: AI is already used by many surveyed agents, including for listing content, but only half reported a significant or moderate positive business impact and nearly half reported no noticeable impact. This supports meaningful implementation over five years without assuming that present experimentation converts fully into labor removal.

caveats: Firm age is not owner age or sale intent.
No source located measures five-year control-transfer probability for LMM brokerages, and closures are excluded from the construct.
s5.change_evidence: Closed brokerage transaction counts from CoStar, SRS, BizBuySell, Axial, or proprietary M&A databases; state broker-license ownership-change filings; and a sampled survey of broker owners covering retirement intentions, succession plans, closures, and completed control transfers.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying-transfer rate cannot be observed from the available industry sources. S3 shows an established, owner-led population: 80% of responding brokers are broker owners and median operating tenure is 19 years for residential firms and 25 years for commercial firms. That supports some succession and sale supply, but brokerages are relationship- and license-holder-dependent, so retirements or departures often do not produce a transferable operation. The estimate reflects those offsetting forces.

records.principal_driver: AI is already entering agent workflows while substantial administrative, marketing, and coordination work remains adjacent to the licensed relationship role.
records.principal_weakness: Brokerage value can be embedded in individual agents and is vulnerable to transaction cycles, fee negotiation, and self-service alternatives.

caveats: The central labor-opportunity inputs combine an occupational duty description and REALTOR-member AI adoption rather than wage-weighted task exposure or measured labor removal at LMM brokerages; the PROXY labels, bridges, and wide H range disclose this limitation.

The all-firm NAR profile is a weak bridge to the estimated 100-499-employee-derived LMM population: single-office prevalence and independence do not directly identify $1-10M EBITDA lens eligibility or qualifying transfers, and the survey's 2.2% response rate adds selection risk.

The included residential, commercial, and rental activities are economically heterogeneous while the operator-durability evidence is residential-consumer-heavy; the packet discloses the population mismatch but marks the lens non-heterogeneous.

The q base reaches the C-factor cap without direct retention evidence. This remains an honest estimate and does not change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action under a plausible evidence-state downgrade, so it is not material under the frozen test.

Employment growth and current agent use are broad proxies for future constant-quality demand and operator requirement, but their direction, error channels, and population limitations are explicitly stated.

caveats: a: No published six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for 541890.
a: The 541800 mix includes advertising and public-relations businesses that are more desk-intensive than much of the retained physical-service work.
a: Exposure describes task substitution or avoidable hiring, not immediately realized savings.
rho: There is no observed 541890 implementation rate.
rho: The range assumes use of commercially available tools and does not assume a proprietary data advantage.
e: NAICS descriptions identify activities, not the share of firms or revenue in each business model.
e: Eligibility is assessed at the firm level and may differ from establishment coding.
s5: The source is a 2024 survey, not an industry transaction census.
s5: A qualifying transfer excludes closures and internal reorganizations, which the survey does not cleanly separate.
q: No industry-specific evidence was found for the portion of automation benefits passed through to customers.
q: Retention can differ sharply between a retainer-led demonstration provider and a transaction-led promotional-products distributor.
d5: Occupation projections are not constant-price, constant-quality demand measures for 541890 and are used only as directional context.
d5: The code's component activities may have materially different demand trajectories.
o: This is a judgment about the retained lens, not an observed industry survival share.
o: The value is lower for digital-first promotional programs and higher for field demonstrations and installed physical graphics.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business customers buy recurring campaign, promotional-program, demonstration, or display-support work under repeat orders, projects, retainers, or account arrangements; merchandise and fabrication can be pass-through components.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because the code spans advertising-specialty coordination, public merchandise demonstrations, and display lettering. The lens retains only repeat outsourced client-service relationships and excludes project-only fabrication and pure resale.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off sign fabrication or installation projects, pure merchandise resale without an outsourced service relationship, captive marketing teams, internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market external providers of recurring or repeat promotional-product coordination, product demonstrations, and display-lettering services, where the provider remains accountable for client service, creative or campaign coordination, and delivery.

caveats: No published six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for 541890.
The 541800 mix includes advertising and public-relations businesses that are more desk-intensive than much of the retained physical-service work.
Exposure describes task substitution or avoidable hiring, not immediately realized savings.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-and-time study across promotional-product distributors, demonstration agencies, and display-lettering firms that maps hours and wages to artwork, sales support, order administration, sourcing, field delivery, and installation; paired before-and-after AI workflow adoption data.
a.rationale: The broad 541800 wage mix contains 16.77% management employment and multiple business, arts/design, sales, office, production, and transport groups (S2). In the retained activities, AI can reduce drafting, artwork variants, proofing, proposal and outreach preparation, order-status responses, and reporting. In-person demonstrations, client persuasion, vendor exception handling, physical lettering, and delivery still require people, so less than half of current wage work is treated as exposed.

caveats: Occupation projections are not constant-price, constant-quality demand measures for 541890 and are used only as directional context.
The code's component activities may have materially different demand trajectories.
d5.change_evidence: Industry revenue volume data split among promotional products, demonstrations, and display lettering; recurring-order cohorts; and customer marketing-budget allocations between physical, experiential, and digital channels.
d5.rationale: Demand for promotion and physical display support can persist where clients need tangible merchandise, field demonstrations, or site-specific graphics, but the current basket faces substitution toward digital channels and self-service tools. Adjacent occupation projections are mixed to negative: advertising sales agents are projected to decline 6%, advertising and promotions managers decline 2%, and graphic designers grow 2% from 2024 to 2034 (S3, S4, S5). This supports a roughly flat central quantity outlook rather than a growth assumption.

caveats: NAICS descriptions identify activities, not the share of firms or revenue in each business model.
Eligibility is assessed at the firm level and may differ from establishment coding.
e.change_evidence: A Census microdata tabulation or a commercial establishment database matched to websites and revenue descriptions, classifying 541890 LMM firms by repeat service revenue, project-only fabrication, and pure resale.
e.rationale: The code explicitly includes advertising-specialty coordination, merchandise demonstrations, and display lettering (S1). The lens excludes the project-only fabrication and pure-resale portion, while repeat client programs in each listed activity can satisfy the outsourced-service requirement. The resulting eligible share is judged to be near one-half rather than universal.

caveats: This is a judgment about the retained lens, not an observed industry survival share.
The value is lower for digital-first promotional programs and higher for field demonstrations and installed physical graphics.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and churn data identifying whether promotional-product ordering, demonstrations, and display graphics were eliminated, self-served, or moved to software or internal teams; vendor records for field-service utilization.
o.rationale: The retained service basket includes physical activities: coordinating delivery of branded specialties, demonstrating merchandise in public places, and applying graphics or lettering to signs (S1). Those outputs require accountable vendors even if planning and administration become more automated. The deduction from one reflects digital promotion, self-service creative tools, and customer in-housing that can eliminate some outsourced coordination.

caveats: No industry-specific evidence was found for the portion of automation benefits passed through to customers.
Retention can differ sharply between a retainer-led demonstration provider and a transaction-led promotional-products distributor.
q.change_evidence: Client contracts and renewal cohorts showing billing basis, pass-through clauses, labor-versus-merchandise gross margin, competitive rebids, and price changes after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Repeat account work and fixed-price project components permit an operator to retain some lower drafting and coordination cost before repricing. However, the service is closely tied to customer marketing budgets and often bundles physical merchandise or fabrication, making labor savings visible and contestable at renewal. BLS notes managers negotiate advertising contracts with clients and partners (S3), consistent with customer sharing and price competition reducing retention.

caveats: There is no observed 541890 implementation rate.
The range assumes use of commercially available tools and does not assume a proprietary data advantage.
rho.change_evidence: Workflow-level adoption and quality-control records from comparable firms, including AI-assisted proofing error rates, client approval-cycle time, rework rates, and labor hours per order or campaign.
rho.rationale: Drafting and administrative uses can be embedded in ordinary account and production workflows, but client approvals, brand accuracy, product specifications, vendor data, and field execution limit operational implementation. BLS describes advertising and promotions work as involving budgets, contracts, campaigns, pricing, client meetings, and direction of staff (S3), supporting a material but incomplete five-year implementation share.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year stated owner plans across U.S. businesses whose owners primarily work in the business, not realized qualifying control transfers in 541890 LMM firms. Its 14% all-owner sell, go-public, or transfer response is used as the central cross-industry starting point; the 17% response among owners aged 55 or older informs the upper range. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers because plans may not close and includes gifts or rare public offerings, but may understate buyer-led sales not planned by respondents.
caveats: The source is a 2024 survey, not an industry transaction census.
A qualifying transfer excludes closures and internal reorganizations, which the survey does not cleanly separate.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed lower-middle-market transaction data for promotional-products, experiential-demonstration, and sign/display-service providers, plus owner-age and succession-plan data for those firms.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 14% of surveyed primary-work business owners planned to sell, go public, or transfer in the next five years; among owners 55 or older, the figure was 17% (S6). The base discounts stated intent for completion, non-control transfers, and the service-specific population mismatch.

records.principal_driver: Repeat client programs can pair AI-assisted coordination with physical delivery that still requires an accountable operator.
records.principal_weakness: The six-digit code is heterogeneous, and the most relevant transfer and labor evidence is not measured for its retained lower-middle-market service subset.

caveats: S1 defines the industry's activities but does not establish the recurring-service share selected by the lens; e = 0.52 and the lens composition remain bounded judgments rather than measured firm shares.

The labor-exposure case relies on the broader NAICS 541800 occupation mix and adjacent occupation descriptions, not a six-digit wage-weighted task study; a and rho are therefore only structured estimates.

S3-S5 report occupational employment outlooks, not constant-price demand for 541890 services. Their use for d5 and o is directional and appropriately caveated, but those demand primitives are not empirically identified.

The s5 rationale says the Gallup stated-intent figure is discounted, yet the 0.14 base equals the cited 14% response. The wide range and explicit PROXY bridge disclose the completion, control-transfer, industry, and population mismatches; lowering s5 within an honest correction would not change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
No cited source quantifies post-automation savings retention. q is an honestly labeled estimate, and its capped C factor does not determine the base tier because H is the binding weakest link.
All seven researched primitives are ESTIMATE or PROXY. The MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action correctly prevent the numeric base case from being presented as substantiated.

caveats: a: O*NET covers a national occupation rather than all occupations within NAICS 541910.
a: The estimate excludes work that is already automated and does not assume that task exposure equals realized savings.
rho: This is a five-year operational judgment, not a forecast of vendor capabilities.
rho: Implementation will vary substantially between panel operators, analytics shops, and political pollsters.
e: NAICS classification does not report customer recurrence or EBITDA.
e: Political polling and bespoke project work can have materially less repeat revenue than audience measurement or panel businesses.
s5: Owner retirement, business closure, and a qualifying control transfer are different events.
s5: The estimate excludes internal reorganizations and does not assume an age distribution from all small businesses applies to this industry.
q: The product classification describes services, not contract economics.
q: The estimate does not assign retention from software savings to demand expansion or volume changes.
d5: This is not an industry revenue forecast.
d5: The occupation projection extends beyond the five-year horizon and includes in-house roles, consulting, and other employers.
o: The estimate concerns the current service basket rather than all future information products.
o: The need for an operator is likely lower for commoditized survey tools and higher for regulated, high-stakes, or proprietary-panel work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, media, political, and institutional customers buy recurring tracking, panel, measurement, fieldwork, or repeat research engagements, usually through project, retainer, subscription, or panel-access fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate research teams, one-off internal projects, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent providers that repeatedly sell marketing research, audience measurement, survey design, fieldwork, panels, data collection, analysis, or public-opinion research to external customers.

caveats: O*NET covers a national occupation rather than all occupations within NAICS 541910.
The estimate excludes work that is already automated and does not assume that task exposure equals realized savings.
a.change_evidence: Firm-level time sheets by role and workflow, plus a task-level pilot that records baseline hours, quality defects, and post-deployment staffing, would materially refine the estimate.
a.rationale: S2 identifies data collection, analysis, processing, survey-method design, and report preparation as core work. AI can substitute for portions of coding, synthesis, tabulation, draft reporting, and routine desk research, while research design, sampling validity, interpretation, client context, and accountability constrain the wage-weighted opportunity.

d5.bridge: S3 measures national employment for Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists across all employing industries and projects 7 percent growth from 2024 to 2034. It is converted into a five-year directional proxy for constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 541910 service basket. Because the occupation is employed broadly outside this industry and employment can diverge from service quantity as productivity changes, the proxy can overstate or understate the target construct.
caveats: This is not an industry revenue forecast.
The occupation projection extends beyond the five-year horizon and includes in-house roles, consulting, and other employers.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census services receipts by detailed product, customer research-budget surveys, and renewal volumes from LMM providers would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: S3 attributes projected occupational growth to increased use of data and market research. That supports a modest central demand-growth assumption, while the lower bound recognizes self-service analytics, platform data, and customer budget pressure that can displace outsourced current-basket work.

caveats: NAICS classification does not report customer recurrence or EBITDA.
Political polling and bespoke project work can have materially less repeat revenue than audience measurement or panel businesses.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level revenue-recurrence screen using financial statements, CRM exports, and customer-concentration schedules for the LMM population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: S1 defines the industry around gathering, recording, tabulating, and presenting marketing and opinion data, activities commonly sold to external users. The estimate removes establishments whose work is principally episodic, captive, or otherwise fails the recurring-or-repeat outsourced-service lens.

caveats: The estimate concerns the current service basket rather than all future information products.
The need for an operator is likely lower for commoditized survey tools and higher for regulated, high-stakes, or proprietary-panel work.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews that distinguish self-serve analytics from externally accountable research purchases, plus vendor retention and replacement data, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: S1's industry definition still requires systematic collection and presentation of data, and S2 shows tasks involving research-method design, interpretation, communication, and judgment. Software can self-serve routine dashboarding and simple surveys, but accountable providers should remain necessary for defensible sampling, difficult fieldwork, panel operations, data governance, and consequential interpretation.

caveats: The product classification describes services, not contract economics.
The estimate does not assign retention from software savings to demand expansion or volume changes.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts showing renewal terms, rate cards, labor-indexation clauses, and win-loss evidence after automation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: S5 documents a service mix spanning research, sampling, and data collection. Retained benefit should be higher for differentiated panels, measurement assets, and fixed-fee managed programs, but competitive rebids, hourly or project pricing, and customer expectations of lower research cost should share a meaningful portion of productivity gains.

caveats: This is a five-year operational judgment, not a forecast of vendor capabilities.
Implementation will vary substantially between panel operators, analytics shops, and political pollsters.
rho.change_evidence: Documented adoption rates and realized-hours data from comparable research agencies, together with client data-processing agreements and quality-assurance logs, would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: The exposed workflows are largely digital and can be redesigned, but S2 also emphasizes exactness, analytical judgment, external communication, and research-method decisions. Client confidentiality, respondent privacy, sampling controls, and reproducibility make implementation materially slower than mere tool availability.

caveats: Owner retirement, business closure, and a qualifying control transfer are different events.
The estimate excludes internal reorganizations and does not assume an age distribution from all small businesses applies to this industry.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal transaction dataset filtered to NAICS 541910 and an LMM revenue or EBITDA band, combined with founder age and succession interviews, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment because no retrieved source measures qualifying transactions for the LMM subset of this six-digit industry. S4 establishes that Census publishes employer-owner age characteristics, which is relevant succession evidence but does not itself measure transfers.

records.principal_driver: Digital research workflows create a meaningful opportunity to reduce repetitive data and reporting work while preserving accountable research delivery.
records.principal_weakness: Direct evidence on LMM recurrence, qualifying transfers, contract pass-through, and industry-specific constant-quality demand is sparse.

caveats: The broad default lens spans panel and audience-measurement businesses, analytics shops, fieldwork providers, bespoke project research, and political pollsters even though their recurrence, AI exposure, retention, and operator necessity can differ substantially. The packet acknowledges those differences, but marking the lens as non-heterogeneous understates them; this does not pass the materiality test because no correction to that metadata alone changes the base tier or action.

The evidence base is primarily qualitative task and product-scope material. It does not directly measure the numeric bases for a, rho, e, s5, q, or o, and S3 is only a broad occupational proxy for d5. The ESTIMATE/PROXY labels, wide ranges, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action make that weakness visible rather than quietly scoring it as observed.

S5 is an old NAPCS product-list artifact and supports service taxonomy, not current contract economics or recurring revenue. It is adequate for the narrow scope claim but should not be read as validation of e or q.

caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation mix was located.
a: Outdoor and transit operators can have materially more field labor than the broader advertising-services proxy.
a: AI exposure is task potential, not a measured reduction in payroll.
rho: This is a five-year operational judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
rho: Public operators may have better systems and implementation resources than lower-middle-market firms.
e: Public classification materials do not report the LMM firm's recurring-revenue share, customer concentration, or EBITDA.
e: The boundary between display service and one-off production varies across firms.
s5: Gallup measures owner plans across industries rather than completed transfers in this NAICS.
s5: Lamar's asset acquisitions demonstrate market activity but are not a count or rate for LMM firm control transfers.
q: The evidence is from large public operators; local operators' contract terms and bargaining power may differ.
q: This estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit and excludes demand changes and implementation difficulty.
d5: OOH sales include categories that may not map perfectly to NAICS 541850.
d5: Political cycles, recessions, local permitting, and shifts toward digital inventory can make revenue diverge from constant-quality service quantity.
o: The estimate does not treat a physical asset as proof that every current operator remains necessary.
o: Digital-out-of-home networks can centralize sales and trafficking away from local operators.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External advertisers buy recurring or repeat display campaigns, commonly with short renewable terms; operators earn display-space revenue and may separately provide creative, production, installation, and maintenance services.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive advertising units, inactive shells, passive landholding or financing vehicles without operating responsibility, and firms whose only customer work is nonrecurring creative or print production.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms that sell or lease indoor, outdoor, transit, or place-based advertising display capacity and provide the associated ongoing sales, posting, installation, maintenance, and campaign administration for external customers.

a.bridge: S2 measures occupation employment and wages for the broader advertising, public-relations, and related-services industry, not NAICS 541850. I mapped its management, sales, business-operations, design, and office work to AI-assisted proposal drafting, asset scheduling, reporting, and creative-production tasks, then discounted it for display operators' field posting, installation, repair, and safety work evidenced by S3. The broader-industry mix likely overstates exposure because it includes more agency and office labor than display advertising.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation mix was located.
Outdoor and transit operators can have materially more field labor than the broader advertising-services proxy.
AI exposure is task potential, not a measured reduction in payroll.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage special tabulation, payroll-coded job families from display operators, and time-and-motion interviews with local sales, traffic, creative, and operations teams would materially refine the wage-weighted task share.
a.rationale: AI can reduce work in sales support, proposal preparation, campaign reporting, creative adaptation, scheduling, and routine administration, but the display asset, field installation, maintenance, local selling, and customer accountability remain labor-intensive. The base reflects exposure of task time and avoidable hiring, rather than full occupation replacement.

d5.bridge: S7 forecasts US out-of-home advertising sales rather than constant-price, constant-quality quantity for the exact NAICS and includes digital formats and market-price effects. I translated the positive category revenue outlook into modest real current-basket quantity growth, discounting for inflation, mix shift, and cyclicality. Because revenue growth can reflect price and digital inventory mix, this transformation may overstate physical-display quantity growth.
caveats: OOH sales include categories that may not map perfectly to NAICS 541850.
Political cycles, recessions, local permitting, and shifts toward digital inventory can make revenue diverge from constant-quality service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Market-level occupancy, booked display periods, impressions, inventory counts, and constant-format rate-card data from Geopath, OAAA, municipal concessionaires, and representative operators would replace the revenue proxy.
d5.rationale: Recent OOH revenue growth and the published category outlook support stable-to-modestly-growing demand. The base is deliberately below the reported sales-growth outlook because advertising budgets are cyclical and the required construct removes price, quality, and format-mix effects.

caveats: Public classification materials do not report the LMM firm's recurring-revenue share, customer concentration, or EBITDA.
The boundary between display service and one-off production varies across firms.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS business list enriched with customer concentration, asset ownership, recurring-revenue share, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS-specific Census activity list is predominantly display and out-of-home advertising, which normally serves external advertisers through repeat campaigns. I exclude the subset dependent on a single asset or customer and firms that are chiefly one-off production providers, leaving most but not all LMM firms eligible.

caveats: The estimate does not treat a physical asset as proof that every current operator remains necessary.
Digital-out-of-home networks can centralize sales and trafficking away from local operators.
o.change_evidence: A split of operator revenue and labor by self-serve versus managed campaigns, municipal concession and permit requirements, and interviews with buyers and local asset operators would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Advertisers can increasingly self-serve creative and buy digital inventory, but display assets still need an accountable operator for site rights, permitting, inventory availability, safety, installation, maintenance, campaign delivery, and customer service. The principal risk is that software and networks absorb more booking and campaign-management work, not that they eliminate the physical operating role.

caveats: The evidence is from large public operators; local operators' contract terms and bargaining power may differ.
This estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit and excludes demand changes and implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples showing rate cards, escalation, renewal, service-level terms, and cost-plus clauses, together with win-loss and renewal analyses after automation, would establish actual pass-through.
q.rationale: Savings in creative adaptation, sales support, reporting, and administration can be retained during a campaign, but short renewable customer contracts, bid competition, and comparison with other media create meaningful repricing and customer-sharing pressure. Field costs and site payments are unaffected, limiting the gross benefit available to retain.

caveats: This is a five-year operational judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Public operators may have better systems and implementation resources than lower-middle-market firms.
rho.change_evidence: Pre- and post-deployment labor-hour data from display operators, vendor adoption data for traffic-management and proposal software, and structured interviews with operations leaders about workflow integration and quality incidents would move this estimate.
rho.rationale: The addressable work is mostly software-mediated and can be introduced in discrete sales, creative, scheduling, reporting, and back-office workflows. Implementation is constrained by local account ownership, brand-review quality control, fragmented software stacks, and the large physical operations component documented by the public operators.

caveats: Gallup measures owner plans across industries rather than completed transfers in this NAICS.
Lamar's asset acquisitions demonstrate market activity but are not a count or rate for LMM firm control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: NAICS-filtered transaction data from CapIQ, PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and business brokers, matched to seller size and control-transfer status, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup found that a minority of owners primarily working in their businesses expected to sell, go public, or transfer ownership within five years. Mature eligible LMM operators should be more transferable than the all-owner survey population, while failures, closures, and internal reorganizations do not qualify. The estimate therefore places qualifying control transfers above the broad stated-intention figure but well below a universal retirement premise.

records.principal_driver: Recurring display campaigns anchored to physical sites leave a meaningful but bounded AI-enabled operating-improvement opportunity.
records.principal_weakness: Short renewable contracts and sparse NAICS-specific transfer and labor data make both value retention and acquisition availability uncertain.

caveats: The a bridge is directionally coherent and honestly labeled PROXY, but it does not show a reproducible wage-weighted mapping from the broad 541800 occupation table to the 0.23 base. The six-digit mix could contain materially more field and production work; this is especially decision-sensitive because H=1.08 is close to the LOW_PRIORITY weakest-link cutoff of 1.

The eligibility and transfer inputs are honest estimates, but S1 supplies an activity taxonomy rather than a firm distribution and S4 measures broad owner intentions rather than completed 541850 transfers. S5 establishes acquisition activity without identifying how many transactions are qualifying LMM control transfers.

The q, rho, and o rationales extrapolate from two very large public operators to LMM firms. The packet discloses this limitation, but local operators can differ in systems maturity, customer bargaining power, asset concentration, and field-labor intensity.

The registered lens spans indoor, roadside, transit, static, and digital operators while marking the industry non-heterogeneous. Those operating models can differ meaningfully in labor mix, contract structure, permitting, site concentration, and centralization, although the broad default lens is disclosed and does not evidence favorable lens shopping.

The q rationale partly invokes unaffected field costs and site payments. Those considerations primarily limit the amount of addressable benefit in a, not the fraction of an implemented benefit retained through pricing; the contract-duration and competition evidence remains construct-relevant to q.

The d5 range converts category advertising revenue and outlooks into five-year constant-quality quantity. Inflation, digital mix, economic cycles, and format shifts leave wider uncertainty than the source measures directly. This does not meet the rejection test because D is not the base weakest link and the record remains LOW_PRIORITY with MODEL_CONDITIONED/VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS treatment.

caveats: a: O*NET describes the Tax Preparer occupation rather than the full NAICS payroll mix, which also includes management and administrative staff.
a: The estimate concerns technically exposed current tasks, not realized savings or customer demand.
rho: This is a five-year operating judgment, not a measured adoption forecast.
rho: Tax-law changes, vendor integrations, data-access permissions, and state-specific filing requirements could move implementation materially.
e: No public source reviewed measures the denominator at the specified EBITDA band or identifies franchise and control restrictions consistently.
e: The supplied firm-count input is not re-estimated here.
s5: This is not a market-wide business exit rate.
s5: Seasonality, preparer licensing, customer concentration, and dependence on a selling preparer can make nominally available firms nontransferable.
q: No public, lens-specific evidence was verified for contractual pass-through or realized post-automation price changes.
q: This excludes demand changes and implementation difficulty, which belong in other primitives.
d5: Filing-season e-file counts are not NAICS revenue, constant-quality service quantity, or a five-year forecast.
d5: The measure is dominated by individual returns and does not distinguish the lower-middle-market lens.
o: This is a judgment about the present service basket, not a forecast of all tax filing.
o: Future federal and state policy, software reliability, and tax-law complexity can change the operator-required share in either direction.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat annual outsourced tax-return service sold directly to individuals or small businesses, generally for a per-return or complexity-based fee.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA offices, accounting, bookkeeping, billing, payroll, software-only products, captive tax departments, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms whose primary external service is preparing federal or state tax returns for repeat individual or small-business clients, including document intake, return preparation, review, filing, and client communication.

caveats: O*NET describes the Tax Preparer occupation rather than the full NAICS payroll mix, which also includes management and administrative staff.
The estimate concerns technically exposed current tasks, not realized savings or customer demand.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level payroll-by-role extract paired with time-and-motion studies for intake, preparation, review, client service, and post-filing work; or a longitudinal tax-firm implementation survey with hours saved by role.
a.rationale: O*NET identifies structured computer, information-processing, document-recording, calculation, verification, and scheduling work alongside client interviews, advice, and compliance judgment [S1]. AI can reduce paid effort in intake, classification, missing-item follow-up, first-draft preparation, and review support, but a substantial portion remains judgment, exception handling, and accountable review. The estimate is wage-weighted rather than a task count.

d5.bridge: S2 measures nationwide e-filed individual returns submitted by tax professionals, not constant-price and constant-quality demand for the full NAICS lens. Its reported year-over-year increase is used only as a directional starting point for a five-year service-quantity judgment; it likely understates paper and non-individual work but may overstate demand for independent outsourced firms because it includes branded and larger preparers.
caveats: Filing-season e-file counts are not NAICS revenue, constant-quality service quantity, or a five-year forecast.
The measure is dominated by individual returns and does not distinguish the lower-middle-market lens.
d5.change_evidence: IRS Statistics of Income filing-mode series segmented by return complexity and preparer type, plus repeated client-count data from standalone tax firms and state filing data.
d5.rationale: IRS reported an increase in e-filed individual returns from tax professionals in the latest comparable filing-season totals [S2]. Tax filing remains recurrent, but self-preparation, software, and policy changes constrain a simple extrapolation; the proxy therefore supports only modest central growth in the current service basket.

caveats: No public source reviewed measures the denominator at the specified EBITDA band or identifies franchise and control restrictions consistently.
The supplied firm-count input is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A sell-side database export and quality-adjusted buyer pipeline for this NAICS with revenue, EBITDA, ownership, franchise status, and service mix; then a buyer review of control eligibility.
e.rationale: The eligible subset excludes very small owner-operated practices, branded or otherwise non-control units, and firms with substantial adjacent accounting or payroll activity. Standalone tax preparation is a distinct Census industry category that excludes CPA, accounting, bookkeeping, billing, and payroll combinations [S4], but public data reviewed here do not map normalized EBITDA to control eligibility.

caveats: This is a judgment about the present service basket, not a forecast of all tax filing.
Future federal and state policy, software reliability, and tax-law complexity can change the operator-required share in either direction.
o.change_evidence: IRS filing-mode and complexity microdata, Direct File or replacement-program scope and adoption, tax-software completion data, and longitudinal client retention by return complexity from tax firms.
o.rationale: Many taxpayers will continue to seek accountable help for interpretation, exceptions, and filing responsibility, while standardized returns can migrate to self-preparation or software. GAO documented Direct File expansion of supported income and import capabilities, while also noting policy uncertainty about its continuation [S3], illustrating both the substitution path and uncertainty.

caveats: No public, lens-specific evidence was verified for contractual pass-through or realized post-automation price changes.
This excludes demand changes and implementation difficulty, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level client pricing, renewal, discount, and hours-per-return data from tax firms before and after workflow automation; paired with local competitor price checks by return complexity.
q.rationale: Per-return and complexity-based fees give an operator some ability to retain workflow savings, particularly where capacity is constrained. Retention is limited by consumer price sensitivity, self-preparation alternatives, competitive local providers, and customers seeking fee reductions as turnaround becomes easier.

caveats: This is a five-year operating judgment, not a measured adoption forecast.
Tax-law changes, vendor integrations, data-access permissions, and state-specific filing requirements could move implementation materially.
rho.change_evidence: Season-by-season implementation data from tax firms showing production hours, error rates, review rates, and adoption by return complexity; plus insurer, E&O carrier, or regulator evidence on acceptable AI-assisted review controls.
rho.rationale: The work is already computer-mediated, but O*NET reports that exactness is extremely important and includes compliance evaluation, client communication, and record verification [S1]. Those requirements support assisted workflows and constrain unattended execution, especially for unusual returns and time-critical filing periods.

caveats: This is not a market-wide business exit rate.
Seasonality, preparer licensing, customer concentration, and dependence on a selling preparer can make nominally available firms nontransferable.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction records from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, or business-broker MLS feeds, matched to tax-preparation firms in the lens; supplemented by owner-age and succession data from a targeted survey.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer requires a saleable client base, transferable preparer relationships, and acceptable quality and compliance records. The base assumes some succession and consolidation activity among eligible firms, while excluding closures and internal reorganizations; no industry-and-size-specific transfer-rate source was verified.

records.principal_driver: Repeat filing cycles paired with automatable information-processing and review-support work.
records.principal_weakness: Uncertain depth and transferability of the lower-middle-market standalone-firm population, alongside software substitution for simple returns.

caveats: S1 supports the occupational task profile but not the wage-weighted a estimate, the full-industry payroll population, or the five-year rho estimate; both primitives are honestly labeled ESTIMATE and the population mismatch is disclosed.

S2 provides only a one-year nationwide individual e-file count proxy for d5, not a five-year constant-quality quantity measure for standalone lower-middle-market firms; the bridge is directionally plausible and explicit.
S3 documents a software-substitution path and policy uncertainty but does not quantify o; the input remains an openly bounded estimate.

The exact S4 page did not expose the cited scope and exclusion language when reopened, leaving that definitional claim unverified from the registered source. This does not pass the materiality test because e is already an estimate, the lack of a size-and-control denominator is disclosed, and removing S4 would not change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

Self-preparation and software substitution appear in both d5 and o reasoning, creating some risk of depressing D twice for the same mechanism. Correcting that allocation could raise D, but F remains the binding weakest link below 1, so the base tier and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action would not change.
