lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Professional design, planning, consulting, documentation, and construction-administration services sold to public agencies, developers, institutions, businesses, and repeat private clients through project, term, hourly, fixed-fee, or hybrid engagements.
lens.excluded_activities: Landscape maintenance and installation, nurseries and garden centers, captive design units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without a repeat or recurring external-service relationship.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat or recurring outsourced landscape architecture, site planning, land-use planning, garden design, golf-course design, or landscape consulting to external customers.

a.bridge: S2 and S3 measure the Landscape Architects occupation, not wage-weighted employment within NAICS 541320 firms. I mapped documented CADD, graphic, specification, estimate, research, and proposal tasks to AI-assisted production and administrative workflows, while retaining client meetings, site inspection, professional judgment, and accountable design as less substitutable. This likely understates exposed wages because it omits firm support and production roles that are generally more automatable than licensed principals.
caveats: The occupation is employed across industries and does not capture every job in a landscape architectural firm.
No source measures AI-susceptible task time or wages for this NAICS.
a.change_evidence: A 2026 NAICS 541320 occupational staffing and wage matrix, plus time-and-billing exports from a representative set of LMM firms split among principals, designers, CAD staff, project managers, and administration.
a.rationale: The occupation has a material document, drawing, visualization, estimating, research, and proposal component, but its core service also requires site-specific analysis, coordination, inspection, and licensed judgment. The resulting exposure is meaningful but not a majority of current wage-weighted work.

d5.bridge: S6 projects real output for the broader 541300 architectural, engineering, and related-services industry, while S3 projects national Landscape Architects employment across all employers. I translate their positive medium-term direction into a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand view for the current 541320 service basket. The broader industry's engineering weight likely makes this proxy upward-biased for landscape architecture if engineering demand outperforms this specialty.
caveats: Neither cited projection is a direct five-year constant-quality demand measure for NAICS 541320.
Project demand is cyclical and can differ sharply among public, residential, commercial, and environmental work.
d5.change_evidence: A NAICS 541320 real-receipts or volume series, public capital-program budgets, building and land-development pipeline data, and customer surveys separating landscape architecture from engineering demand.
d5.rationale: The broader industry has positive real-output projections and the core occupation has positive projected employment, consistent with modest demand growth. The lower bound allows development and public-capital cycles to reduce demand for discretionary projects.

caveats: The Census classification establishes included activities, not eligibility under this specific lens.
The supplied LMM count may include firms whose actual customer pattern cannot be observed from establishment data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level screen of the supplied LMM universe using websites, state registrations, revenue concentration, client history, ownership, and service descriptions.
e.rationale: The Census industry questionnaire places design, planning, consulting, and golf-course design in this code and separates maintenance and installation into another code. Most lower-middle-market firms in the supplied population should therefore be external professional-service providers, but some will be one-off residential practices, captive-like affiliates, inactive entities, or otherwise fail the repeat-service lens.

caveats: The result is a judgment about operator-required service demand, not an observed industry statistic.
The lowest-complexity residential and visualization work may behave differently from public, institutional, or regulated projects.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win-loss data on self-service design tools, insurance and licensing guidance on professional responsibility, and workflow evidence showing which project stages clients now buy from a firm.
o.rationale: Most current-service demand should continue to need an accountable operator because work spans site conditions, coordination, inspections, permits, construction administration, and professional responsibility. Some simple concept, garden-design, visualization, and information tasks may be self-served or supplied primarily through software, which keeps the value below full retention.

q.bridge: S5 is a municipal landscape architecture and site-planning term solicitation, not a representative national revenue sample or a measure of benefit retention. Its required multi-year hourly-rate schedule and rate cap demonstrate a contract form in which customers can observe and constrain labor pricing. I use that mechanism to bound retention after customer sharing and repricing; the public, rate-capped context likely understates retention for fixed-fee private work.
caveats: One municipal solicitation cannot establish the national mix of contract types.
Competitive intensity and client bargaining power vary materially by geography, client segment, and specialty.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of executed client agreements and renewal history, paired with time-and-billing data showing hourly, fixed-fee, and hybrid revenue shares and post-productivity price changes.
q.rationale: Hourly and term arrangements can expose savings to customers through fewer billable hours, negotiated rates, and renewal pricing, while fixed-fee scopes permit the firm to retain more productivity benefit until repricing. A mixed book supports partial rather than full retention.

caveats: No industry-specific implementation-rate study was located.
State licensing and client risk tolerance may produce substantial variation by project type.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after workflow studies from landscape architecture firms using AI or design automation, errors-and-omissions data, and interviews with licensed principals, project managers, and public-agency reviewers.
rho.rationale: Drafting, visualization, document preparation, information retrieval, and initial estimates can be embedded in workflows, but quality control, state-specific conditions, client coordination, design liability, and review by a licensed professional constrain implementation. The base assumes adoption as assisted production rather than autonomous issuance.

caveats: S4 establishes that the ABS collects owner characteristics but does not provide a verified 541320 transfer probability here.
Closures, asset sales without an operating business, and internal reorganizations are excluded from this judgment.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction cohort from an M&A database filtered to US NAICS 541320 firms in the LMM band, together with Census owner-age microdata or a targeted principal succession survey.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded conservatively because no observed NAICS 541320 LMM transfer cohort or owner-age distribution was located. Professional-service firms can transfer when principals retire or succession is planned, but personal client relationships, licenses, key-person design reputation, and small-team dependence can prevent a qualifying transferable sale.

records.principal_driver: A substantial share of documentation and design-production work can be assisted while accountable site and client work remains operator-led.
records.principal_weakness: The service is project-based and principal-dependent, making transferability and realized retention difficult to establish without firm-level diligence.

caveats: The a bridge uses Landscape Architects occupational tasks rather than a NAICS 541320 staffing-and-wage mix, and neither S2 nor S3 measures AI-susceptible task time; the 0.15-0.40 range is therefore a transparent but judgment-heavy proxy.
The 0.12 five-year transfer probability is an honest estimate without a transaction cohort or owner-age distribution; S4 only establishes that owner-characteristic data are collected.
The q range is inferred from one municipal hourly-rate contract and is not evidence of the national mix of hourly, fixed-fee, and hybrid revenue or of realized customer sharing.

The d5 bridge combines a broader-industry real-output projection with an occupation-level employment projection; neither source directly measures five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541320.

The source-backed limitations and all material estimates are disclosed, the default lens is coherent, construct assignments are not reversed or double-counted, and none of the identified source gaps meets the literal materiality test: correcting or downgrading its evidence state would not change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or the MODEL_CONDITIONED/VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

caveats: a: The injected compensation-to-receipts input combines QCEW 2024 wages and ECEC benefits with SUSB 2022 receipts and is IPS-harmonized; that vintage and construction mismatch can distort the labor base to which this exposure applies.
a: Occupational sources do not reveal the actual occupation mix of six-digit drafting-services establishments.

rho: S3 describes CAD/BIM productivity generally and does not establish implementation of AI tools by these firms.
e: S1 classifies establishments by primary activity and does not identify recurrence, customer type, or LMM EBITDA.

s5: The industry has only a small injected LMM candidate count, so a few unobserved transactions could move the realized rate substantially.

q: This does not include volume loss or implementation difficulty, which are assessed separately.

o: No source directly measures whether customers will accept software-only delivery in five years.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business-to-business and project-based outsourced technical drawing work, commonly sold as scoped deliverables, drawing packages, revisions, or staff augmentation to architecture, engineering, construction, and industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive drafting departments; architectural, engineering, surveying, inspection, construction, manufacturing, software, and financing businesses whose primary activity is outside NAICS 541340; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent firms providing detailed layouts, plans, illustrations, CAD drawings, models, revisions, and drawing-production support from engineering or architectural specifications to external customers.

a.bridge: S2 measures tasks of architectural and civil drafters rather than wage-weighted work inside NAICS 541340, and S3 measures the broader drafter occupation rather than AI exposure. I treat CAD drawing production, plan drafting, document review, and design coordination as the central wage tasks of a drafting-services firm, then estimate the share of still-current work that AI can substitute or avert hiring for. This likely overstates exposure where source files, liability, or client-specific standards require manual rework, and may understate exposure in routine revision-heavy work.
caveats: The injected compensation-to-receipts input combines QCEW 2024 wages and ECEC benefits with SUSB 2022 receipts and is IPS-harmonized; that vintage and construction mismatch can distort the labor base to which this exposure applies.
Occupational sources do not reveal the actual occupation mix of six-digit drafting-services establishments.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level time-and-motion sample by task and wage, plus vendor telemetry for CAD/BIM copilots and blind quality-review results on client-ready drawing sets, would directly measure avoidable labor.
a.rationale: The occupation tasks are highly digital and structured: producing CAD drawings and drafting plans from sketches are exposed to generation, extraction, revision, checking, and documentation automation. Code analysis and cross-discipline coordination require contextual review, so the exposed share is well below the whole wage bill. BLS also reports that CAD and BIM raise drafter productivity and move some former drafting tasks to engineers and architects.

d5.bridge: S3 measures national drafter employment over 2024-34, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541340 services. I use its near-flat ten-year employment outlook and explanation that CAD/BIM raises productivity as a directional proxy, transform it into a five-year service-quantity ratio centered on flat demand, and widen it for architectural/civil growth versus mechanical and electrical decline. The proxy likely understates service quantity if productivity permits more drawing output with flat employment, but can overstate outsourced demand if customers bring work in-house.
caveats: Occupation employment is not an industry output or outsourced-service demand measure.
The horizon and service mix differ from the BLS projection, and construction cycles can dominate near-term demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar revenue by service line, architectural and engineering backlog data, construction and manufacturing capex forecasts, and customer surveys on outsourcing versus in-house drafting would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects total drafter employment from 192,100 in 2024 to 191,500 in 2034, while architectural and civil drafters grow and mechanical and electrical specialties decline. That mixed outlook supports a roughly flat five-year current-service basket rather than a strong secular expansion or contraction.

caveats: S1 classifies establishments by primary activity and does not identify recurrence, customer type, or LMM EBITDA.
The injected LMM count is an estimate based on a SUSB size-class margin bridge and the Damodaran B&CS 15.65% assumption, rather than a census of qualifying firms.
e.change_evidence: A commercial firmographic file matched to service descriptions, customer concentration, revenue recurrence, and ownership relationships for the LMM candidates would determine the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition is already an external drafting-service activity, making most LMM firms directionally eligible. The deduction recognizes that a material share may depend on one-off projects, staff augmentation, closely tied affiliates, or owner-specific technical relationships rather than recurring or repeat outsourced revenue under the fixed lens.

o.bridge: S2 measures current architectural and civil drafter tasks, including coordinating disciplines and analyzing code and technical documents, rather than the share of five-year NAICS 541340 service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I infer that these review, coordination, and client-facing tasks preserve an operator role for much of the service basket after automation. Because the source lists only current human work, this proxy likely overstates future operator need where customers can self-serve simple drawings, though it may understate responsibility requirements in regulated projects.
caveats: The proxy is concentrated in architectural and civil work and may not represent mechanical, electrical, or specialty drafting.
No source directly measures whether customers will accept software-only delivery in five years.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and procurement data separating template work from regulated or coordinated drawing sets, plus acceptance testing of software-only outputs and liability requirements, would directly measure operator necessity.
o.rationale: Even when drafting software produces a first pass, customer-specific specifications, code implications, coordination across structural, electrical, and mechanical inputs, and client questions leave a substantial accountable-operator role. Commodity templates and straightforward revisions remain vulnerable to software-only or self-service delivery.

caveats: No public NAICS 541340 source observed billing mix, contractual pass-through, or realized AI-related repricing.
This does not include volume loss or implementation difficulty, which are assessed separately.
q.change_evidence: A sample of customer master service agreements, statements of work, rate cards, renewal histories, and win-loss interviews segmented by fixed-fee, hourly, and staff-augmentation revenue would directly test retention.
q.rationale: Scoped drawing packages and fixed-fee revision work can allow the operator to retain faster production and turnaround value during a contract term. Staff augmentation and hourly work pass savings through more quickly, while technical-service competition and renewals should share part of the benefit with customers. The range is therefore centered on partial, not complete, retention of implemented gross benefit.

caveats: There is no observed NAICS 541340 adoption or production-quality series for generative AI workflows.
S3 describes CAD/BIM productivity generally and does not establish implementation of AI tools by these firms.
rho.change_evidence: Reference checks with drafting-service owners, production-system logs from CAD/BIM deployments, error and rework rates before and after rollout, and insurer or client acceptance requirements would materially change the implementation range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should cover first-pass drawing generation, revisions, standards retrieval, QA assistance, and document administration, but client acceptance, version control, interdisciplinary coordination, code interpretation, and accountable sign-off constrain fully operational use. The estimate applies only after exposed work has been identified and excludes pricing and demand effects.

caveats: Neither source measures qualifying control transfers or owner age for the LMM lens.
The industry has only a small injected LMM candidate count, so a few unobserved transactions could move the realized rate substantially.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NAICS 541340 transaction screen from PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, Axial, and state corporate filings, paired with owner-age and succession interviews, would materially improve the estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded below typical annual transaction exposure because many specialist firms can continue under founders, close without a transferable operation, or reorganize internally. It is bounded above zero because the employer base and SBA's size analysis indicate a population of independent small service firms that can be sold when customer relationships and technical staff are transferable.

records.principal_driver: Digital, repeatable CAD drawing and revision work offers a meaningful but quality-constrained labor opportunity.
records.principal_weakness: A very small estimated LMM pool and uncertain retention of savings limit confidence in a transferable operator opportunity.

caveats: Every researched primitive is a proxy or estimate. The sources establish the industry boundary, current tasks, employer count, occupational outlook, and SBA size-standard context, but they do not directly observe the numerical AI exposure, implementation, eligible-firm share, transfer rate, retention, outsourced-service demand, or future operator-necessity inputs. This limitation is disclosed and correctly produces MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The lens marks the industry non-heterogeneous even though BLS projects architectural and civil drafting differently from electrical, mechanical, and other drafting, and S2 covers only architectural and civil drafters. The packet discloses the specialty limitation in the d5 and o bridges and caveats; correcting the heterogeneity flag or plausibly widening those ranges would not change the F-constrained LOW_PRIORITY base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

The base F factor of 1.157 is close to the LOW_PRIORITY weakest-link cutoff of 1, while its drivers e and s5 have no direct firm-level or transaction evidence and the fixed dataset anchor n is itself an estimate. A modest downward revision could move the tier to STRUCTURAL_OUT, but the inputs are honestly labeled ESTIMATE with wide ranges, so this is sensitivity to disclosed uncertainty rather than a material research defect.

caveats: a: The published industry occupation mix is at NAICS 561500, not six digits.
a: Occupation titles do not reveal each firm's automation baseline or share of high-touch specialty itineraries.
rho: No source measured five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market travel agencies.
rho: Implementation may differ sharply between corporate, cruise, luxury, and general leisure agencies.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers pay directly through planning or service fees and indirectly through supplier commissions; repeat business can arise from corporate accounts, returning leisure clients, groups, and referrals.
lens.excluded_activities: Tour operators, guide services, other reservation-service businesses, captive internal travel departments, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market travel agencies that act as external agents selling travel, tour, and accommodation services, including repeat leisure and corporate travel planning, booking, ticketing, changes, and account support.

a.bridge: S2 measures 2023 employment and mean wages for the broader NAICS 561500 travel-arrangement-and-reservation-services group, not NAICS 561510 or task automation. I wage-weighted its process-heavy roles using S3 travel-agent task content: travel agents are 25.43% of employment, customer-service representatives 7.75%, reservation and ticket agents 9.04%, and office-support occupations 27.45%; booking, fare lookup, cost calculation, recordkeeping, payment, and routine customer communications are treated as AI-exposed, while advice, selling, exception handling, and accountable decisions are not. The broader source includes tour and other reservation services, so it likely overstates exposure if those operations are more standardized than travel agencies.
caveats: The published industry occupation mix is at NAICS 561500, not six digits.
Occupation titles do not reveal each firm's automation baseline or share of high-touch specialty itineraries.
a.change_evidence: A NAICS 561510 six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage file, agency time studies split by booking, servicing, sales, and disruption tasks, and anonymized GDS or CRM workflow logs would materially revise the wage-weighted exposure estimate.
a.rationale: The role mix is heavily administrative, reservation, and information-handling work, while a material share still requires customer discovery, recommendation, selling, supplier coordination, and disruption resolution. The base reflects assistive automation and avoided hiring rather than full replacement.

d5.bridge: S4 projects US Travel Agent occupation employment from 65,700 in 2024 to 67,200 in 2034, a 2% ten-year increase, rather than constant-price, constant-quality output for the frozen travel-agency service basket. I scale that ten-year employment change to a five-year demand proxy and widen it for travel cyclicality and self-service. If labor productivity rises without a parallel fall in service quantity, this proxy likely understates demand; online self-booking creates a countervailing risk.
caveats: Occupational employment is not industry output and includes agents outside the exact industry.
Travel demand is exposed to macroeconomic, health, geopolitical, and supplier-disruption shocks.
d5.change_evidence: BEA constant-dollar output for travel-agency services, ARC agency transaction volumes by customer segment, and multi-year corporate-travel and leisure-agency booking cohorts would materially revise year-five demand.
d5.rationale: The official outlook describes gradual continuing demand for personalized travel advice and issue handling, but also says online research and booking may limit demand. That combination supports roughly stable year-five quantity rather than a high growth assumption.

caveats: S1 defines the industry but does not measure customer recurrence, owner dependence, or the frozen EBITDA-band firms.
The supplied firm count is frozen and was not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm microdata cut showing revenue by corporate account, repeat-client cohort, supplier concentration, host or franchise affiliation, and owner dependence for the frozen EBITDA band would materially revise eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly describes external agency services to public and commercial clients, so the activity boundary matches the lens. Eligibility is below the full frozen LMM count because the lens requires recurring or repeat outsourced service and excludes businesses whose economics depend mainly on a founder's personal book, a captive relationship, or isolated one-off leisure transactions.

o.bridge: S4 describes the current Travel Agent occupation and its outlook, not the year-five share of the frozen service basket requiring an accountable travel-agency operator. I translate its evidence that clients value personalized recommendations and help with travel issues, alongside its warning that online resources enable self-booking, into an operator-required share. This occupational framing likely overstates durability because it does not separately measure fully software-supplied transactions.
caveats: The evidence is occupational and includes self-employed agents, not only acquired lower-middle-market firms.
Operator need is likely higher in complex corporate, group, and luxury travel than in commodity leisure bookings.
o.change_evidence: Segmented booking data separating advisor-assisted, direct-online, and AI-assisted transactions; corporate travel-program procurement data; and client interviews following disruption events would materially revise this share.
o.rationale: Complex trips, corporate policy support, supplier coordination, and disruption handling preserve an accountable service role, while simple planning and booking can migrate to online or software-led self-service. The base keeps a clear majority of the current basket operator-required but leaves substantial substitution risk.

caveats: No source measures the five-year retention of AI-derived savings for this lens.
Commission, fee, and supplier-override exposure varies by agency specialty and host arrangement.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level revenue contracts, fee schedules, commission statements, renewal pricing, and a customer win-loss study after automation would materially establish how gross benefits are shared.
q.rationale: The base allows an operator to retain some internal efficiency because agent compensation includes commissions and service fees, and specialized planning can carry differentiated value. Retention is constrained by fee-sensitive customers, supplier-controlled commission terms, host and consortium economics, and competitors using similar tools. ASTA's statement that non-commissionable fares have been a barrier to compensation underscores the dependence on supplier-set economics.

caveats: No source measured five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market travel agencies.
Implementation may differ sharply between corporate, cruise, luxury, and general leisure agencies.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after studies from travel-management companies or host agencies showing task-level adoption, error rates, booking throughput, and headcount effects; supplier API coverage; and interviews with agency operations leaders would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years should allow deployment of drafting, knowledge retrieval, intake, itinerary preparation, QA support, and back-office workflow tools. Implementation is bounded by reservation-system integration, supplier content accuracy, payment and identity handling, and the need for a human owner of exceptions. BLS describes computer-system training and travel agents' responsibility for changes, which supports a moderate rather than near-complete implementation expectation.

s5.bridge: S6 is a voluntary, nationwide broker-market dataset for small businesses and reports that retirement is the leading stated reason among owners planning to sell; it does not measure NAICS 561510, the specified EBITDA band, or a five-year control-transfer probability. I translate its evidence of active small-business transfers, owner retirement motivation, and buyer selectivity into a bounded five-year probability for eligible firms. Because broker-market observations are selected toward marketed businesses, the proxy likely overstates the probability for the full eligible population, although off-market sales create an offsetting omission.
caveats: S6 is not an industry-specific census of closed transactions and reports voluntary broker submissions.
A stated reason for planned sale is not a completed qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed NAICS 561510 transactions from a business-broker database, SBA 7(a) acquisition-loan originations by NAICS, and seller interviews recording ownership age, sale intent, and completed transfers would materially revise this probability.
s5.rationale: There is evidence of a functioning small-business transaction market and retirement-led seller motivation, but agency books can be owner-dependent and buyer underwriting now emphasizes cash-flow stability and reduced owner dependence. The base therefore assumes a meaningful but far from universal control-transfer flow.

records.principal_driver: A task mix rich in booking, information handling, recordkeeping, and service workflows creates a practical automation surface.
records.principal_weakness: Direct booking and software substitution threaten both demand durability and the operator's ability to retain productivity gains.

caveats: The a bridge does not show a reproducible wage-weighted calculation for 0.48, and its listing of the 27.45% office-support aggregate alongside the 7.75% customer-service and 9.04% reservation subgroups creates an overlap risk. The value is honestly labeled PROXY, the population mismatch is disclosed, and correcting this ambiguity alone is not shown to change the base tier or action.

The d5 and o values translate an occupational employment outlook into service-basket demand and operator-required quantity. BLS supports the direction but not those constructs directly; the packet discloses the bridge and uses broad ranges.

The s5 proxy rests on a voluntary nationwide broker-market sample rather than travel-agency transfers in the frozen EBITDA band. Selection toward marketed firms and the distinction between planned and completed sales are disclosed.

The e, rho, and q bases remain bounded judgments without direct LMM travel-agency measurements. Their ESTIMATE labels appropriately keep the record MODEL_CONDITIONED and the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and S2 is a four-digit-sector proxy rather than this six-digit industry.
a: No public 541870 wage-weighted task inventory was located.
rho: This is a judgment about implementation after task exposure, not a survey measurement.
rho: It should not be read as a forecast of revenue, customer retention, or pricing.
e: No public source identifies the lower-middle-market subset or tests transferability for this NAICS code.
e: S3 covers US business owners generally, not this industry.
s5: S3 is a national owner survey and reports intended actions, not completed deals.
s5: The survey includes business sizes outside the lower-middle-market lens and does not isolate advertising-material distributors.
q: No public contract-price study was located for this narrow NAICS code.
q: The estimate excludes any demand change and does not assume that field-labor savings are AI-derived.

d5: The measure is a judgment about constant-price, constant-quality service quantity, not nominal postal revenue.

o: It assumes the current physical service basket; it does not model new software-native advertising services.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat outsourced campaign execution for advertisers, retailers, agencies, and brand owners; revenue is generally campaign, route, event, staffing, assembly, or distribution fee based rather than software subscription revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Mail delivery, wholly electronic distribution, advertising-agency creative and media-buying work, promotional-specialty distributors, captive marketing teams, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly distribute printed advertising materials or product samples door-to-door or in public locations for external customers, including associated campaign coordination, field staffing, assembly, routing, reporting, and client service.

a.bridge: S2 measures the occupation mix of the much broader NAICS 541800 advertising, public relations, and related-services sector, which includes creative and management-heavy agencies. I use its documented white-collar mix only to identify automatable coordination, sales, account, and administrative work, then reduce it materially because S1 shows this code's core output is physical door-to-door or public-place distribution and sampling. The proxy therefore likely overstates direct substitution for 541870 field work.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and S2 is a four-digit-sector proxy rather than this six-digit industry.
No public 541870 wage-weighted task inventory was located.
a.change_evidence: A 541870 establishment-level payroll and occupational file, time-and-motion study separating field distribution from dispatch, account, and administrative hours, and customer contracts showing reporting or creative scope would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can reduce labor in campaign briefing, list and route preparation, field scheduling, client reporting, sales support, invoicing, and quality-review workflows. It does not physically distribute flyers or hand out samples; the source-defined physical field component dominates the service, so the wage-weighted exposed share is low.

d5.bridge: S4 measures USPS Marketing Mail pieces, whereas this industry includes non-mail door-to-door and public-place flyers and samples. I use the observed multi-year decline in a close physical-advertising channel as a directional demand proxy, with a wide range because sampling and hand distribution can move differently from mail. The proxy can understate demand where experiential sampling gains share, and can overstate it where the code's operators rely on printed circulars.
caveats: USPS marketing mail is not the same as non-mail distribution services.
The measure is a judgment about constant-price, constant-quality service quantity, not nominal postal revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of 541870 constant-dollar receipts, campaign-unit volumes from major distributors, retailer sampling budgets, and channel-level advertiser spend by geography would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: The service basket remains useful for local promotion, retail activation, and sampling, but electronic advertising and lower physical-mail volumes create a persistent substitution risk. The base expects continued, moderated real-volume erosion rather than disappearance.

caveats: No public source identifies the lower-middle-market subset or tests transferability for this NAICS code.
S3 covers US business owners generally, not this industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level list with revenue concentration, employee counts, customer contract recurrence, ownership links, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The defined service is ordinarily sold to external customers and can recur by campaign, route, retailer, or event, but a meaningful minority of lower-middle-market firms can be owner-dependent, episodic, concentrated in a single client, or economically tied to an agency or distributor and therefore unsuitable under the lens.

caveats: This is not an observed operator-retention rate.
It assumes the current physical service basket; it does not model new software-native advertising services.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on substitution to digital channels, operational audits of unmanned distribution methods, and service-level requirements for proof of placement or sampling would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: For the portion of physical promotion that remains, accountable operators are still needed to recruit and supervise field staff, obtain site access, handle samples and materials, execute routes, document completion, and resolve exceptions. Software can replace some campaign selection and reporting but cannot supply the physical handoff; the main risk is channel elimination, captured separately in demand.

caveats: No public contract-price study was located for this narrow NAICS code.
The estimate excludes any demand change and does not assume that field-labor savings are AI-derived.
q.change_evidence: A sample of executed master-service agreements and statements of work, renewal pricing, bid-win histories, labor-escalator clauses, and customer concentration data would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Campaign and staffing work is commonly rebid and can be priced per route, event, unit, or campaign, leaving customers able to request lower fees at renewal. An operator should retain some execution savings through fixed-price commitments, faster turnaround, and capacity utilization, but competitive bidding and agency procurement are likely to share much of the benefit with customers.

caveats: This is a judgment about implementation after task exposure, not a survey measurement.
It should not be read as a forecast of revenue, customer retention, or pricing.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation interviews with operators, timekeeping and dispatch-system exports, AI-vendor deployment records, and before/after headcount data for campaign operations would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, tools for dispatch, staffing communications, proposal preparation, reporting, and back-office processing are practical, but fragmented field execution, client-specific proof-of-performance requirements, and small-firm workflow change limit implementation. Physical distribution tasks are deliberately excluded from the implementable portion.

s5.bridge: S3 surveys US owners who primarily work in their businesses, rather than eligible lower-middle-market 541870 firms. Its five-year sale-or-transfer response is used as a starting point and adjusted down from employer owners' stated intentions because an intention is not a completed qualifying control transfer and because some transfers are family gifts rather than arm's-length control sales. The resulting proxy may still overstate completed transferrable deals.
caveats: S3 is a national owner survey and reports intended actions, not completed deals.
The survey includes business sizes outside the lower-middle-market lens and does not isolate advertising-material distributors.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-coded transaction databases, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loan records, brokered-sale listings matched to closed deals, and owner interviews on retirement and succession would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that employer owners have materially more stated five-year transfer intent than nonemployers, while closures, uncertainty, and owner dependence remain substantial. The midpoint treats stated owner plans as an upper-bound signal rather than observed deal flow.

records.principal_driver: The service's physical execution requirement preserves an operator role even as AI improves the back office.
records.principal_weakness: Channel substitution away from printed and in-person promotion can shrink the current service basket before operational improvements compound.

caveats: S1 establishes the service taxonomy but not many operating-model assertions used in rho, e, q, and o. Those values are honestly labeled ESTIMATE, so the unsupported details reduce substantiation without creating a hidden scored observation.

The transformation from S2's broad NAICS 541800 occupation mix to a = 0.15 is not reproducible from the cited table. The bridge clearly identifies the population mismatch, adjustment direction, and likely overstatement, and the range is wide enough to expose this uncertainty.
S3 measures stated plans among U.S. employer owners, not completed transfers among lower-middle-market 541870 firms. This limitation and the downward completion adjustment are explicit.

S4 is a mail-channel series applied to a principally non-mail industry. The proxy may miss materially different trends in street, retail, door-to-door, and experiential sampling, as the packet discloses.

The lens says there is no deviation while excluding wholly electronic distribution even though S1 lists electronic methods within the 541870 product set. That is a narrowing that should be disclosed; its net factor effect is indeterminate, so the record does not establish that correcting it would change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
All four factors inherit ESTIMATE status, and the base retention assumption q = 0.50 already caps C at 10. The output is useful as a conditioned screen, not as a substantiated industry measurement.

caveats: a: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure for the narrowed agency segment.
a: The occupation evidence includes government employees and captioners, not solely private legal reporting agencies.
rho: The arbitration case study is a provider-supported example, not a representative industry survey.
rho: State licensing and court rules can produce materially different implementation paths.
e: The Census definition is establishment-based and does not disclose revenue mix or ownership status.
e: Occupational employer shares do not measure the composition of NAICS firms.
s5: The cited acquisition is one disclosed transaction and cannot establish a population rate.
s5: The Census owner-age table is available for employer businesses but was not retrieved at the narrowed industry and size level.
q: The public rate description confirms billing components but does not disclose realized prices, discounts, or margins.
q: Large national vendors and local agencies may have different pricing power.
d5: Occupational employment is not a direct measure of constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed service basket.
d5: The BLS projection includes government and captioning employment and spans a different horizon.
o: Association policy is advocacy rather than an independent forecast.
o: The operator requirement differs across jurisdictions, proceeding types, and whether a transcript is certified.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat and referral-based outsourced legal-service bookings, billed through attendance or appearance charges, per-page transcript charges, and optional expedited, realtime, exhibit, and digital-delivery services.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 561492 combines legal proceeding reporting with live captioning. The legal reporting agency segment is retained because its legal-record requirements, buyers, and repeat booking model are coherent; standalone captioning has different accessibility and media demand drivers.
lens.excluded_activities: Official court employees and other captive government units, standalone broadcast or meeting-captioning providers, document-preparation businesses, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Private agencies furnishing reporter-staffed verbatim deposition, arbitration, hearing, and related certified-transcript services to law firms and corporate legal departments.

caveats: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure for the narrowed agency segment.
The occupation evidence includes government employees and captioners, not solely private legal reporting agencies.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study from legal reporting agencies, with labor hours by live capture, scoping, proofreading, production, scheduling, and billing before and after AI deployment.
a.rationale: O*NET separates capture, proofreading, transcript production, filing, and customer-facing tasks. AI can draft from recorded audio and reduce preparation, formatting, indexing, and agency administration, but it does not remove the need to monitor a live record, identify speakers, resolve inaudibility, administer oaths, and certify an accurate transcript. The estimate assigns exposure only to the supportable portion of that workflow.

caveats: Occupational employment is not a direct measure of constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed service basket.
The BLS projection includes government and captioning employment and spans a different horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Deposition-volume panels, court and arbitration hearing counts, and agency booking data separated by stenographic, digital, remote, and captioning method.
d5.rationale: BLS projects no occupational employment change through its published projection horizon while stating that detailed, accurate legal records continue to be needed and that technology may make work more efficient. For the current legal reporting service basket, that supports a near-flat five-year quantity estimate, with downside for digital recording substitution and upside from matter volume or accessibility demand.

caveats: The Census definition is establishment-based and does not disclose revenue mix or ownership status.
Occupational employer shares do not measure the composition of NAICS firms.
e.change_evidence: A business-directory or establishment microdata review that codes each lower-middle-market NAICS firm by legal reporting, captioning, government-contract, and document-preparation revenue.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition covers verbatim legal reporting and also live captioning. Within the supplied lower-middle-market firm population, most private establishments plausibly sell an external service, but the narrowed lens excludes captioning-led and nonlegal activities. The estimate reflects that boundary rather than treating every coded firm as eligible.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: Association policy is advocacy rather than an independent forecast.
The operator requirement differs across jurisdictions, proceeding types, and whether a transcript is certified.
o.change_evidence: Court and state rule changes, client acceptance data for digital-only records, and audit data comparing admissibility, correction, and turnaround outcomes by record-making method.
o.rationale: The current evidence describes hybrid AI drafting with a certified reporter monitoring, reviewing, and certifying the record, and BLS likewise expects human review and editing to remain needed. Legal-record accuracy, speaker handling, oaths, privacy, and certification support a substantial continuing accountable-operator role, although some matters can shift to digital recording and post-event transcription.

caveats: The public rate description confirms billing components but does not disclose realized prices, discounts, or margins.
Large national vendors and local agencies may have different pricing power.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, rate cards, win-loss records, and cohort gross-margin data comparing AI-assisted matters with comparable conventional matters.
q.rationale: Per-page transcript, appearance, expedited, realtime, exhibit, and digital-delivery charges create several service components whose pricing is not purely cost-plus. This permits some operational benefit retention, but legal buyers can compare vendors and faster AI-enabled production may become an expected service level or reduce transcript prices. The estimate represents retained benefit after that commercial response.

caveats: The arbitration case study is a provider-supported example, not a representative industry survey.
State licensing and court rules can produce materially different implementation paths.
rho.change_evidence: Agency-level adoption and quality data showing the share of matters using AI drafting, reviewer minutes per transcript, error corrections, client acceptance, and the governing state or court rules.
rho.rationale: A deployed arbitration example uses AI to create a draft while a certified reporter monitors, reviews, and certifies the record. That supports practical workflow adoption, but certification, confidentiality, difficult audio, and customer acceptance constrain replacement of live and final-review work. The estimate reflects partial implementation of the exposed opportunity within the horizon.

caveats: The cited acquisition is one disclosed transaction and cannot establish a population rate.
The Census owner-age table is available for employer businesses but was not retrieved at the narrowed industry and size level.
s5.change_evidence: Court-reporting business-broker closed-sale data, sector transaction databases with target counts, and an owner-age and succession survey for private legal reporting agencies.
s5.rationale: Observed acquisitions show that established local court-reporting agencies can transfer to strategic buyers, including a company operating for more than three decades. The probability remains bounded because public announcements are selected examples, many agencies rely on owner and reporter relationships, and no industry-specific five-year control-transfer denominator was found.

records.principal_driver: AI-assisted drafting and production can reduce portions of transcript and agency workflow while preserving a certified human quality-control role.
records.principal_weakness: The core legal-record service has limited observable demand growth and meaningful risk of digital-recording substitution and price competition.

caveats: All seven researched primitives are estimates. The reopened sources support the qualitative direction and boundary choices, but none directly measures the packet's numerical bases for automation exposure, realization, eligible-firm share, transfer probability, retained benefit, five-year service quantity, or operator persistence.
S3 is a provider-supported use case presented by AAA rather than representative adoption or quality evidence, so it establishes feasibility but not the prevalence assumed in rho.
S6 demonstrates that one longstanding agency transferred, while S8 only identifies an owner-age table; together they do not establish the s5 base of 0.18 or a sector-wide five-year transfer rate.

The BLS evidence is occupation-wide, includes government workers and captioners, and covers 2024-2034; translating it into the narrowed private-agency five-year d5 range remains a judgment, and the 0.92 downside may underrepresent the packet's own stated digital-recording substitution risk.
The q base of 0.45 is inferred from fee architecture rather than observed contracts, discounts, margins, or customer response to AI-assisted delivery.

The a and rho rationales both invoke certification, difficult audio, and retained human review. Stage 2 should separate technical task exposure from implementation constraints to avoid discounting the same limitation twice; the record does not establish that resolving this boundary would change the base tier or action, so this is not material under the frozen test.
The legal-reporting-only lens is a disclosed and coherent narrowing of a heterogeneous NAICS code, but the resulting estimates should not be generalized to standalone captioning firms.

caveats: a: No published NAICS 541922 wage-by-task staffing matrix was located.
a: The occupation includes noncommercial photographers and self-employed workers, so it is not a direct industry labor mix.
rho: This is a forecast judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
rho: Rights, brand-safety, and client-specific constraints can vary sharply by vertical and campaign.
e: No public LMM firm-level panel identifies repeat outsourced-service revenue and normalized EBITDA for this NAICS.
e: The injected firm-count input is not re-estimated here.
s5: Occupational replacement openings are not evidence of ownership succession and are used only to frame the need for direct succession evidence.
s5: Private small-business transactions are incompletely disclosed.
q: No public contract dataset quantifies pass-through for NAICS 541922.
q: Retention differs materially between specialized campaign work and commoditized catalog or product imagery.
d5: This is an occupation employment projection, not NAICS 541922 constant-quality demand.
d5: The five-year conversion assumes a smooth projection path and does not measure price changes.

o: High-end, regulated, location-specific, and product-accurate shoots may be more durable than the overall estimate.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers, commonly project, day-rate, usage-license, or negotiated production-fee engagements; repeat work is through recurring campaigns, catalog refreshes, marketplaces, and agency or brand relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer portrait and event photography, freelance artistic or news photography, photofinishing, motion-picture or video production, captive brand studios, and firms outside the roughly $1–10M normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent commercial-photography firms serving advertising agencies, publishers, and other business or industrial customers with still-image production, pre-production, post-production, asset selection, and delivery.

a.bridge: S2 measures the Photographer occupation across commercial, portrait, news, scientific, and other settings rather than NAICS 541922. Its task list is translated to commercial-photography labor: computer-based transfer, editing, retouching, resizing, image selection, scheduling, and client-preparation work are exposed, while on-site capture, lighting, composition, creative direction, and relationship work are retained. The proxy likely understates exposure for studio post-production-heavy firms but overstates it for shoot-intensive firms.
caveats: No published NAICS 541922 wage-by-task staffing matrix was located.
The occupation includes noncommercial photographers and self-employed workers, so it is not a direct industry labor mix.
a.change_evidence: A commercial-photography payroll and time-and-motion sample segmented by shoot, pre-production, retouching, asset management, sales, and administration; interviewable studio operators; and job-level adoption data from Adobe or industry production-management vendors.
a.rationale: O*NET identifies computer editing, retouching, image transfer, image selection, scheduling, and office work alongside capture, lighting, composition, and physical equipment work [S2]. Generative editing and workflow tools can substitute for part of the former group and reduce incremental hiring, but not most on-site creative production; 0.28 is the bounded wage-weighted judgment.

d5.bridge: S3 projects US Photographer employment, all employer types and uses, from 151,200 in 2024 to 154,000 in 2034, a 2% ten-year increase. Applying half of that cumulative rate gives approximately 1.01 for five-year quantity demand in the commercial-service lens. Employment can diverge from constant-price, constant-quality service demand and includes noncommercial work; smartphone and stock-image substitution may make this proxy directionally optimistic for simpler commercial imagery.
caveats: This is an occupation employment projection, not NAICS 541922 constant-quality demand.
The five-year conversion assumes a smooth projection path and does not measure price changes.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar commercial-photography revenue series by client vertical; agency production-spend surveys; stock-image subscription and in-house-content spend; and studio booking-volume records split between campaigns, catalogs, and product imagery.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 2% photographer employment growth over 2024–34 and says corporations will continue to require commercial photographers for advertisements, while smartphones and subscription stock photography may dampen demand [S3]. The central proxy is approximately flat over five years, with a downside for substitution and upside for continuing commercial-image needs.

caveats: No public LMM firm-level panel identifies repeat outsourced-service revenue and normalized EBITDA for this NAICS.
The injected firm-count input is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level financial statements and customer-concentration reports from commercial studios; a broker or M&A database filtered to NAICS 541922; and operator interviews on repeat-contract revenue and management depth.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already directed to commercial services for agencies, publishers, and business or industrial users [S1], but the LMM count can still include project-only studios, owner-operator practices with weak repeat revenue, and businesses whose normalized earnings do not support a transferable operation. The range is a bounded screen-eligibility judgment.

caveats: This is a five-year judgment, not a measured software-substitution share.
High-end, regulated, location-specific, and product-accurate shoots may be more durable than the overall estimate.
o.change_evidence: Brand and agency procurement interviews; booked-shoot data versus stock, user-generated, and generative-image use; client surveys on brand-risk approval; and workflow studies distinguishing physical shoots from post-production-only assignments.
o.rationale: BLS states that corporations will continue to require commercial photographers for advertising, but also identifies smartphone quality and online stock services as substitutes [S3]. O*NET shows that capture, lighting, composition, direct client work, equipment setup, and judgment remain important [S2]. The 0.80 judgment allows for substantial accountable-operator demand while recognizing that simpler image needs can be self-served or software supplied.

caveats: No public contract dataset quantifies pass-through for NAICS 541922.
Retention differs materially between specialized campaign work and commoditized catalog or product imagery.
q.change_evidence: Redacted master service agreements, rate cards, renewal and bid histories, customer-interview evidence on savings sharing, and margin changes at studios before and after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Commercial photography sells differentiated creative output through project, usage, and negotiated production arrangements rather than a regulated cost-plus tariff [S1]. That permits an operator to retain some workflow savings, but agencies and brands can solicit competing bids, bring simple work in-house, or demand lower renewal pricing; 0.57 is the resulting five-year retained-share judgment.

caveats: This is a forecast judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Rights, brand-safety, and client-specific constraints can vary sharply by vertical and campaign.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour records from commercial studios deploying generative retouching or digital-asset workflows; client contract reviews for approval and indemnity clauses; and structured interviews with creative directors, producers, and retouchers.
rho.rationale: Implementation requires reliable brand-consistent output, rights clearance, client approval, asset-system integration, and changed shoot and retouch workflows. The computer-based tasks in S2 support meaningful adoption, while its high-importance creative, client-facing, lighting, and physical tasks constrain five-year realization; 0.48 is a judgment about the exposed portion only.

caveats: Occupational replacement openings are not evidence of ownership succession and are used only to frame the need for direct succession evidence.
Private small-business transactions are incompletely disclosed.
s5.change_evidence: Completed lower-middle-market transaction records from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, or business-broker archives; owner-age and succession responses from a commercial-photography association survey; and state business-sale filings where available.
s5.rationale: The BLS occupation outlook reports 12,700 annual openings over 2024–34, with most from workers leaving or retiring [S3], but that is worker replacement rather than firm-control transfers. A five-year 0.12 probability is therefore a deliberately cautious judgment for eligible firms, after excluding closures and reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: Digitally mediated editing, retouching, image management, and production administration create a bounded but practical workflow opportunity.
records.principal_weakness: Commercial-image demand and pricing are exposed to low-cost substitutes, while the most valuable capture and creative tasks remain operator dependent.

caveats: S1 establishes the industry boundary but provides no quantitative basis for e or q. In particular, the stated pricing forms and the 0.45-0.70 retention range require contract, bid, and margin evidence; q is already labeled ESTIMATE, and lowering C would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

S2 is an occupation-wide task inventory rather than a NAICS 541922 staffing or time-allocation study. The bridge for a names this population mismatch and gives plausible error directions, while rho and o remain explicit estimates, so no evidence state is overstated.

S3's occupational replacement openings do not evidence firm-control transfers and therefore do not constrain the numerical s5 range. The packet says this directly and treats s5 as an ESTIMATE rather than presenting the openings as observed succession.

Using occupation employment growth as d5 and then applying an o discount informed partly by the same substitution discussion creates some risk that substitution is counted in both components of D. Re-estimating that decomposition could move D in either direction, but F remains the weakest link below 1 throughout the registered envelope, so this does not change the base tier or action category.

The quantitative ranges for a, rho, e, s5, q, and o are judgment-heavy and cannot be independently pinned down by S1-S3. This is reflected in MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action rather than concealed as observed evidence.
