caveats: a: The proxy includes self-employed advisers and advisers working outside the target NAICS.
a: No source measures current AI exposure or wage shares for the frozen LMM lens.
rho: This is an operational-adoption judgment, not a forecast of AI vendor revenue or client acceptance.
rho: Implementation will differ sharply between retail advice, institutional portfolio management, and firms with mature compliance teams.
e: Employee count and AUM are weak substitutes for normalized EBITDA.
e: S1 is an investment-adviser population, whereas NAICS 523940 also includes portfolio-management businesses with different economics.
s5: AUM thresholds are not EBITDA thresholds.
s5: Transaction announcements can omit private, internal, and failed processes and do not by themselves identify control transfers.
q: The SEC disclosure source identifies fee schedules and negotiability but does not measure savings pass-through.
q: AUM-fee, flat-fee, commission, and performance-fee models retain savings differently.
d5: Employment is not service quantity and could change with productivity or staffing models.
d5: The forecast is national and not limited to LMM firms or outsourced providers.
o: This is a judgment about the operator requirement, not a claim that software cannot provide investment advice.
o: Institutional clients and mass-affluent retail clients may retain human accountability at different rates.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External individual or institutional clients receiving recurring advice, monitoring, discretionary management, or planning; revenue is commonly an advisory fee, AUM-based fee, retainer, flat fee, commission, or performance-based fee where permitted.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive family offices and internal investment units, shells, control-financing vehicles, execution-only broker activity, and firms without a recurring or repeat external service relationship.
lens.included_activities: LMM operating firms supplying recurring outsourced portfolio-management or investment-advice services to external investor clients.

a.bridge: S2 measures US personal financial advisors, not wage-weighted employment in NAICS 523940. I map its listed recurring tasks of research, account monitoring, explaining options, preparing advice, and answering routine questions to AI-exposed work, while retaining relationship, judgment, sales, and accountable recommendation work as less substitutable. This proxy likely understates exposure for portfolio-management firms with more investment-analysis and reporting operations than the source occupation.
caveats: The proxy includes self-employed advisers and advisers working outside the target NAICS.
No source measures current AI exposure or wage shares for the frozen LMM lens.
a.change_evidence: A 2026 OEWS occupation-by-industry wage file for 523940, paired with time-and-motion interviews at LMM RIAs and portfolio managers that separate advisers, analysts, compliance, and client-service hours.
a.rationale: A material but minority share of present labor can be reduced through research synthesis, meeting preparation, communications, monitoring, reporting, and workflow automation. Client-specific judgment, relationship building, sales, and fiduciary accountability remain substantial wage-bearing work.

d5.bridge: S2 projects US personal-financial-advisor employment to rise 10 percent from 2024 to 2034, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the NAICS 523940 service basket. I convert that ten-year occupation-growth proxy to an approximately five-year demand ratio and widen the range for productivity and non-target employment. It likely overstates codewide demand because the occupation includes self-employed and financial-planning work outside the target industry.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity and could change with productivity or staffing models.
The forecast is national and not limited to LMM firms or outsourced providers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar series for 523940 advisory revenue and client accounts, segmented by retail, institutional, discretionary, and non-discretionary services, plus price-index data for advisory fees.
d5.rationale: Aging clients, longer retirements, and the shift from traditional pensions to individual retirement accounts support ongoing advice demand. Robo-advisors may temper routine demand, while complex or specialized advice continues to require human service.

e.bridge: S1 reports the size distribution of investment advisers, not normalized EBITDA or the frozen NAICS/lens population. I use the reported prevalence of advisers with 100 or fewer employees and the eight-employee average for individual-client advisers as a proxy for the share plausibly inside the LMM operating-firm band after excluding captive and non-service entities. It likely overstates eligibility because a small headcount can still support EBITDA above the band, especially at high AUM.
caveats: Employee count and AUM are weak substitutes for normalized EBITDA.
S1 is an investment-adviser population, whereas NAICS 523940 also includes portfolio-management businesses with different economics.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Form ADV data joined to private-company financials or confidential QoE reports that identify revenue, compensation, EBITDA, client type, and ownership for 523940 businesses.
e.rationale: The adviser market contains many small practices, yet the target EBITDA band excludes very small owner-operator books, high-margin firms with substantial AUM, and entities outside the external recurring-service lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5239 ($166,457,424,125) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5239 ($454,212,096,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5239. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1165 (margins.json[523]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Investments & Asset Management EBITDA/Sales 11.65%); revenue/employee $732,061 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 5239); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a judgment about the operator requirement, not a claim that software cannot provide investment advice.
Institutional clients and mass-affluent retail clients may retain human accountability at different rates.
o.change_evidence: Client-level adoption and attrition data comparing digital-only and human-advised accounts, and supervisory records showing which advice, monitoring, and disclosure duties remain assigned to humans after AI deployment.
o.rationale: Most surviving demand should still need an accountable operator for client discovery, suitability and best-interest obligations, nuanced planning, relationship management, and review of recommendations. Software can eliminate some routine allocation and reporting service, especially for simpler accounts.

caveats: The SEC disclosure source identifies fee schedules and negotiability but does not measure savings pass-through.
AUM-fee, flat-fee, commission, and performance-fee models retain savings differently.
q.change_evidence: A sample of LMM advisory agreements and Form ADV brochures before and after workflow automation, paired with client retention, fee-rate, and service-level changes at renewal.
q.rationale: Recurring advisory arrangements can retain a meaningful share of workflow savings because fees are commonly set in advance and are not automatically cost-plus. Retention is capped by transparent fee schedules, negotiability, client expectations, competitive rebids, and the need to reinvest savings in service and compliance.

caveats: This is an operational-adoption judgment, not a forecast of AI vendor revenue or client acceptance.
Implementation will differ sharply between retail advice, institutional portfolio management, and firms with mature compliance teams.
rho.change_evidence: A representative RIA operations survey showing production use by workflow, documented AI governance policies, and examination or enforcement outcomes involving AI-assisted advice.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should reach routine research, drafting, service, surveillance, and internal knowledge workflows, but regulated advice requires review, recordkeeping, supervision, model validation, and careful client communication. SEC enforcement over misleading AI claims makes unchecked deployment especially unsuitable.

s5.bridge: S5 counts 2025 RIA transactions with at least $100 million AUM, while S1 counts advisers and neither isolates LMM EBITDA firms or only qualifying control transfers. I use the recorded transaction activity as evidence of a functioning transfer market and translate its annual incidence into a conservative five-year probability for eligible firms. The proxy likely overstates the LMM rate because S5 says smaller sellers declined in both count and share and excludes sub-$100 million-AUM deals from its methodology.
caveats: AUM thresholds are not EBITDA thresholds.
Transaction announcements can omit private, internal, and failed processes and do not by themselves identify control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year longitudinal extract of Form ADV ownership changes and Form ADV-W filings matched to transaction announcements, with deal terms coded for control, firm size, and internal versus external succession.
s5.rationale: RIA transactions are active, but the observed market is concentrated in larger sellers and repeat acquirers. A minority of qualifying LMM firms should transfer control within five years; internal succession, client portability, founder dependence, and buyer selectivity limit the rate.

records.principal_driver: Recurring advice relationships and substantial research, service, monitoring, and documentation workflows create a credible but bounded operating-improvement opportunity.
records.principal_weakness: Public evidence does not isolate the LMM transferable-firm subset, and fiduciary accountability plus founder/client dependence limit both automation and transferability.

caveats: The record calls the lens non-heterogeneous even though its own narrative distinguishes retail advice from institutional portfolio management and acknowledges sharply different implementation and operator-retention behavior. The combined lens is disclosed and coherent enough for screening, but the heterogeneous=false flag understates within-lens variation.

The q range of 0.50-0.78 maps entirely to the C-factor cap of 10, so it does not express score sensitivity to plausible pass-through below the cap. S3 establishes fee disclosure and negotiability but supplies no empirical anchor for the numeric retention range; the packet honestly labels q ESTIMATE and says what evidence would change it.

S1 and S5 verify a large small-adviser population and an active RIA transaction market, but the conversions to e=0.48 and s5=0.10 remain broad model judgments because neither source identifies the frozen EBITDA band or qualifying control transfers. The PROXY labels, bridges, and caveats disclose this limitation.

S2 concerns personal financial advisers and employment, not the full retail-and-institutional 523940 lens, wage-weighted AI exposure, or constant-quality service demand. The a and d5 ranges therefore remain proxy sensitivity rather than direct measurements, as disclosed.
S3 and S4 provide regulatory and enforcement context but do not empirically measure rho or o. Those values are correctly labeled ESTIMATE, but their numeric ranges remain judgment-heavy.
The missing n anchor eliminates the F base, base tier, and economic action beyond EVIDENCE_FIRST; the interval therefore serves only as a stress-test envelope and cannot establish current priority.

caveats: a: No current occupation or task mix is published for this suppressed and unusually narrow NAICS industry.
a: The legal-profession proxy includes lawyers and paralegals with work unlike this lens.
rho: The state-law evidence establishes permitted technology and controls, not actual AI adoption or savings in this NAICS.
rho: Requirements vary by state and document type.
e: The frozen n input is unavailable by instruction and must remain dataset-injected MISSING.
e: NAICS classification alone does not establish recurring revenue or normalized EBITDA.
s5: General small-business exit statistics would not distinguish transfers from closures or internal reorganizations.
q: Florida's fee rule is not a national fee survey and does not govern non-notarial document work.
q: Actual fee structure and competition vary by state and customer type.
d5: Legal-services employment is a broader population and is not a volume measure.
d5: A direct five-year forecast for this NAICS was not found.
o: The evidence is about remote notarization generally, while this NAICS excludes signature-only notary services and includes broader document work.
o: Future changes in state law or identity technology could alter the operator requirement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers pay per document matter or under a repeat service relationship; this screen excludes one-off consumer stamp-only work unless the firm also supplies the NAICS-defined document service on a repeat outsourced basis.
lens.excluded_activities: Law firms, title abstract and settlement offices, internal corporate units, financing vehicles, and signature-only notary public services that NAICS places in 541199.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent offices that repeatedly provide external customers with the NAICS-defined services of drafting, approving, executing, receiving, indexing, or storing legal documents.

a.bridge: S2 reports that a legal-profession study estimated 44 percent of tasks susceptible to automation, rather than measuring NAICS 541120. I translate that legal-services proxy downward for the required human identity, execution, and custody work in the NAICS definition; the proxy likely overstates substitution for this industry.
caveats: No current occupation or task mix is published for this suppressed and unusually narrow NAICS industry.
The legal-profession proxy includes lawyers and paralegals with work unlike this lens.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-and-occupation survey of 541120 offices, including time spent on document drafting, custody, customer intake, and formal execution, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Document intake, classification, drafting support, retrieval, and routine correspondence are exposed, but execution and accountability require human review. The base is below the cited legal-services task proxy.

d5.bridge: S2 projects legal-services employment growth of 1.6 percent from 2023 to 2033, not constant-price demand for NAICS 541120. I use this slow legal-services trend as a five-year directional proxy for the current document-service basket; it may overstate demand if remote platforms displace operators, or understate it if document activity grows faster than legal employment.
caveats: Legal-services employment is a broader population and is not a volume measure.
A direct five-year forecast for this NAICS was not found.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar revenue and transaction series specifically for 541120, split among real-estate, estate, contract, and record-custody matters, would materially change this estimate.
d5.rationale: The available legal-services proxy indicates near-flat long-run activity. The range allows for document-transaction cyclicality and substitution by remote platforms while retaining the need for legal-document processing.

caveats: The frozen n input is unavailable by instruction and must remain dataset-injected MISSING.
NAICS classification alone does not establish recurring revenue or normalized EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue, EBITDA, customer-concentration, and repeat-revenue data from a business-information vendor or operator interviews would permit an eligibility estimate.
e.rationale: No defensible count identifies which 541120 firms are both in the normalized EBITDA band and provide a recurring or repeat outsourced service under the frozen lens.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5411. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The evidence is about remote notarization generally, while this NAICS excludes signature-only notary services and includes broader document work.
Future changes in state law or identity technology could alter the operator requirement.
o.change_evidence: State-level records showing remote-notarization volumes, platform market share, operator staffing per transaction, and acceptance by end users would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: A large share should still require an accountable commissioned operator because remote notarization retains personal appearance, identity verification, credential analysis, proofing, recording, and security controls. Software can remove coordination and document handling from some current work, so this is below full retention.

caveats: Florida's fee rule is not a national fee survey and does not govern non-notarial document work.
Actual fee structure and competition vary by state and customer type.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, realized price changes after workflow automation, and state-by-state fee schedules from a representative set of operators would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Per-matter billing can initially retain some productivity benefit, but standardized technology, low switching friction, and regulated notarial pricing constrain durable retention. Florida caps the online notarial-act fee at $25 while allowing separate provider charges, illustrating both pricing constraint and partial pass-through risk.

caveats: The state-law evidence establishes permitted technology and controls, not actual AI adoption or savings in this NAICS.
Requirements vary by state and document type.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of 541120 operators on remote-notarization adoption, AI workflow use, error rates, and time savings, segmented by state, would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Remote delivery is legally available in most states, enabling workflow change, but legal accuracy, identity proofing, audiovisual records, credential analysis, and security rules leave a meaningful human-controlled implementation boundary.

caveats: General small-business exit statistics would not distinguish transfers from closures or internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal business-owner dataset matched to 541120 firms, or transaction and succession records from specialist M&A intermediaries, would permit an estimate.
s5.rationale: No defensible source measures five-year qualifying control transfers for eligible 541120 firms, and the public evidence found does not identify owner succession for this population.

records.principal_driver: Document-processing workflows have real automation exposure while the formal act remains accountable and regulated.
records.principal_weakness: There is no defensible public evidence for eligible LMM-firm share or five-year qualifying transfer probability in this narrow NAICS.

caveats: S1 is an archived 1997 NAICS page. It directly supports the classification facts used here, but the packet does not establish from a current NAICS source that the wording remains unchanged.

The a proxy is drawn from the much broader legal profession, particularly lawyer and paralegal work. The downward translation to 0.38 is transparent but remains judgment rather than industry-specific task evidence.

The rho and o rationales use remote-notarization authorization and required controls to inform operational feasibility and operator retention, but those sources do not measure AI adoption, savings, staffing, or transaction substitution in 541120.

The d5 range is comparatively narrow for a translation from broad legal-services employment to constant-price demand in this unusually sparse narrow industry; legal employment is neither transaction volume nor revenue demand.
S5 is a single-state rule for the notarial act, while the registered lens includes broader document services and a national operator population, so q remains a weak but honestly labeled estimate.

The lens excludes one-off stamp-only work consistently with the NAICS cross-reference and discloses its repeat-service focus. It is coherent for the roll-up screen, but the packet provides no evidence on how much of the already narrow 541120 population actually has that revenue model.
